The ABC framework (Airway, Breathing, Circulation) is grounded in the physiological principle that organ systems have varying tolerances to oxygen deprivation. The brain sustains irreversible damage within 4-6 minutes of oxygen deprivation, making airway and breathing the highest priorities in any clinical situation. Without a patent airway, no amount of breathing effort is effective. Without adequate breathing, circulation of deoxygenated blood provides no benefit. Without circulation, even well-oxygenated blood cannot reach the tissues.

This hierarchy directly mirrors Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs applied to clinical nursing. Physiological needs (oxygen, perfusion, thermoregulation) must be addressed before safety needs (fall prevention, infection control), which must be addressed before psychosocial needs (anxiety, education, spiritual care). On the licensing exam, examinations and in clinical practice, ABCs override all other considerations when a patient is acutely deteriorating.

The primary survey (ABCDE: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) provides a systematic approach to identifying and treating life-threatening conditions in order of physiological priority. Each step is addressed sequentially, and any abnormality found is treated before moving to the next letter. A compromised airway is always addressed first, regardless of other dramatic findings such as active bleeding or altered consciousness.
diagnostics: Airway: Look for chest rise, listen for air movement, feel for breath on cheek
Airway: Assess for stridor, gurgling, snoring, or silent chest
Breathing: Assess respiratory rate, depth, pattern, and effort
Breathing: Auscultate bilateral lung sounds for symmetry and adventitious sounds
Circulation: Assess heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color/temperature
Circulation: Palpate central and peripheral pulses for rate, rhythm, and quality
management: Airway obstruction: Head-tilt chin-lift (trauma: jaw thrust only), suction, oropharyngeal or nasopharyngeal airway
Breathing failure: Apply high-flow oxygen, assist ventilations with bag-valve-mask if apneic
Circulatory failure: Control hemorrhage with direct pressure, establish IV access, administer fluid bolus as ordered
Position patient appropriately: high-Fowler's for respiratory distress, supine with legs elevated for shock
Prepare for advanced airway management if basic maneuvers fail
Activate rapid response or code team when ABCs cannot be stabilized with initial interventions
nursingActions: Always assess airway FIRST - before blood pressure, pain, or any other parameter
Clear the airway: suction visible secretions, position for optimal airway opening
If the patient can speak in full sentences, the airway is patent and breathing is adequate
Assess for signs of tension pneumothorax: tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds, JVD, hypotension
Apply pulse oximetry continuously for any patient with respiratory or circulatory compromise
Document ABC assessment findings and interventions in real-time

unstable-vs-stable.title: Dengesiz vs Denge: Önceliklendirme Çerçevesi
unstable-vs-stable.pathophysiology:
The ability to differentiate stable from unstable patients is one of the most critical clinical reasoning skills in nursing. An unstable patient is one whose condition is actively deteriorating, whose vital signs are trending in a dangerous direction, or who presents with new-onset symptoms that may indicate a life-threatening process. A stable patient has a predictable clinical course, vital signs within an acceptable range for their condition, and symptoms that are expected given their diagnosis.

Key indicators of instability include: new-onset symptoms that were not previously present, acute changes from the patient's baseline, vital sign parameters outside safe ranges, signs of organ hypoperfusion (altered mental status, decreased urine output, mottled skin), and symptoms that are worsening despite current interventions. The word 'new' is critical - a patient with chronic stable heart failure who reports their usual dyspnea with exertion is stable, but a patient with heart failure who develops new-onset resting dyspnea is unstable.

Expected findings are symptoms or assessment data that are consistent with the patient's known diagnosis, current treatment plan, and stage of recovery. Unexpected findings are new symptoms, worsening trends, or assessment data that deviate from the anticipated clinical trajectory. Every unexpected finding warrants immediate assessment and potential intervention. The nurse must constantly compare the patient's current status to what is expected and recognize when the clinical picture deviates from the norm.
diagnostics: Compare current vital signs to patient's established baseline (not just textbook normals)
Assess for new-onset symptoms not present on previous assessment
Evaluate level of consciousness using AVPU (Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive) or GCS
Monitor trends over time rather than relying on single-point-in-time assessments
Assess for signs of end-organ perfusion: mental status, urine output, skin color and temperature
Review current medications and treatments for potential contribution to clinical changes
management: Unstable patients: Immediate bedside assessment, notify provider, prepare for escalation
Assess ABCs for any patient identified as potentially unstable
Increase monitoring frequency for patients showing early signs of instability
Implement standing orders or protocols for acute changes (e.g., hypoglycemia protocol, chest pain protocol)
Prepare for potential transfer to higher level of care (ICU)
Document timeline of changes, interventions, and patient responses
nursingActions: Always prioritize the unstable patient over stable patients, regardless of scheduled tasks
Report any NEW onset of chest pain, dyspnea, altered mental status, or hemodynamic changes
Reassess the patient after every intervention to determine if stability is improving or worsening
Compare assessment findings to the last documented assessment to identify trends
Use clinical judgment to differentiate between expected post-procedural findings and complications
Communicate urgency appropriately when reporting unstable findings to the provider

who-to-see-first.title: Öncelikle Kim Görülmeli: Klinik Karar Çerçevesi
who-to-see-first.pathophysiology:
The 'Who to See First' question is one of the most frequently tested clinical judgment domains on licensing examinations and one of the most critical skills in daily nursing practice. When multiple patients require attention simultaneously, the nurse must rapidly assess each situation and determine the order of priority based on clinical acuity, potential for deterioration, and the ABCs/Maslow framework.

The priority patient is always the one with the greatest threat to life or the highest potential for rapid clinical deterioration. This is determined by applying a systematic framework: (1) Identify any airway, breathing, or circulation threats - these are always first; (2) Identify any new-onset, unexpected, or acute findings - these take priority over chronic or expected findings; (3) Identify patients who are at risk for complications if not assessed promptly; (4) Address routine needs and scheduled tasks last.

CommOn the licensing exam, distractors include patients with concerning but stable chronic conditions, expected post-procedural findings, and psychosocial needs. While all patients deserve attention, the nurse must recognize that a patient with acute respiratory distress takes priority over a patient with chronic pain, and a patient with signs of hemorrhage takes priority over a patient requesting a sleeping pill. The key principle is that unstable, acute, and life-threatening conditions are always assessed before stable, chronic, and non-urgent conditions.
diagnostics: Rapidly assess all patients using a brief visual and verbal check at start of shift
Identify which patients have the highest acuity based on diagnosis, interventions, and stability
Review overnight events and recent trend data before making prioritization decisions
Apply ABC framework to any acute situation requiring immediate triage
Assess which patients are at greatest risk for clinical deterioration within the next hour
Consider which patients have time-sensitive needs (medications, procedures, discharge)
management: See the patient with the most acute or life-threatening condition first
Delegate appropriate tasks to UAP to manage stable patients while addressing critical needs
Reassess priorities throughout the shift as patient conditions change
Use SBAR to communicate with the healthcare team about changes in priority
Document the rationale for prioritization decisions when they impact care delivery
Escalate when patient acuity exceeds safe staffing capacity
nursingActions: ABCs first: always see the patient with airway, breathing, or circulation compromise first
New onset > chronic: see the patient with new symptoms before the patient with stable chronic conditions
Unstable > stable: assess deteriorating patients before patients with predictable courses
Acute pain > chronic pain: but both are secondary to ABC emergencies
Post-procedure complications > routine post-procedure checks
Time-sensitive interventions (blood transfusion reaction, chest pain protocol) supersede scheduled tasks

delegation-rules-scope.title: Delegasyon Kuralları & Uygulama Kapsamı
delegation-rules-scope.pathophysiology:
Delegation is the transfer of responsibility for performing a nursing activity to another person while retaining accountability for the outcome. The delegating nurse remains legally accountable for the appropriateness of the delegation decision, for supervising the delegated task, and for evaluating the outcome. Understanding scope of practice and delegation rules is essential for patient safety and legal protection.

The Five Rights of Delegation provide a framework for safe delegation decisions: Right Task (the activity is within the delegate's scope and competency), Right Circumstance (the patient's condition is stable and predictable enough for the task to be safely delegated), Right Person (the delegate has the appropriate training, skills, and legal authority), Right Direction/Communication (clear, specific instructions are given including expected findings to report), and Right Supervision/Evaluation (the delegating nurse follows up to ensure the task was completed correctly and the patient responded appropriately).

Scope of practice varies by licensure level. Registered Nurses (RNs) can perform comprehensive assessment, develop nursing care plans, administer all medications (including IV push and blood products), provide patient education, and delegate to RPNs/LPNs and UAPs. Registered Practical Nurses/Licensed Practical Nurses (RPNs/LPNs) work under RN supervision for complex patients and can perform focused assessments, administer most medications (with jurisdictional variations for IV push), reinforce education, and delegate to UAPs. Unregulated/Unlicensed Assistive Personnel (UAPs/PSWs/CNAs) can perform activities of daily living, vital signs on stable patients, specimen collection, and routine tasks that do not require clinical judgment.
diagnostics: Determine the stability and predictability of the patient's condition
Verify the delegate's licensure level and scope of practice
Assess the delegate's demonstrated competency for the specific task
Evaluate the complexity of the task and required level of clinical judgment
Consider the availability of supervision and support
Review facility policy and jurisdictional regulations regarding delegation
management: Apply the Five Rights of Delegation before every delegation decision
Provide clear, specific verbal and/or written instructions for delegated tasks
Include specific parameters for when the delegate must report findings immediately
Follow up within an appropriate timeframe to evaluate task completion and patient response
Document delegation decisions, instructions given, and evaluation of outcomes
Intervene immediately if the delegate reports unexpected findings or if the patient's condition changes
nursingActions: NEVER delegate: Initial assessment, care planning, patient education, evaluation of outcomes, or unstable patient care
RN can delegate to RPN/LPN: focused assessments, medication administration (within scope), reinforcement of established teaching
RN can delegate to UAP: vital signs on stable patients, ADLs, I&O measurement, specimen collection, ambulation assistance
UAP must report to the nurse: any change in patient condition, vital signs outside normal range, patient complaints, refusal of care
Always verify task completion - delegation does not transfer accountability
Document what was delegated, to whom, instructions given, and follow-up evaluation

sbar-escalation.title: SBAR Yükseltme: Ne Zaman & Nasıl Yükseltmeli
sbar-escalation.pathophysiology:
Failure to rescue - the inability to recognize and respond to clinical deterioration in a timely manner - is a leading cause of preventable hospital deaths. Studies consistently demonstrate that patients who experience cardiac arrest, respiratory failure, or unexpected ICU transfer display warning signs hours before the event. The nurse's ability to recognize these warning signs and communicate effectively with the healthcare team is the critical link between early detection and successful intervention.

SBAR (Situation-Background-Assessment-Recommendation) is the standardized communication framework that structures clinical communication to ensure essential information is conveyed clearly and completely. When escalating a concern about a deteriorating patient, the SBAR format reduces ambiguity, prevents important details from being omitted, and empowers the nurse to make a specific recommendation rather than simply reporting data.

Escalation pathways exist for situations when the initial provider response is inadequate or when the nurse has ongoing concerns despite communication. The chain of command (bedside nurse → charge nurse → nursing supervisor → medical director → chief of staff) provides a structured escalation pathway that nurses are legally and ethically obligated to use when patient safety is at risk. Failure to escalate when a patient is deteriorating can constitute negligence, even if the nurse communicated with one provider who chose not to act.
diagnostics: Monitor for early warning signs: increasing heart rate trend, rising respiratory rate, subtle mental status changes
Calculate Early Warning Scores (NEWS2, MEWS) to quantify deterioration objectively
Assess for new-onset symptoms that were not present during previous assessment
Compare current status to expected clinical trajectory for the patient's condition
Review recent medication administration for potential adverse effects
Assess for signs of systemic deterioration: new confusion, decreased urine output, cool extremities
management: Formulate SBAR communication before making the call - be organized and concise
State the situation and your level of concern clearly at the beginning of the conversation
Provide your clinical assessment, not just raw data - tell the provider what you think is happening
Make a specific recommendation - 'I think the patient needs X' is more effective than 'What would you like me to do?'
If the provider's response does not address your concern, respectfully advocate and escalate
Document every communication including: time, provider name, information shared, response, and orders received
nursingActions: Prepare before calling: have vital signs, assessment data, relevant labs, current medications, and allergies ready
S: 'This is [name], RN on [unit]. I am calling about [patient] in room [X]. The situation is [specific concern].'
B: 'The patient was admitted for [diagnosis]. Relevant history includes [PMH]. Current treatment is [medications/interventions].'
A: 'I have assessed the patient. My concern is [clinical judgment]. I believe this may be [suspected problem].'
R: 'I would like you to [specific action]. Would you like me to [specific intervention] in the meantime?'
If inadequate response: 'I remain concerned about this patient's safety. I need to escalate this through the chain of command.'

post-op-prioritization.title: Post-Op Komplikasyonlar: Önceliklendirme
post-op-prioritization.pathophysiology:
Post-operative complications follow a predictable temporal pattern that guides nursing assessment priorities. Understanding WHEN complications are most likely to occur allows the nurse to focus assessments on the highest-risk complications for each post-operative timeframe. Failure to recognize early complications can result in rapid deterioration, reoperation, prolonged hospitalization, or death.

Immediate post-operative complications (0-24 hours) are primarily related to the effects of anesthesia and the surgical procedure itself. Airway compromise from residual anesthesia, hemorrhage from surgical site bleeding, and hemodynamic instability from fluid shifts are the primary threats during this period. The nurse must assess ABCs frequently, monitor for active bleeding (surgical site, drains, vital sign trends), and ensure adequate pain management without respiratory depression.

Early post-operative complications (24-72 hours) include atelectasis (the most common complication), deep vein thrombosis (DVT) development, paralytic ileus, urinary retention, and surgical site infection onset. The shift from immediate to early complications requires a change in assessment focus from anesthesia recovery to systemic complications of immobility, inflammation, and surgical stress.

Late post-operative complications (72 hours to weeks) include wound dehiscence, evisceration, pulmonary embolism (typically days 5-7), pneumonia, and delayed surgical site infections. These complications often develop insidiously and require vigilant ongoing assessment even as the patient appears to be improving.
diagnostics: Immediate (0-24h): Vital signs every 15 min × 4, then every 30 min × 2, then every hour × 4
Immediate: Assess airway patency, respiratory rate and depth, oxygen saturation continuously
Immediate: Monitor surgical site and drains for output (color, amount, consistency)
Early (24-72h): Auscultate lung sounds every shift - assess for atelectasis (diminished breath sounds at bases)
Early: Assess for DVT signs (calf pain, warmth, swelling, positive Homans sign)
Late (72h+): Assess wound for dehiscence (separation), evisceration (organ protrusion), and infection signs
management: Immediate: Maintain airway, monitor for hemorrhage, manage pain while preventing respiratory depression
Early: Incentive spirometry every 1-2 hours while awake, early ambulation, DVT prophylaxis as ordered
Early: Monitor for return of bowel function (bowel sounds, flatus) before advancing diet
Late: Assess wound healing, monitor for signs of infection (redness, warmth, drainage, fever)
Late: Continue DVT prophylaxis and encourage mobility throughout hospitalization
All phases: Strict I&O monitoring, adequate nutrition, pain management, and fall prevention
nursingActions: Priority assessment for IMMEDIATE post-op: Airway → Breathing → Circulation → Surgical site → Pain
Report active bleeding: increasing drain output, saturated dressings, tachycardia, hypotension
Report respiratory depression: RR < 10, SpO2 < 92%, excessive sedation (Ramsay > 4)
Enforce incentive spirometry: 10 breaths every 1-2 hours while awake to prevent atelectasis
Report signs of wound dehiscence/evisceration: if evisceration occurs, cover with sterile saline-moistened gauze, position supine with knees bent, notify surgeon STAT
Report signs of PE: sudden dyspnea, chest pain, tachycardia, hypoxemia (typically post-op days 5-7)

psoriasis.title: Psoriasis
psoriasis.pathophysiology: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune inflammatory skin disorder driven by T-cell mediated immune dysregulation. Activated T-helper cells release cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-17, IL-23) that accelerate keratinocyte proliferation from a normal 28-day cell turnover cycle to 3-4 days. This rapid epidermal turnover causes accumulation of immature skin cells on the surface, forming thick silvery-white scales over erythematous plaques. The inflammatory cascade also causes dermal capillary dilation and increased blood flow, producing the characteristic erythematous base beneath scales. Koebner phenomenon occurs when new psoriatic lesions develop at sites of skin trauma.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on characteristic plaque appearance
Monitor for Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding when scales removed)
Expect skin biopsy if diagnosis uncertain
Monitor for psoriatic arthritis symptoms (joint pain, stiffness)
Assess Psoriasis Area and Severity Index (PASI) score
management: Apply topical corticosteroids as prescribed for mild-moderate disease
Administer topical vitamin D analogues (calcipotriene) as ordered
Coordinate phototherapy (UVB) sessions as ordered
Administer systemic biologics as ordered for moderate-severe disease
Apply emollients liberally to maintain skin moisture
Avoid known triggers (stress, skin trauma, infections)
nursingActions: Assess skin for extent, location, and severity of plaques
Educate on proper application of topical medications (thin layer)
Monitor for side effects of systemic therapy (immunosuppression, hepatotoxicity)
Provide emotional support for body image concerns and chronic disease management
Teach trigger avoidance strategies (stress management, skin protection)
Report signs of psoriatic arthritis (joint swelling, morning stiffness)
Educate on importance of medication adherence for flare prevention

eczema-atopic-dermatitis.title: Egzama (Atopik Dermatit)
eczema-atopic-dermatitis.pathophysiology: Atopic dermatitis is a chronic relapsing inflammatory skin condition caused by impaired epidermal barrier function, often due to filaggrin gene mutations. Filaggrin deficiency disrupts the stratum corneum, allowing increased transepidermal water loss and penetration of environmental allergens and irritants. This triggers a Th2-dominant immune response with elevated IgE production and release of inflammatory mediators (IL-4, IL-13, IL-31). IL-31 specifically stimulates sensory nerve fibers, causing intense pruritus. Chronic scratching leads to lichenification (thickened, leathery skin). The condition is part of the atopic triad with asthma and allergic rhinitis.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on history and characteristic distribution
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization)
Expect elevated serum IgE in severe cases
Monitor skin integrity at each assessment
Expect allergy testing if triggers are unclear
Assess for signs of herpes simplex superinfection (eczema herpeticum)
management: Apply emollients liberally within 3 minutes of bathing (soak-and-seal method)
Administer topical corticosteroids as prescribed for acute flares
Use lukewarm water for short baths (5-10 minutes)
Avoid known triggers (harsh soaps, wool, extreme temperatures)
Keep fingernails short to prevent excoriation
Dress in soft cotton clothing
nursingActions: Assess skin for erythema, dryness, scaling, excoriation, and lichenification
Educate on soak-and-seal technique (emollient within 3 minutes of bathing)
Report signs of secondary infection (weeping, crusting, increased erythema, warmth)
Teach trigger identification and avoidance strategies
Monitor for skin thinning from prolonged topical corticosteroid use
Provide emotional support for chronic condition and body image concerns
Educate caregivers on bathing and moisturizing routines for pediatric patients

contact-dermatitis.title: Kontakt Dermatit
contact-dermatitis.pathophysiology: Contact dermatitis occurs in two forms. Allergic contact dermatitis is a Type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction where sensitized T-cells recognize a hapten (small molecule bound to skin proteins) upon re-exposure, triggering inflammatory cytokine release and tissue damage within 24-72 hours. Irritant contact dermatitis results from direct chemical or physical damage to the epidermal barrier without immune sensitization, causing keratinocyte injury, cytokine release, and inflammation. Both forms lead to erythema, edema, vesicles, and pruritus at the contact site. Common allergens include poison ivy (urushiol), nickel, and latex.
diagnostics: Expect patch testing to identify specific allergen in allergic type
Monitor distribution pattern to determine causative agent
Expect clinical history and physical examination for diagnosis
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection of excoriated areas
Assess occupational and environmental exposure history
management: Remove and avoid the offending agent (primary intervention)
Apply cool compresses for acute weeping lesions
Administer topical corticosteroids as prescribed
Administer oral antihistamines as ordered for pruritus
Apply barrier creams for occupational prevention
Use protective gloves appropriate for the irritant
nursingActions: Assess distribution pattern of lesions to help identify causative agent
Educate on identification and avoidance of triggering substances
Report signs of secondary infection (increased warmth, purulent drainage)
Teach proper use of protective equipment for occupational exposures
Apply cool compresses and medications as ordered for symptom relief
Document detailed exposure history for allergen identification
Educate on cross-reactivity (e.g., poison ivy, oak, sumac all contain urushiol)

cellulitis.title: Selülit
cellulitis.pathophysiology: Cellulitis is an acute spreading bacterial infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, most commonly caused by Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A Strep) and Staphylococcus aureus. Bacteria enter through breaks in the skin (wounds, fissures, insect bites, surgical sites) and release enzymes (hyaluronidase, streptokinase) that break down connective tissue, allowing rapid lateral spread through tissue planes. The resulting inflammatory response causes vasodilation, increased capillary permeability, and neutrophil migration, producing diffuse erythema, warmth, edema, and tenderness without a well-defined border. If untreated, bacteria may enter the bloodstream causing bacteremia and sepsis.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on physical examination findings
Monitor CBC for elevated WBC indicating systemic infection
Expect blood cultures if systemic signs present (fever, tachycardia)
Monitor wound for expansion by marking borders with skin marker
Expect ultrasound if abscess suspected
Monitor serial vital signs for sepsis indicators
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered (oral for mild, IV for severe)
Elevate affected extremity to reduce edema
Mark borders of erythema with skin marker to track progression
Apply warm compresses as ordered for comfort
Administer analgesics as ordered for pain management
Monitor for signs of compartment syndrome in severe cases
nursingActions: Assess and document erythema borders, warmth, edema, and tenderness
Mark the advancing edge of erythema with a skin marker and time/date
Monitor vital signs for signs of systemic infection (fever, tachycardia, hypotension)
Elevate affected extremity above heart level
Administer antibiotics on time as ordered
Report expanding erythema beyond marked borders
Assess and treat underlying portal of entry (tinea pedis, wound)

herpes-zoster-shingles.title: Herpes Zoster (Zona)
herpes-zoster-shingles.pathophysiology: Herpes zoster results from reactivation of latent varicella-zoster virus (VZV) residing in dorsal root ganglia following primary varicella (chickenpox) infection. Declining cell-mediated immunity (aging, immunosuppression, stress) allows viral replication and migration along sensory nerve fibers to the skin, causing intense inflammation of the nerve and surrounding dermatome. This produces a characteristic unilateral vesicular eruption confined to a single dermatome, preceded by prodromal pain, burning, or tingling 2-3 days before the rash appears. Postherpetic neuralgia (PHN) occurs when nerve damage from inflammation causes persistent neuropathic pain lasting months to years after the rash resolves.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on characteristic dermatomal rash
Monitor for vesicle progression and crusting timeline
Expect Tzanck smear or PCR for atypical presentations
Monitor pain severity using standardized pain scale
Assess for signs of disseminated zoster in immunocompromised patients
Monitor for ophthalmic involvement (herpes zoster ophthalmicus)
management: Administer antiviral therapy as ordered within 72 hours of rash onset
Administer analgesics as ordered for pain management
Apply calamine lotion or cool compresses for comfort
Keep vesicles clean and covered to prevent secondary infection
Implement contact precautions until lesions are crusted over
Administer gabapentin or pregabalin as ordered for postherpetic neuralgia
nursingActions: Assess rash distribution, vesicle stage, and pain level
Administer antivirals within 72 hours of rash onset as ordered (emphasize time-sensitive)
Implement airborne and contact precautions for immunocompromised or disseminated cases
Keep lesions loosely covered and avoid occlusive dressings
Monitor for complications (postherpetic neuralgia, ophthalmic involvement, secondary infection)
Educate that the patient can transmit varicella (not shingles) to non-immune individuals
Report vesicles near the eye immediately (risk of corneal damage)

acne-vulgaris.title: Akne Vulgaris
acne-vulgaris.pathophysiology: Acne vulgaris results from four key pathogenic factors: excess sebum production driven by androgens, follicular hyperkeratinization, colonization by Cutibacterium acnes (formerly Propionibacterium acnes), and inflammation. Androgens stimulate sebaceous glands to produce excessive sebum. Abnormal keratinization of the follicular epithelium causes obstruction of the pilosebaceous unit, forming a microcomedone. Trapped sebum creates an anaerobic environment favoring C. acnes proliferation. C. acnes metabolizes sebum triglycerides into free fatty acids and activates innate immune receptors (TLR-2), triggering release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, TNF-alpha) and neutrophil recruitment, causing inflammatory papules, pustules, nodules, and cysts.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on lesion morphology and distribution
Monitor lesion types (comedones, papules, pustules, nodules, cysts)
Expect hormonal evaluation if signs of hyperandrogenism present
Monitor treatment response over 6-8 weeks
Expect pregnancy testing before isotretinoin therapy
management: Apply topical retinoids as prescribed (first-line for comedonal acne)
Apply benzoyl peroxide as prescribed to reduce C. acnes colonization
Administer oral antibiotics as ordered for moderate inflammatory acne
Administer isotretinoin as ordered for severe nodulocystic acne
Avoid picking or squeezing lesions to prevent scarring
Use non-comedogenic skin care products
nursingActions: Assess acne severity and distribution (face, chest, back)
Educate on proper application of topical medications (pea-sized amount)
Warn about photosensitivity with retinoids and tetracyclines
Monitor for isotretinoin side effects (teratogenicity, mood changes, dry skin)
Educate that improvement takes 6-8 weeks with consistent treatment
Provide emotional support for body image and self-esteem concerns
Educate female patients on iPLEDGE program requirements for isotretinoin

melanoma.title: Melanom
melanoma.pathophysiology: Melanoma is a malignant neoplasm arising from melanocytes, the pigment-producing cells in the epidermis. Ultraviolet (UV) radiation causes direct DNA damage (cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers) and mutations in key oncogenes (BRAF V600E in ~50% of cases) and tumor suppressor genes (CDKN2A/p16). These mutations lead to uncontrolled melanocyte proliferation, resistance to apoptosis, and eventual invasion through the basement membrane into the dermis. Once dermal invasion occurs, melanoma cells gain access to lymphatic and vascular channels, enabling regional and distant metastasis to lymph nodes, lungs, liver, brain, and bone. Breslow depth (tumor thickness in millimeters) is the most important prognostic factor.
diagnostics: Expect excisional biopsy of suspicious lesion (full-thickness preferred)
Monitor regional lymph nodes for enlargement
Expect sentinel lymph node biopsy for staging
Expect imaging (CT, PET, MRI brain) for staging of advanced disease
Monitor using ABCDE criteria at each skin assessment
Expect lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) if elevated, indicating advanced disease
management: Prepare patient for wide local excision with appropriate margins
Coordinate sentinel lymph node biopsy as ordered
Administer immunotherapy as ordered for advanced disease (checkpoint inhibitors)
Administer targeted therapy as ordered for BRAF-mutant melanoma
Educate on lifelong sun protection and skin surveillance
Coordinate multidisciplinary follow-up care
nursingActions: Perform thorough skin assessment using ABCDE criteria
Document location, size, color, and borders of suspicious lesions with photographs
Educate on monthly self-skin examinations
Teach sun protection measures (sunscreen SPF 30+, protective clothing, avoid peak UV hours)
Monitor surgical site for infection and healing post-excision
Provide emotional support after cancer diagnosis
Educate on importance of regular dermatology follow-up

basal-cell-carcinoma.title: Bazal Hücreli Karsinom
basal-cell-carcinoma.pathophysiology: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common skin cancer, arising from the basal layer keratinocytes of the epidermis. Chronic cumulative ultraviolet radiation exposure causes mutations in the PTCH1 tumor suppressor gene within the hedgehog signaling pathway, leading to uncontrolled basal cell proliferation. BCC is locally invasive and destructive to surrounding tissue but metastasizes very rarely (<0.1%). The tumor grows slowly, often over months to years, and is characterized by pearly, translucent nodules with visible telangiectasias and rolled borders. Neglected lesions can cause significant local tissue destruction, especially on the face near the eyes, nose, and ears.
diagnostics: Expect shave or punch biopsy for histological confirmation
Monitor lesion characteristics (size, borders, morphology)
Expect dermoscopy for lesion evaluation
Monitor for recurrence at treatment sites
Assess for additional suspicious lesions on sun-exposed areas
management: Prepare patient for surgical excision or Mohs micrographic surgery
Coordinate electrodessication and curettage for superficial lesions as ordered
Administer topical imiquimod or 5-fluorouracil as ordered for superficial BCC
Educate on sun protection to prevent new lesions
Schedule regular follow-up skin examinations
Coordinate radiation therapy referral for non-surgical candidates
nursingActions: Assess lesion characteristics and document with measurements
Educate on sun protection (SPF 30+ sunscreen, protective clothing, hat, sunglasses)
Provide post-procedural wound care instructions after excision or Mohs surgery
Monitor treatment site for signs of infection, bleeding, or recurrence
Teach monthly self-skin examination for new suspicious lesions
Report any new pearly or non-healing lesions to the provider
Reassure patient about low metastatic risk while emphasizing importance of treatment

squamous-cell-carcinoma.title: Skuamöz Hücreli Karsinom
squamous-cell-carcinoma.pathophysiology: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) arises from malignant transformation of keratinocytes in the epidermal squamous layer. Chronic ultraviolet radiation causes cumulative DNA damage, particularly mutations in the TP53 tumor suppressor gene, leading to loss of cell cycle control and uncontrolled keratinocyte proliferation. SCC often arises from precancerous actinic keratoses. Unlike BCC, SCC has a significant metastatic potential (2-6%), spreading to regional lymph nodes and distant sites via lymphatic and hematogenous routes. The tumor typically presents as a firm, flesh-colored or erythematous, scaly, keratotic papule or plaque that may ulcerate. Immunosuppressed patients (organ transplant recipients) have a dramatically increased risk.
diagnostics: Expect punch or excisional biopsy for histological diagnosis
Monitor regional lymph nodes for palpable enlargement
Expect imaging studies if lymph node involvement suspected
Monitor actinic keratoses for changes suggesting SCC transformation
Assess lesion depth, differentiation grade, and perineural invasion on pathology
management: Prepare patient for surgical excision with appropriate margins
Coordinate Mohs surgery for high-risk locations (face, ears, lips)
Coordinate radiation therapy for non-surgical candidates
Administer systemic therapy as ordered for metastatic disease
Treat precursor actinic keratoses to prevent SCC development
Schedule regular surveillance examinations
nursingActions: Assess lesion characteristics including size, borders, ulceration, and induration
Palpate regional lymph nodes and report enlargement
Educate on sun protection and avoidance of tanning beds
Provide post-surgical wound care education
Monitor for recurrence at treatment site during follow-up
Educate immunosuppressed patients on increased skin cancer risk and need for frequent screening
Report any new firm, scaly, or non-healing lesions on sun-exposed areas

stevens-johnson-syndrome.title: Stevens-Johnson Sendromu (SJS)
stevens-johnson-syndrome.pathophysiology: Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a severe, life-threatening immune-mediated hypersensitivity reaction most commonly triggered by medications. Drug metabolites bind to keratinocyte surface proteins, creating neoantigens recognized by cytotoxic CD8+ T-cells and natural killer cells. These immune cells release granulysin, perforin, and granzyme B, which induce massive keratinocyte apoptosis. Fas-FasL interactions further amplify apoptotic cascades across the epidermis. This causes full-thickness epidermal necrosis and detachment from the dermis. SJS involves less than 10% of total body surface area (TBSA) epidermal detachment. Mucous membranes (oral, ocular, genital) are affected in over 90% of cases, causing severe pain and functional impairment.
diagnostics: Expect skin biopsy showing full-thickness epidermal necrosis
Monitor body surface area of epidermal detachment (less than 10% TBSA for SJS)
Expect CBC, CMP, and liver function studies
Monitor Nikolsky sign (positive - skin slides off with lateral pressure)
Expect ophthalmologic examination for ocular involvement
Monitor fluid balance, electrolytes, and renal function
management: Discontinue the offending medication immediately (most critical intervention)
Transfer to burn unit or ICU for management
Provide aggressive IV fluid resuscitation as ordered
Provide meticulous wound care similar to burn management
Administer analgesics as ordered for severe pain
Coordinate ophthalmology, dermatology, and urology consultations
nursingActions: Identify and report suspected causative medication immediately
Monitor skin for progression of erythema, blistering, and epidermal detachment
Assess Nikolsky sign (gentle lateral pressure on erythematous skin)
Provide gentle wound care with non-adherent dressings
Monitor oral mucosa and assist with oral care using gentle mouthwashes
Maintain strict intake and output measurements
Assess for signs of sepsis (fever, tachycardia, hypotension)

toxic-epidermal-necrolysis.title: Toksik Epidermal Nekroliz (TEN)
toxic-epidermal-necrolysis.pathophysiology: Toxic epidermal necrolysis is the most severe form of the SJS-TEN spectrum, involving greater than 30% total body surface area epidermal detachment. The pathophysiology is identical to SJS: drug-induced cytotoxic T-cell and NK-cell activation causes widespread keratinocyte apoptosis through granulysin release and Fas-FasL signaling. However, TEN represents the most catastrophic immune response, with massive full-thickness epidermal necrosis creating wounds functionally similar to extensive burns. Patients lose thermoregulatory capacity, develop massive fluid and protein losses, and are at extreme risk for sepsis. Mortality ranges from 25-35%, primarily from sepsis and multi-organ failure. SCORTEN severity score predicts mortality.
diagnostics: Expect skin biopsy showing full-thickness epidermal necrosis
Monitor TBSA of epidermal detachment (>30% defines TEN)
Expect SCORTEN severity assessment for mortality prediction
Monitor electrolytes, albumin, renal function, and glucose
Expect blood cultures and wound cultures for infection surveillance
Monitor for signs of multi-organ dysfunction
management: Discontinue all suspected medications immediately
Transfer to burn unit or specialized ICU
Provide aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (Parkland-like formula adaptation)
Manage wounds with non-adherent dressings and gentle debridement
Maintain thermoregulatory support (warm environment)
Administer nutritional support via enteral feeding as ordered
nursingActions: Monitor and document TBSA of epidermal detachment hourly during acute phase
Handle patient with extreme gentleness to avoid further skin loss (minimal handling)
Maintain strict aseptic technique for all wound care
Monitor strict intake and output with hourly urine output measurement
Assess for sepsis signs frequently (temperature instability, tachycardia, hypotension)
Provide gentle eye care with saline irrigation and lubricant as ordered
Administer pain management as ordered before wound care procedures

urticaria.title: Ürtiker (Kurdeşen)
urticaria.pathophysiology: Urticaria results from mast cell activation and degranulation in the superficial dermis, releasing histamine and other vasoactive mediators (leukotrienes, prostaglandins). Histamine binds H1 receptors on postcapillary venules, causing vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and transudation of plasma into the surrounding dermis, forming raised, erythematous, edematous wheals. Histamine also stimulates sensory nerve endings, causing intense pruritus. Acute urticaria (<6 weeks) is commonly triggered by IgE-mediated allergic reactions (foods, medications, insect stings), while chronic urticaria (>6 weeks) is often autoimmune or idiopathic. Angioedema involves deeper dermal and subcutaneous tissue swelling, particularly periorbital, labial, and lingual areas.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on characteristic wheal appearance
Monitor for progression to angioedema (lip, tongue, periorbital swelling)
Expect allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE) for suspected triggers
Monitor for signs of anaphylaxis (stridor, wheezing, hypotension)
Expect CBC with differential and thyroid function in chronic urticaria
management: Administer second-generation antihistamines as ordered (first-line)
Administer epinephrine as ordered for anaphylaxis or severe angioedema
Remove or avoid identified triggers
Administer systemic corticosteroids as ordered for severe acute episodes
Administer omalizumab as ordered for refractory chronic urticaria
Maintain IV access if angioedema present
nursingActions: Assess distribution, size, and number of wheals and document findings
Monitor airway, breathing, and hemodynamic status for anaphylaxis
Administer antihistamines and epinephrine as ordered and monitor response
Educate on trigger identification and avoidance
Instruct patient on use of epinephrine auto-injector if prescribed
Report any lip, tongue, or throat swelling immediately
Monitor for biphasic anaphylactic reaction (can occur up to 12 hours after initial event)

scabies.title: Skarifikasyon
scabies.pathophysiology: Scabies is caused by the human itch mite Sarcoptes scabiei var. hominis. The fertilized female mite burrows into the stratum corneum of the epidermis, creating tunnels where she deposits eggs and fecal pellets (scybala). The mite lifecycle takes approximately 30 days. Intense pruritus, the hallmark symptom, results from a Type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction to mite proteins, eggs, and fecal matter. Initial infestation produces symptoms in 4-6 weeks; re-infestation causes symptoms within 1-3 days due to prior sensitization. The hypersensitivity reaction causes eosinophil and lymphocyte infiltration in the dermis, producing papular and vesicular eruptions at and beyond the burrow sites.
diagnostics: Expect microscopic examination of skin scraping for mites, eggs, or fecal pellets
Monitor distribution pattern (web spaces, wrists, axillae, waistline)
Expect dermoscopy for visualization of burrows and mites
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection from excoriation
Assess close contacts and household members for symptoms
management: Apply permethrin 5% cream as prescribed from neck to toes
Treat all household members and close contacts simultaneously
Wash all clothing and bedding in hot water and dry on high heat
Seal non-washable items in plastic bags for 72 hours
Repeat permethrin treatment in 7 days as directed
Administer antihistamines as ordered for pruritus
nursingActions: Implement contact precautions until treatment is completed
Educate on proper permethrin application (apply to entire body from neck down, leave on 8-14 hours)
Instruct that pruritus may persist 2-4 weeks after successful treatment (post-scabies itch)
Ensure all household members and sexual contacts are treated simultaneously
Educate on environmental decontamination (hot water wash, high heat dryer)
Report crusted (Norwegian) scabies immediately as it is highly contagious
Document and assess for secondary bacterial skin infection

impetigo.title: İmpetigo
impetigo.pathophysiology: Impetigo is a highly contagious superficial bacterial infection of the epidermis caused primarily by Staphylococcus aureus or Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes). Bacteria enter through breaks in the skin barrier (insect bites, abrasions, eczema) and colonize the superficial epidermis. Non-bullous impetigo (70% of cases) begins as vesicles that rapidly rupture, and serous exudate dries to form characteristic honey-colored (golden-yellow) crusted lesions. Bullous impetigo is caused by exfoliative toxin-producing S. aureus strains that cleave desmoglein-1 in the granular layer, creating large, fragile, fluid-filled bullae. The infection spreads rapidly through direct contact and autoinoculation when the patient touches lesions and then touches unaffected skin.
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on characteristic honey-crusted lesion appearance
Monitor for spread to other body areas via autoinoculation
Expect wound culture if lesions are not responding to empiric therapy
Monitor for post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (cola-colored urine, facial edema)
Assess for signs of deeper skin infection (cellulitis)
management: Apply topical mupirocin as prescribed for localized disease
Administer oral antibiotics as ordered for widespread or unresponsive cases
Gently remove crusts with warm soaks before applying topical medication
Keep affected areas clean and covered
Prevent spread through strict hand hygiene and contact precautions
nursingActions: Assess and document lesion characteristics, location, and progression
Teach proper wound cleansing technique (warm soaks to soften and remove crusts)
Educate on preventing spread (separate towels, hand hygiene, avoid touching lesions)
Monitor for signs of post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (dark urine, edema, hypertension)
Ensure completion of full antibiotic course as prescribed
Report worsening, spreading, or deepening lesions to the provider
Educate that child may return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics

pemphigus-vulgaris.title: Pemfigus Vulgaris
pemphigus-vulgaris.pathophysiology: Pemphigus vulgaris is a severe autoimmune blistering disease caused by IgG autoantibodies directed against desmoglein-3 (and sometimes desmoglein-1), which are critical components of desmosomes that hold keratinocytes together within the epidermis. Autoantibody binding causes disruption of intercellular adhesion (acantholysis), resulting in loss of keratinocyte cohesion and formation of intraepidermal blisters. The suprabasal cleavage plane creates fragile, flaccid blisters that rupture easily, leaving painful erosions. Oral mucosal involvement typically precedes skin lesions by weeks to months. Without treatment, pemphigus vulgaris has historically had a high mortality rate due to fluid loss, protein depletion, and secondary infection.
diagnostics: Expect skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence showing IgG deposits at intercellular junctions
Monitor positive Nikolsky sign (skin shearing with lateral pressure)
Expect serum anti-desmoglein antibody titers (correlate with disease activity)
Monitor oral mucosa for erosions (often the first site involved)
Expect indirect immunofluorescence on serum
Monitor for secondary wound infections
management: Administer systemic corticosteroids as ordered (first-line treatment)
Administer steroid-sparing immunosuppressants as ordered (azathioprine, mycophenolate)
Administer rituximab as ordered for refractory cases
Provide meticulous wound care for denuded skin areas
Maintain nutritional support (high-protein, high-calorie diet)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (dermatology, oral medicine)
nursingActions: Assess skin and mucous membranes for new blisters and erosions daily
Handle the patient gently to avoid inducing new blisters (Nikolsky phenomenon)
Provide gentle oral care with soft-bristled brush and non-irritating mouthwash
Monitor for signs of infection at erosion sites (increased erythema, purulence, fever)
Monitor for immunosuppressive therapy side effects (infection, bone marrow suppression)
Maintain fluid balance and nutritional support, especially with extensive oral involvement
Provide emotional support for chronic, disfiguring condition

potassium-imbalance-rpn.title: Hiperpotasemi vs Hipopotasemi - EKG Değişiklikleri
potassium-imbalance-rpn.pathophysiology: Potassium (K⁺) is the dominant intracellular cation, with approximately 98% residing inside cells and only 2% in the extracellular fluid. This steep concentration gradient  -  maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump  -  is the primary determinant of the resting membrane potential (RMP) of excitable cells, including cardiac myocytes and skeletal muscle fibers. The normal RMP of a cardiac ventricular cell is approximately −90 mV, generated largely by potassium leak channels that allow K⁺ to flow down its concentration gradient from intracellular to extracellular space. When extracellular K⁺ rises (hyperkalemia), the gradient decreases, the RMP becomes less negative (partially depolarized), and the cell becomes hyperexcitable initially but progressively inexcitable as sodium channels inactivate. When extracellular K⁺ falls (hypokalemia), the gradient steepens, the RMP becomes more negative (hyperpolarized), making the cell harder to depolarize  -  but paradoxically more prone to ectopic pacemaker activity and re-entrant circuits due to prolonged repolarization. This is why both extremes of potassium produce life-threatening dysrhythmias: they disrupt the precisely calibrated electrical cycling of the heart. Normal serum potassium: 3.5-5.0 mEq/L. Even small deviations (0.5-1.0 mEq/L) can produce clinically significant cardiac conduction changes.
diagnostics: Serum potassium level (critical values: <2.5 or >6.5 mEq/L require immediate intervention)
12-lead ECG  -  assess for peaked T waves, widened QRS, absent P waves (hyperkalemia) or flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression (hypokalemia)
Continuous cardiac monitoring on telemetry for any K⁺ outside 3.0-5.5 mEq/L
Basic metabolic panel (BMP) including BUN, creatinine, glucose, magnesium, calcium
Serum magnesium level  -  hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia refractory to correction
Arterial blood gas if acid-base disturbance suspected (pH affects transcellular K⁺ shifts)
Urine potassium and urine creatinine to differentiate renal vs extrarenal K⁺ losses
Digoxin level if applicable  -  hypokalemia potentiates digoxin toxicity
management: HYPERKALEMIA: Calcium gluconate IV as ordered  -  stabilizes cardiac membrane (does NOT lower K⁺, buys time)
HYPERKALEMIA: Regular insulin IV + dextrose as ordered  -  shifts K⁺ intracellularly within 15-30 minutes
HYPERKALEMIA: Sodium bicarbonate IV as ordered for concurrent metabolic acidosis  -  shifts K⁺ into cells
HYPERKALEMIA: Nebulized albuterol as ordered  -  beta-2 stimulation shifts K⁺ intracellularly
HYPERKALEMIA: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) orally or rectally as ordered  -  exchanges Na⁺ for K⁺ in GI tract (slow onset, hours)
HYPERKALEMIA: Patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) as ordered  -  newer oral K⁺ binders with better GI safety profile
HYPERKALEMIA: Emergent hemodialysis for refractory or severe hyperkalemia (K⁺ > 6.5 with ECG changes)
HYPOKALEMIA: Oral potassium chloride (KCl) replacement as ordered  -  preferred route for mild-moderate deficits
HYPOKALEMIA: IV potassium replacement as ordered  -  never exceed 10 mEq/hr via peripheral line or 20 mEq/hr via central line
HYPOKALEMIA: NEVER give IV potassium as bolus push  -  fatal cardiac arrest risk
HYPOKALEMIA: Correct concurrent hypomagnesemia  -  refractory hypokalemia will not correct until magnesium is repleted
HYPOKALEMIA: Potassium-sparing diuretics may be added if ongoing losses from loop/thiazide diuretics
nursingActions: Place patient on continuous cardiac telemetry monitoring for any critical K⁺ value
Monitor serum potassium levels every 2-4 hours during active correction
Assess ECG rhythm strip for changes: peaked T waves, widened QRS, U waves, ST changes
Monitor urine output hourly  -  adequate renal function is essential for safe K⁺ correction
Verify IV potassium is diluted properly and infusing on a pump  -  NEVER bolus IV KCl
Assess IV site frequently during potassium infusion  -  KCl is a vesicant and causes phlebitis and tissue necrosis if infiltrated
Monitor for signs of hyperkalemia: muscle weakness (ascending pattern), paresthesias, bradycardia, cardiac arrest
Monitor for signs of hypokalemia: skeletal muscle weakness (legs first), leg cramps, diminished deep tendon reflexes, paralytic ileus, cardiac dysrhythmias
Hold potassium supplements and notify provider if urine output < 30 mL/hr (risk of iatrogenic hyperkalemia)
Teach patients to recognize potassium-rich foods: bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes, spinach, avocados, salt substitutes
Administer oral KCl with food and a full glass of water to prevent GI ulceration
Report any new cardiac rhythm changes immediately  -  both hypo and hyperkalemia cause lethal dysrhythmias

hyperkalemia-emergency-np.title: Hiperpotasemi Acil Durumu: EKG Gelişimi
hyperkalemia-emergency-np.pathophysiology: In hyperkalemia, elevated extracellular K⁺ reduces the electrochemical gradient across the cardiac cell membrane, decreasing the resting membrane potential from its normal −90 mV toward −70 to −60 mV. At this partially depolarized state, voltage-gated sodium channels transition from their resting (closed, activatable) state to their inactivated (closed, non-activatable) state. This means that when the cell attempts to depolarize, fewer functional sodium channels are available, producing a slower and lower-amplitude Phase 0 upstroke. The clinical result is slowed impulse conduction velocity, which manifests as progressive QRS widening on the ECG. Simultaneously, the reduced gradient accelerates Phase 3 repolarization (K⁺ efflux), producing the characteristic peaked, narrow T waves that are the earliest ECG sign of hyperkalemia. As K⁺ continues to rise, atrial myocytes  -  which are more sensitive to hyperkalemia than ventricular myocytes  -  lose their ability to depolarize, producing P wave flattening and eventual absence (sinoatrial arrest). The final pre-arrest pattern is the sine wave: a smooth, undulating waveform representing extreme QRS widening merging with the T wave, indicating imminent ventricular fibrillation or asystole.
diagnostics: STAT serum potassium with repeat to rule out hemolysis artifact
12-lead ECG immediately  -  compare with prior tracings for progression
Continuous cardiac monitoring on telemetry
Point-of-care blood gas with electrolytes for rapid K⁺ and pH assessment
Comprehensive metabolic panel (creatinine, BUN, glucose, calcium, magnesium, phosphorus)
Serum CK and myoglobin if rhabdomyolysis suspected
Uric acid and LDH if tumor lysis syndrome suspected
Digoxin level if applicable (hyperkalemia + digoxin = extreme arrhythmia risk)
management: STEP 1  -  STABILIZE: Calcium gluconate 10% IV over 2-3 minutes (onset 1-3 min, duration 30-60 min). Repeat in 5 minutes if ECG unchanged
STEP 2  -  SHIFT: Regular insulin 10 units IV + D50W 25g IV (onset 15-30 min, lowers K⁺ by 0.5-1.5 mEq/L)
STEP 2 ALT  -  SHIFT: Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV if concurrent metabolic acidosis (shifts K⁺ intracellularly)
STEP 2 ALT  -  SHIFT: Nebulized albuterol 10-20 mg (onset 15-30 min, lowers K⁺ by 0.5-1.0 mEq/L, additive with insulin)
STEP 3  -  REMOVE: Sodium polystyrene sulfonate 15-30g orally or rectally (onset 1-6 hours), OR patiromer 8.4g orally (onset 4-7 hours)
STEP 3  -  REMOVE: Emergent hemodialysis for K⁺ > 6.5 with ECG changes refractory to medical therapy, or K⁺ > 7.0
STEP 3  -  REMOVE: Loop diuretic (furosemide 40-80mg IV) if renal function preserved  -  promotes renal K⁺ excretion
Identify and treat underlying cause: stop offending medications, treat AKI, manage tumor lysis or rhabdomyolysis
nursingActions: Recognize ECG progression: peaked T → prolonged PR → absent P → wide QRS → sine wave → arrest
Ensure IV calcium gluconate is immediately available and drawn up when K⁺ > 6.0 mEq/L
Monitor blood glucose every 30 minutes for 4-6 hours after insulin/dextrose administration
Ensure defibrillator is at bedside and functioning for any patient with K⁺ > 6.5 mEq/L with ECG changes
Recheck serum potassium 1 hour after each intervention and then every 2 hours
Monitor strict intake and output  -  document urine output hourly
Assess for neuromuscular symptoms: ascending weakness, paresthesias, respiratory muscle involvement
Discontinue all potassium-containing IV fluids and supplements
Review medication list and hold ACE inhibitors, ARBs, K⁺-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs, and trimethoprim
Prepare for possible emergent dialysis (verify vascular access, contact nephrology)
Notify rapid response team or code team if sine wave pattern or hemodynamic instability develops
Document interventions, timing, K⁺ levels, and ECG changes meticulously for continuity

shock-syndromes.title: Şok Durumları
shock-syndromes.pathophysiology: Shock is a life-threatening syndrome of inadequate tissue perfusion. Hypovolemic (low volume), Cardiogenic (pump failure), Septic (vasodilation/infection), and Anaphylactic (allergic vasodilation).
diagnostics: Monitor lactate levels as ordered
Expect CBC, metabolic panel, and coagulation studies
Monitor continuous cardiac telemetry
Expect arterial blood gas (ABG) to be drawn
Monitor urine output hourly via catheter
Expect blood cultures before antibiotics in septic shock
management: Maintain large-bore IV access as ordered
Position supine with legs elevated (hypovolemic) or per orders
Administer IV fluids and vasopressors as prescribed
Maintain airway and oxygen delivery as ordered
Keep patient warm (prevent hypothermia)
Prepare for possible emergency interventions as directed
nursingActions: Monitor vital signs continuously or every 5-15 minutes
Report MAP < 65 mmHg immediately
Monitor urine output hourly (report < 30 mL/hr)
Assess level of consciousness frequently
Document all fluid intake and medication responses
Report signs of end-organ dysfunction (confusion, anuria, mottled skin)
Maintain strict intake and output records

sepsis-mastery.title: Sepsis & SIRS Tanıma
sepsis-mastery.pathophysiology: Sepsis is a dysregulated host response to infection. It progress from SIRS to Sepsis, Severe Sepsis, and finally Septic Shock (refractory hypotension).
diagnostics: Expect blood cultures to be drawn BEFORE antibiotics
Monitor lactate levels as ordered
Expect CBC with differential and metabolic panel
Monitor urine output hourly
Expect procalcitonin to be ordered
Monitor continuous vital signs on telemetry
management: Administer antibiotics within 1 hour as prescribed
Administer IV fluid bolus as ordered (30 mL/kg)
Maintain IV access with large-bore catheter
Position to optimize perfusion as ordered
Maintain oxygen delivery as prescribed
Follow sepsis bundle protocols as directed
nursingActions: Monitor vital signs every 15 minutes during acute phase
Report SIRS criteria (temp, HR, RR, WBC) to physician immediately
Monitor MAP and report < 65 mmHg
Assess mental status changes frequently
Monitor urine output hourly (report < 30 mL/hr)
Document time of antibiotic administration
Assess skin for mottling, delayed capillary refill
Report lactic acid trends to healthcare team

burn-management.title: Yanık Yaralanması & Yeniden Canlandırma
burn-management.pathophysiology: Thermal or chemical injury triggers a massive systemic inflammatory response, leading to increased capillary permeability, massive fluid shifts (edema), and hypovolemic shock.
diagnostics: Expect CBC, metabolic panel, and ABG to be drawn
Monitor urine output hourly (Parkland formula guide)
Expect type and crossmatch for large burns
Monitor oxygen saturation and carboxyhemoglobin if inhalation suspected
Expect wound cultures to be ordered
Expect chest X-ray for inhalation injury
management: Maintain airway as top priority (assess for inhalation injury)
Administer IV fluids as prescribed (Parkland formula)
Maintain NPO for major burns as ordered
Keep patient warm to prevent hypothermia
Follow wound care protocol as ordered
Maintain tetanus prophylaxis as ordered
Elevate burned extremities as directed
nursingActions: Monitor airway for signs of inhalation injury (singed nasal hairs, hoarseness)
Monitor urine output hourly (target 30-50 mL/hr adult, 1 mL/kg/hr child)
Assess and document burn depth and TBSA accurately
Monitor vital signs frequently during resuscitation
Report signs of hypovolemic shock immediately
Provide pain management and reassess effectiveness
Use strict aseptic technique during wound care

compartment-syndrome.title: Kompartment Sendromu
compartment-syndrome.pathophysiology:
Compartment syndrome is a limb-threatening emergency caused by increased pressure within a closed fascial compartment. The extremities are divided into distinct compartments by tough, inelastic fascia: the leg has four (anterior, lateral, superficial posterior, deep posterior), the forearm has three (volar, dorsal, mobile wad). When pressure within a compartment rises: from internal swelling (fracture bleeding, reperfusion edema, crush injury) or external compression (tight cast, circumferential burn eschar, prolonged limb compression during unconsciousness): the fascia cannot expand to accommodate it.

The critical threshold occurs when intracompartmental pressure (ICP) exceeds capillary perfusion pressure. Normal ICP is 0-8 mmHg. When ICP rises above 30 mmHg (or within 30 mmHg of the patient's diastolic blood pressure: the delta pressure concept), capillary blood flow is obstructed. Arterioles in the compartment collapse, and venous outflow is blocked first (venous pressure is lower than arterial), further worsening edema in a vicious cycle.

At the cellular level, skeletal muscle cells (myocytes) and peripheral nerve axons are exquisitely sensitive to ischemia. Within 2 hours of ischemia, reversible cellular injury begins: ATP depletion causes the Na+/K+ ATPase pump to fail, leading to intracellular sodium and water accumulation (cellular edema), which further raises compartmental pressure. Calcium floods into cells through damaged membranes, activating destructive proteases and phospholipases. After 4-6 hours of sustained ischemia, irreversible necrosis of muscle (rhabdomyolysis) and nerve begins. The dying myocytes release myoglobin, potassium, phosphate, and creatine kinase (CK) into the bloodstream. Myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules (especially in acidic urine), causing acute kidney injury: a potentially fatal secondary complication.

If untreated beyond 6-8 hours, the compartment undergoes Volkmann's ischemic contracture: muscle is replaced by fibrotic scar tissue that permanently shortens, resulting in a clawed, non-functional limb. Peripheral nerves within the compartment suffer Wallerian degeneration, causing permanent motor and sensory deficits.

The most common causes are tibial shaft fractures (40% of cases), forearm fractures (especially in children), crush injuries, vascular injuries with reperfusion, burns (circumferential), snake bites, IV infiltration, and prolonged surgical positioning. Abdominal compartment syndrome is a separate entity involving intra-abdominal pressure >20 mmHg with organ dysfunction.
diagnostics: Expect CK/CPK levels to be ordered (rhabdomyolysis indicator)
Monitor urine for myoglobinuria (dark tea-colored)
Expect compartment pressure measurement (Stryker needle)
Monitor serum potassium levels
Expect metabolic panel and renal function tests
Monitor urine output hourly
management: Remove ALL constrictive dressings, casts, or splints immediately
Keep extremity at HEART level (not elevated)
Maintain IV access for aggressive fluid resuscitation as ordered
Prepare patient for possible fasciotomy as directed
Administer pain medication as prescribed
Do NOT apply ice to affected compartment
nursingActions: Assess pain level frequently (pain out of proportion is key finding)
Report unrelieved pain despite opioids immediately
Perform neurovascular checks (6 Ps) every 1-2 hours
Report tense or wooden-feeling compartment on palpation
Monitor urine color and output hourly
Document capillary refill, sensation, and movement
Report any paresthesia or paralysis immediately to surgeon

malignant-hyperthermia.title: Malign Hipertermi
malignant-hyperthermia.pathophysiology: Life-threatening pharmacogenetic reaction triggered by volatile anesthetics (halothane, sevoflurane, desflurane) or succinylcholine. Genetic defect in ryanodine receptor (RYR1 gene) on skeletal muscle sarcoplasmic reticulum causes uncontrolled calcium release, leading to sustained muscle contraction, hypermetabolism, rapidly rising temperature (>104°F/40°C), rhabdomyolysis, metabolic acidosis, hyperkalemia, and death if untreated.
diagnostics: End-tidal CO2 monitoring (rapidly rising EtCO2 is earliest sign)
Temperature monitoring (rapid rise > 104°F/40°C)
Heart rate monitoring (unexplained tachycardia)
Urine assessment for dark color (myoglobinuria)
management: STOP triggering agents immediately
Administer dantrolene IV as ordered (2.5 mg/kg, repeat as needed)
Apply cooling measures (ice packs, cooling blanket, cold IV fluids)
Administer 100% oxygen
Notify anesthesiologist immediately
nursingActions: Recognize early signs: unexplained tachycardia, rising EtCO2, jaw rigidity
Assist with dantrolene reconstitution and administration
Apply active cooling measures (ice packs to groin, axillae, neck)
Monitor urine output and color
Report family history of MH during pre-op assessment
Ensure dantrolene is available wherever anesthesia is administered

anaphylaxis.title: Anafilaksi Yönetimi
anaphylaxis.pathophysiology: Severe IgE-mediated Type I hypersensitivity reaction. Prior sensitization produces IgE antibodies that bind to mast cells and basophils. Re-exposure triggers massive degranulation releasing histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins. This causes bronchospasm, systemic vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, and cardiovascular collapse within minutes.
diagnostics: Airway assessment (stridor, tongue/lip swelling)
Vital signs (hypotension, tachycardia)
Skin assessment (urticaria, flushing, angioedema)
Respiratory assessment (wheezing, dyspnea)
Oxygen saturation monitoring
management: Administer epinephrine IM (lateral thigh) as ordered - FIRST priority
Call for emergency assistance
Maintain airway and administer oxygen
Position supine with legs elevated if hypotensive
Establish IV access and administer fluids as ordered
nursingActions: Administer epinephrine IM as first-line treatment as ordered
Monitor airway continuously for compromise
Apply oxygen and monitor SpO2
Establish large-bore IV access
Monitor for biphasic reaction (recurrence 4-12 hours later)
Document allergen and reaction in chart and allergy band

siadh-di.title: SIADH vs Diabetes İnsipidus
siadh-di.pathophysiology:
The syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion (SIADH) and diabetes insipidus (DI) represent two diametrically opposed disorders of antidiuretic hormone (ADH, also known as arginine vasopressin or AVP) regulation, both originating from disruptions in the hypothalamic-posterior pituitary axis. Understanding these conditions requires a thorough grasp of normal ADH physiology, the cellular mechanisms by which ADH acts on renal tissue, and the systemic consequences of ADH excess or deficiency on fluid balance, electrolyte homeostasis, and organ function.

Normal ADH Physiology and Cellular Mechanisms: ADH is a nine-amino-acid peptide hormone synthesized in the supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei of the hypothalamus. After synthesis, ADH is transported along axonal projections to the posterior pituitary gland (neurohypophysis), where it is stored in secretory granules within nerve terminals. Release is primarily regulated by osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus that detect changes in plasma osmolality. When plasma osmolality rises above approximately 280-285 mOsm/kg (as occurs with dehydration or sodium excess), osmoreceptors shrink due to water movement out of the cells, triggering increased ADH secretion. Conversely, when plasma osmolality falls, osmoreceptors swell, suppressing ADH release. Baroreceptors in the carotid sinus, aortic arch, and left atrium also modulate ADH release in response to changes in blood volume and pressure, though this mechanism requires a more substantial change (approximately 10-15% decrease in blood volume) to activate. Once released into the circulation, ADH binds to vasopressin V2 receptors on the basolateral membrane of principal cells in the renal collecting duct. This binding activates a Gs-protein-coupled signaling cascade that stimulates adenylate cyclase, increasing intracellular cyclic AMP (cAMP). Elevated cAMP activates protein kinase A (PKA), which phosphorylates aquaporin-2 (AQP2) water channel proteins stored in intracellular vesicles. These phosphorylated AQP2 channels are then trafficked to and inserted into the apical (luminal) membrane of the collecting duct cells via exocytosis. The insertion of AQP2 channels dramatically increases the water permeability of the collecting duct, allowing water to move from the tubular lumen (urine) down its osmotic gradient into the hypertonic renal medullary interstitium, and subsequently into the peritubular capillaries (vasa recta) for return to the systemic circulation. Water reabsorption through AQP2 is facilitated by constitutively expressed aquaporin-3 and aquaporin-4 channels on the basolateral membrane of collecting duct cells. When ADH levels fall, AQP2 channels are retrieved from the apical membrane via endocytosis and recycled back into intracellular vesicles, reducing water permeability and allowing dilute urine to be excreted. ADH also acts on V1a receptors in vascular smooth muscle, causing vasoconstriction, and on V1b receptors in the anterior pituitary, stimulating ACTH release.

SIADH Pathophysiology at the Cellular Level: In SIADH, ADH is secreted continuously and excessively, independent of the normal osmotic and hemodynamic stimuli that should suppress its release. This autonomous ADH secretion results in persistent activation of V2 receptors on collecting duct principal cells, causing continuous insertion of AQP2 channels into the apical membrane. The consequence is relentless water reabsorption from the collecting duct, regardless of the body's actual hydration status. The excess water reabsorbed from the kidneys expands the extracellular fluid volume, diluting serum sodium and all other solutes. Serum osmolality drops below 275 mOsm/kg, and serum sodium typically falls below 135 mEq/L, often reaching dangerously low levels of 110-120 mEq/L in severe cases. Despite the expansion of extracellular fluid volume, the body's volume-regulatory mechanisms partially compensate. The volume expansion triggers release of atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP) and brain natriuretic peptide (BNP) from cardiac myocytes, which promote renal sodium excretion (natriuresis). This natriuresis prevents overt hypervolemia and edema formation, which is why SIADH patients characteristically appear euvolemic - they have no peripheral edema, no jugular venous distension, and no signs of overt volume overload. However, the urinary sodium excretion further worsens the hyponatremia. The urine in SIADH is inappropriately concentrated (osmolality >100 mOsm/kg, often >300 mOsm/kg) relative to the dilute serum, and urine sodium is elevated (>40 mEq/L). This pattern of dilute serum with concentrated urine is the pathognomonic laboratory hallmark of SIADH. The most dangerous consequence of hyponatremia is its effect on the central nervous system. Sodium is the primary determinant of plasma osmolality, and plasma osmolality determines water movement across cell membranes. When serum sodium falls, the extracellular fluid becomes hypotonic relative to the intracellular fluid of neurons. Water moves into brain cells via osmosis, causing cellular swelling (cytotoxic cerebral edema). The rigid skull provides no room for brain expansion, so even modest cerebral edema increases intracranial pressure. Mild hyponatremia (sodium 125-134 mEq/L) produces headache, nausea, malaise, and difficulty concentrating as neurons swell and neurotransmission is impaired. Moderate hyponatremia (sodium 120-125 mEq/L) causes confusion, lethargy, muscle cramps, and gait disturbances. Severe hyponatremia (sodium below 120 mEq/L) can precipitate seizures, coma, respiratory arrest, and transtentorial brainstem herniation, which can be fatal. Over 24-48 hours, brain cells partially adapt to chronic hyponatremia by extruding intracellular osmolytes (potassium, chloride, taurine, glutamate, myo-inositol) to reduce intracellular osmolality and limit further water influx. This adaptation is protective against cerebral edema but creates a critical vulnerability: if serum sodium is corrected too rapidly (more than 8-10 mEq/L in 24 hours), water rapidly exits brain cells that have lost their intracellular osmolytes, causing osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS, formerly called central pontine myelinolysis). In ODS, the myelin sheaths of neurons in the central pons and occasionally extrapontine areas are destroyed by the rapid osmotic shift, producing devastating and often irreversible neurological deficits including dysarthria, dysphagia, quadriparesis, locked-in syndrome, and death. Common causes of SIADH include ectopic ADH production by malignancies (small cell lung cancer is the most common malignant cause, as neuroendocrine tumor cells synthesize and secrete ADH autonomously), central nervous system disorders (stroke, subarachnoid hemorrhage, traumatic brain injury, meningitis, encephalitis) that disrupt hypothalamic regulation, pulmonary conditions (pneumonia, tuberculosis, positive-pressure ventilation) that trigger ADH release through unclear mechanisms possibly involving pulmonary baroreceptors, and medications (SSRIs augment ADH release and potentiate its renal effects, carbamazepine stimulates ADH secretion, cyclophosphamide and vincristine cause ADH release, opioids reduce ADH clearance).

Diabetes Insipidus Pathophysiology at the Cellular Level: Diabetes insipidus represents the opposite end of the ADH spectrum - insufficient ADH action leading to the kidney's inability to concentrate urine. There are two major forms: central (neurogenic) DI and nephrogenic DI. In central DI, the hypothalamic-posterior pituitary axis fails to produce or secrete adequate ADH. This can result from destruction of ADH-producing neurons in the supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei, damage to the hypothalamic-hypophyseal tract (the axonal pathway connecting the hypothalamus to the posterior pituitary), or destruction of the posterior pituitary itself. Common causes include neurosurgery (especially transsphenoidal pituitary surgery, which can damage the pituitary stalk), traumatic brain injury (shearing forces damage the delicate axonal tracts), brain tumors (craniopharyngiomas and metastatic disease are particularly common), infiltrative diseases (sarcoidosis, Langerhans cell histiocytosis), autoimmune hypophysitis, and genetic mutations in the AVP-neurophysin II gene. Without adequate ADH, V2 receptors on collecting duct cells are not activated, AQP2 channels remain sequestered in intracellular vesicles, and the collecting duct remains impermeable to water. The result is excretion of massive volumes of very dilute urine (often 5-20 liters per day, with osmolality as low as 50-100 mOsm/kg and specific gravity below 1.005). In nephrogenic DI, the kidneys are resistant to the action of ADH despite normal or elevated circulating ADH levels. The V2 receptor-AQP2 signaling pathway is disrupted at the cellular level. This can be caused by lithium therapy (lithium enters collecting duct cells through epithelial sodium channels and accumulates intracellularly, where it inhibits adenylate cyclase and glycogen synthase kinase-3, reducing cAMP generation and AQP2 expression - lithium is the most common acquired cause), hypercalcemia (calcium activates the calcium-sensing receptor on collecting duct cells, which suppresses AQP2 expression and trafficking), hypokalemia (reduces AQP2 expression through unclear mechanisms), genetic mutations in the AVPR2 gene encoding the V2 receptor (X-linked recessive, presenting in male infants) or the AQP2 gene itself (autosomal recessive or dominant), and chronic kidney disease. The massive water loss in both forms of DI leads to a predictable cascade of physiological consequences. As free water is excreted in excess, the extracellular fluid becomes progressively hypertonic. Serum sodium rises above 145 mEq/L (hypernatremia), and serum osmolality increases above 295 mOsm/kg. The rising plasma osmolality triggers intense thirst via hypothalamic osmoreceptors (the thirst center in the anterior hypothalamus), driving patients to drink enormous volumes of water (polydipsia). If the patient has intact thirst mechanisms and access to water, they can often maintain near-normal sodium levels through compensatory polydipsia, though quality of life is severely impacted by constant thirst and urinary frequency. However, if the patient cannot drink adequately (altered mental status, intubated, post-surgical, infant), hypernatremia progresses rapidly and can become life-threatening. Hypernatremia causes water to shift out of cells via osmosis, leading to cellular dehydration. In the brain, this cellular shrinkage can tear bridging veins between the brain surface and the dural sinuses, causing subdural or subarachnoid hemorrhage. Neuronal dehydration produces progressive neurological deterioration: irritability, lethargy, hyperreflexia, seizures, and coma. The hypovolemia from massive water loss also causes decreased cardiac preload, tachycardia, hypotension, and potentially circulatory shock if not corrected.
