Assess attachment quality using validated measures (Strange Situation paradigm for younger children)
Screen for comorbid major depressive disorder, ADHD, and OCD
Expect thyroid function evaluation to rule out endocrine contribution to anxiety symptoms
management: Prescribe first-line SSRI therapy (fluoxetine 5-10 mg initial dose, titrate to 20-40 mg over 4-6 weeks based on response)
Consider sertraline as alternative (25 mg initial, titrate to 50-200 mg) based on CAMS trial evidence
Implement combination CBT + pharmacotherapy for moderate-severe cases (CAMS trial demonstrated 80.7% response rate for combination vs 59.7% CBT alone vs 54.9% sertraline alone)
Monitor for SSRI activation syndrome in first 2 weeks (paradoxical agitation, insomnia, disinhibition)
Consider augmentation with buspirone (5-HT1A partial agonist) for partial SSRI responders
Evaluate benzodiazepine short-term bridge therapy only for acute crisis situations (avoid chronic use due to dependence risk and cognitive effects in developing brain)
Develop pharmacogenomic-informed prescribing approach considering CYP2D6 and CYP2C19 metabolizer status for SSRI selection and dosing
nursingActions: Conduct advanced psychopharmacological assessment including medication history, prior SSRI trials, and adverse effects
Monitor for FDA black box warning signs: suicidal ideation, behavioral activation, and agitation in first 4-6 weeks of SSRI therapy
Assess CYP2D6/CYP2C19 metabolizer status implications for drug selection and dosing
Evaluate for serotonin syndrome risk if multiple serotonergic agents prescribed (mental status changes, autonomic instability, neuromuscular hyperactivity)
Monitor treatment response using standardized outcome measures (SCARED score reduction ≥50%)
Educate family on neurobiological basis of anxiety to reduce stigma and improve treatment adherence
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (prescriber, therapist, school) for integrated treatment plan
Document detailed psychiatric review of systems at each follow-up visit

lead-poisoning.title: Kurşun Zehirlenmesi
lead-poisoning.pathophysiology: Lead interferes with heme synthesis (inhibits ALA dehydratase and ferrochelatase), disrupts calcium signaling in neurons, causes oxidative damage. Children absorb more lead than adults and are more vulnerable due to developing brain.
diagnostics: Expect venous blood lead level (BLL) for screening and confirmation
Monitor CBC (microcytic anemia, basophilic stippling)
Expect free erythrocyte protoporphyrin level
Monitor renal function during chelation therapy
Expect abdominal X-ray if recent ingestion suspected (lead is radiopaque)
Expect developmental screening assessment
management: Remove child from lead exposure source immediately
Ensure adequate nutrition (calcium, iron, vitamin C reduce absorption)
Administer chelation therapy as prescribed for elevated levels
Maintain adequate hydration during chelation
Implement lead abatement in the home environment
Follow up with serial blood lead levels
nursingActions: Screen blood lead levels at ages 1 and 2 per guidelines
Educate parents on lead sources and prevention (wet mopping, hand washing)
Report elevated blood lead levels to public health authorities
Monitor for developmental delays and neurobehavioral changes
Assess home environment for lead exposure sources
Educate on high-calcium, high-iron diet to reduce lead absorption

varicella.title: Varisella (Su Çiçeği)
varicella.pathophysiology: Varicella is caused by the Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV), a member of the herpesvirus family. The virus enters through the respiratory tract and conjunctivae, replicates in regional lymph nodes, and spreads via primary viremia to the liver and spleen. Secondary viremia seeds the skin, producing the characteristic vesicular rash. The virus then becomes latent in dorsal root ganglia and can reactivate later in life as herpes zoster (shingles). Transmission occurs via airborne droplets and direct contact with vesicular fluid. The incubation period is 10-21 days, and the patient is contagious from 1-2 days before rash onset until all lesions have crusted over.
diagnostics: Visual assessment of rash characteristics (crops in different stages)
Temperature monitoring
Assess for secondary bacterial infection of lesions
Monitor respiratory status in immunocompromised patients
management: Implement airborne AND contact precautions
Isolate until ALL lesions have crusted over
Keep fingernails short to prevent scratching and secondary infection
Administer antihistamines as ordered for pruritus
Administer acyclovir as ordered for high-risk patients
nursingActions: Implement airborne and contact precautions
Assess rash progression (macule → papule → vesicle → crust)
Maintain skin care to prevent secondary infection
Apply calamine lotion as ordered for itching
Monitor for complications (pneumonia, encephalitis)
Verify immunity status of healthcare workers before entering room

dehydration-peds.title: Pediatrik Dehidrasyon
dehydration-peds.pathophysiology: Pediatric dehydration occurs when fluid output exceeds intake, most commonly from gastroenteritis (vomiting and diarrhea). Children are at higher risk than adults due to their higher body surface area-to-weight ratio, higher metabolic rate, immature renal concentrating ability, and greater percentage of total body water. Dehydration is classified by percentage of body weight loss: Mild (3-5%), Moderate (6-9%), and Severe (≥10%). The type of dehydration (isotonic, hypotonic, or hypertonic) depends on the relative loss of water versus electrolytes. Isotonic dehydration (equal loss of water and sodium) is most common. Prompt assessment and rehydration are critical as severe dehydration can rapidly progress to hypovolemic shock in pediatric patients.
diagnostics: Anterior fontanelle assessment in infants (sunken = dehydration)
Skin turgor assessment (tenting)
Mucous membrane assessment (dry)
Urine output monitoring (wet diapers count)
Daily weight measurement
management: Administer oral rehydration solution (Pedialyte) in small frequent amounts
Administer IV fluids as ordered for severe dehydration
Continue breastfeeding if applicable
Resume age-appropriate diet as tolerated
Avoid juice, soda, and sports drinks
nursingActions: Monitor strict intake and output (count wet diapers)
Weigh daily at same time, same scale, same clothing
Assess anterior fontanelle in infants (sunken indicates dehydration)
Administer oral rehydration in small frequent sips (5mL every 1-2 minutes)
Monitor for signs of worsening dehydration (tachycardia, lethargy, hypotension)
Report decreased urine output or altered mental status immediately

foreign-body-aspiration.title: Yabancı Cisim Aspirasyo
foreign-body-aspiration.pathophysiology: Foreign body aspiration (FBA) occurs when an object is inhaled into the airway, causing partial or complete obstruction of the tracheobronchial tree. It is most common in children aged 6 months to 3 years due to their tendency to explore objects orally, incomplete dentition (poor chewing ability), immature swallowing coordination, and small airway diameter. The most commonly aspirated items include round foods (grapes, hot dogs, nuts, popcorn, hard candy), small toys/toy parts, coins, and buttons/batteries. The right main bronchus is more commonly affected due to its wider diameter and more vertical orientation. FBA is a leading cause of accidental death in children under 5 years. Prompt recognition and intervention are life-saving, as complete obstruction can lead to respiratory arrest within minutes.
diagnostics: Assess respiratory status (stridor, wheezing, coughing)
Monitor oxygen saturation
Observe for asymmetric breath sounds
Assess for signs of complete airway obstruction (inability to speak, cyanosis)
management: For partial obstruction: encourage coughing, do NOT perform blind finger sweeps
For complete obstruction: back blows and chest thrusts for infants; abdominal thrusts (Heimlich) for children >1 year
Maintain airway and administer oxygen as ordered
Prepare for bronchoscopy for object removal
Monitor post-removal for laryngeal edema
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status continuously
Keep emergency equipment at bedside (suction, oxygen)
Educate parents on age-appropriate food preparation (cut grapes lengthwise, no nuts < 4 years)
Teach parents choking rescue techniques
Report sudden onset coughing, gagging, or wheezing in a previously well child
Supervise young children during meals and play

androgen-insensitivity-rpn.title: Androjen Duyarsızlık Sendromu
androgen-insensitivity-rpn.pathophysiology: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS) is a condition in which a person who is genetically male (46,XY karyotype) is unable to respond to androgens (male hormones such as testosterone and dihydrotestosterone). Because the body's cells cannot use these hormones, the person develops with a female or partially female external appearance despite having male chromosomes. The testes produce testosterone normally, but the cells lack functioning androgen receptors, so the masculinizing effects of testosterone do not occur. The testes also produce Müllerian Inhibiting Substance (MIS), which causes the internal female reproductive structures (uterus, fallopian tubes, upper vagina) to regress during fetal development. As a result, individuals with complete AIS are born appearing phenotypically female but have no uterus or fallopian tubes. Undescended testes may be located in the abdomen or inguinal canal and may present as inguinal masses in a phenotypically female newborn.
diagnostics: Expect karyotype analysis to be ordered (will show 46,XY)
Monitor for inguinal masses in phenotypically female newborns
Expect hormone levels to be drawn (testosterone may be elevated)
Expect pelvic ultrasound to assess for absence of uterus and ovaries
Monitor growth and pubertal development milestones
management: Provide supportive and sensitive communication with family
Maintain confidentiality and use family-preferred language
Support psychosocial needs of patient and family
Reinforce information provided by the healthcare team regarding diagnosis
Assist with referrals to genetics, endocrinology, and psychology as ordered
Provide age-appropriate education to the patient
nursingActions: Use sensitive, non-judgmental language when discussing the condition with family
Respect patient and family privacy regarding the diagnosis
Document using family-preferred terms and pronouns
Assess family coping and refer to support services as needed
Monitor for signs of psychological distress in the patient (especially adolescents)
Educate family on the importance of follow-up appointments with specialists
Report any signs of inguinal herniation in phenotypically female infants to provider
Provide emotional support during diagnostic workup and disclosure

androgen-insensitivity.title: Androjen Duyarsızlık Sendromu
androgen-insensitivity.pathophysiology: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome (AIS) results from mutations in the androgen receptor (AR) gene located on the X chromosome. This X-linked recessive condition causes partial or complete inability of target cells to respond to testosterone and dihydrotestosterone (DHT), despite normal or elevated production of these hormones by the testes. During embryonic development, the SRY gene on the Y chromosome directs gonadal differentiation into testes, which then produce two critical hormones: testosterone (and its more potent derivative DHT) for masculinization of external genitalia, and Müllerian Inhibiting Substance (MIS, also called Anti-Müllerian Hormone or AMH) for regression of the Müllerian duct structures. In complete AIS (CAIS), the androgen receptor is entirely non-functional. Testosterone and DHT are produced normally by the fetal testes, but target tissues cannot respond. Consequently, external genital masculinization fails entirely, and the individual develops phenotypically female external genitalia. However, because MIS/AMH production and its receptor function are intact, the Müllerian ducts still regress. This results in the absence of a uterus, fallopian tubes, and the upper two-thirds of the vagina. The Wolffian duct structures (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles) also fail to develop because their differentiation requires local androgen action. The net result is a 46,XY individual with female external genitalia, a blind-ending vaginal pouch, absent uterus, and intra-abdominal or inguinal testes. At puberty, the testes produce testosterone which is aromatized to estrogen via the aromatase enzyme (CYP19A1), driving breast development and a female body habitus. However, pubic and axillary hair growth is minimal or absent because hair follicle androgen receptors are non-functional. In partial AIS (PAIS), the androgen receptor retains some function, resulting in ambiguous genitalia at birth with variable degrees of virilization depending on the residual receptor activity. The spectrum ranges from predominantly female phenotype with mild clitoromegaly to predominantly male phenotype with hypospadias and micropenis.
diagnostics: Expect karyotype analysis (reveals 46,XY despite female phenotype)
Expect serum testosterone levels (normal male range or elevated due to lack of negative feedback)
Expect serum LH levels (elevated due to impaired androgen-mediated negative feedback on hypothalamus)
Expect pelvic ultrasound or MRI (absent uterus, may identify intra-abdominal or inguinal gonads)
Expect AMH/MIS levels (normal male range, confirming presence of functional testicular tissue)
Monitor estradiol levels (produced via aromatization of testosterone)
Expect genetic testing for AR gene mutations to confirm diagnosis and classify as complete vs partial
management: Coordinate multidisciplinary team approach (endocrinology, genetics, psychology, gynecology, surgery)
Support patient-centered gender identity: individuals with CAIS are raised female and typically identify as female
Manage gonadectomy decision-making: discuss timing, risks of gonadal malignancy vs benefits of endogenous hormone production
Initiate estrogen replacement therapy after gonadectomy to maintain bone density and cardiovascular health
Address vaginal length adequacy: vaginal dilation therapy is first-line; surgical vaginoplasty only if dilation fails
Provide fertility counseling: individuals with CAIS are infertile (no uterus, no oocytes)
Ensure ongoing bone density monitoring (DEXA scans) given gonadectomy and estrogen dependence
nursingActions: Assess patient and family understanding of the diagnosis and provide education using clear, sensitive language
Monitor for signs of gonadal malignancy (abdominal pain, palpable mass, hormonal changes) in patients who retain gonads
Educate patient on estrogen replacement therapy adherence and side effects after gonadectomy
Screen for psychological distress, depression, and anxiety related to diagnosis disclosure
Coordinate specialist referrals and ensure continuity of care across disciplines
Educate adolescent patients on the condition in an age-appropriate, supportive manner
Assess bone health indicators and reinforce importance of DEXA scan follow-up
Facilitate connection with peer support groups and advocacy organizations (e.g., AIS-DSD Support Group)

androgen-insensitivity-np.title: Androjen Duyarsızlık Sendromu
androgen-insensitivity-np.pathophysiology: Androgen Insensitivity Syndrome arises from loss-of-function mutations in the androgen receptor (AR) gene, located on the long arm of the X chromosome at Xq11-12. The AR gene spans over 90 kb and contains 8 exons encoding a 919-amino acid nuclear receptor protein. The AR protein belongs to the nuclear receptor superfamily and contains four functional domains: the N-terminal transactivation domain (NTD, exon 1), the DNA-binding domain (DBD, exons 2-3) containing two zinc finger motifs that bind androgen response elements (AREs) in target gene promoters, the hinge region (exon 4) containing the nuclear localization signal, and the C-terminal ligand-binding domain (LBD, exons 4-8) that binds testosterone and DHT. Over 1,000 distinct AR mutations have been catalogued in the Androgen Receptor Gene Mutations Database. These mutations can be classified by their functional consequence: complete loss of ligand binding (LBD missense mutations disrupting the hydrophobic ligand-binding pocket), impaired DNA binding (DBD mutations affecting zinc finger coordination or ARE recognition), defective nuclear translocation (hinge region mutations), and impaired coactivator recruitment (mutations affecting the AF-1 and AF-2 transactivation surfaces that interact with p160 coactivators such as SRC-1, SRC-2/TIF2, and SRC-3). In CAIS, the AR is completely non-functional, whereas in PAIS, residual receptor activity ranges from minimal to substantial, explaining the clinical spectrum. The molecular mechanism of normal androgen signaling involves testosterone diffusing into the cell and either binding directly to the AR or being converted to the more potent DHT by 5-alpha reductase type 2 in genital skin. Upon ligand binding, the AR undergoes conformational change, dissociates from heat shock proteins (HSP90, HSP70), homodimerizes, and translocates to the nucleus where it binds AREs and recruits the transcriptional machinery to activate androgen-responsive genes. In AIS, this signaling cascade is disrupted at various points depending on the specific mutation. The testosterone-to-estrogen conversion pathway remains intact: aromatase (CYP19A1) in peripheral tissues converts testosterone to estradiol, which acts through functional estrogen receptors to drive breast development and fat distribution. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis is affected because testosterone cannot exert negative feedback on GnRH and LH secretion through the dysfunctional AR, resulting in elevated LH, elevated testosterone, and elevated estradiol levels. The ratio of testosterone to DHT and the absolute testosterone level can help distinguish AIS from 5-alpha reductase deficiency, another important cause of 46,XY DSD.
diagnostics: Expect karyotype: 46,XY in phenotypically female individual
Expect serum testosterone: normal male or elevated range (lack of AR-mediated negative feedback)
Expect serum LH: elevated (impaired androgen negative feedback on hypothalamus)
Expect serum DHT and testosterone-to-DHT ratio (to differentiate from 5-alpha reductase deficiency: in AIS, ratio is normal; in 5-ARD, ratio is elevated >20:1)
Expect serum AMH/MIS: normal male range (confirms functional Sertoli cells and testicular tissue)
Expect serum estradiol: elevated for 46,XY due to peripheral aromatization
Expect pelvic MRI: absent uterus, may identify gonadal location (inguinal canal or intra-abdominal)
Expect AR gene sequencing to identify specific mutation and classify complete vs partial AIS
Expect gonadal biopsy histopathology if malignancy risk stratification needed (Sertoli cell nodules, intratubular germ cell neoplasia)
management: Order AR gene sequencing for definitive molecular diagnosis and mutation classification
Stratify gonadal malignancy risk: CAIS lifetime risk 2-5% (low), PAIS with intra-abdominal gonads up to 15-50% (high) - individualize gonadectomy timing
For CAIS: consider delaying gonadectomy until after puberty to allow spontaneous breast development and pubertal maturation from endogenous estrogen
Prescribe estrogen replacement post-gonadectomy: transdermal estradiol 50-100 mcg/day or oral estradiol 1-2 mg/day, titrated to maintain premenopausal estradiol levels
No progestogen required (no uterus present)
Order baseline and serial DEXA scans for bone mineral density monitoring (every 2 years post-gonadectomy)
Refer for vaginal dilation therapy (Frank method) when patient is psychologically ready; reserve McIndoe vaginoplasty or Vecchietti procedure for dilation failure
Classify within DSD framework: AIS falls under 46,XY DSD (disorders of androgen action) per 2006 Chicago Consensus/2016 Updated Guidelines
Provide genetic counseling: carrier testing for female relatives, preimplantation genetic diagnosis options for carrier mothers
nursingActions: Conduct comprehensive endocrine assessment including AR gene mutation classification and genotype-phenotype correlation
Monitor gonadal malignancy surveillance: annual tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) and imaging if gonads retained
Assess estrogen replacement therapy compliance and efficacy (symptom control, estradiol levels, bone density)
Evaluate for osteoporosis risk factors and ensure DEXA monitoring adherence
Screen for psychological comorbidities: depression, anxiety, body image concerns, sexual identity distress using validated instruments
Coordinate multidisciplinary DSD team care (endocrinology, genetics, gynecology, psychology, surgery, ethics)
Educate patient on fertility options and limitations: egg donation with gestational carrier is the only biological parenting option
Facilitate informed consent process for gonadectomy: discuss malignancy risk, hormone implications, irreversibility, and timing options
Document using patient-preferred terminology and ensure all team members are informed of communication preferences

herbals-safety.title: Bitkisel ve Takviye Güvenliği
herbals-safety.pathophysiology: Herbals can have potent physiological effects and significant interactions with conventional medications, especially anticoagulants and CNS depressants.
diagnostics: Expect liver and renal function tests if hepatotoxicity suspected
Monitor coagulation studies (PT/INR) in patients on anticoagulants
Expect medication reconciliation to be completed
Monitor for signs of drug-herb interactions
Expect blood glucose monitoring if hypoglycemia risk
management: Document all herbal supplements in medication history
Advise to stop herbals 2 weeks before surgery as ordered
Report herbal use to healthcare team
Educate patients that herbals are not FDA-regulated
Avoid concurrent use of herbals with prescribed medications without approval
nursingActions: Ask about herbal and supplement use during every assessment
Report use of bleeding-risk herbals (Ginkgo, Garlic, Ginger, Ginseng) to physician
Monitor for signs of adverse interactions (bleeding, sedation, serotonin syndrome)
Document all herbals and supplements in the medication record
Educate patients about potential herb-drug interactions
Report unexplained symptoms that could indicate herbal interactions

electrolyte-safety.title: Elektrolit Dengesizlikleri
electrolyte-safety.pathophysiology: Electrolytes (K, Na, Ca, Mg) are critical for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, and fluid balance. Imbalances lead to lethal cardiac and neurological complications.
diagnostics: Monitor serum electrolyte levels (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, PO4)
Monitor ECG for electrolyte-related changes
Expect metabolic panel to be drawn regularly
Monitor serum osmolality
Expect ABG if acid-base imbalance suspected
Monitor urine specific gravity
management: Administer electrolyte replacements as prescribed
Follow dietary modifications as ordered
Maintain IV access for replacement therapy
Implement cardiac monitoring for K+ and Ca2+ abnormalities
Follow fluid restriction or replacement orders
Maintain seizure precautions for hyponatremia/hypocalcemia
nursingActions: Monitor and report electrolyte results promptly
NEVER give potassium IV push (lethal)
Assess for signs of cardiac instability (palpitations, irregular pulse)
Monitor for neuromuscular changes (weakness, tremors, tetany)
Report ECG changes (peaked T waves, U waves) immediately
Educate patients on dietary sources of electrolytes
Document intake and output strictly

cardiac-meds.title: Vazoaktif ve Kardiyak İlaçlar
cardiac-meds.pathophysiology: Inotropes (contractility), Vasopressors (SVR), and Antiarrhythmics are used to stabilize hemodynamics in critical care. Each class has narrow therapeutic windows and specific monitoring requirements.
diagnostics: Monitor drug levels (digoxin, theophylline) as ordered
Monitor electrolytes (K+ affects digoxin toxicity)
Expect ECG monitoring for cardiac medications
Monitor renal and hepatic function tests
Monitor vital signs before and after administration
Expect CBC if bleeding risk from anticoagulants
management: Check apical pulse for 1 full minute before giving digoxin
Hold beta blockers if HR < 60 or SBP < 100 per protocol
Administer cardiac medications at scheduled times
Maintain continuous cardiac monitoring as ordered
Report signs of drug toxicity immediately
Follow medication administration safety checks
nursingActions: Assess vital signs before administering vasoactive medications
Report bradycardia (< 60) or hypotension (SBP < 90) before giving dose
Monitor for signs of digoxin toxicity (yellow halos, nausea, bradycardia)
Educate patients on medication adherence and side effects
Monitor for angioedema with ACE inhibitors
Report signs of drug toxicity to physician promptly
Document medication administration and patient response

anticoagulant-safety.title: Antikoagülasyon Ustalığı
anticoagulant-safety.pathophysiology: Anticoagulants prevent thrombus formation by interrupting the coagulation cascade. They do not dissolve existing clots but prevent them from growing larger.
diagnostics: Monitor PT/INR for warfarin therapy
Monitor aPTT for heparin therapy
Expect CBC with platelet count to be drawn
Monitor stool and urine for occult blood
Expect coagulation panel regularly
Monitor hemoglobin and hematocrit trends
management: Administer anticoagulants at consistent scheduled times
Maintain fall precautions for patients on anticoagulants
Apply pressure to venipuncture sites for 5+ minutes
Avoid IM injections when possible
Use electric razors instead of straight razors
Use soft-bristled toothbrush only
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, gum bleeding, melena)
Report INR > 3.0 or aPTT above therapeutic range immediately
Educate on consistent vitamin K intake with warfarin
Report black tarry stools or blood in urine immediately
Assess for signs of internal bleeding (abdominal distension, back pain)
Document all anticoagulant administration times
Educate on wearing medical alert identification

insulin-safety.title: İnsülin ve Diyabet Güvenliği
insulin-safety.pathophysiology: Insulin facilitates glucose entry into cells. Over-administration or inadequate intake leads to hypoglycemia (neuroglycopenia), a medical emergency.
diagnostics: Monitor blood glucose before meals and at bedtime
Expect HbA1c to be ordered quarterly
Monitor serum potassium during DKA treatment
Expect metabolic panel for acid-base status in DKA
Monitor urine ketones as ordered
Expect point-of-care glucose checks per protocol
management: Check blood glucose before insulin administration
Ensure food is available before giving rapid-acting insulin
Follow sliding scale insulin protocol as ordered
Rotate injection sites systematically
Store insulin properly (refrigerate unopened, room temp opened)
Follow Rule of 15 for hypoglycemia (15g carbs, recheck in 15 min)
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of hypoglycemia (shakiness, sweating, confusion)
Report blood glucose < 70 mg/dL and treat immediately
Verify insulin type, dose, and expiration before administration
Monitor for signs of DKA (Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath)
Educate on sick day management and when to seek care
Document blood glucose levels and insulin administered
Report hyperglycemia > 300 mg/dL to physician

lithium-toxicity.title: Lityum ve Duygu Durum Dengeleyicileri
lithium-toxicity.pathophysiology: Lithium is a salt that competes with Sodium in the kidneys. Dehydration or low sodium intake causes the kidneys to reabsorb lithium, leading to toxicity.
diagnostics: Expect serum lithium level to be drawn (therapeutic 0.6-1.2 mEq/L)
Monitor BUN and creatinine for renal function
Expect BMP to be ordered (electrolytes, especially sodium)
Expect thyroid function tests (lithium affects thyroid)
Monitor ECG for cardiac conduction changes
Expect urine specific gravity to be checked
management: Hold lithium dose and notify provider immediately if toxicity suspected
Maintain adequate hydration (2-3 L/day)
Maintain normal sodium intake (do not restrict)
Avoid NSAIDs, which increase lithium levels
Position safely if confusion or ataxia present
Prepare for possible hemodialysis if levels > 2.5 mEq/L
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of toxicity (coarse tremors, confusion, ataxia, blurred vision)
Report GI symptoms (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) as early toxicity signs
Weigh patient daily to assess hydration status
Monitor intake and output
Educate patient on maintaining consistent fluid and sodium intake
Report changes in mental status or coordination immediately

factor-xa-inhibitors.title: Factor Xa İnhibitörleri (DOAC'lar)
factor-xa-inhibitors.pathophysiology: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) selectively inhibit Factor Xa in the coagulation cascade, preventing conversion of prothrombin to thrombin and subsequent fibrin clot formation. Unlike warfarin, they have predictable pharmacokinetics requiring no routine INR monitoring. Examples: Rivaroxaban (Xarelto), Apixaban (Eliquis), Edoxaban (Savaysa).
diagnostics: Monitor for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, blood in stool/urine)
Assess vital signs for unexplained tachycardia or hypotension
Monitor renal function trends as reported
Assess for neurological changes (intracranial bleed)
management: Administer medication at consistent times as prescribed
Do not crush or chew extended-release formulations
Report any signs of bleeding immediately
Hold medication and notify provider before procedures
Avoid concurrent NSAIDs and aspirin unless prescribed
nursingActions: Assess for signs of bleeding at every interaction
Monitor stool color (melena) and urine color (hematuria)
Educate patient on bleeding precautions (soft toothbrush, electric razor)
Remind patient of consistent dosing schedule
Report unexplained bruising or prolonged bleeding
Teach patient to inform all healthcare providers about anticoagulant use

methotrexate-safety-rpn.title: Metotreksat Güvenliği
methotrexate-safety-rpn.pathophysiology: Methotrexate is a folate antagonist used in autoimmune conditions, certain cancers, and ectopic pregnancy. It inhibits cell division by blocking folic acid metabolism. understanding safe handling, side effect monitoring, and timely reporting of abnormal findings is critical for patient safety.
diagnostics: Monitor CBC results for signs of myelosuppression (low WBC, low platelets, low RBC)
Monitor liver function test results as reported
Assess for signs of infection (fever, sore throat, malaise)
Monitor oral cavity for stomatitis and mucositis
Assess for signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, blood in stool/urine)
Monitor intake and output
management: Administer folic acid supplement as prescribed (reduces side effects)
Follow safe handling procedures for cytotoxic medication
Ensure adequate hydration as ordered
Hold dose and report if patient shows signs of infection, bleeding, or severe mouth sores
Avoid administering if recent lab values show myelosuppression
Report any abnormal lab results to the nurse or physician immediately
nursingActions: Inspect oral mucosa at each assessment for stomatitis (redness, ulceration, white patches)
Monitor temperature and report fever >38°C immediately (may indicate neutropenia)
Report any signs of bleeding (bruising, petechiae, gum bleeding, dark stools)
Use safe handling procedures: wear gloves when handling the medication
Educate patient to report sore throat, fever, mouth sores, or unusual bruising
Monitor for nausea and vomiting and report if persistent
Ensure patient is taking prescribed folic acid supplementation
Report signs of hepatotoxicity (jaundice, right upper quadrant pain, dark urine)

methotrexate-safety.title: Metotreksat
methotrexate-safety.pathophysiology: Methotrexate (MTX) exerts its primary mechanism of action through competitive inhibition of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR), the enzyme responsible for converting dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate. Tetrahydrofolate is essential for the synthesis of thymidylate, purines, and amino acids required for DNA and RNA synthesis. By blocking DHFR, methotrexate disrupts the folate cycle, halting DNA replication and cell division. This mechanism is particularly effective against rapidly dividing cells, which explains its therapeutic utility in both malignancies (cancer cells) and autoimmune conditions (activated lymphocytes and inflammatory cells). At low doses used for rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune conditions, methotrexate also acts through adenosine-mediated anti-inflammatory pathways. The drug accumulates intracellularly as polyglutamated forms, which have prolonged half-lives and sustained DHFR inhibition. In ectopic pregnancy management, methotrexate targets the rapidly dividing trophoblastic tissue, causing dissolution of the ectopic implantation without surgical intervention. Leucovorin (folinic acid) serves as the rescue agent by providing a reduced folate that bypasses the DHFR block, allowing normal cells to resume DNA synthesis while cancer cells (which take up leucovorin less efficiently) remain affected.
diagnostics: Monitor CBC with differential weekly during initiation, then regularly (nadir for myelosuppression typically 7-14 days)
Monitor liver function tests (AST, ALT) at baseline and regularly throughout therapy
Monitor serum creatinine and BUN (renal function critical for drug clearance)
Expect serum methotrexate levels for high-dose oncology protocols
Monitor for signs of pulmonary toxicity (chest X-ray if new respiratory symptoms)
Assess oral mucosa systematically for early stomatitis
Monitor serum albumin (low albumin increases free drug concentration)
management: Administer leucovorin rescue as prescribed within 24 hours of high-dose methotrexate (timing is critical)
Ensure aggressive IV hydration and urine alkalinization (pH >7.0) for high-dose protocols to prevent crystalluria
Administer folic acid 1 mg daily (except on methotrexate day) for chronic low-dose therapy
Hold methotrexate and notify provider for WBC <3,000, platelets <100,000, or rising creatinine
Implement neutropenic precautions if ANC falls below threshold
Educate on strict pregnancy avoidance during treatment and for 3 months after discontinuation (teratogenic)
nursingActions: Verify lab results (CBC, LFTs, renal function) before each dose administration
Assess for drug interactions: NSAIDs, trimethoprim, proton pump inhibitors all increase toxicity
Monitor for signs of methotrexate pneumonitis (non-productive cough, dyspnea, fever - can occur at any dose)
Educate patient on teratogenicity and need for reliable contraception
Implement infection prevention measures during myelosuppressive nadir
For ectopic pregnancy protocol: educate patient on expected separation pain vs emergency symptoms
Monitor for signs of tumor lysis syndrome in oncology patients
Document methotrexate levels and leucovorin rescue timing precisely

methotrexate-safety-np.title: Metotreksat
methotrexate-safety-np.pathophysiology: Methotrexate pharmacology extends far beyond simple DHFR inhibition when examined at the molecular level. After cellular uptake via the reduced folate carrier (RFC1/SLC19A1), methotrexate undergoes intracellular polyglutamation by folylpolyglutamate synthetase (FPGS), adding up to 5-6 glutamate residues. These polyglutamated forms (MTX-PG) are retained intracellularly with half-lives of weeks to months, creating a sustained pharmacological effect even with weekly dosing. MTX-PG directly inhibits multiple enzymes in the folate pathway: DHFR (blocking dihydrofolate to tetrahydrofolate conversion), thymidylate synthase (TS, blocking dTMP synthesis required for DNA replication), and critically, AICAR transformylase (aminoimidazole carboxamide ribonucleotide transformylase, also called ATIC). Inhibition of AICAR transformylase leads to intracellular accumulation of AICAR, which inhibits adenosine deaminase (ADA) and AMP deaminase, resulting in increased extracellular adenosine release. This adenosine pathway is now understood to be the PRIMARY anti-inflammatory mechanism of low-dose methotrexate in autoimmune disease. Adenosine binds to A2A and A3 receptors on inflammatory cells, suppressing NF-κB activation, reducing TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8 production, inhibiting neutrophil adhesion, and promoting anti-inflammatory macrophage polarization (M2 phenotype). Pharmacogenomic variation significantly impacts methotrexate efficacy and toxicity. The MTHFR (methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase) C677T and A1298C polymorphisms reduce enzyme activity, leading to altered folate metabolism and potentially increased methotrexate toxicity. Patients homozygous for MTHFR C677T (TT genotype, prevalence ~10-15% in Caucasians) have 30-50% reduced enzyme activity, resulting in elevated homocysteine levels and increased susceptibility to methotrexate-induced myelosuppression, hepatotoxicity, and mucositis. ABCB1 (P-glycoprotein) and ABCC2 (MRP2) transporter polymorphisms affect methotrexate efflux from cells, while RFC1 (SLC19A1) G80A polymorphism affects cellular drug uptake, collectively influencing both efficacy and toxicity profiles.
diagnostics: Order baseline and serial CBC with differential, comprehensive metabolic panel, hepatic panel
Monitor serum methotrexate levels at 24, 48, and 72 hours post high-dose infusion (target <0.05 μmol/L for leucovorin discontinuation)
Consider MTHFR genotyping for patients with unexplained toxicity at standard doses
Order baseline chest X-ray and pulmonary function tests for high-risk patients
Monitor liver fibrosis risk: consider FibroScan or liver biopsy after cumulative dose of 3.5-4 g in chronic therapy
Evaluate beta-hCG trends in ectopic pregnancy management (expect ≥15% decline between days 4-7)
Assess anti-MTX-PG antibody formation in patients with unexplained treatment failure
management: Prescribe methotrexate dosing based on indication: RA (7.5-25 mg/week PO/SC), ectopic pregnancy (50 mg/m² single-dose or multi-dose protocol), oncology (varies widely by protocol)
For ectopic pregnancy single-dose protocol: MTX 50 mg/m² IM on day 1, measure beta-hCG days 4 and 7, repeat dose if <15% decline between days 4-7
Risk stratify ectopic pregnancy patients: single-dose protocol for beta-hCG <5,000; multi-dose protocol for beta-hCG >5,000 or ectopic mass >3.5 cm or fetal cardiac activity present
Initiate leucovorin rescue protocol: 15 mg/m² IV/PO every 6 hours starting 24 hours after high-dose MTX, continue until serum MTX <0.05 μmol/L
Dose adjust for renal function: reduce by 50% for GFR 30-60 mL/min; contraindicated if GFR <30 mL/min
Consider pharmacogenomic-guided prescribing: patients with MTHFR TT genotype may benefit from higher folic acid supplementation (5 mg/week) and lower starting doses
nursingActions: Evaluate pharmacogenomic risk factors before initiating methotrexate therapy
Calculate BSA-based dosing accurately for oncology and ectopic pregnancy protocols
Monitor methotrexate clearance kinetics: delayed clearance (level >1 μmol/L at 48 hours) requires escalated leucovorin rescue
Assess for signs of methotrexate-induced lymphoproliferative disorder (rare but serious complication of chronic immunosuppression)
Manage ectopic pregnancy protocol: counsel patient on expected course, separation pain timeline, and emergency warning signs (severe abdominal pain, syncope, shoulder pain indicating hemoperitoneum)
Evaluate cumulative hepatotoxicity risk for long-term therapy and recommend appropriate monitoring intervals
Coordinate multidisciplinary care for complex oncology protocols including hydration, alkalinization, and rescue timing
Develop individualized monitoring plans based on patient-specific risk factors and pharmacogenomic profile

oxybutynin-rpn.title: Oxybutynin (Ditropan) - Antikolinerjik
oxybutynin-rpn.pathophysiology: Oxybutynin is an anticholinergic and antispasmodic agent used to treat overactive bladder (OAB) and urinary urgency or incontinence. It works by blocking muscarinic M3 receptors on the detrusor muscle of the bladder, reducing involuntary detrusor contractions and increasing bladder capacity. This parasympathetic blockade decreases the urge to void and reduces episodes of urge incontinence. Because muscarinic receptors are found throughout the body (salivary glands, GI tract, eyes, sweat glands, CNS), systemic anticholinergic side effects are common and clinically significant.
diagnostics: Monitor urinary output and voiding patterns
Assess post-void residual volume (bladder scan)
Monitor cognitive status in elderly patients at each assessment
Assess for signs of urinary retention (distended bladder, inability to void)
Monitor bowel function for constipation
Assess intraocular pressure in patients with glaucoma risk
management: Administer medication as prescribed (oral or transdermal patch)
Encourage adequate fluid intake to prevent urinary retention
Implement fall precautions especially in elderly patients
Ensure toileting schedule to support bladder retraining
Discontinue and notify provider if urinary retention develops
Avoid use in patients with uncontrolled narrow-angle glaucoma
nursingActions: Assess for dry mouth and provide comfort measures (sugar-free candy, frequent oral care)
Monitor for urinary retention (palpate bladder, assess voiding frequency and volume)
Assess cognitive function in elderly patients and report any new confusion or agitation
Educate patient to avoid hot environments due to impaired sweating and heat stroke risk
Monitor for constipation and encourage dietary fiber and fluids
Report falls or near-falls immediately (anticholinergic medications increase fall risk)
Educate patient to report blurred vision, dizziness, or difficulty urinating
Review medication list for other anticholinergic drugs (additive burden)

levetiracetam-rpn.title: Levetiracetam (Keppra) - Antikonvülsan
levetiracetam-rpn.pathophysiology: Levetiracetam is a broad-spectrum anticonvulsant used for partial-onset seizures, myoclonic seizures, and primary generalized tonic-clonic seizures. Unlike older antiepileptics, levetiracetam has a unique mechanism of action - it binds to synaptic vesicle protein SV2A in the brain, which modulates neurotransmitter release from presynaptic vesicles. This binding reduces excessive neuronal firing without broadly suppressing normal brain activity. Levetiracetam is primarily eliminated by the kidneys (66% unchanged in urine), has minimal hepatic metabolism, and does not induce or inhibit cytochrome P450 enzymes, resulting in very few drug interactions compared to older anticonvulsants.
diagnostics: Monitor seizure activity (type, frequency, duration, postictal state)
Assess behavioral and mood changes at every visit (irritability, aggression, depression)
Monitor renal function (BUN, creatinine) for dose adjustment
Screen for suicidal ideation per FDA black box warning for antiepileptics
Assess for somnolence and dizziness affecting safety and daily function
Monitor for signs of infection (rare leukopenia)
management: Administer medication as prescribed - can be given with or without food
NEVER stop abruptly - taper gradually over 2 to 4 weeks to prevent status epilepticus
Adjust dose based on renal function as ordered
Implement seizure precautions (padded side rails, suction at bedside, oxygen available)
Ensure consistent dosing schedule to maintain therapeutic levels
Report behavioral changes or mood disturbances promptly for evaluation
nursingActions: Monitor and document seizure activity (onset, duration, type of movements, postictal state)
Assess for behavioral and mood changes at each interaction - report irritability, aggression, or mood swings (Keppra rage)
Screen for depression and suicidal ideation per facility protocol
Educate patient and family that medication must not be stopped abruptly
Monitor for excessive drowsiness or dizziness and implement fall precautions
Educate patient to avoid driving or operating heavy machinery until response to medication is known
Report any new psychiatric symptoms (hostility, anxiety, psychosis) immediately
Ensure medication is available and administered on time to prevent breakthrough seizures

short-acting-insulin-rpn.title: Kısa Etkili İnsülin (Regular Insulin)
short-acting-insulin-rpn.pathophysiology: Regular insulin (Humulin R, Novolin R) is a short-acting insulin that mimics the body's natural insulin response to meals. It facilitates glucose uptake into cells by binding to insulin receptors on cell membranes, activating glucose transporter proteins (GLUT4) that move glucose from the bloodstream into skeletal muscle, adipose tissue, and other cells. Regular insulin is the ONLY insulin that can be administered intravenously, making it critical for emergency management of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) and hyperkalemia. When given subcutaneously, onset is 30 to 60 minutes with a peak at 2 to 4 hours. When given IV, onset is approximately 15 minutes with a much shorter duration, requiring continuous infusion and close monitoring.
diagnostics: Monitor blood glucose before meals and at bedtime per protocol
Monitor blood glucose every 1 to 2 hours during IV insulin infusion
Assess for signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia at each interaction
Monitor serum potassium during IV insulin administration (insulin shifts K+ intracellularly)
Assess injection sites for lipodystrophy
Monitor intake and output during DKA management
management: Administer subcutaneous regular insulin 30 minutes before meals
Follow sliding scale insulin protocol as ordered for glucose correction
For IV administration: use an infusion pump with continuous glucose monitoring
Draw up regular insulin BEFORE NPH when mixing in the same syringe (clear before cloudy)
Verify insulin type, dose, and expiration with a second nurse per facility policy
Follow Rule of 15 for hypoglycemia: give 15 g fast-acting carbohydrate, recheck glucose in 15 minutes
Ensure meal tray is available before administering mealtime insulin
nursingActions: Verify that regular insulin is a CLEAR solution - do NOT administer if cloudy (cloudy indicates NPH or contamination)
Check blood glucose before every insulin administration
Monitor for hypoglycemia signs: tremors, sweating, tachycardia, confusion, irritability
When mixing insulins: draw up Regular (clear) BEFORE NPH (cloudy) to prevent contamination
During IV insulin infusion: monitor glucose every 1 to 2 hours and potassium levels as ordered
Ensure patient eats within 30 minutes of subcutaneous regular insulin injection
Report blood glucose below 70 mg/dL and treat immediately per protocol
Educate patient on timing of meals relative to insulin injection and importance of not skipping meals

infant-reflexes-rpn.title: Bebek Refleksleri
infant-reflexes-rpn.pathophysiology: Infant reflexes are involuntary motor responses present at birth that are controlled by the brainstem and spinal cord. These primitive reflexes are essential for survival and neurological assessment. They emerge during fetal development and normally disappear (integrate) at specific ages as the cerebral cortex matures and assumes voluntary motor control. Persistence of primitive reflexes beyond expected age or absence at birth indicates potential neurological dysfunction. The most clinically important reflexes include Moro (startle), rooting, sucking, palmar grasp, plantar grasp, Babinski, tonic neck (fencing), and stepping reflexes. Assessment of these reflexes is a fundamental component of the newborn neurological examination and ongoing developmental screening.
diagnostics: Assess Moro reflex: hold infant supine, slightly lower head - expect symmetric arm extension then embrace
Assess rooting reflex: stroke cheek - infant turns toward stimulus
Assess sucking reflex: place finger in mouth - strong coordinated sucking
Assess palmar grasp: place finger in palm - infant grasps tightly
Assess Babinski reflex: stroke lateral sole - toes fan outward (normal in infants)
Report ABSENT or ASYMMETRIC reflexes to RN/physician immediately
management: Document reflex presence, quality (strong, weak, absent), and symmetry
Report any absent, weak, or asymmetric reflexes promptly
Monitor feeding ability (rooting and sucking reflexes essential for nutrition)
Support parents in understanding normal infant behaviors related to reflexes
Report persistence of reflexes beyond expected age at follow-up visits
nursingActions: Perform reflex assessment as part of newborn physical examination
Document reflex findings accurately using standardized terminology
Assess bilateral symmetry of all reflexes (asymmetry suggests nerve injury)
Monitor sucking and rooting effectiveness for feeding adequacy
Report abnormal findings to RN/physician for further neurological evaluation
Educate parents on normal reflex behaviors to reduce anxiety

infant-reflexes-np.title: Bebek Refleksleri
infant-reflexes-np.pathophysiology: Primitive reflexes are mediated by brainstem and spinal cord circuits that develop in utero between 25-38 weeks gestation. The Moro reflex arc involves vestibular nuclei sensing head position change, with efferent signals via the reticulospinal tract producing bilateral upper extremity abduction-extension followed by adduction-flexion. Cortical maturation progressively inhibits these subcortical pathways through descending corticospinal tract myelination, which occurs in a cephalocaudal pattern explaining the sequential disappearance of reflexes. The corticospinal tract achieves functional myelination between 12-24 months, coinciding with disappearance of Babinski sign. Persistent primitive reflexes beyond expected age indicate failure of cortical inhibition - the hallmark of upper motor neuron dysfunction. This is a key diagnostic feature of cerebral palsy, where periventricular leukomalacia or cortical injury prevents normal corticospinal tract development. The tonic neck reflex is mediated by cervical proprioceptors activating ipsilateral extensor motor neurons and contralateral flexor motor neurons through the vestibulospinal tract. Obligatory tonic neck response (inability to turn out of position) is always pathological and suggests severe cortical dysfunction. Postural reflexes (Landau, parachute, lateral propping) are cortically mediated protective responses that EMERGE as primitive reflexes disappear - their absence beyond expected age also indicates developmental delay.
diagnostics: Comprehensive primitive reflex assessment with timing of emergence and disappearance
Assess postural reflex emergence (Landau 3 months, parachute 8-9 months)
Standardized developmental screening tools (Ages and Stages Questionnaire, Denver II)
Cranial ultrasound for periventricular leukomalacia in premature infants
MRI brain if persistent primitive reflexes or delayed postural reflexes
EEG if seizures suspected alongside abnormal reflex patterns
Genetic testing if dysmorphic features present with reflex abnormalities
Assess muscle tone patterns (hypotonia progressing to spasticity in CP)
management: Refer for comprehensive neurodevelopmental evaluation if reflexes persist beyond expected age
Order brain MRI for structural assessment of cortical and white matter pathology
Initiate early intervention referral for physical therapy and occupational therapy
Screen for associated conditions: visual impairment, hearing loss, feeding difficulties
Prescribe botulinum toxin for focal spasticity in established cerebral palsy
Coordinate multidisciplinary team: neurology, developmental pediatrics, PT/OT, speech therapy
Assess for hip dysplasia in infants with abnormal leg reflexes or tone asymmetry
Develop individualized Family Service Plan (IFSP) for infants under 3 years with delays
nursingActions: Perform serial reflex assessments at well-child visits to track developmental trajectory
Document primitive reflex persistence and postural reflex emergence timeline
Use standardized developmental screening at 9, 18, 24, and 30 months
Educate families on developmental milestones and red flags requiring evaluation
Coordinate early intervention services and follow-up appointments
Assess parent-infant interaction and provide anticipatory guidance
Screen for parental anxiety and depression related to developmental concerns
Maintain detailed developmental record for longitudinal comparison

wound-care-basics.title: Yara Bakımı ve Bandaj Değişiklikleri
wound-care-basics.pathophysiology: Wound healing occurs in 4 phases: Hemostasis (platelet plug), Inflammation (WBCs migrate), Proliferation (granulation tissue, new vessels), and Remodeling (scar maturation over months). Healing by primary intention (surgical closure), secondary intention (open wound fills in), or tertiary intention (delayed closure).
diagnostics: Expect wound culture if infection suspected
Monitor wound measurements (length x width x depth) with each dressing change
Expect nutritional labs (albumin, prealbumin) to assess healing potential
Monitor WBC count for signs of infection
Expect wound photography per facility protocol
Monitor blood glucose in diabetic patients (affects healing)
management: Maintain sterile or clean technique per wound type and order
Follow prescribed dressing change schedule
Keep wound bed moist but surrounding skin dry
Ensure adequate nutrition (protein, Vitamin C, zinc)
Position patient to offload pressure from wound
Follow clean-to-dirty irrigation technique
nursingActions: Assess wound with each dressing change (size, bed color, drainage, odor)
Report signs of infection (redness, warmth, purulent drainage, odor)
Document wound assessment findings accurately
Maintain aseptic technique during dressing changes
Educate patient on wound care and signs of infection
Ensure adequate pain management before dressing changes
Report failure to progress in healing to provider

catheterization.title: İdrar Kateterizasyonu
catheterization.pathophysiology: Catheterization provides drainage when normal voiding is impaired. Indwelling (Foley) catheters carry CAUTI risk - the #1 healthcare-associated infection. Biofilm formation on catheter surfaces begins within 24 hours. Strict sterile technique during insertion and closed system maintenance are essential to prevent infection.
diagnostics: Expect urinalysis and culture if CAUTI suspected
Monitor urine output hourly (report < 30 mL/hr in adults)
Expect bladder scan before catheterization to confirm retention
Monitor urine color, clarity, and odor
Expect CBC if infection suspected
Monitor temperature for fever (CAUTI indicator)
management: Maintain strict sterile technique during insertion
Secure catheter to prevent traction injury
Keep drainage bag below bladder level at all times
Maintain closed drainage system (do not disconnect)
Remove catheter as soon as clinically indicated
Follow facility CAUTI prevention bundle
nursingActions: Assess for continued need for catheter daily
Monitor and document urine output every shift
Perform perineal hygiene and catheter care per protocol
Report signs of CAUTI (cloudy urine, fever, new confusion)
Ensure drainage bag is below bladder level and not touching floor
Empty drainage bag when 2/3 full using aseptic technique
Document catheter insertion date and size

ngtube-care.title: NG Tüpü Takma ve Bakım
ngtube-care.pathophysiology: Nasogastric tubes serve two purposes: decompression (removing gas/fluid from stomach) and enteral feeding. Proper placement verification is critical to prevent aspiration. The tube passes through the nasopharynx, down the esophagus, and into the stomach. Misplacement into the lungs is the most dangerous complication.
diagnostics: Expect chest/abdominal X-ray for placement verification (gold standard)
Monitor pH of gastric aspirate (should be < 5.5)
Monitor electrolytes (risk of imbalances from suctioning)
Expect I&O monitoring to be ordered
Monitor for metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric acid
Expect abdominal assessment for distension and bowel sounds
management: Verify placement before each use (pH testing, X-ray)
Maintain HOB elevation 30-45 degrees during feedings
Flush tube with 30 mL water every 4 hours and before/after medications
Follow ordered suction settings (low intermittent for decompression)
Secure tube to nose to prevent migration
Do not mix medications together through the tube
nursingActions: Verify tube placement before each feeding or medication administration
Monitor and document output amount, color, and consistency
Assess nares for skin breakdown and reposition tape as needed
Report increased or bloody output immediately
Check gastric residual before intermittent feedings
Provide frequent oral and nasal care for comfort
Report abdominal distension or vomiting around tube

iv-therapy.title: IV Terapisi ve Venipunktur
iv-therapy.pathophysiology: Intravenous therapy delivers fluids, medications, and blood products directly into the vascular system. Peripheral IV catheters access superficial veins (cephalic, basilic, median cubital). Osmolarity determines vein compatibility: isotonic (250-375 mOsm), hypertonic (>375 mOsm requires central access for prolonged use), hypotonic (<250 mOsm).
diagnostics: Monitor IV site for signs of infiltration or phlebitis per facility protocol
Expect CBC and metabolic panel for patients on IV fluids
Monitor fluid balance with I&O documentation
Expect daily weight monitoring for patients on IV fluids
Monitor infusion rate and volume infused
Assess site patency by flushing before medication administration
management: Change peripheral IV every 72-96 hours per facility policy
Maintain aseptic technique during insertion and access
Select appropriate gauge and site for prescribed therapy
Calculate and verify drip rates before starting infusion
Use only compatible fluids and medications
Secure IV catheter to prevent dislodgement
nursingActions: Assess IV site at least every 1-2 hours for complications
Report signs of infiltration (swelling, coolness, pallor) immediately
Report signs of phlebitis (redness, warmth, tenderness along vein)
Document site assessment, patency, and any complications
Verify the 5 Rights before each IV medication administration
Flush IV line before and after medication with saline
Educate patient to report pain, swelling, or burning at IV site

blood-transfusion.title: Kan Transfüzyonu Yönetimi
blood-transfusion.pathophysiology: Blood products replace oxygen-carrying capacity (pRBCs), clotting factors (FFP), or platelets. Transfusion reactions range from mild febrile to fatal hemolytic. ABO incompatibility triggers complement-mediated hemolysis destroying donor RBCs. Type and crossmatch ensures compatibility. Two-nurse verification at bedside is mandatory.
diagnostics: Expect type and crossmatch to be ordered before transfusion
Monitor baseline vital signs before starting transfusion
Expect hemoglobin and hematocrit to be checked post-transfusion
Monitor for signs of transfusion reaction during infusion
Expect two-nurse verification of blood product at bedside
Monitor vital signs at baseline, 15 min, 30 min, and completion
management: Use only 0.9% Normal Saline with blood products (never LR or D5W)
Infuse blood products through appropriate blood tubing with 170-micron filter
Complete transfusion within 4 hours of leaving blood bank
Stay with patient for first 15 minutes of transfusion
STOP transfusion immediately if reaction occurs
Maintain large-bore IV access (18-20 gauge preferred)
nursingActions: Verify two forms of patient identification with two nurses at bedside
Obtain and document baseline vital signs before starting
Monitor vital signs at 15 min, 30 min, and at completion
Stay with patient for first 15 minutes and assess for reactions
Report any signs of reaction immediately (fever, chills, flank pain, hives)
If reaction occurs: STOP transfusion, maintain IV with NS, notify provider
Document blood product type, unit number, start/stop times, and patient response

chest-tube-mgmt.title: Göğüs Tüpü Yönetimi
chest-tube-mgmt.pathophysiology: Chest tubes drain air (pneumothorax) or fluid (hemothorax/pleural effusion) from the pleural space, restoring negative intrapleural pressure needed for lung expansion. The drainage system has three chambers: collection, water seal (prevents air re-entry), and suction control. Tidaling in the water seal chamber indicates proper function.
diagnostics: Expect chest X-ray after insertion to verify placement
Monitor drainage output hourly (report > 100 mL/hr)
Expect serial chest X-rays to assess lung re-expansion
Monitor water seal chamber for tidaling (normal) vs continuous bubbling (air leak)
Expect ABG or SpO2 monitoring for oxygenation status
Monitor respiratory rate and breath sounds bilaterally
management: Keep drainage system below chest level at all times
Maintain water seal with sterile water at 2 cm level
Encourage deep breathing and coughing exercises
Never clamp chest tube without a physician order
Keep petroleum gauze dressing at bedside for accidental dislodgement
Ensure all connections are securely taped
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status and breath sounds every 2-4 hours
Monitor and document drainage amount, color, and consistency hourly
Report sudden increase in drainage or change in character
Check for tidaling in water seal chamber (absence may indicate obstruction)
Report continuous bubbling in water seal (indicates air leak)
Maintain patient in semi-Fowler position to promote drainage
Have emergency supplies at bedside (petroleum gauze, sterile occlusive dressing)

trach-care.title: Trakeostomi Bakımı ve Sıklama
trach-care.pathophysiology: A tracheostomy bypasses the upper airway, eliminating the nose and mouth's natural warming, humidifying, and filtering functions. This increases infection risk and secretion production. The cuff, when inflated, seals the airway for mechanical ventilation but can cause tracheal necrosis if pressure exceeds capillary perfusion pressure (>25 cmH2O).
diagnostics: Expect chest X-ray to verify tracheostomy placement
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect sputum culture if infection suspected
Monitor cuff pressure (maintain < 25 cmH2O)
Expect ABG if respiratory status changes
Monitor for signs of tracheal stenosis or granulation tissue
management: Clean inner cannula every 8 hours or per facility protocol
Maintain humidification of inspired air at all times
Suction only as needed (not on a schedule)
Keep spare tracheostomy tube and obturator at bedside
Maintain cuff pressure below 25 cmH2O to prevent tracheal necrosis
Change tracheostomy ties when soiled (two-person technique)
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status and oxygen saturation frequently
Hyperoxygenate before suctioning (10-15 seconds max per pass)
Report changes in secretion color, amount, or consistency
Maintain stoma site cleanliness with NS and half-strength H2O2
Provide alternative communication methods (writing board, speaking valve)
Report signs of accidental decannulation or mucus plugging immediately
Assess skin around stoma for breakdown or infection

osteoporosis-basics.title: Osteoporoz
osteoporosis-basics.pathophysiology: Imbalance between osteoblast bone formation and osteoclast bone resorption. Peak bone mass by age 30, then gradual loss. Estrogen decline accelerates loss in menopause.
diagnostics: Expect DEXA scan for bone density measurement
Monitor serum calcium and vitamin D levels
Expect alkaline phosphatase levels
Monitor height annually for loss (vertebral compression)
Expect X-ray if fracture suspected
Monitor renal function before bisphosphonate therapy
management: Administer bisphosphonate as prescribed (upright with full glass of water)
Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D supplementation
Implement fall prevention measures
Encourage weight-bearing exercise
Avoid bending and twisting movements
Maintain safe home environment
nursingActions: Educate on proper bisphosphonate administration (upright 30 minutes, empty stomach)
Implement fall prevention measures (clear pathways, adequate lighting, non-slip footwear)
Report any new pain suggesting fracture (hip, wrist, back)
Encourage weight-bearing exercise as tolerated
Monitor calcium and vitamin D intake
Educate on avoidance of smoking and excessive alcohol

inhaled-spacers.title: İnhale Spacer'lar
inhaled-spacers.pathophysiology: Spacers are holding chambers that slow the velocity of aerosolized medication from MDIs, allowing more drug to reach the lungs instead of depositing in the oropharynx. Reduces systemic absorption and side effects (thrush from corticosteroids, tachycardia from bronchodilators).
diagnostics: Observe patient inhaler technique
Monitor respiratory rate and effort
Assess breath sounds before and after treatment
Monitor oxygen saturation with pulse oximetry
Assess peak flow readings as ordered
Observe for medication side effects (tremor, tachycardia)
management: Ensure spacer is attached properly to MDI before use
Administer bronchodilator before corticosteroid as ordered
Provide mouth care after inhaled corticosteroid use
Report ineffective symptom relief to RN/provider
Clean spacer weekly per manufacturer instructions
Replace spacer as recommended
nursingActions: Teach and assess proper spacer technique at each visit
Remind patient to rinse mouth after corticosteroid use
Monitor heart rate after bronchodilator administration
Assess for oral thrush with each assessment
Document inhaler technique proficiency
Report signs of uncontrolled symptoms to RN/provider

meds-to-infants.title: Bebeklere İlaç Uygulama
meds-to-infants.pathophysiology: Infants have immature liver/kidney function affecting drug metabolism and excretion. Body composition (higher water content, lower fat) affects drug distribution. Dosing is always weight-based (mg/kg). Oral route preferred when possible.
diagnostics: Verify current weight before dose calculation
Monitor vital signs before and after medication administration
Assess swallowing ability before oral administration
Monitor for adverse effects after administration
Assess pain level using age-appropriate scale
Monitor intake and output
management: Calculate dose based on current weight (mg/kg)
Use calibrated oral syringe for accurate measurement
Administer small amounts into inner cheek
Hold infant semi-upright during administration
Never add medication to infant formula bottle
Report adverse reactions to RN/provider immediately
nursingActions: Verify weight-based dose calculation with second nurse
Check allergies with parent/caregiver before administration
Use two patient identifiers before giving medication
Document medication administration accurately
Observe infant for 15-30 minutes after new medication
Educate caregivers on proper home medication administration
Report vomiting within 30 minutes of dose to RN/provider

cleansing-enemas.title: Temizleme Lavmanları
cleansing-enemas.pathophysiology: Enemas introduce fluid into the rectum/colon to stimulate peristalsis and evacuate fecal matter. Types: tap water (hypotonic - risk of fluid/electrolyte imbalance), normal saline (isotonic - safest), soap suds (irritant), fleet (hypertonic phosphate).
diagnostics: Assess bowel sounds before administration
Monitor vital signs before and after procedure
Assess abdominal distension and tenderness
Document last bowel movement and characteristics
Monitor for cramping during administration
Assess results (amount, color, consistency)
management: Position patient in left Sims position
Warm solution to 105°F (40.5°C)
Insert tubing gently 3-4 inches in adults
Hang bag 12-18 inches above anus level
Administer solution slowly and stop if severe cramping occurs
Report absence of results to RN/provider
nursingActions: Verify order and type of enema prescribed
Explain procedure and provide privacy
Assist patient to left lateral (Sims) position
Monitor patient tolerance during administration
Document time, type, amount administered, and results
Report complications (bleeding, severe pain, no results) to RN/provider
Assist patient to bathroom or commode after procedure

wound-irrigation.title: Yara İrrigasyonu
wound-irrigation.pathophysiology: Wound irrigation mechanically removes debris, bacteria, and necrotic tissue from wound bed. The goal is to deliver enough pressure to cleanse without damaging healthy granulation tissue. Optimal pressure: 8-15 psi (achieved with 35mL syringe and 19-gauge needle/angiocatheter).
diagnostics: Assess wound bed appearance before irrigation
Measure wound dimensions (length x width x depth)
Monitor wound drainage color, amount, and odor
Monitor vital signs if large wound irrigation
Assess periwound skin integrity
Document wound appearance before and after irrigation
management: Use normal saline at body temperature as standard irrigant
Use 35mL syringe with 19-gauge angiocatheter for optimal pressure (8-15 psi)
Irrigate from clean area to dirty area
Wear appropriate PPE including splash protection
Apply prescribed dressing after irrigation
Report signs of wound infection to RN/provider
nursingActions: Verify wound irrigation order and solution type
Pre-medicate for pain as ordered before procedure
Position patient to allow drainage away from wound
Use aseptic or sterile technique per wound type and order
Document wound assessment findings before and after procedure
Report changes in wound appearance or new drainage to RN/provider
Educate patient on wound care and infection signs

vac-dressing.title: VAC Bandaj: Negatif Basınç Yara Terapisi
vac-dressing.pathophysiology:
Negative Pressure Wound Therapy (NPWT), commonly known as wound VAC (Vacuum-Assisted Closure), applies controlled sub-atmospheric (negative) pressure to a wound bed through a sealed dressing system. Understanding how it works requires understanding wound healing at the cellular level.

Normal wound healing progresses through four overlapping phases: hemostasis (platelet plug and fibrin clot within minutes), inflammation (neutrophils arrive within 6 hours, macrophages by 24-48 hours to phagocytose debris and bacteria, releasing cytokines like TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-6 that recruit fibroblasts), proliferation (fibroblasts deposit collagen, endothelial cells form new capillaries via angiogenesis guided by VEGF, and keratinocytes migrate across the wound bed: this phase requires a moist, well-perfused wound bed), and remodeling (collagen cross-linking and maturation over months to years, reaching only ~80% of original tensile strength).

Chronic wounds stall in the inflammatory phase due to excessive matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) that degrade new collagen faster than fibroblasts can deposit it, persistent bacterial biofilm that maintains inflammation, poor perfusion (diabetes, PVD), and excessive exudate that contains inflammatory cytokines and proteases that destroy growth factors.

NPWT addresses multiple barriers to healing simultaneously through several mechanisms:

1. Macrodeformation: The negative pressure physically draws wound edges together (reducing wound volume by 2-4% with each dressing change), decreasing the distance new tissue must cover.

2. Microdeformation: At the cellular level, the foam-tissue interface creates thousands of tiny mechanical forces on individual cells. This microstrain activates mechanotransduction pathways: integrins on cell membranes detect mechanical stretch and activate intracellular signaling cascades (FAK, ERK, MAPK pathways) that stimulate cell proliferation, migration, and extracellular matrix production. Fibroblasts under mechanical strain produce 2-3x more collagen.

3. Fluid removal: Continuous or intermittent suction removes excess wound exudate that contains inhibitory MMPs, inflammatory cytokines, and bacterial toxins. This shifts the wound microenvironment from inflammatory to proliferative.

4. Angiogenesis stimulation: The sub-atmospheric pressure creates a hypoxic gradient at the wound edges (relative to the wound center), and hypoxia is the most potent natural stimulus for VEGF release by macrophages, driving new blood vessel formation into the wound bed. Studies show NPWT increases local blood flow by 2-4x within the first few days.

5. Biofilm disruption: Mechanical removal of exudate disrupts bacterial biofilm and reduces bacterial load, though NPWT is NOT a substitute for surgical debridement of frankly infected wounds.

The system consists of: open-cell foam (black polyurethane: more aggressive granulation stimulation, or white polyvinyl alcohol: gentler, for painful wounds or those near tendons/organs), a non-adherent wound contact layer if needed, an adhesive transparent drape creating an airtight seal, a suction tube connecting to a portable pump, and a canister collecting wound fluid. Pressure is typically set at -75 to -125 mmHg, either continuous or intermittent (intermittent may stimulate more granulation but is less tolerated due to discomfort).
diagnostics: Wound measurement at each dressing change (length x width x depth)
Tissue assessment (granulation, slough, necrotic tissue percentage)
Drainage assessment (color, amount, odor)
Peri-wound skin assessment
Monitor for signs of wound infection
management: Maintain prescribed negative pressure setting (typically -125 mmHg)
Ensure airtight seal of drape over wound and foam
Change dressing every 48-72 hours (every 24 hours if infected)
Keep VAC pump on minimum 22 hours/day
Moisten foam with NS 15-30 minutes before removal to prevent pain
nursingActions: Assess wound at each dressing change and document findings
Count foam pieces placed in wound and verify all removed at dressing change
Monitor for airtight seal and troubleshoot air leaks
Report changes in drainage amount, color, or odor
Ensure VAC pump remains on and alarms are addressed promptly
Educate patient on importance of continuous therapy and reporting machine alarms

hemodialysis-care.title: Hemo-diyaliz: İlkeler, Prosedür
hemodialysis-care.pathophysiology:
Hemodialysis is a life-sustaining renal replacement therapy that performs the critical functions of failed kidneys. To understand hemodialysis, one must first understand what the kidneys do at the cellular level.

Healthy kidneys contain approximately 1 million nephrons each. Each nephron consists of a glomerulus (a capillary tuft that filters blood under hydrostatic pressure: producing ~180 liters of filtrate per day) and a tubular system (proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, collecting duct) that selectively reabsorbs 99% of filtrate and secretes additional waste products. The nephron performs four critical functions: (1) filtration of metabolic waste products (urea from protein metabolism, creatinine from muscle metabolism, uric acid from nucleic acid metabolism), (2) regulation of fluid balance (adjusting water reabsorption via ADH-mediated aquaporin-2 channels in the collecting duct), (3) electrolyte homeostasis (Na+/K+ balance via aldosterone at the distal tubule, calcium/phosphorus balance via PTH and vitamin D), and (4) acid-base balance (bicarbonate reabsorption in the proximal tubule, hydrogen ion secretion, ammonia buffer generation).

When kidney function declines below ~10-15% (GFR <15 mL/min: Stage 5 CKD, also called end-stage renal disease/ESRD), these functions fail catastrophically. Uremic toxins accumulate, causing uremic syndrome: encephalopathy (confusion, asterixis: 'liver flap'), pericarditis (uremic pericarditis: friction rub, can progress to tamponade), peripheral neuropathy, platelet dysfunction (bleeding tendency), nausea/vomiting, pruritus (phosphate crystal deposition in skin), and uremic frost (rare: urea crystals on skin in extreme cases). Fluid overload causes pulmonary edema. Hyperkalemia causes fatal cardiac arrhythmias (peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave → asystole). Metabolic acidosis develops as the kidneys cannot regenerate bicarbonate.

Hemodialysis replaces these functions using three physical principles:

1. Diffusion: Movement of solutes across the semipermeable dialysis membrane from an area of higher concentration to lower concentration. Uremic toxins (urea, creatinine, potassium, phosphorus) are in high concentration in the patient's blood and low/absent in the dialysate, so they diffuse across the membrane into the dialysate. Conversely, bicarbonate is in higher concentration in the dialysate and diffuses INTO the patient's blood, correcting metabolic acidosis. The dialysate composition is carefully formulated: it contains sodium, chloride, calcium, magnesium, glucose, and bicarbonate buffer at physiologic concentrations but NO urea, creatinine, or potassium (or low potassium, typically 2 mEq/L).

2. Ultrafiltration: Removal of excess water by applying hydrostatic pressure across the dialysis membrane (transmembrane pressure). The machine creates a pressure gradient that pushes water from the blood compartment through the membrane into the dialysate compartment. The amount of fluid removed is programmed based on the patient's 'dry weight' (ideal weight without excess fluid). Typical fluid removal: 2-4 liters per session.

3. Convection: Water movement across the membrane drags dissolved solutes with it (solvent drag), removing 'middle molecules' (larger toxins like β2-microglobulin, MW ~11,800 Da) that diffusion alone cannot efficiently clear.

The dialysis membrane (dialyzer, also called the 'artificial kidney') contains thousands of hollow fiber capillaries made of synthetic biocompatible membranes (polysulfone, polyethersulfone). Blood flows inside the fibers, dialysate flows outside (countercurrent flow for maximum diffusion efficiency), and solute/water exchange occurs across the fiber walls. A standard hemodialysis session runs 3-4 hours, 3 times per week.

Vascular access is the 'lifeline' for hemodialysis patients. Three types exist in order of preference: (1) AV fistula (surgical anastomosis of artery to vein: lowest infection and thrombosis rates, longest lifespan, requires 2-3 months to mature), (2) AV graft (synthetic tube connecting artery to vein: higher infection and stenosis rates, can be used in 2-3 weeks), (3) Central venous catheter/CVC (tunneled catheter in internal jugular or femoral vein: highest infection rate, used when urgent dialysis is needed or fistula/graft is not available).
diagnostics: Pre- and post-dialysis weight measurement
Vital signs monitoring (pre, during, post dialysis)
Access site assessment (thrill, bruit, signs of infection)
Monitor for signs of disequilibrium syndrome during dialysis
Assess electrolyte levels pre- and post-dialysis
management: NO BP, IV, or blood draws from fistula/graft arm
Administer medications as ordered (hold antihypertensives pre-dialysis if ordered)
Maintain dietary restrictions (low sodium, potassium, phosphorus)
Monitor fluid intake between sessions
Administer heparin during dialysis as ordered
nursingActions: Assess AV fistula/graft for thrill and bruit every shift
Weigh patient before and after dialysis
Monitor BP during dialysis (hypotension is most common complication)
Report signs of disequilibrium syndrome (headache, nausea, confusion, seizures)
Hold antihypertensives and medications dialyzed out per orders
Educate on fluid restriction between dialysis sessions

fracture-sprain-care.title: Kırıklar, Burkulmalar
fracture-sprain-care.pathophysiology:
Understanding fractures, sprains, and strains requires knowledge of the three tissue types involved and how they respond to traumatic force.

Bone physiology and fracture healing: Bone is a dynamic, living tissue composed of an organic matrix (type I collagen: provides tensile strength and flexibility) and an inorganic mineral component (hydroxyapatite: calcium phosphate crystals that provide compressive strength and rigidity).
