Precocious puberty is defined as the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in females or age 9 in males. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent, gonadotropin-mediated) or peripheral (GnRH-independent, autonomous sex steroid production).

Central precocious puberty (CPP) results from premature activation of the hypothalamic GnRH pulse generator. The most common cause in girls is idiopathic, while in boys CNS pathology is more frequently identified. Hypothalamic hamartomas are the most common identifiable CNS lesion  -  these ectopic masses contain GnRH-secreting neurons that function autonomously. Other causes include CNS tumors (gliomas, astrocytomas), hydrocephalus, post-CNS infection, and neurofibromatosis type 1. At the molecular level, gain-of-function mutations in KISS1 or KISS1R (GPR54) and loss-of-function mutations in MKRN3 (makorin ring finger protein 3, an inhibitor of GnRH secretion) have been identified in familial CPP.

Peripheral precocious puberty (PPP) involves sex steroid production independent of GnRH and gonadotropins. McCune-Albright syndrome results from postzygotic activating mutations in GNAS1, causing constitutive activation of the Gsα subunit and autonomous cAMP production in affected tissues  -  leading to autonomous ovarian follicle activation, café-au-lait macules, and polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. Other causes include congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency causing excess adrenal androgens), sex-steroid-secreting tumors (granulosa cell, Leydig cell, adrenal), familial male-limited precocious puberty (testotoxicosis from activating LH receptor mutations), and exogenous sex steroid exposure.
diagnostics: Order basal LH, FSH, estradiol/testosterone  -  pubertal LH >0.3 IU/L (immunochemiluminometric assay) suggests CPP
Order GnRH stimulation test (leuprolide 20 mcg/kg SC)  -  peak LH >5 IU/L with LH/FSH ratio >1 confirms CPP
Order bone age radiograph  -  advanced bone age (>2 SD above chronological age) indicates significant sex steroid exposure
Order brain MRI with contrast for all boys with CPP and girls with CPP onset <6 years (higher rate of CNS pathology)
Order pelvic ultrasound (females)  -  pubertal uterine length >3.5 cm, bilateral follicle development
Order 17-hydroxyprogesterone to rule out congenital adrenal hyperplasia
Order DHEA-S and androstenedione for adrenal androgen excess
Order β-hCG if gonadotropin-independent puberty in males (hCG-secreting germ cell tumor)
Order adrenal/gonadal imaging if peripheral source suspected
Consider genetic testing (MKRN3, KISS1R, GNAS1) for familial or syndromic presentations
management: Prescribe GnRH agonist depot therapy for CPP: Leuprolide depot 11.25 mg IM every 3 months or 30 mg IM every 6 months (or 45 mg every 6 months)
Alternative: Histrelin subcutaneous implant (Supprelin LA)  -  50 mg, provides continuous GnRH agonist delivery for 12 months
Monitor treatment efficacy with suppressed LH (<1 IU/L after GnRH stimulation), regression of pubertal signs, and deceleration of bone age advancement
Prescribe aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole) for McCune-Albright syndrome or familial male-limited precocious puberty (off-label)
Prescribe ketoconazole or anti-androgens for peripheral causes when surgery not feasible
Treat underlying etiology (neurosurgical resection for CNS tumors, glucocorticoid replacement for CAH)
Predict adult height using Bayley-Pinneau tables from bone age
Refer to pediatric endocrinology for all cases
Refer for psychological support  -  address psychosocial impact of early development
Discontinue GnRH agonist therapy at age-appropriate puberty onset (typically age 11-12 in girls) and monitor for spontaneous pubertal progression
nursingActions: Assess Tanner staging at each visit to monitor suppression or progression
Monitor height velocity  -  should decelerate to prepubertal rate on GnRH agonist therapy
Obtain bone age radiographs every 6-12 months to assess skeletal maturation rate
Monitor for GnRH agonist side effects (initial pubertal flare in first 2-4 weeks, injection site reactions, headache)
Educate family that initial hormonal flare may cause transient worsening of pubertal signs before suppression occurs
Monitor for sterile abscess at depot injection sites
Assess histrelin implant site for complications (migration, breakage, infection)
Coordinate with pediatric endocrinology for dose adjustments
Screen for psychological distress and behavioral changes
Educate families on expected treatment duration and timeline for discontinuation

dysmenorrhea-np.title: Dismenore: COX-2 Yolları
dysmenorrhea-np.pathophysiology:
Dysmenorrhea is classified as primary (no underlying pelvic pathology) or secondary (identifiable organic cause such as endometriosis, adenomyosis, or fibroids).

Primary dysmenorrhea results from excessive prostaglandin production by the secretory endometrium at the time of menstruation. Following progesterone withdrawal at the end of the luteal phase, membrane phospholipids are cleaved by phospholipase A2 (PLA2) to release arachidonic acid. Arachidonic acid is then converted by cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) to prostaglandin H2 (PGH2), which is subsequently converted to prostaglandin F2α (PGF2α) and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) by tissue-specific synthases.

PGF2α is the primary mediator of dysmenorrhea. It causes potent myometrial smooth muscle hypercontractility, increasing intrauterine pressure above 150-180 mmHg (resting: 10 mmHg) and exceeding arterial perfusion pressure. This produces myometrial ischemia with anaerobic metabolism and lactic acid accumulation, activating pain-sensitive nerve fibers. PGF2α also sensitizes type C pain afferents to bradykinin and histamine. PGE2 contributes through vasodilation, edema, and further nociceptor sensitization. Vasopressin (AVP) independently increases myometrial contractility via V1a receptors and exacerbates uterine ischemia through vasoconstriction of uterine arterioles.

Secondary dysmenorrhea involves structural or inflammatory pathology. Endometriosis causes ectopic endometrial implants that produce prostaglandins, cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), and nerve growth factor, creating peripheral and central sensitization. Adenomyosis involves ectopic endometrial glands within the myometrium, disrupting normal contractile patterns and causing diffuse uterine enlargement with heavy, painful menses.
diagnostics: Order pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal preferred) to evaluate for secondary causes (endometriomas, adenomyosis, fibroids)
Order MRI pelvis if adenomyosis suspected (junctional zone thickness >12 mm is diagnostic)
Order CBC to assess for anemia from heavy menstrual bleeding
Order ESR/CRP if infection or inflammatory process suspected
Order CA-125 if endometriosis or adenomyosis suspected (may be mildly elevated)
Refer for diagnostic laparoscopy if empirical treatment fails and endometriosis is suspected
Order STI screening if secondary dysmenorrhea with risk factors for PID
Assess pain diary for cyclical pattern, onset, duration, and severity using validated scales (VAS, NRS)
management: Prescribe NSAIDs as first-line: Naproxen 500 mg loading dose then 250 mg every 6-8 hours, or Ibuprofen 400-800 mg every 6-8 hours  -  initiate 1-2 days before expected menses onset for maximum efficacy
Prescribe combined hormonal contraceptives (continuous or extended cycle) as second-line or co-therapy  -  suppress ovulation and reduce endometrial prostaglandin production

Prescribe levonorgestrel IUD (52 mg Mirena) for secondary dysmenorrhea and patients desiring long-acting contraception  -  causes endometrial decidualization and atrophy, reducing prostaglandin production by 50-75%

Prescribe GnRH agonist (leuprolide depot 3.75 mg IM monthly) for severe endometriosis-related dysmenorrhea refractory to first-line therapy  -  add-back therapy with norethindrone 5 mg/day to mitigate bone loss
Consider vasopressin V1a receptor antagonists (investigational) for dysmenorrhea with prominent vasopressin-mediated component
Prescribe progestin-only therapy (norethindrone, medroxyprogesterone) for patients with contraindications to estrogen
Refer for surgical evaluation (laparoscopic excision) if endometriosis confirmed and medical management fails
Refer for adenomyomectomy or hysterectomy if adenomyosis refractory to medical therapy in completed childbearing
nursingActions: Educate on NSAID timing  -  maximum efficacy when started 1-2 days before menses onset (pre-emptive prostaglandin inhibition)
Monitor for NSAID gastrointestinal side effects (prescribe PPI cover if prolonged use)
Assess pain response using standardized scales at follow-up visits
Educate on levonorgestrel IUD  -  irregular bleeding common in first 3-6 months, amenorrhea develops in 20-50%
Monitor for GnRH agonist menopausal side effects (hot flashes, vaginal dryness, mood changes, bone density loss)
Counsel on non-pharmacological adjuncts (heat therapy, TENS, exercise, omega-3 fatty acids)
Assess for red flags suggesting secondary causes (progressive worsening, new onset after age 25, abnormal bleeding patterns)
Coordinate referral to gynecology if empirical therapy fails after 3-6 months

aub-np.title: Anormal Uterin Kanama: PALM-COEIN
aub-np.pathophysiology:
Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) is classified using the FIGO PALM-COEIN system, which divides causes into structural (PALM: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy/hyperplasia) and non-structural (COEIN: Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified).

Normal endometrial hemostasis depends on a precise balance between vasoconstriction, platelet aggregation, coagulation, and fibrinolysis. During menstruation, spiral arteriolar vasoconstriction limits blood loss, while tissue factor (TF) activates the coagulation cascade locally. Endometrial fibrinolytic activity (tissue plasminogen activator, tPA) is regulated to allow orderly tissue shedding. The endometrium regenerates from the basalis layer under estrogen stimulation.

In AUB-E (endometrial causes), molecular defects in local hemostatic mechanisms include increased endometrial fibrinolysis (elevated tPA and reduced PAI-1), deficient endothelin-1 (ET-1) mediated vasoconstriction, and altered prostaglandin ratios (increased PGE2 and prostacyclin relative to PGF2α and thromboxane A2). This shifts the hemostatic balance toward excessive bleeding.

In AUB-O (ovulatory dysfunction), anovulation results in unopposed estrogen exposure. Without progesterone-mediated secretory transformation, the endometrium undergoes prolonged proliferation, becoming fragile and vascular. Disordered shedding produces irregular, heavy, and prolonged bleeding. Chronic unopposed estrogen also increases the risk of endometrial hyperplasia (with or without atypia) and progression to endometrial carcinoma.

AUB-C (coagulopathy) accounts for approximately 13% of heavy menstrual bleeding. Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder causing AUB, followed by platelet function defects and coagulation factor deficiencies.
diagnostics: Order CBC with iron studies (ferritin, TIBC) to assess severity of blood loss anemia
Order β-hCG to exclude pregnancy-related bleeding
Order TSH to exclude thyroid dysfunction
Order coagulation studies (PT, aPTT, VWF antigen, VWF activity, factor VIII)  -  screen for coagulopathy in all adolescents and adults with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche
Order transvaginal ultrasound as first-line imaging for structural evaluation
Order saline infusion sonohysterography (SIS) for superior delineation of intracavitary lesions (polyps, submucosal fibroids)
Order endometrial biopsy for all women >45 years with AUB, or <45 years with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia (obesity, chronic anovulation, unopposed estrogen, tamoxifen)
Order MRI pelvis for adenomyosis confirmation or fibroid mapping prior to surgery
Order prolactin, DHEA-S, testosterone if PCOS or adrenal pathology suspected
Consider hysteroscopy for both diagnostic and therapeutic evaluation of intracavitary pathology
management: Prescribe tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO TID for up to 5 days during menses for AUB-E (reduces blood loss by 40-50%)
Prescribe combined oral contraceptive (monophasic, 30-35 mcg ethinyl estradiol) for ovulatory dysfunction  -  regulates cycles and reduces endometrial proliferation
Prescribe levonorgestrel IUD (52 mg) as first-line long-term therapy  -  superior to oral progestins for reducing menstrual blood loss (71-95% reduction)
Prescribe high-dose oral progestin (medroxyprogesterone 20 mg TID for 7 days, or norethindrone 5 mg TID for 7 days) for acute heavy bleeding
Prescribe conjugated equine estrogen 25 mg IV every 4-6 hours for acute hemorrhagic AUB unresponsive to progestins (follow with progestin)
Prescribe cyclic progestin therapy (medroxyprogesterone 10 mg for 12-14 days/month) for anovulatory bleeding when contraception not desired
Prescribe GnRH agonist (leuprolide) or GnRH antagonist (elagolix) for fibroid-related AUB as bridge to surgery
Refer for hysteroscopic polypectomy or myomectomy for structural lesions
Refer for endometrial ablation if completed childbearing and medical management fails
Refer for hysterectomy as definitive treatment for refractory AUB with completed childbearing
Prescribe desmopressin nasal spray for Von Willebrand disease-related AUB
nursingActions: Assess menstrual bleeding patterns using pictorial blood loss assessment chart (PBAC)  -  score >100 indicates heavy menstrual bleeding
Monitor hemoglobin, hematocrit, and iron stores  -  initiate IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin <30 or Hb <10 with oral intolerance
Educate on tranexamic acid  -  take only during heavy bleeding days, do not combine with hormonal contraceptives containing estrogen (increased VTE risk per some guidelines)
Monitor for side effects of high-dose progestin therapy (bloating, mood changes, breakthrough bleeding)
Assess for signs of hemodynamic instability in acute hemorrhagic AUB (tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension)
Educate on endometrial biopsy indications and procedure expectations
Coordinate gynecology referral for surgical evaluation when medical management fails
Screen all adolescents presenting with heavy menstrual bleeding for underlying coagulopathy

pcos-management-np.title: PCOS: İnsülin-Androjen Kaskadı
pcos-management-np.pathophysiology:
Polycystic ovary syndrome is a complex endocrine disorder diagnosed using the Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound with exclusion of other etiologies).

The molecular pathogenesis centers on a self-amplifying insulin-androgen feedback loop. Insulin resistance (present in 50-70% of PCOS patients regardless of BMI) leads to compensatory hyperinsulinemia. Elevated insulin acts synergistically with LH on ovarian theca cells to upregulate steroidogenic enzymes  -  specifically CYP17A1 (17α-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase)  -  increasing androstenedione and testosterone production. Insulin simultaneously suppresses hepatic production of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG), increasing free (bioavailable) testosterone levels. Insulin also stimulates adrenal androgen production and enhances IGF-1 bioactivity by suppressing IGF-binding proteins (IGFBPs).

At the ovarian level, excess androgens disrupt folliculogenesis. Granulosa cells in developing follicles are unable to aromatize sufficient androgens to estrogens (impaired CYP19A1/aromatase activity), leading to follicular arrest at 2-9 mm. Multiple arrested antral follicles give the classic polycystic ovarian morphology (≥12 follicles per ovary or ovarian volume >10 mL). Chronic anovulation leads to absence of corpus luteum formation, resulting in progesterone deficiency and unopposed estrogen exposure on the endometrium  -  increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia (2.7-fold) and endometrial cancer (2.7-4-fold).

Metabolic consequences include impaired glucose tolerance (30-40% by age 40), type 2 diabetes (10% by age 40), dyslipidemia (elevated triglycerides, low HDL, increased small dense LDL), non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and accelerated atherosclerosis. Cardiovascular risk is estimated at 2-fold increased compared to age-matched controls.
diagnostics: Order total and free testosterone (elevated free testosterone is the most sensitive biochemical marker)
Order DHEA-S (mildly elevated in 20-30%  -  adrenal component)
Order 17-hydroxyprogesterone (early morning, follicular phase) to exclude non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (>200 ng/dL warrants ACTH stimulation test)
Order SHBG (characteristically low due to hyperinsulinemia)
Order fasting glucose with fasting insulin (calculate HOMA-IR >2.5 suggests insulin resistance)
Order 2-hour 75g oral glucose tolerance test (preferred over fasting glucose  -  detects impaired glucose tolerance earlier)
Order lipid panel (screen for metabolic syndrome)
Order transvaginal ultrasound (≥12 follicles of 2-9 mm per ovary or ovarian volume >10 mL using updated Rotterdam criteria)
Order TSH and prolactin to exclude thyroid dysfunction and hyperprolactinemia
Order AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone)  -  elevated in PCOS and may supplement ultrasound for diagnosis
Order liver function tests if considering hepatically-metabolized medications
Screen for obstructive sleep apnea (increased prevalence in PCOS, especially with obesity)
Screen for depression and anxiety (PHQ-9, GAD-7)  -  3-fold increased prevalence
management: Prescribe combined oral contraceptive (low androgenic progestin preferred  -  norgestimate, desogestrel, drospirenone) as first-line for menstrual regulation and anti-androgen therapy
Prescribe metformin 500 mg titrated to 1500-2000 mg/day for insulin resistance (extended-release formulation reduces GI side effects)
Prescribe spironolactone 50-200 mg/day for hirsutism and acne (wait 6 months for clinical effect on hirsutism)
Prescribe letrozole 2.5-7.5 mg/day (cycle days 3-7) as first-line for ovulation induction (superior to clomiphene per NICHD trial)
Prescribe clomiphene citrate 50-150 mg/day (cycle days 3-7) as alternative for ovulation induction
Prescribe topical eflornithine cream for facial hirsutism (adjunct to systemic therapy)
Prescribe GLP-1 receptor agonists (liraglutide, semaglutide) for obese PCOS patients for weight loss and insulin sensitization (emerging evidence)
Prescribe inositol (myo-inositol 4g/day) as adjunct insulin sensitizer (lower evidence grade but favorable safety profile)
Refer for bariatric surgery evaluation if BMI >40 or BMI >35 with metabolic comorbidities
Order endometrial ultrasound surveillance every 6-12 months if chronic anovulation without progestin protection  -  endometrial biopsy if thickness >7 mm
Counsel on fertility options: letrozole > clomiphene > gonadotropins > IVF (stepwise approach)
Coordinate cardiovascular risk stratification (10-year ASCVD risk calculator, annual metabolic screening)
nursingActions: Monitor metabolic panel annually (fasting glucose or OGTT, lipid panel, blood pressure)
Monitor HbA1c every 6-12 months in patients with impaired glucose tolerance or on metformin
Assess Ferriman-Gallwey score for hirsutism at baseline and follow-up (score ≥8 = hirsutism in most populations)
Monitor liver function and potassium with spironolactone therapy
Educate on reliable contraception during spironolactone use (teratogenic  -  feminization of male fetus)
Monitor for metformin GI side effects and vitamin B12 deficiency with long-term use
Educate on lifestyle modifications (150 min/week moderate exercise, Mediterranean or DASH diet, 5-10% weight loss target)
Screen for depression and eating disorders at each visit
Educate on ovulation induction monitoring requirements (follicle tracking to prevent ovarian hyperstimulation)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (endocrinology, dermatology, psychology, reproductive endocrinology)

pid-np.title: Pelvik İnflamatuar Hastalık: CDC Rehberleri
pid-np.pathophysiology:
Pelvic inflammatory disease (PID) is an ascending polymicrobial infection of the upper female genital tract, involving the endometrium (endometritis), fallopian tubes (salpingitis), ovaries (oophoritis), and pelvic peritoneum (peritonitis). The primary causative organisms are Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, though the infection is typically polymicrobial, involving anaerobes (Bacteroides, Prevotella, Peptostreptococcus), facultative gram-negative rods (E. coli), genital mycoplasmas (M. genitalium, M. hominis), and Gardnerella vaginalis.

The pathogenesis involves bacterial ascent from the lower genital tract through the cervical os into the endometrial cavity and fallopian tubes. This process is facilitated by cervical mucus disruption during menstruation, endocervical instrumentation, or bacterial mucinase production. N. gonorrhoeae directly damages ciliated tubal epithelium through lipooligosaccharide (LOS)-mediated inflammatory cascades, activating complement, recruiting neutrophils, and triggering TNF-α and IL-1β release. C. trachomatis induces a delayed-type hypersensitivity response through chlamydial heat shock protein 60 (cHSP60), which shares molecular homology with human HSP60  -  this molecular mimicry drives chronic inflammatory damage and autoimmune tubal destruction even after the organism is cleared.

Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome occurs when infection spreads from the fallopian tubes to the liver capsule (Glisson capsule) via peritoneal or lymphatic routes, causing perihepatitis with characteristic violin-string adhesions between the liver and anterior abdominal wall. It presents with right upper quadrant pain that may mimic cholecystitis.

Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA) develops when fallopian tube infection leads to tubal obstruction (pyosalpinx), extension to the ovary, and walled-off collection of purulent material involving the tube, ovary, and adjacent structures. TOAs contain a mixed aerobic-anaerobic bacterial community within a biofilm matrix. Rupture of a TOA is a surgical emergency causing peritonitis and sepsis.
diagnostics: Clinical diagnosis based on minimum criteria (CDC): uterine tenderness, adnexal tenderness, or cervical motion tenderness in a sexually active young woman with no other identifiable cause
Order endocervical NAAT for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis (most sensitive diagnostic test for causative organisms)
Order M. genitalium NAAT (emerging as significant PID pathogen  -  available at reference labs)
Order CBC with differential (leukocytosis >10,000 supports diagnosis)
Order ESR (>15 mm/hr) and CRP (elevated) as supportive criteria
Order transvaginal ultrasound to evaluate for TOA (complex adnexal mass with internal echoes, thick irregular walls)
Order β-hCG to exclude ectopic pregnancy
Order endometrial biopsy (endometritis on histology  -  plasma cells in endometrial stroma  -  is the most specific diagnostic criterion)
Order blood cultures if systemic sepsis suspected
Consider CT pelvis with contrast for complex presentations or suspected peritonitis
Consider diagnostic laparoscopy for equivocal cases (gold standard but rarely needed)
management: Outpatient regimen (CDC 2021): Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM x1 (or 1g IM if ≥150 kg) PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x14 days PLUS Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x14 days
Inpatient regimen A (CDC 2021): Cefoxitin 2g IV q6h OR Cefotetan 2g IV q12h PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV q12h  -  transition to oral after 24-48h clinical improvement, complete 14 days total
Inpatient regimen B: Clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h PLUS Gentamicin loading dose 2 mg/kg IV then 1.5 mg/kg IV q8h  -  transition to oral doxycycline or clindamycin after improvement
Add metronidazole to all regimens for enhanced anaerobic coverage (especially if TOA or BV present)

Criteria for inpatient treatment: TOA, pregnancy, surgical emergency cannot be excluded, failure of outpatient therapy at 48-72 hours, inability to tolerate oral medications, severe illness with high fever or peritonitis
TOA management: IV antibiotics first  -  70% respond to medical therapy alone within 48-72 hours; percutaneous or surgical drainage if no improvement or abscess >8 cm
Ruptured TOA: emergent surgical intervention (laparotomy or laparoscopy) with drainage, irrigation, and possible salpingectomy/oophorectomy
Treat all sexual partners from the preceding 60 days for gonorrhea and chlamydia (expedited partner therapy where legal)
Test of cure: repeat NAAT at 3 months for gonorrhea and chlamydia

Counsel on long-term sequelae: infertility risk increases with each PID episode (8% after one, 20% after two, 40% after three), ectopic pregnancy (7-10 fold increased risk), chronic pelvic pain (18-35%)
nursingActions: Monitor temperature trends  -  defervescence expected within 48-72 hours of IV antibiotics; persistent fever suggests TOA or treatment failure
Assess for rebound tenderness and guarding (peritoneal signs suggest TOA rupture  -  surgical emergency)
Monitor hemodynamic stability (tachycardia, hypotension may indicate sepsis from ruptured TOA)
Administer IV antibiotics on schedule and monitor for adverse effects (gentamicin: nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity  -  monitor trough levels)
Position in semi-Fowler's to promote pelvic abscess drainage by gravity
Educate on completing full 14-day antibiotic course even after symptom improvement
Facilitate expedited partner treatment (provide partner prescriptions or referral)
Provide contraception counseling and barrier method education
Educate on IUD safety  -  current IUD does not need to be removed unless no clinical improvement after 48-72 hours of appropriate antibiotics
Screen for HIV, syphilis, and hepatitis B/C given STI exposure

vaginitis-np.title: Vajinit: Vajinal Mikrobiyom
vaginitis-np.pathophysiology:
The healthy vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species (L. crispatus, L. iners, L. jensenii, L. gasseri), which maintain an acidic pH (3.8-4.5) through lactic acid production from glycogen metabolism. Lactobacilli produce both D- and L-lactic acid isomers, hydrogen peroxide, and bacteriocins that collectively inhibit pathogenic bacterial colonization. L. crispatus is considered the most protective species, producing the highest levels of D-lactic acid (a more potent antimicrobial isomer) and maintaining the most stable community state type (CST-I).

Bacterial vaginosis (BV) represents a dysbiotic shift from Lactobacillus-dominant to a polymicrobial community dominated by Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, Prevotella bivia, Mobiluncus species, and Megasphaera. G. vaginalis initiates BV by forming a biofilm on the vaginal epithelium  -  a structured polymicrobial community encased in an extracellular polysaccharide matrix that confers antibiotic resistance. This biofilm persists after standard metronidazole therapy in up to 50% of cases, explaining the high recurrence rate (50-80% within 12 months). BV biofilm produces sialidases and prolidases that degrade cervical mucus and vaginal epithelial protective factors, and amines (trimethylamine, putrescine, cadaverine) that produce the characteristic fishy odor and elevate vaginal pH >4.5.

Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) is caused by Candida species, with C. albicans responsible for 85-90% of cases. C. albicans exhibits morphological switching between yeast (commensal) and hyphal (invasive) forms  -  hyphae penetrate vaginal epithelium, activate epithelial danger response pathways (NLRP3 inflammasome), and trigger IL-1β release and neutrophil recruitment. Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year) is associated with genetic polymorphisms in mannose-binding lectin and IL-4 receptor, creating a hyperinflammatory response to low fungal burdens. Azole resistance is emerging, particularly in non-albicans species (C. glabrata accounts for 5-10% of VVC and has intrinsic reduced azole susceptibility due to upregulation of CDR efflux pumps and ERG11 mutations).

Trichomoniasis is caused by Trichomonas vaginalis, an anaerobic flagellated protozoan. It adheres to vaginal epithelial cells via adhesin proteins (AP65, AP51, AP33, AP23) and disrupts epithelial integrity through contact-dependent cytolysis and protease secretion. T. vaginalis phagocytoses Lactobacilli, further disrupting the microbiome. Metronidazole resistance (2-5% of isolates) results from mutations in oxygen-scavenging enzymes (ferredoxin, flavin reductase) that reduce intracellular activation of the drug.
diagnostics: Order vaginal pH testing: BV >4.5, Trichomoniasis >4.5, VVC normal (4.0-4.5)

Order wet mount saline preparation: BV (clue cells  -  vaginal epithelial cells stippled with adherent bacteria), Trichomoniasis (motile trichomonads with jerky motility), VVC (pseudohyphae and budding yeast on KOH preparation)
Order whiff test (amine/KOH test): BV positive (fishy odor with KOH  -  release of volatile amines)
Apply Amsel criteria for BV diagnosis (3 of 4): thin homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells on wet mount
Order Nugent score (Gram stain scoring 0-10) for research or equivocal cases: 7-10 = BV, 4-6 = intermediate, 0-3 = normal
Order T. vaginalis NAAT (most sensitive diagnostic test  -  95-100% sensitivity vs 60% for wet mount)
Order Candida culture with species identification and antifungal susceptibility testing for recurrent VVC (R/O non-albicans species)
Order molecular panel testing (Affirm VPIII or BD MAX) for simultaneous detection of BV, VVC, and trichomoniasis
Screen for concurrent STIs (gonorrhea, chlamydia, HIV, syphilis) in patients with trichomoniasis or BV
management: BV first-line: Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days OR Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel daily x5 days OR Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream at bedtime x7 days
BV alternative: Secnidazole 2g PO single dose (FDA-approved, improved compliance)

Recurrent BV (≥3 episodes/12 months): Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel twice weekly for 4-6 months suppressive therapy, then PRN; consider boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppository daily x21 days followed by suppressive twice weekly; consider TOC lactobacillus-based probiotics (L. crispatus CTV-05  -  limited but emerging evidence)
VVC uncomplicated: Fluconazole 150 mg PO single dose OR topical azole (miconazole, clotrimazole) x3-7 days
Recurrent VVC (≥4 episodes/year): Fluconazole 150 mg PO every 72 hours x3 doses (induction) then 150 mg PO weekly x6 months (maintenance suppression)
Non-albicans VVC (C. glabrata): Boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppository daily x14-21 days (first-line); Amphotericin B vaginal suppository if boric acid fails; Flucytosine 5% vaginal cream (compounded)
Trichomoniasis: Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days (preferred over single 2g dose due to higher cure rates) OR Tinidazole 2g PO single dose
Metronidazole-resistant trichomoniasis: Tinidazole 2g PO daily x7 days; if persistent, consult CDC STD Clinical Consultation Network for high-dose regimens
Treat sexual partners for trichomoniasis (expedited partner therapy)  -  retest at 3 months
BV in pregnancy: treat with oral metronidazole 500 mg BID x7 days or oral clindamycin 300 mg BID x7 days (intravaginal clindamycin may increase preterm birth risk if used late in pregnancy)
nursingActions: Educate on completing full antibiotic course even after symptom resolution
Educate on metronidazole-alcohol interaction (disulfiram-like reaction  -  avoid alcohol during treatment and for 24 hours after metronidazole, 72 hours after tinidazole)
Educate on proper vaginal gel/cream application technique (insert at bedtime, remain recumbent)
Counsel that BV is not an STI but is associated with sexual activity  -  consistent condom use reduces recurrence
Educate VVC patients that over-the-counter antifungals are appropriate only for infrequent, mild episodes  -  recurrent symptoms require provider evaluation and species identification
Monitor for treatment side effects (metronidazole: metallic taste, nausea; fluconazole: headache, hepatotoxicity with prolonged use; boric acid: vaginal burning)
Educate on vaginal microbiome health (avoid douching, scented products, unnecessary antibiotics)
Counsel trichomoniasis patients on STI screening for partners and retesting at 3 months
Assess for BV-associated complications in pregnancy (preterm birth, premature rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis)

bacterial-vaginosis-np.title: Bakteriyel Vaginoz: Moleküler Mikrobiyom
bacterial-vaginosis-np.pathophysiology:
Bacterial vaginosis (BV) represents a polymicrobial dysbiosis of the vaginal microbiome characterized by depletion of hydrogen peroxide-producing Lactobacillus species and overgrowth of anaerobic and facultative bacteria. The healthy vaginal microbiome is classified into Community State Types (CSTs): CST-I (L. crispatus dominant  -  most protective), CST-II (L. gasseri), CST-III (L. iners  -  transitional, less protective), CST-V (L. jensenii), and CST-IV (diverse anaerobes  -  BV-associated). Transition from Lactobacillus-dominant CSTs to CST-IV is the molecular hallmark of BV.

Gardnerella vaginalis is the primary architect of BV pathogenesis through its unique biofilm-forming capacity. G. vaginalis produces sialidase, an enzyme that cleaves sialic acid residues from the protective mucin layer (MUC1, MUC4) coating vaginal epithelial cells, exposing adhesion receptors and facilitating bacterial attachment. The biofilm is a structured polymicrobial community embedded in an extracellular polysaccharide matrix that provides physical protection against antibiotics and host immune defenses. Within this biofilm, Atopobium vaginae acts synergistically with Gardnerella  -  A. vaginae has intrinsic metronidazole resistance (lacks nitroreductase enzymes required for prodrug activation) and perpetuates biofilm integrity after antibiotic treatment.

The anaerobic shift in BV alters vaginal biochemistry. Prevotella bivia and Mobiluncus species produce short-chain fatty acids (succinate, acetate) that replace the protective D- and L-lactic acid normally produced by Lactobacillus. This raises vaginal pH from the normal 3.8-4.5 to >4.5, further inhibiting Lactobacillus growth and perpetuating dysbiosis. Bacterial decarboxylases convert amino acids to volatile amines (trimethylamine, putrescine, cadaverine), producing the characteristic fishy odor that is accentuated by alkaline conditions (KOH whiff test). The loss of Lactobacillus-derived hydrogen peroxide and bacteriocins removes a critical antimicrobial barrier, increasing susceptibility to STI acquisition including HIV, HSV-2, and ascending infections.
diagnostics: Apply Amsel criteria (3 of 4 required): thin homogeneous grayish-white discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive whiff test (amine odor with 10% KOH), clue cells on saline wet mount (>20% of epithelial cells)
Order Nugent score (Gram stain): 0-3 = normal Lactobacillus morphotype, 4-6 = intermediate, 7-10 = BV (Gardnerella/Mobiluncus morphotypes predominate)
Order molecular diagnostic panels (BD MAX BV, Aptima BV)  -  detect G. vaginalis, A. vaginae, and Lactobacillus relative abundance with higher sensitivity than clinical criteria
Order NAAT for concurrent STI screening (Chlamydia, Gonorrhea, Trichomonas)  -  BV increases STI acquisition risk
Order vaginal culture with sensitivity only for treatment-resistant cases
Assess for clue cells microscopically  -  vaginal epithelial cells with borders obscured by adherent coccobacilli (granular stippled appearance)
management: First-line: Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days (targets anaerobes via nitro-group reduction to cytotoxic radicals causing DNA strand breakage)
Alternative first-line: Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel 5g intravaginally daily x5 days (comparable efficacy, fewer systemic side effects)
Alternative: Clindamycin 2% vaginal cream 5g intravaginally at bedtime x7 days (inhibits 50S ribosomal subunit  -  broader anaerobic coverage but more Lactobacillus disruption)
Single-dose option: Secnidazole 2g PO single dose (FDA-approved, improved adherence  -  longer half-life than metronidazole allows single-dose efficacy)
Recurrent BV (≥3 episodes/12 months): Metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel twice weekly for 4-6 months suppressive therapy
Recurrent BV adjunct: Boric acid 600 mg vaginal suppository daily x21 days followed by twice weekly maintenance
Emerging therapy: Vaginal microbiome transplant (VMT) from L. crispatus-dominant donors  -  Phase 2 trials showing promising durable remission rates
Emerging therapy: LACTIN-V (live L. crispatus CTV-05 vaginal probiotic) applied after standard antibiotic therapy  -  reduces recurrence by 30% vs placebo in RCTs
Pregnancy management: Oral metronidazole 500 mg BID x7 days or oral clindamycin 300 mg BID x7 days  -  intravaginal clindamycin may increase preterm birth risk if used after 20 weeks
Partner treatment: Concurrent male partner treatment with combined antibiotic therapy (oral penile and penile antiseptic wash) reduces recurrence  -  StepUpRCT trial data
nursingActions: Educate on metronidazole-alcohol interaction (disulfiram-like reaction via aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibition  -  avoid alcohol during treatment and 24-48 hours after)
Educate on completing full antibiotic course despite symptom resolution
Counsel against vaginal douching (eliminates protective Lactobacillus and raises pH)
Educate on consistent condom use (reduces BV-associated bacterial exchange and semen-related pH disruption)
Monitor for treatment side effects (metallic taste, nausea, GI upset with metronidazole; vaginal candidiasis with clindamycin)
Assess for BV complications in pregnancy (preterm birth, premature rupture of membranes, chorioamnionitis, postpartum endometritis)
Educate on vaginal microbiome health (cotton underwear, avoidance of scented products, probiotics)
Schedule follow-up at 1 month for test of cure in recurrent BV and pregnant patients

cervicitis-np.title: Servisit: Patogen Moleküler Biyolojisi
cervicitis-np.pathophysiology:
Cervicitis is inflammation of the uterine cervix, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted pathogens. The cervical transformation zone  -  where columnar epithelium meets squamous epithelium  -  is particularly vulnerable to infection due to its single-layer columnar cells and high density of immune cells.

Chlamydia trachomatis has a unique biphasic developmental cycle. The elementary body (EB) is the infectious extracellular form  -  metabolically inert, resistant to environmental stress, with a rigid disulfide-cross-linked outer membrane. EBs attach to host columnar epithelial cells via heparan sulfate proteoglycans and MOMP (Major Outer Membrane Protein) interaction with host cell receptors. Following receptor-mediated endocytosis, EBs reside within membrane-bound inclusions that evade lysosomal fusion through type III secretion system (T3SS) effector proteins (Inc proteins  -  IncA, IncD, IncG) that modify the inclusion membrane. Within 6-8 hours, EBs differentiate into reticulate bodies (RBs)  -  the metabolically active, non-infectious intracellular form that replicates by binary fission. After 48-72 hours, RBs condense back into EBs, and the inclusion ruptures (or is extruded), releasing 200-1000 infectious EBs to infect adjacent cells. Chlamydia induces a chronic inflammatory response dominated by Th1 cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-12) and tissue remodeling factors that can cause tubal scarring and infertility.

Neisseria gonorrhoeae evades host immunity through several molecular mechanisms. Antigenic variation of pili (via RecA-mediated gene conversion at the pilE/pilS loci) and opacity proteins (Opa) allows continuous immune evasion. Porin protein PorB inserts into host cell membranes, inhibiting phagosome-lysosome fusion and inducing host cell apoptosis of neutrophils. Gonococcal IgA1 protease cleaves mucosal IgA, neutralizing a key first-line mucosal defense. Lipooligosaccharide (LOS) sialylation with host-derived sialic acid provides molecular mimicry, reducing complement activation. Gonococcal infection triggers a robust neutrophilic response (mucopurulent discharge), but the organism survives within neutrophils through catalase and superoxide dismutase production.
diagnostics: Order NAAT (Nucleic Acid Amplification Testing) for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae  -  sensitivity 95-99%, specificity >99%; endocervical swab or self-collected vaginal swab acceptable

Perform speculum examination: assess for mucopurulent endocervical discharge (yellow/green exudate visible at the os or on a white swab  -  the swab test), cervical friability (easily induced bleeding on contact with swab or spatula)
Order NAAT for Mycoplasma genitalium (emerging pathogen  -  associated with persistent/recurrent cervicitis unresponsive to standard treatment)
Order NAAT for Trichomonas vaginalis
Order wet mount and vaginal pH for concurrent BV/VVC assessment
Order Gram stain of endocervical discharge: >30 PMNs per high-power field supports cervicitis diagnosis; intracellular Gram-negative diplococci suggest Gonorrhea
Order gonorrhea culture with antibiotic susceptibility testing if treatment failure suspected (antimicrobial resistance surveillance)
Order HIV, syphilis (RPR/VDRL), Hepatitis B/C screening for comprehensive STI evaluation
Perform bimanual examination to assess for cervical motion tenderness (chandelier sign  -  suggests ascending infection/PID)
management: Empiric treatment per CDC 2021 STI guidelines when mucopurulent cervicitis identified (do not wait for NAAT results): Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (1g if ≥150 kg) PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x7 days
If Chlamydia-only confirmed: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x7 days (preferred over azithromycin 1g single dose per updated CDC guidelines  -  superior microbiologic cure)
If Gonorrhea confirmed: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (test of cure NAAT at 14 days if pharyngeal gonorrhea)

If Mycoplasma genitalium confirmed: Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x7 days THEN Moxifloxacin 400 mg PO daily x7 days (if macrolide resistance detected) OR Azithromycin 1g day 1 then 500 mg days 2-4 (if macrolide-sensitive by resistance-guided testing)
Expedited partner therapy (EPT): prescribe treatment for sexual partners without requiring clinical examination (legal in most jurisdictions)
Test of cure: retest NAAT at 4 weeks post-treatment for Gonorrhea if pharyngeal, at 3-4 weeks for M. genitalium; retest all patients at 3 months for reinfection screening
Treat concurrent BV/Trichomonas if identified

If persistent cervicitis after appropriate antimicrobial therapy: evaluate for non-infectious causes (reactive cervicitis from chemical irritants, cervical ectropion), refer for colposcopy to exclude cervical neoplasia
nursingActions: Counsel on partner notification and treatment  -  all sexual partners within 60 days should be tested and treated
Educate on completing full doxycycline course (avoid dairy, antacids, iron within 2 hours  -  chelation reduces absorption)
Educate on abstinence from sexual intercourse until patient and partner(s) complete treatment and symptoms resolve (minimum 7 days)
Counsel on consistent condom use for future STI prevention
Educate on ascending infection risk: untreated cervicitis can progress to PID, tubal scarring, ectopic pregnancy, and infertility
Monitor for PID symptoms: worsening pelvic pain, fever, cervical motion tenderness  -  escalate to inpatient IV antibiotics if severe
Facilitate expedited partner therapy prescription when appropriate
Schedule 3-month reinfection screening (high reinfection rates  -  10-20% within 3-6 months)

vulvodynia-np.title: Vulvodini: Periferik Sensitizasyon
vulvodynia-np.pathophysiology:
Vulvodynia is defined as chronic vulvar pain lasting ≥3 months without an identifiable cause. It is classified by the 2015 ISSVD/ISSWSH/IPPS consensus as either localized (vestibulodynia  -  most common) or generalized, and as provoked (triggered by touch/pressure), unprovoked (spontaneous), or mixed.

The molecular pathophysiology involves peripheral and central nervous system sensitization. In the vestibular mucosa of affected patients, there is a significant increase in free nerve fiber density (up to 10-fold increase in intraepithelial nerve fiber density compared to controls), with upregulation of transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) channels on nociceptive C-fibers. TRPV1 is a ligand-gated non-selective cation channel that responds to capsaicin, protons (pH <6), heat (>43°C), and endogenous lipid mediators (anandamide, 12-HPETE). Upregulated TRPV1 lowers the activation threshold, causing allodynia (pain from normally non-painful stimuli) and hyperalgesia (exaggerated pain response).

Mast cell proliferation in the vestibular stroma is another key finding. Activated mast cells release nerve growth factor (NGF), histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins (PGE2), and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) that promote neuronal sprouting (increased nerve fiber density), nociceptor sensitization, and neurogenic inflammation. NGF binds TrkA receptors on sensory neurons, upregulating TRPV1 and substance P expression through the p38 MAPK signaling pathway. This creates a positive feedback loop: mast cell mediators sensitize nerves → sensitized nerves release substance P and CGRP → these neuropeptides degranulate mast cells → perpetuating chronic neuroinflammation.

Pelvic floor myalgia is present in up to 80% of vulvodynia patients. Chronic guarding and hypertonicity of the levator ani, bulbospongiosus, and ischiocavernosus muscles create myofascial trigger points that contribute to pain through muscle ischemia, lactic acid accumulation, and further peripheral nerve sensitization. Central sensitization develops over time as persistent nociceptive input from the periphery causes neuroplastic changes in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord  -  increased excitability of wide dynamic range (WDR) neurons, loss of descending inhibitory modulation, and expansion of receptive fields. This manifests clinically as pain spreading beyond the original site and hypersensitivity to non-noxious stimuli.
diagnostics: Perform cotton swab test (Q-tip test): systematically apply gentle pressure to vestibular and vulvar sites using a cotton swab  -  positive test reproduces pain at specific sites, especially at the posterior vestibule (5 and 7 o'clock positions)
Map pain distribution to classify localized vs generalized vulvodynia
Assess provoked vs unprovoked character through detailed pain history
Perform pelvic floor muscle assessment: evaluate levator ani tone, trigger points, and voluntary relaxation capacity (digital palpation or surface EMG)

Order vulvar biopsy only if clinical appearance suggests dermatosis, neoplasia, or infection  -  biopsy is NOT routinely recommended for vulvodynia (histology often shows only chronic inflammation and increased nerve fibers)
Perform sensory testing (quantitative sensory testing  -  QST) to assess thermal and mechanical pain thresholds
Exclude other causes: fungal culture (recurrent VVC), bacterial culture, STI screening, vulvar dermatology evaluation (lichen sclerosus, lichen planus)
Assess psychosocial impact: PHQ-9 (depression), GAD-7 (anxiety), Female Sexual Function Index (FSFI)
management: Prescribe topical lidocaine 5% ointment for provoked vestibulodynia  -  apply 15-20 minutes before intercourse or as needed for pain relief (blocks voltage-gated sodium channels on nociceptive nerve fibers)
Prescribe topical estrogen/testosterone compound cream (0.01% estradiol + 0.1% testosterone) for vestibular atrophy component  -  restores mucosal thickness and receptor expression

Prescribe tricyclic antidepressant for central pain modulation: Amitriptyline 10-25 mg PO at bedtime, titrate to 50-100 mg (inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake, blocks sodium channels, and antagonizes NMDA receptors  -  multimodal analgesic mechanism)

Prescribe gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime, titrate to 900-3600 mg/day in divided doses (binds α2δ subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing excitatory neurotransmitter release  -  modulates central sensitization)
Alternative: Pregabalin 75 mg PO BID, titrate to 150-300 mg BID
Prescribe SNRIs as alternative to TCAs: Duloxetine 30-60 mg daily or Venlafaxine 37.5-150 mg daily

Refer for pelvic floor physical therapy with a specialist trained in vulvar pain  -  manual therapy, myofascial release, biofeedback, dilator therapy (evidence grade A  -  first-line alongside pharmacotherapy)
Refer for cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) targeting pain catastrophizing, fear-avoidance behavior, and sexual dysfunction

Vestibulectomy (surgical excision of the posterior vestibule with vaginal advancement flap) for refractory provoked vestibulodynia  -  success rates 60-90% in carefully selected patients who have failed conservative therapy ≥6 months
Consider botulinum toxin A injection into levator ani/bulbospongiosus muscles for refractory pelvic floor hypertonicity (off-label  -  reduces muscle spasm and may modulate nociceptive signaling)
nursingActions: Validate the patient's pain experience  -  vulvodynia is often dismissed or misdiagnosed for years (average diagnostic delay 4-5 years)
Educate on vulvar care: cotton underwear, mild unscented soap, avoid irritants (fabric softeners, scented pads, tight clothing), pat-dry technique
Teach sitz bath technique (warm water, 15-20 minutes, 2-3 times daily for symptom relief)
Educate on topical lidocaine application technique and timing (apply 15-20 minutes before provoked activities)
Monitor for TCA side effects (sedation, dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, orthostatic hypotension, QT prolongation)
Monitor for gabapentin/pregabalin side effects (sedation, dizziness, weight gain, peripheral edema)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (gynecology, pelvic floor PT, psychology/psychiatry, pain management)
Assess sexual function and relationship impact  -  refer for sex therapy if needed
Screen for comorbid chronic pain syndromes (fibromyalgia, IBS, IC/PBS  -  central sensitization phenotype)
Educate that treatment is multimodal and gradual  -  set realistic expectations for 3-6 month improvement timeline

bartholinitis-np.title: Bartolinit: Bez Anatomisi, Abse
bartholinitis-np.pathophysiology:
The Bartholin glands (greater vestibular glands) are bilateral, pea-sized, tubuloalveolar glands located at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions of the vaginal vestibule, deep to the posterior labia majora. Each gland drains through a 2-2.5 cm duct that opens at the posterolateral vaginal introitus between the hymeneal ring and the labia minora. The glands produce mucoid secretions composed of mucin glycoproteins (MUC5B, MUC6) that provide vaginal lubrication during sexual arousal. Secretion is mediated by parasympathetic cholinergic stimulation via the pudendal nerve, triggering intracellular calcium signaling and exocytosis of mucin granules from acinar cells.

Bartholin cyst formation occurs when the narrow duct orifice becomes obstructed, most commonly by inspissated mucus, trauma, or inflammation. Continued mucin secretion from acinar cells against a blocked duct creates progressive cystic dilation filled with sterile mucoid fluid. Cysts are typically painless and range from 1-8 cm. The cyst lining transitions from the normal transitional epithelium of the duct to a flattened cuboidal or squamous epithelium under pressure.

Bartholin abscess develops when a cyst becomes secondarily infected or when primary glandular infection occurs. The microbiology is polymicrobial in most cases: anaerobic organisms (Bacteroides fragilis, Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella) predominate, often mixed with aerobic bacteria (Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus including MRSA, Streptococcus species). Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis are causative in a minority of cases (less than 10-15%) but should be tested for in sexually active women. The confined glandular space creates an ideal environment for abscess formation  -  bacterial proliferation triggers neutrophilic infiltration, tissue necrosis, pus formation, and an expanding walled-off collection. The surrounding tissue becomes edematous, erythematous, and exquisitely tender, often causing labial asymmetry and severe pain with sitting and ambulation.

In women over 40, Bartholin gland carcinoma must be excluded. These rare neoplasms (adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, adenoid cystic carcinoma) can mimic benign cysts or abscesses. Biopsy is recommended for all Bartholin gland masses in postmenopausal women or those >40 years, and for any mass with irregular borders, solid components, or recurrence after adequate treatment.
diagnostics: Perform clinical examination: unilateral labial swelling at 4 or 8 o'clock position; cyst is fluctuant, non-tender, and cystic; abscess is tense, erythematous, fluctuant, and exquisitely tender with surrounding cellulitis
Differentiate cyst vs abscess: cyst  -  painless, cystic, no erythema or warmth; abscess  -  painful, tense, warm, erythematous, may have pointing or spontaneous drainage
Order wound culture and sensitivity from abscess drainage fluid (identify causative organisms including MRSA)
Order NAAT for Gonorrhea and Chlamydia from endocervical swab (not from abscess fluid  -  low sensitivity from abscess)
Order CBC if systemic infection suspected (leukocytosis, left shift)
Order blood cultures if sepsis criteria met (rare complication)
Obtain biopsy of cyst/abscess wall in ALL patients >40 years or postmenopausal to exclude Bartholin gland carcinoma (adenocarcinoma, squamous cell, adenoid cystic)
Order imaging (ultrasound or MRI) only if uncertain diagnosis or suspicion of deep-space extension, ischiorectal abscess, or malignancy
management: Asymptomatic Bartholin cyst <3 cm: observation with warm sitz baths (warm water, 15-20 min, 3-4 times daily)  -  many resolve spontaneously

Symptomatic cyst or abscess: Incision and drainage (I&D) with Word catheter placement  -  1 cm incision on mucosal surface (medial aspect), drain purulent material, insert Word catheter (inflatable bulb-tip catheter), inflate bulb with 2-4 mL saline, leave in place 4-6 weeks to create epithelialized fistula tract for permanent drainage

Alternative: Marsupialization (create permanent stoma)  -  excise elliptical window in cyst wall, evert and suture cyst lining to vestibular mucosa with absorbable sutures; lower recurrence rate than I&D alone (5-10% vs 10-15%)

Prescribe antibiotics if surrounding cellulitis, systemic symptoms (fever, chills), immunocompromised, or MRSA risk: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg PO BID x7-10 days (MRSA coverage) OR Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID x7-10 days (polymicrobial coverage)
Add metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days if anaerobic component suspected
Treat concurrent Gonorrhea/Chlamydia per CDC guidelines if NAAT positive
Gland excision: reserved for recurrent cysts/abscesses after failed Word catheter and marsupialization (higher complication rate  -  hemorrhage, scarring, pudendal nerve injury, hematoma formation)
Refer for biopsy and gynecologic oncology evaluation if age >40, postmenopausal, solid mass component, irregular borders, or rapid growth
nursingActions: Educate on warm sitz baths for comfort and to promote drainage (warm water, 15-20 minutes, 3-4 times daily)
Educate on Word catheter care: keep catheter in place for 4-6 weeks, avoid removing or tugging, expect some drainage around catheter (normal), gently clean area with warm water
Monitor for catheter complications (premature dislodgement  -  most common reason for recurrence, infection, discomfort)
Educate on post-marsupialization wound care (sitz baths, keep area clean, pat dry, wear loose cotton underwear)
Monitor for signs of worsening infection (expanding erythema, increasing pain, fever, purulent drainage)  -  report immediately
Educate on antibiotic completion and side effects
Counsel that recurrence is common (10-15% with I&D)  -  prompt evaluation of any new labial swelling
Educate women >40 on importance of biopsy to exclude malignancy
Assess pain management needs (NSAIDs, acetaminophen, ice packs)

uterine-prolapse-np.title: Uterin Prolaps: Bağ Dokusu Biyolojisi
uterine-prolapse-np.pathophysiology:
Pelvic organ prolapse (POP) results from failure of the pelvic floor support system  -  an integrated complex of muscles (levator ani group: pubococcygeus, puborectalis, iliococcygeus), endopelvic fascia (pubocervical fascia anteriorly, rectovaginal fascia posteriorly), and ligaments (cardinal and uterosacral ligaments). Uterine prolapse specifically involves descent of the uterus and cervix through the vaginal canal due to failure of the cardinal-uterosacral ligament complex.

At the molecular level, POP is characterized by alterations in the extracellular matrix (ECM) of pelvic connective tissues. Type I and III collagen are the primary structural proteins providing tensile strength to pelvic fascia and ligaments. In women with POP, there is decreased total collagen content with an increased ratio of type III collagen (thinner, less rigid) to type I collagen (thick, high tensile strength). This shift weakens the tissue's ability to resist gravitational and intra-abdominal forces. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs)  -  particularly MMP-1 (interstitial collagenase), MMP-2 (gelatinase A), and MMP-9 (gelatinase B)  -  are significantly upregulated in the vaginal wall and uterosacral ligaments of women with POP. These zinc-dependent endopeptidases degrade collagen, elastin, and other ECM components. Simultaneously, tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) are downregulated, creating an imbalance favoring ECM degradation.

Elastin fiber fragmentation is another key finding. Elastin provides tissue recoil and resilience  -  its degradation by elastases and MMPs reduces the ability of pelvic tissues to recover from stretch (as occurs during vaginal delivery). The elastic fiber assembly protein fibulin-5, which anchors elastin to the ECM, is deficient in POP tissue. Animal models with fibulin-5 knockout develop severe prolapse, confirming its essential role.

The levator ani muscle is the primary muscular support for pelvic organs. Vaginal delivery causes direct mechanical injury to the pubovisceral portion of the levator ani in 13-36% of vaginal deliveries (detected by 3D transperineal ultrasound or MRI). Denervation injury to the pudendal nerve (S2-S4) during prolonged second stage of labor causes neurogenic atrophy of the levator ani over time. The combination of muscle injury, nerve damage, and ECM degradation creates progressive weakening of pelvic support.
diagnostics: Perform POP-Q (Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification) examination  -  standardized 9-point measurement system replacing Baden-Walker grading: measure 6 defined points (Aa, Ba, C, D, Ap, Bp) relative to the hymen, plus genital hiatus (gh), perineal body (pb), and total vaginal length (tvl)

Classify POP-Q stage: Stage 0 (no prolapse), Stage I (most distal point >1 cm above hymen), Stage II (most distal point within 1 cm of hymen), Stage III (most distal point >1 cm below hymen but <tvl-2 cm), Stage IV (complete eversion/procidentia)

Identify compartment(s) involved: anterior (cystocele  -  Aa/Ba points), apical (uterine prolapse or vault prolapse  -  C/D points), posterior (rectocele  -  Ap/Bp points)  -  multicompartment prolapse is common
Order post-void residual (PVR) measurement  -  assess for urinary retention with advanced prolapse
Order urodynamic studies if stress urinary incontinence coexists or if occult SUI is suspected (may be unmasked by prolapse reduction  -  the pessary test)
Order renal ultrasound if stage III-IV prolapse present (assess for hydronephrosis from ureteral kinking)
Assess pelvic floor muscle strength (Modified Oxford Scale 0-5) and voluntary contraction quality
Order Pap smear and endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding present (prolapsed cervix may develop keratinization, ulceration, or occult malignancy)
management: Conservative management (first-line for mild-moderate prolapse or patients declining surgery): prescribe pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT/Kegel exercises)  -  instruct on correct technique: contract levator ani (simulate stopping urine flow), hold 5-10 seconds, relax 5-10 seconds, perform 3 sets of 10-15 repetitions daily; supervised physiotherapy superior to self-directed exercises

Prescribe vaginal pessary for symptomatic relief: Ring pessary (first-line  -  easy to insert/remove, suitable for Stage I-III with intact perineal body); Gellhorn pessary (for more advanced prolapse with deficient perineum  -  suction cup provides stronger support); space-filling pessaries (cube, donut) for stage III-IV with wide introitus

Prescribe topical vaginal estrogen (estradiol 0.01% cream or vaginal estradiol tablet 10 mcg 2x/week) for all pessary users and postmenopausal women  -  restores vaginal mucosal thickness, reduces erosion risk, improves tissue quality by stimulating collagen synthesis and glycosaminoglycan production

Surgical options for symptomatic patients who fail conservative management: Native tissue repair  -  Manchester procedure (cervical amputation + cardinal ligament shortening), uterosacral ligament suspension, sacrospinous fixation

Mesh-augmented repair: Abdominal sacrocolpopexy (laparoscopic or robotic)  -  gold standard for apical prolapse with highest long-term cure rates (90% at 5 years); attaches polypropylene mesh from vaginal apex to anterior longitudinal ligament of sacrum
Colpocleisis (vaginal closure) for elderly patients who are no longer sexually active  -  simplest surgical option with lowest recurrence, can be performed under local/regional anesthesia
Concurrent anti-incontinence procedure (midurethral sling) if occult SUI demonstrated on urodynamics after prolapse reduction
Hysterectomy is NOT required for prolapse repair  -  uterine-preserving procedures (sacrohysteropexy, Manchester) are increasingly preferred for fertility preservation and patient preference
nursingActions: Teach correct Kegel exercise technique  -  validate proper levator ani contraction (patient should feel perineal lift, not bear down; palpate during digital exam to confirm correct muscle activation)
Educate on pessary self-management: insertion/removal technique (ring pessary), cleaning schedule (remove weekly, wash with mild soap and water, inspect for damage), when to follow up
Monitor for pessary complications: vaginal erosion/ulceration (most common), malodorous discharge, urinary retention, fistula formation (rare with neglected pessary), incarceration
Apply topical vaginal estrogen as prescribed and educate on proper application technique

Educate on lifestyle modifications: weight management (reduce BMI to <30), treat chronic constipation (high-fiber diet, adequate hydration, stool softeners), avoid heavy lifting and straining, smoking cessation (chronic cough)
Monitor for urinary retention symptoms with advanced prolapse  -  teach intermittent self-catheterization if PVR >150 mL
Provide pre-operative education for surgical candidates (procedure-specific risks, expected recovery, activity restrictions, pelvic rest 6-8 weeks post-op)
Monitor for post-operative complications: mesh erosion/exposure (abdominal sacrocolpopexy  -  3-5% risk), recurrent prolapse, de novo stress urinary incontinence, infection
Assess impact on sexual function and quality of life  -  refer for sexual health counseling if needed

benign-ovarian-cysts-np.title: Benign Over Kistleri: Folikülogenezi, IOTA
benign-ovarian-cysts-np.pathophysiology:
Ovarian cysts are fluid-filled sacs arising from or within the ovary. The vast majority are benign and classified as functional (physiologic) or neoplastic.

Functional cysts arise from normal ovarian folliculogenesis. During each menstrual cycle, FSH stimulates recruitment of a cohort of primordial follicles. The dominant follicle reaches 18-25 mm under FSH/LH stimulation, producing increasing estradiol through granulosa cell aromatase (CYP19A1) activity converting thecal androgens to estrogens. The LH surge triggers ovulation  -  rupture of the follicle wall via MMP activation, prostaglandin-mediated inflammation, and proteolytic degradation of the follicular surface. A follicular cyst forms when the dominant follicle fails to ovulate or fails to regress  -  continued FSH/estradiol stimulation causes progressive fluid accumulation, typically reaching 3-8 cm. Most resolve within 1-3 menstrual cycles.

Corpus luteum cysts form when the postovulatory corpus luteum fails to regress normally. After ovulation, granulosa cells undergo luteinization (stimulated by LH), transforming into large lutein cells that produce progesterone via cholesterol side-chain cleavage enzyme (CYP11A1) and 3β-HSD. The corpus luteum is highly vascular  -  angiogenesis driven by VEGF creates a rich capillary network. Corpus luteum cysts may accumulate hemorrhagic fluid (hemorrhagic corpus luteum) and can cause significant pain if they rupture, with hemoperitoneum sometimes requiring surgical intervention.

Dermoid cysts (mature cystic teratomas) are the most common benign ovarian neoplasm in reproductive-age women. They arise from totipotent germ cells that undergo abnormal meiotic division (failure of meiosis II completion), resulting in a cyst lined by mature ectodermal, mesodermal, and endodermal tissue derivatives  -  skin, hair follicles, sebaceous glands, teeth, bone, thyroid tissue (struma ovarii), and neural tissue. Malignant transformation occurs in <1-2% (squamous cell carcinoma arising from the ectodermal component is most common).

Endometriomas (chocolate cysts) result from ectopic endometrial tissue implanting on the ovarian surface. Repeated cyclical hemorrhage from the ectopic endometrium creates a cyst filled with old blood (dark brown hemosiderin-laden fluid). The cyst wall develops dense fibrosis and inflammatory adhesions. Endometriomas impair ovarian reserve through cortical fibrosis and oxidative stress-mediated follicle damage.
diagnostics: Order transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)  -  first-line imaging for ovarian mass characterization: assess size, laterality, internal echogenicity, septations, solid components, papillary projections, vascularity (color Doppler)

Apply IOTA (International Ovarian Tumor Analysis) Simple Rules for benign (B-rules) vs malignant (M-rules) classification: B1 (unilocular cyst), B2 (solid component <7 mm), B3 (acoustic shadows), B4 (smooth multilocular <100 mm), B5 (no blood flow); M1 (irregular solid tumor), M2 (ascites), M3 (≥4 papillary projections), M4 (irregular multilocular-solid >100 mm), M5 (very strong blood flow)

Calculate RMI (Risk of Malignancy Index) = U × M × CA-125: U = ultrasound score (0, 1, or 3 based on bilateral, multilocular, solid, metastases, ascites); M = menopausal status (1 = premenopausal, 3 = postmenopausal); RMI >200 suggests malignancy  -  refer to gynecologic oncology
Order serum CA-125 (elevated >35 U/mL suggests epithelial malignancy, but non-specific  -  elevated in endometriosis, PID, fibroids, pregnancy, menses)
Order HE4 (Human Epididymis Protein 4)  -  more specific than CA-125 for epithelial ovarian cancer, especially in premenopausal women
Calculate ROMA (Risk of Ovarian Malignancy Algorithm) using CA-125 + HE4 + menopausal status for refined risk stratification
Order AFP (alpha-fetoprotein) and β-hCG if germ cell tumor suspected (young patients with solid ovarian mass)
Order LDH if dysgerminoma suspected
Order inhibin A/B if granulosa cell tumor suspected (sex cord-stromal tumor)
Order thyroid function tests if struma ovarii suspected (dermoid with thyroid tissue  -  can cause hyperthyroidism)
management: Observation with serial ultrasound for simple functional cysts <5 cm in premenopausal women  -  reimage at 6-8 weeks (most resolve within 1-3 cycles)
Simple cysts 5-7 cm in premenopausal women: observation with annual ultrasound follow-up (very low malignancy risk if unilocular and anechoic)
Simple cysts >7 cm: consider surgical evaluation due to torsion risk and inability to fully characterize by imaging alone

Any complex cyst with suspicious features (solid components, papillary projections, irregular septations, high vascularity) in postmenopausal women: refer to gynecologic oncology for surgical evaluation
Prescribe combined oral contraceptives for functional cyst PREVENTION (suppress ovulation), but OCPs do NOT accelerate resolution of existing functional cysts  -  a common misconception

Dermoid cysts: surgical excision (laparoscopic cystectomy with ovarian preservation) recommended for cysts >5 cm or symptomatic  -  risk of torsion increases with size; intraoperative spillage of sebaceous contents causes chemical peritonitis (copious irrigation required)

Endometriomas: medical management with hormonal suppression (continuous OCPs, progestins, GnRH agonists) or surgical excision (laparoscopic cystectomy)  -  surgical excision may reduce ovarian reserve (counsel on fertility implications before surgery, consider pre-operative AMH measurement)

Emergency management of ovarian torsion: laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian preservation (do NOT perform oophorectomy unless tissue is clearly necrotic  -  ovarian salvage is possible even with dusky appearance)
Emergency management of ruptured hemorrhagic cyst: hemodynamic stabilization, serial hematocrit monitoring, surgical intervention (laparoscopy) if hemodynamic instability or hemoperitoneum >500 mL
nursingActions: Educate on expectant management  -  reassure that most functional cysts resolve spontaneously within 1-3 cycles
Monitor for ovarian torsion red flags: sudden onset severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting, guarding  -  emergency surgical evaluation required
Monitor for cyst rupture signs: acute onset sharp pelvic pain (may have transient relief if tension is released), peritoneal signs, hypotension/tachycardia if hemorrhagic
Educate on serial ultrasound schedule for observation (6-8 weeks for functional cysts, annually for persistent simple cysts)
Assess pain management needs (NSAIDs for mild cyst pain, avoid aspirin  -  may increase hemorrhagic risk)
Pre-operative education for cystectomy candidates: laparoscopic approach, ovarian preservation goal, fertility counseling
Post-operative monitoring: assess for postoperative hemorrhage, infection, ileus, shoulder pain (from CO2 insufflation)
Educate endometrioma patients on recurrence risk (30-50% within 2 years after cystectomy) and need for hormonal suppression
Counsel on CA-125 limitations  -  elevated in many benign conditions in premenopausal women; avoid unnecessary anxiety from mildly elevated results

endometrial-polyp-np.title: Endometrial Polipler: Moleküler Proliferasyon
endometrial-polyp-np.pathophysiology:
Endometrial polyps are localized overgrowths of endometrial glands, stroma, and blood vessels that project from the endometrial surface into the uterine cavity. They are extremely common, affecting 10-40% of women with abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB), and their prevalence increases with age, peaking in the perimenopausal period.

At the molecular level, endometrial polyps demonstrate autonomous proliferative behavior driven by several key mechanisms. Polyp tissue shows overexpression of estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) and reduced expression of progesterone receptors (PR-A and PR-B) compared to adjacent normal endometrium. This receptor imbalance creates localized estrogen hypersensitivity  -  polyp tissue proliferates in response to circulating estrogen while being relatively resistant to the anti-proliferative effects of progesterone. During the secretory phase, while normal endometrium undergoes progesterone-mediated differentiation, polyp tissue continues to proliferate.

Aromatase (CYP19A1) is overexpressed in polyp stromal cells, enabling local conversion of androgens (androstenedione, testosterone) to estrogens (estrone, estradiol) through intracrine and paracrine mechanisms. This creates an autonomous local estrogen microenvironment that drives polyp growth independent of systemic estrogen levels  -  explaining why polyps can develop and persist even in postmenopausal women with low circulating estrogen.

PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog) is a tumor suppressor gene on chromosome 10q23 that negatively regulates the PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling pathway  -  a key cell survival and proliferation cascade. Loss of PTEN expression (through gene deletion, mutation, or promoter hypermethylation) is found in approximately 20-35% of endometrial polyps, leading to constitutive activation of PI3K/AKT signaling, increased cell proliferation, and reduced apoptosis. PTEN loss is considered an early molecular event in the potential progression from polyp to endometrial hyperplasia and carcinoma.

Tamoxifen, a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM), acts as an estrogen AGONIST on the endometrium (while being an antagonist on breast tissue). Tamoxifen-induced polyps are common (incidence 30-60% in tamoxifen-treated breast cancer patients) and tend to be larger, more often multiple, and have a higher rate of atypical histology. The mechanism involves tamoxifen binding ERα in endometrial tissue and activating estrogenic transcriptional programs that promote glandular proliferation.
diagnostics: Order transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)  -  identify endometrial thickening; polyps appear as focal echogenic masses within the endometrial cavity, often with a feeder vessel on color Doppler (vascular pedicle sign)

Order saline infusion sonography (SIS/sonohysterography)  -  instill 5-10 mL sterile saline into the uterine cavity during TVUS; polyps appear as intracavitary masses outlined by anechoic saline, allowing differentiation from diffuse endometrial thickening or submucosal fibroids; sensitivity 90-95% and specificity 80-90% for polyps

Perform office hysteroscopy (gold standard)  -  direct visualization of the endometrial cavity with concurrent polypectomy capability; allows assessment of polyp size, number, location, and vascular pattern

Order endometrial biopsy (Pipelle sampling)  -  low sensitivity for polyps (10-30% detection rate) because blind sampling often misses focal lesions, but useful for assessing background endometrial pathology
Order postmenopausal bleeding workup if applicable: TVUS endometrial thickness ≥4 mm warrants tissue sampling; endometrial biopsy or hysteroscopy with targeted biopsy
Order CBC for anemia assessment if heavy menstrual bleeding present
Order CA-125 if concurrent adnexal pathology suspected

Risk-stratify for malignancy: factors increasing malignant potential include age >60, postmenopausal status, polyp size >1.5 cm, tamoxifen use, diabetes, hypertension, obesity  -  combined risk score guides management
management: Asymptomatic polyps <10 mm in premenopausal women: observation with serial ultrasound at 6-12 month intervals  -  spontaneous regression occurs in up to 25% of small polyps
Symptomatic polyps (AUB) or polyps >10 mm: hysteroscopic polypectomy (gold standard treatment)  -  office or operative hysteroscopy with direct visualization and targeted excision

Hysteroscopic polypectomy techniques: mechanical (grasping forceps, scissors  -  suitable for small pedunculated polyps), electrosurgical (bipolar resectoscope  -  preferred for large or sessile polyps, allows coagulation of the base), morcellator (intrauterine morcellator/tissue removal system  -  efficient for large or multiple polyps with minimal thermal damage)
ALL polyps in postmenopausal women should be removed and sent for histopathologic evaluation (malignant transformation rate 1-5%, higher with increasing age and risk factors)
ALL tamoxifen-associated polyps should be removed (higher rates of atypia and malignant transformation  -  3-10%)

If endometrial hyperplasia without atypia found on polypectomy histology: prescribe progestin therapy (levonorgestrel IUD preferred, or medroxyprogesterone 10 mg/day for 14 days/cycle) with follow-up endometrial biopsy at 3-6 months

If atypical endometrial hyperplasia (EIN  -  endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia) found: recommend hysterectomy for patients with completed childbearing (15-30% concurrent carcinoma risk); high-dose progestin therapy (megestrol 160 mg/day or levonorgestrel IUD) with close surveillance for fertility-preserving management
Recurrent polyps: consider levonorgestrel IUD for secondary prevention (reduces polyp recurrence by 65-75% through local progestin-mediated endometrial suppression)
Address modifiable risk factors: weight loss counseling, glycemic control, discuss tamoxifen alternatives (aromatase inhibitor switch in postmenopausal breast cancer patients  -  lower polyp risk)
nursingActions: Educate on abnormal uterine bleeding patterns that warrant evaluation: intermenstrual bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding (ANY amount), heavy menstrual bleeding requiring pad changes >hourly
Teach menstrual diary/pad count monitoring to quantify blood loss objectively

Provide pre-procedure education for hysteroscopy: NPO instructions if operative, expected procedure duration (15-30 minutes for office, 30-60 minutes for operative), anesthesia options, post-procedure cramping and light bleeding expected

Post-hysteroscopy care: mild cramping and spotting for 1-7 days is normal, avoid intercourse/tampons/douching for 1-2 weeks, report heavy bleeding (soaking >1 pad/hour), fever, or foul-smelling discharge
Educate on importance of histopathology results  -  follow-up appointment to review pathology and determine if further treatment is needed
Monitor for post-polypectomy complications: hemorrhage (uncommon  -  <1%), uterine perforation (rare), infection, intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome  -  rare)
Coordinate referral to gynecologic oncology if atypical hyperplasia or carcinoma found on histology
Educate tamoxifen patients on need for annual gynecologic assessment and reporting of any vaginal bleeding
Support patients through fertility discussions if atypical hyperplasia requires conservative management vs hysterectomy

endometriosis-np.title: Endometriozis
endometriosis-np.pathophysiology:
Endometriosis is defined by the presence of endometrial-like tissue (glands and stroma) outside the uterine cavity, most commonly on the pelvic peritoneum, ovaries (endometriomas), and rectovaginal septum. At the molecular level, Sampson's theory of retrograde menstruation provides the mechanical seeding mechanism  -  viable endometrial cells reflux through the fallopian tubes during menses  -  but immune dysregulation is required for implant survival.

In healthy individuals, peritoneal macrophages and natural killer (NK) cells clear ectopic endometrial cells. In endometriosis, defective NK cell cytotoxicity (reduced expression of activating receptors NKG2D and NKp46) and altered macrophage polarization (shift from M1 pro-inflammatory to M2 reparative phenotype) allow implant survival. Ectopic implants secrete matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9) that degrade the extracellular matrix, enabling tissue invasion and neovascularization via vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) overexpression.

Local aromatase (CYP19A1) overexpression in ectopic stromal cells drives autonomous estrogen biosynthesis through intracrine signaling. Simultaneously, 17-beta-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (17β-HSD-2)  -  which normally inactivates estradiol to estrone  -  is downregulated due to progesterone resistance mediated by epigenetic silencing of progesterone receptor B (PR-B) via promoter hypermethylation. This creates a self-perpetuating estrogen-dominant microenvironment.
