Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) is overexpressed in ectopic implants, generating prostaglandin E2 (PGE2), which stimulates aromatase activity in a positive feedback loop (PGE2 → aromatase → estradiol → COX-2 → PGE2). Inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and oxidative stress from iron deposition (from repeated hemorrhage within implants) promote fibrosis through TGF-β signaling, contributing to adhesion formation and pain sensitization via peripheral nerve fiber sprouting.
diagnostics: Order transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)  -  endometriomas appear as homogeneous hypoechoic cysts with ground-glass echogenicity and low-level internal echoes (chocolate cysts); sensitivity 90-95% for ovarian endometriomas

Order MRI pelvis with fat saturation  -  T1 hyperintense (blood products), T1 fat-sat hyperintense (confirms blood not fat), T2 shading sign (characteristic); superior to TVUS for deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE) mapping of rectovaginal, uterosacral, and bladder involvement
Consider CA-125 level  -  often elevated but non-specific; useful for monitoring treatment response, not for primary diagnosis
Laparoscopy with histologic confirmation remains the gold standard for definitive diagnosis  -  visual identification with biopsy showing endometrial glands and stroma
Order anti-mullerian hormone (AMH) to assess ovarian reserve in patients with bilateral endometriomas before surgical planning
Classify by revised ASRM staging (I-IV) at surgery  -  stage does not correlate well with pain severity but guides fertility prognosis
Order renal ultrasound if deep infiltrating endometriosis suspected near ureters (ureteral involvement causes silent hydronephrosis)
Assess pain using validated instruments (visual analog scale, Endometriosis Health Profile-30)
management: Prescribe combined oral contraceptives (continuous regimen  -  eliminate withdrawal bleeding) as first-line medical therapy for pain  -  suppress ovulation and reduce endometrial proliferation
Prescribe dienogest 2 mg daily  -  progestin with specific anti-proliferative activity on endometriotic tissue, inhibits aromatase activity in ectopic implants

Prescribe GnRH agonist depot (leuprolide 11.25 mg IM every 3 months) with hormonal add-back therapy (norethindrone 5 mg/day + conjugated estrogen 0.625 mg/day) for severe refractory pain  -  add-back prevents bone loss and vasomotor symptoms
Prescribe GnRH antagonist (elagolix 150 mg daily for non-dysmenorrhea pain or 200 mg BID for dysmenorrhea)  -  oral, dose-dependent partial estrogen suppression without flare effect
Prescribe NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg BID) for dysmenorrhea  -  inhibit COX-2/PGE2 pathway
Refer for laparoscopic excision (preferred over ablation) of endometriotic implants in patients with failed medical therapy, deep infiltrating disease, or endometriomas >4 cm
Levonorgestrel IUD placement for localized progestin delivery  -  particularly effective for rectovaginal disease and post-surgical prevention of recurrence
Fertility management: refer to reproductive endocrinology  -  IVF is often necessary as endometriosis reduces fecundity; consider empiric superovulation with IUI for mild disease

Prescribe aromatase inhibitor (letrozole 2.5 mg daily) in combination with progestin or GnRH agonist for aromatase-overexpressing refractory disease  -  blocks local estrogen biosynthesis in ectopic implants
nursingActions: Assess pain quality, location, and temporal pattern (cyclic vs acyclic, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia)
Monitor for medication side effects  -  breakthrough bleeding on progestins, vasomotor symptoms on GnRH agonists, hepatotoxicity with dienogest
Educate on continuous OCP use  -  skip placebo pills to eliminate withdrawal bleeding and reduce implant stimulation
Monitor bone mineral density (DEXA) for patients on GnRH agonist therapy beyond 6 months even with add-back
Assess for bladder and bowel symptoms (dysuria, hematuria, dyschezia, rectal bleeding)  -  may indicate deep infiltrating disease
Screen for depression and anxiety  -  chronic pelvic pain has significant psychosocial impact
Educate on fertility preservation options before surgical management of endometriomas (cystectomy reduces ovarian reserve)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (gynecology, colorectal surgery, urology) for deep infiltrating disease
Teach non-pharmacologic pain management strategies (pelvic floor physiotherapy, heat therapy, mindfulness)
Monitor treatment response and adjust regimen in collaboration with prescribing provider

infertility-np.title: Kısırlık
infertility-np.pathophysiology:
Infertility is defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse (6 months if female partner is ≥35 years). The process of conception requires coordinated function across multiple systems: hypothalamic GnRH pulsatility driving pituitary gonadotropin secretion, ovarian folliculogenesis and ovulation, tubal patency for oocyte-sperm interaction, adequate sperm production and function, and a receptive endometrium for implantation.

At the molecular level, ovarian reserve reflects the remaining pool of primordial follicles established during fetal life. Each menstrual cycle, a cohort of follicles is recruited by rising FSH, but only the dominant follicle (with the highest FSH receptor density and lowest FSH threshold) survives selection while others undergo atresia via Fas/FasL-mediated apoptosis. Anti-mullerian hormone (AMH), produced by granulosa cells of pre-antral and small antral follicles, serves as the primary biomarker of ovarian reserve  -  it reflects the recruitable follicle pool independent of cycle day.

Ovulatory dysfunction (WHO classification) accounts for approximately 25-30% of female infertility. Class I (hypogonadotropic hypogonadism) results from hypothalamic or pituitary failure  -  inadequate GnRH pulsatility from functional hypothalamic amenorrhea (energy deficit, stress), pituitary tumors, or Sheehan syndrome. Class II (normogonadotropic  -  most commonly PCOS) involves follicular arrest due to hyperandrogenism and insulin resistance disrupting FSH-dependent follicle selection. Class III (hypergonadotropic) reflects premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) with accelerated follicle depletion.

Tubal factor infertility (25-35%) most commonly results from Chlamydia trachomatis or Neisseria gonorrhoeae-induced salpingitis causing epithelial damage, deciliation, and peritubal adhesions. The inflammatory cascade activates toll-like receptors (TLR2, TLR4) on tubal epithelial cells, triggering NF-kB-mediated cytokine release (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), leading to fibroblast activation and tubal fibrosis/occlusion.

Male factor (30-40% as sole or contributing cause) encompasses abnormalities in spermatogenesis, sperm transport, or sperm function. Varicocele causes testicular hyperthermia and oxidative stress via reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, damaging sperm DNA integrity and mitochondrial membrane potential.
diagnostics: Order cycle day 2-4 FSH and estradiol  -  elevated FSH with elevated estradiol suggests diminished ovarian reserve (early follicle recruitment from accelerated selection)
Order AMH  -  low AMH reflects reduced recruitable follicle pool; used for IVF protocol selection and prognosis counseling
Order antral follicle count (AFC) via transvaginal ultrasound on cycle day 2-4  -  combined with AMH provides most accurate ovarian reserve assessment
Order mid-luteal progesterone (cycle day 21 in 28-day cycle)  -  confirms ovulation occurred
Order hysterosalpingography (HSG)  -  radiopaque dye injection under fluoroscopy to assess tubal patency and uterine cavity contour
Order thyroid function tests and prolactin level  -  screen for thyroid dysfunction and hyperprolactinemia as reversible causes
Order semen analysis (WHO criteria)  -  evaluate volume, concentration, motility, and morphology; repeat if abnormal
Order karyotype and FMR1 premutation testing if premature ovarian insufficiency diagnosed (fragile X premutations cause accelerated follicle depletion)
Consider saline infusion sonography (SIS) or hysteroscopy if uterine cavity abnormality suspected
Order 21-hydroxylase antibodies if autoimmune POI suspected (associated with autoimmune adrenal insufficiency)
management: Prescribe letrozole 2.5-7.5 mg (cycle days 3-7) as first-line ovulation induction for anovulatory infertility (PCOS)  -  aromatase inhibition lowers estrogen, releasing hypothalamic-pituitary axis from negative feedback, promoting FSH-driven monofollicular development

Prescribe clomiphene citrate 50-150 mg (cycle days 5-9) as alternative ovulation induction  -  selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that blocks estrogen negative feedback at hypothalamus, increasing GnRH pulsatility and gonadotropin release

Prescribe metformin 500-2000 mg daily as adjunct for PCOS with insulin resistance  -  activates AMPK pathway, reduces hepatic glucose output, decreases hyperinsulinemia, and lowers ovarian androgen production

Prescribe gonadotropins (recombinant FSH  -  follitropin alfa/beta) for controlled ovarian stimulation when oral agents fail  -  requires close ultrasound monitoring to prevent ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS)
Prescribe cabergoline 0.25-1 mg twice weekly for hyperprolactinemia-induced anovulation  -  D2 dopamine agonist restoring GnRH pulsatility
Refer for IVF when tubal factor, severe male factor, advanced age, or failed ovulation induction  -  controlled ovarian hyperstimulation, oocyte retrieval, in vitro fertilization, embryo transfer
Prescribe trigger injection (hCG 5000-10000 IU or GnRH agonist trigger) to induce final oocyte maturation 36 hours before timed intercourse or IUI

Surgical management: laparoscopic tubal surgery for mild distal tubal disease, hysteroscopic polypectomy or myomectomy for intracavitary lesions, varicocelectomy for clinical varicocele with abnormal semen parameters

Counsel on lifestyle modifications  -  weight optimization (5-10% weight loss restores ovulation in 50-80% of obese anovulatory women), smoking cessation, alcohol moderation, folic acid supplementation
nursingActions: Educate on fertility awareness  -  optimal timing of intercourse (every 1-2 days during fertile window, 5 days before and day of ovulation)
Teach ovulation predictor kit (OPK) use  -  LH surge detection 24-36 hours before ovulation
Monitor for OHSS symptoms during gonadotropin therapy  -  abdominal distension, rapid weight gain (>1 kg/day), nausea, dyspnea, oliguria (capillary leak from VEGF-mediated vascular permeability)
Educate on letrozole vs clomiphene  -  letrozole has higher live birth rates in PCOS and lower risk of multiple gestations
Coordinate cycle monitoring  -  serial transvaginal ultrasounds and estradiol levels during ovulation induction
Screen for psychological distress  -  infertility is associated with anxiety, depression, and relationship strain
Educate on realistic success rates  -  per-cycle fecundity with timed intercourse is 15-20% in young couples, IVF live birth rates are age-dependent
Assess for medication side effects  -  hot flashes and visual disturbances with clomiphene, ovarian enlargement with gonadotropins
Ensure preconception counseling  -  folic acid 400 mcg daily (4 mg if history of neural tube defect), rubella immunity, genetic carrier screening
Coordinate multidisciplinary care (reproductive endocrinology, urology, psychology)

galactorrhea-np.title: Galaktore
galactorrhea-np.pathophysiology:
Galactorrhea is the non-puerperal secretion of milk or milk-like discharge from the breast, resulting from dysregulated prolactin signaling on mammary epithelial cells. Prolactin is synthesized and secreted by lactotroph cells of the anterior pituitary, and its regulation is unique among pituitary hormones  -  it is predominantly under tonic inhibitory control by hypothalamic dopamine (prolactin-inhibiting factor).

Dopamine released from tuberoinfundibular dopaminergic (TIDA) neurons in the arcuate nucleus travels via the hypophyseal portal system to the anterior pituitary, where it binds dopamine D2 receptors on lactotroph cell membranes. D2 receptor activation inhibits adenylyl cyclase, reduces intracellular cAMP, and suppresses prolactin gene transcription (Pit-1 transcription factor pathway) and prolactin secretion. Any process that disrupts dopamine synthesis, transport, or D2 receptor signaling will release lactotrophs from tonic inhibition and cause hyperprolactinemia.

Prolactinomas (lactotroph adenomas) are the most common functioning pituitary adenoma, accounting for 40-50% of all pituitary tumors. They arise from monoclonal lactotroph proliferation  -  somatic mutations in genes involved in cell cycle regulation (MEN1, AIP, CDKN1B) have been identified. Microprolactinomas (<10 mm) are far more common than macroprolactinomas (≥10 mm). Prolactin levels generally correlate with tumor size  -  the 'stalk effect' must be distinguished from true prolactinomas: any sellar mass that compresses the pituitary stalk disrupts dopamine transport, causing mild-moderate hyperprolactinemia without a prolactin-secreting tumor.

Drug-induced hyperprolactinemia is the most common cause overall. Typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, chlorpromazine) and risperidone are potent D2 receptor antagonists causing marked prolactin elevation. Atypical antipsychotics (quetiapine, aripiprazole) have less D2 antagonism or partial agonist activity. Metoclopramide and domperidone (D2 antagonists at the chemoreceptor trigger zone) cross the blood-brain barrier (metoclopramide) or act at the pituitary level outside the BBB (domperidone) to raise prolactin.

Hypothyroidism causes hyperprolactinemia through TRH cross-stimulation  -  elevated TRH (thyrotropin-releasing hormone) stimulates both TSH and prolactin secretion from the anterior pituitary. This is a reversible cause that resolves with thyroid hormone replacement.
diagnostics: Order serum prolactin level  -  drawn fasting, avoid breast stimulation and stress before sampling; mild elevation (<100 ng/mL) suggests drug-induced or stalk effect; marked elevation (>200 ng/mL) strongly suggests macroprolactinoma
Order thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4)  -  rule out hypothyroidism as reversible cause
Order pregnancy test (β-hCG)  -  pregnancy is the most common physiologic cause of galactorrhea with hyperprolactinemia

Order brain MRI with gadolinium and pituitary protocol (thin coronal cuts through sella)  -  indicated when prolactin is elevated and drug-induced/physiologic causes excluded; identifies adenoma size and extension

Screen for macroprolactinemia (big-big prolactin)  -  immunoreactive but biologically inactive prolactin-IgG complexes; diagnosed by polyethylene glycol (PEG) precipitation; causes falsely elevated prolactin without clinical symptoms
Order visual field testing (Goldmann perimetry or automated Humphrey) if macroprolactinoma  -  suprasellar extension compresses optic chiasm causing bitemporal hemianopia
Order renal function (BUN, creatinine) to exclude chronic kidney disease as cause of impaired prolactin clearance
Evaluate medication history comprehensively  -  dopamine antagonists are the most common cause of hyperprolactinemia in clinical practice
management: Prescribe cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly, titrate every 4 weeks based on prolactin level (maximum 1 mg twice weekly for most patients)  -  first-line pharmacotherapy for prolactinomas; normalizes prolactin in 85-90% and causes tumor shrinkage in 80%
Prescribe bromocriptine 1.25 mg at bedtime, titrate to 2.5-7.5 mg BID  -  alternative to cabergoline; less well-tolerated but longer safety data in pregnancy
Treat underlying hypothyroidism with levothyroxine  -  galactorrhea resolves as TSH normalizes and TRH stimulation decreases
Discontinue or substitute offending dopamine antagonist medication when psychiatrically safe  -  switch risperidone to aripiprazole (partial D2 agonist that suppresses rather than elevates prolactin)
Refer to neurosurgery for transsphenoidal adenomectomy when medical therapy fails, is not tolerated, or for giant prolactinomas (>4 cm) with acute visual compromise
Monitor visual fields serially in macroprolactinomas  -  improvement expected within days to weeks of dopamine agonist initiation
Counsel on fertility restoration  -  ovulation typically returns within 3-6 months of prolactin normalization; switch to bromocriptine if pregnancy desired (more safety data than cabergoline)
Monitor for pituitary apoplexy symptoms in large adenomas  -  sudden severe headache, visual loss, ophthalmoplegia (hemorrhage or infarction of tumor)  -  surgical emergency
Assess bone mineral density if prolonged amenorrhea from hyperprolactinemia (estrogen deficiency accelerates bone loss)
nursingActions: Educate patient on proper prolactin sampling  -  fasting, avoid breast manipulation for 24 hours before draw, minimize stress
Administer cabergoline with food at bedtime to reduce nausea and orthostatic hypotension
Monitor blood pressure  -  dopamine agonists can cause orthostatic hypotension, especially during dose titration
Assess for medication side effects  -  nausea, dizziness, nasal congestion, constipation; impulse control disorders (pathologic gambling, hypersexuality) reported with dopamine agonists
Monitor prolactin levels every 4-8 weeks during dose titration, then every 6-12 months once stable
Educate on signs of pituitary apoplexy  -  sudden severe headache, vision changes, altered consciousness  -  seek emergency care immediately
Assess menstrual pattern restoration  -  return of regular menses indicates effective prolactin suppression
Coordinate with ophthalmology for serial visual field testing in macroprolactinomas
Screen for depression and sexual dysfunction  -  hyperprolactinemia causes hypogonadism with associated mood changes and decreased libido
Educate on contraception needs during treatment  -  fertility may return rapidly with prolactin normalization

breast-cancer-np.title: Meme Kanseri Taraması
breast-cancer-np.pathophysiology:
Breast cancer arises from the malignant transformation of mammary epithelial cells through the accumulation of genetic and epigenetic alterations affecting oncogenes and tumor suppressor genes. Understanding molecular subtypes is essential for advanced prescriptive management and screening risk stratification.

Molecular subtypes are classified by gene expression profiling into four clinically actionable categories. Luminal A (ER+/PR+/HER2-/low Ki-67) is the most common (40-50%), driven by estrogen receptor alpha (ERα) transcriptional activation of cell proliferation genes. These tumors have the best prognosis and respond to endocrine therapy. Luminal B (ER+/PR+/-/HER2+/-/high Ki-67) has higher proliferative index and poorer prognosis than Luminal A. HER2-enriched (ER-/PR-/HER2+) tumors overexpress human epidermal growth factor receptor 2 (HER2/ERBB2) through gene amplification on chromosome 17q12, causing constitutive activation of the RAS-MAPK and PI3K/AKT proliferation cascades. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC  -  ER-/PR-/HER2-) lacks all three receptors, is highly proliferative, and disproportionately affects BRCA1 mutation carriers and young African American women.

Hereditary breast cancer accounts for 5-10% of all breast cancers. BRCA1 (chromosome 17q21) and BRCA2 (chromosome 13q12.3) are tumor suppressor genes encoding proteins essential for homologous recombination repair (HRR) of double-strand DNA breaks. Loss-of-function mutations impair DNA repair fidelity, leading to genomic instability and cancer predisposition. BRCA1 carriers have 60-80% lifetime breast cancer risk and 40-60% ovarian cancer risk. BRCA2 carriers have 45-70% lifetime breast cancer risk and 15-25% ovarian cancer risk.

Risk assessment models guide screening intensity. The Gail model calculates 5-year and lifetime risk using age, menarche, first live birth, prior biopsies, atypia, and first-degree relatives  -  it underestimates risk in women with strong paternal family history or known genetic mutations. The Tyrer-Cuzick (IBIS) model is more comprehensive, incorporating second-degree family history, BRCA status, breast density, BMI, and hormone use  -  recommended by NCCN for risk stratification. Lifetime risk ≥20% qualifies for supplemental screening with breast MRI.
diagnostics: Order screening mammography  -  USPSTF recommends biennial screening ages 50-74 (2024 update: consider starting at 40); ACR/NCCN recommend annual screening starting at 40 for average risk
Order breast MRI with gadolinium contrast for high-risk patients (lifetime risk ≥20% by Tyrer-Cuzick model)  -  annual MRI alternating with mammography every 6 months
Calculate lifetime breast cancer risk using Tyrer-Cuzick (IBIS) model  -  most comprehensive validated risk calculator incorporating genetics, family history, density, and hormonal factors
Assess breast density on mammography (ACR BI-RADS density categories A-D)  -  dense breasts (C/D) reduce mammographic sensitivity from 85% to 48-64% and independently increase cancer risk 1.5-2 fold
Order supplemental screening ultrasound for dense breasts when MRI is not indicated or available

Refer for genetic counseling and BRCA1/BRCA2 testing when family history meets NCCN criteria  -  triple-negative breast cancer <60 years, Ashkenazi Jewish heritage, male breast cancer, breast + ovarian cancer in family
Perform clinical breast exam (CBE) at screening visits  -  palpate all four quadrants, axillary and supraclavicular lymph nodes
Order diagnostic mammography with spot compression and magnification views for palpable masses or screening abnormalities
Order core needle biopsy (ultrasound-guided or stereotactic) for BI-RADS 4 or 5 lesions  -  histologic diagnosis before any treatment planning
Consider contrast-enhanced mammography (CEM) as alternative to MRI for high-risk screening when MRI is contraindicated or unavailable
management: Risk reduction counseling for high-risk patients  -  chemoprevention with tamoxifen (premenopausal  -  SERM blocking ER in breast tissue), raloxifene (postmenopausal), or aromatase inhibitors (exemestane, anastrozole  -  postmenopausal only)
Prescribe tamoxifen 20 mg daily for 5 years for chemoprevention in high-risk premenopausal women  -  reduces invasive breast cancer risk by 49% (NSABP P-1 trial)
Prescribe exemestane 25 mg daily or anastrozole 1 mg daily for 5 years for chemoprevention in high-risk postmenopausal women  -  65% relative risk reduction (MAP.3 and IBIS-II trials)

Refer BRCA1/BRCA2 carriers for discussion of risk-reducing bilateral mastectomy (reduces risk by 90-95%) and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (reduces ovarian cancer risk by 80% and breast cancer risk by 50% if done premenopausal)

Implement high-risk screening protocol  -  annual mammography + annual breast MRI starting age 25-30 (or 10 years before youngest family member's diagnosis, whichever is earlier) for BRCA carriers and other high-penetrance mutation carriers
Refer all newly diagnosed breast cancers for tumor molecular profiling  -  Oncotype DX (21-gene recurrence score) for ER+/HER2-/node-negative to guide adjuvant chemotherapy decisions
Coordinate multidisciplinary tumor board referral for all new diagnoses  -  surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, genetics, reconstructive surgery
Address modifiable risk factors  -  weight management, physical activity (≥150 minutes/week reduces risk 20-25%), alcohol limitation (each drink/day increases risk 7-10%), smoking cessation
Prescribe BRCA carrier fertility preservation counseling before risk-reducing oophorectomy  -  oocyte/embryo cryopreservation options
nursingActions: Educate on breast self-awareness  -  know normal breast tissue and report changes (new lump, skin dimpling, nipple retraction, bloody discharge)
Teach proper breast self-exam technique if patient desires  -  systematic palpation of all quadrants and axillary tail in both upright and supine positions
Coordinate screening schedule  -  ensure mammography and MRI are appropriately timed and alternated for high-risk patients
Provide pre-procedure education for core biopsy  -  local anesthesia, minimal pain, bruising expected, results typically available in 3-5 days
Monitor for tamoxifen side effects  -  hot flashes, VTE risk, endometrial cancer risk (report any abnormal vaginal bleeding), visual changes (rare retinopathy)

Monitor for aromatase inhibitor side effects  -  arthralgias/myalgias (most common reason for discontinuation), bone density loss (order baseline DEXA, repeat at 1-2 years), fracture risk, cardiovascular effects
Educate on genetic testing implications  -  emotional impact, insurance protections (GINA Act), family implications, reproductive planning
Screen for anxiety related to high-risk status or screening callbacks  -  screening mammography has a 10% recall rate (most are false positives)
Assess medication adherence for chemoprevention  -  only 50% of patients complete 5-year course; address barriers and side effects proactively
Provide survivorship care coordination for breast cancer survivors  -  surveillance mammography, symptom monitoring, psychosocial support

urethritis-np.title: Üretrit
urethritis-np.pathophysiology:
Urethritis is inflammation of the urethral mucosa, predominantly caused by sexually transmitted pathogens. It is classified as gonococcal urethritis (GU  -  caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae) or non-gonococcal urethritis (NGU  -  most commonly Chlamydia trachomatis, Mycoplasma genitalium, Ureaplasma urealyticum, and Trichomonas vaginalis).

Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a gram-negative diplococcus that attaches to columnar urethral epithelial cells via type IV pili and opacity-associated (Opa) proteins. Following attachment, the organism is internalized through receptor-mediated endocytosis into non-acidic vacuoles, evading lysosomal destruction. Gonococcal lipooligosaccharide (LOS) activates toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4) on epithelial and immune cells, triggering NF-kB-mediated release of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and massive neutrophil recruitment  -  producing the characteristic purulent urethral discharge.

Gonococcal antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a critical public health concern. Penicillinase-producing N. gonorrhoeae (PPNG) emerged through acquisition of the bla-TEM plasmid. Chromosomal mutations in penA (encoding penicillin-binding protein 2), mtrR (efflux pump regulator), and porB (outer membrane porin) have progressively expanded resistance to penicillins, tetracyclines, fluoroquinolones, and now threaten extended-spectrum cephalosporins. The mosaic penA allele (incorporating sequences from commensal Neisseria species through horizontal gene transfer) reduces ceftriaxone binding affinity  -  this is the molecular basis of emerging ceftriaxone resistance.

Chlamydia trachomatis (serovars D-K) is an obligate intracellular bacterium with a unique biphasic developmental cycle. The infectious elementary body (EB) attaches to epithelial cells and is internalized into a membrane-bound inclusion. Within the inclusion, EBs differentiate into metabolically active reticulate bodies (RBs) that replicate by binary fission. RBs then re-condense into EBs, which are released to infect adjacent cells. Chlamydia evades immune detection by inhibiting phagosome-lysosome fusion and suppressing MHC class I expression.

Mycoplasma genitalium is increasingly recognized as a major cause of NGU (15-25% of cases). It lacks a cell wall (making beta-lactams and glycopeptides inherently ineffective) and demonstrates rapidly emerging macrolide resistance through 23S rRNA point mutations (A2058G, A2059G) and fluoroquinolone resistance through parC and gyrA mutations.
diagnostics: Order nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis from first-void urine (preferred in males) or urethral swab  -  NAAT is the gold standard with sensitivity >95% and specificity >99%
Order Mycoplasma genitalium NAAT with concurrent macrolide resistance testing (detects 23S rRNA mutations  -  guides antibiotic selection before treatment)
Order gram stain of urethral discharge (point-of-care)  -  intracellular gram-negative diplococci within neutrophils confirms gonococcal urethritis with >95% sensitivity in symptomatic males
Order gonococcal culture with antimicrobial susceptibility testing when treatment failure suspected or for surveillance of resistance patterns
Order Trichomonas vaginalis NAAT if initial gonorrhea/chlamydia testing is negative (T. vaginalis causes 2-5% of NGU)
Screen for concurrent STIs  -  HIV, syphilis (RPR/VDRL with reflex treponemal test), hepatitis B and C
Test all sexual partners from the preceding 60 days
Order urinalysis  -  pyuria (≥10 WBC/HPF) supports urethritis diagnosis; first-void urine leukocyte esterase positive
Consider pharyngeal and rectal NAAT for gonorrhea and chlamydia in MSM (extragenital infection often asymptomatic)
management: Prescribe dual therapy for gonococcal urethritis (CDC 2021 guidelines): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose (1 g if weight ≥150 kg) PLUS treat for chlamydia if not excluded (doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7 days)
Prescribe doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7 days as first-line for chlamydial urethritis
For Mycoplasma genitalium  -  if macrolide-sensitive (no 23S rRNA mutations): prescribe doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7 days followed by azithromycin 1 g day 1, then 500 mg days 2-4
For Mycoplasma genitalium  -  if macrolide-resistant (23S rRNA mutation detected): prescribe doxycycline 100 mg BID for 7 days followed by moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 7 days
For persistent/recurrent NGU after doxycycline: prescribe moxifloxacin 400 mg daily for 7-14 days (covers M. genitalium fluoroquinolone-susceptible strains)
Prescribe expedited partner therapy (EPT) where legally permitted  -  provide prescription or medication for patient to give to sexual partners without clinical evaluation
Recommend abstinence from sexual activity for 7 days after treatment initiation and until all partners are treated

Test of cure (TOC) is recommended for all M. genitalium infections and gonococcal infections treated with alternative regimens  -  NAAT at 21 days post-treatment (allows for clearance of dead organism nucleic acid)
Report gonorrhea and chlamydia to public health as required by law
nursingActions: Collect first-void urine specimen correctly  -  patient should not have urinated for at least 1 hour before collection; first 10-20 mL of voided urine contains highest concentration of urethral organisms
Educate on completing full antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve  -  incomplete treatment promotes resistance
Provide safer sex counseling  -  consistent condom use, partner reduction, regular screening for sexually active individuals
Facilitate partner notification and EPT  -  provide partner treatment packets where available

Educate on medication side effects  -  doxycycline (photosensitivity, GI upset  -  take with food and water, avoid lying down for 30 minutes), ceftriaxone (injection site pain  -  reconstitute with lidocaine)
Monitor for treatment failure  -  persistence of symptoms at 7-14 days warrants repeat testing and culture with susceptibility
Screen for intimate partner violence (IPV) during STI encounters  -  STIs may be associated with coerced unprotected sex
Provide pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) counseling for HIV-negative patients with recurrent STIs  -  high-risk indicator
Educate on retesting in 3 months after treatment  -  reinfection rate is high (10-20%) due to re-exposure from untreated partners
Assess for extragenital symptoms (pharyngitis, proctitis) in MSM  -  extragenital gonorrhea/chlamydia is often asymptomatic

urethral-stricture-np.title: Üretral Darlık
urethral-stricture-np.pathophysiology:
Urethral stricture is a pathologic narrowing of the urethral lumen caused by fibrosis of the corpus spongiosum (spongiofibrosis) surrounding the urethra. The process begins with mucosal injury to the urethral epithelium, progresses through submucosal inflammation, and culminates in dense fibrotic scar formation that reduces luminal diameter and impairs urinary flow.

At the molecular level, urethral injury activates a wound healing cascade that becomes dysregulated, leading to excessive fibrosis rather than normal tissue repair. Initial epithelial disruption triggers platelet degranulation and release of platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF) and transforming growth factor beta (TGF-β). TGF-β is the master fibrotic cytokine  -  it activates resident fibroblasts through SMAD2/3 signaling pathway, inducing their differentiation into myofibroblasts (characterized by alpha-smooth muscle actin expression). Myofibroblasts produce excessive extracellular matrix (ECM) components, particularly collagen types I and III, fibronectin, and proteoglycans.

Simultaneously, TGF-β suppresses matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) expression while upregulating tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs), creating an imbalance favoring collagen deposition over degradation. The resulting dense scar tissue (spongiofibrosis) is poorly vascularized, inelastic, and progressively contracts, narrowing the urethral lumen.

Iatrogenic causes are the most common etiology in developed countries  -  traumatic urethral catheterization, transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP), hypospadias repair, and cystoscopy. Each instrumentation event causes epithelial abrasion, exposing the submucosal layer to urine (which is chemically irritating due to its acidity, ammonia content, and solute concentration), perpetuating inflammation. Lichen sclerosus (balanitis xerotica obliterans  -  BXO) is an autoimmune chronic inflammatory dermatosis that causes progressive fibrosis of the glans, foreskin, and distal urethra through T-lymphocyte-mediated epithelial destruction and basement membrane sclerosis. Infectious urethritis (particularly gonococcal) historically was the leading cause and remains significant in resource-limited settings  -  post-inflammatory fibrosis from repeated infections leads to stricture formation.
diagnostics: Order retrograde urethrogram (RUG)  -  gold standard imaging; instill contrast through urethral meatus under fluoroscopy to delineate stricture location, length, and degree of luminal narrowing
Order voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG)  -  complements RUG by visualizing the proximal extent of stricture and bladder neck during micturition
Order uroflowmetry  -  non-invasive screening; obstructed pattern shows decreased peak flow rate (Qmax) with prolonged voiding time and flattened/plateau curve pattern
Order post-void residual (PVR) measurement via bladder ultrasound  -  elevated PVR indicates incomplete emptying from outflow obstruction
Order urethral ultrasound (sonoelastography)  -  emerging modality that quantifies spongiofibrosis density and depth, aiding surgical planning (predicts success of endoscopic vs open repair)
Order cystourethroscopy  -  direct visualization of stricture caliber, mucosal appearance, and passage of the scope; complements radiographic assessment
Order renal ultrasound to assess for upper tract changes (hydronephrosis) in chronic high-pressure retention
Order urinalysis and urine culture to evaluate for concurrent urinary tract infection (common in urinary stasis)
Order serum creatinine to assess renal function impact of chronic obstruction
management: Prescribe tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily (alpha-1A adrenergic antagonist) for symptomatic relief of obstructive voiding symptoms  -  relaxes smooth muscle of bladder neck and proximal urethra but does not treat the structural stricture

Refer for urethral dilation as initial office-based intervention for short (<2 cm) and non-dense strictures  -  serial passage of progressively larger dilators (sounds or balloons) to mechanically disrupt scar tissue

Refer for direct visual internal urethrotomy (DVIU)  -  endoscopic cold-knife or laser incision of stricture under direct vision; best for short bulbar strictures <1.5 cm; recurrence rate 50-70% for repeated procedures
Refer for urethroplasty (open surgical reconstruction)  -  gold standard for definitive treatment of strictures >2 cm, recurrent strictures after endoscopic failure, or dense spongiofibrosis

Urethroplasty techniques: excision and primary anastomosis (EPA  -  for short <2 cm bulbar strictures  -  95% long-term success), buccal mucosal graft (BMG) onlay urethroplasty for longer strictures (85-90% success)

Prescribe low-dose self-catheterization protocol for patients with recurrent stricture after endoscopic treatment  -  clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) with 14-16 Fr catheter twice weekly to maintain urethral caliber

Prescribe intralesional injection of mitomycin C (0.4 mg/mL) at the time of DVIU  -  alkylating agent inhibits fibroblast proliferation and collagen deposition, reducing recurrence (emerging evidence)
Manage lichen sclerosus-associated stricture with topical clobetasol 0.05% for meatal/fossa navicularis disease  -  high-potency corticosteroid reduces inflammatory fibrosis
Refer for suprapubic catheter placement for acute urinary retention when urethral catheterization fails  -  avoids further urethral trauma
nursingActions: Assess voiding symptoms using International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS) or AUA Symptom Index  -  quantifies obstructive and irritative symptoms for treatment monitoring
Teach clean intermittent catheterization (CIC) technique for patients prescribed self-dilation protocol  -  sterile/clean technique, adequate lubrication, gentle passage without force
Monitor for urinary retention signs  -  suprapubic fullness, dribbling, weak stream, straining to void, recurrent UTIs
Educate on post-urethral dilation or DVIU care  -  mild dysuria and hematuria expected for 24-48 hours, increase fluid intake, avoid strenuous activity for 48 hours
Post-urethroplasty care  -  Foley catheter remains 2-3 weeks; educate on catheter care, leg bag use, and signs of infection; pericatheter leakage is common and not alarming
Monitor for post-operative complications  -  wound infection, urethrocutaneous fistula, recurrence of stricture (repeat uroflowmetry at 3, 6, and 12 months post-surgery)
Educate on proper urethral catheter insertion technique to prevent iatrogenic stricture  -  adequate lubrication, appropriate catheter size, never force past resistance
Assess sexual function  -  stricture and its treatment may affect erectile function and ejaculation; provide supportive counseling
Coordinate follow-up uroflowmetry and RUG at scheduled intervals to detect early recurrence
Educate patients with lichen sclerosus on long-term topical steroid application and regular urologic follow-up

phimosis-np.title: Fimosis
phimosis-np.pathophysiology:
Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin (prepuce) over the glans penis. It is classified as physiologic (normal developmental  -  present in 96% of newborns, resolving by age 3-5 through progressive keratinization and intermittent erections that naturally separate preputial adhesions) or pathologic (acquired fibrotic narrowing from scarring or inflammation).

Physiologic phimosis results from natural epithelial adhesions between the inner prepuce and glans. During fetal development, the prepuce and glans are fused as a single epithelial sheet. Postnatal desquamation produces smegma (collections of shed epithelial cells and sebaceous secretions) that gradually separates the preputial space. By age 17, only 1% of males retain physiologic phimosis.

Pathologic phimosis develops from chronic inflammation and fibrosis of the preputial tissue. The most important cause is lichen sclerosus (balanitis xerotica obliterans  -  BXO), an autoimmune chronic inflammatory dermatosis. At the molecular level, BXO involves T-lymphocyte (predominantly CD4+ and CD8+) infiltration of the dermal-epidermal junction, targeting basal keratinocytes through perforin/granzyme-mediated cytotoxicity and Fas/FasL apoptotic signaling. This leads to vacuolar degeneration of the basal layer, progressive basement membrane thickening (sclerosis), and upper dermal hyalinization with loss of elastic fibers.

Autoimmune mechanisms in BXO include molecular mimicry (cross-reactive antibodies between streptococcal or spirochetal antigens and genital skin), autoantibodies against extracellular matrix protein 1 (ECM1  -  found in 67-80% of BXO patients), and HLA class II associations (HLA-DQ7 and HLA-DQ8). The resulting progressive fibrosis converts the normally elastic preputial tissue into a rigid, inelastic ring that cannot retract over the glans.

Recurrent forceful retraction attempts or recurrent balanitis/posthitis (prepuce infection) cause repeated epithelial injury and healing, each cycle depositing additional collagen through TGF-β-driven fibroblast activation  -  the same pathway involved in urethral stricture formation. This creates a cicatricial (scar) phimosis with a characteristic white fibrotic band at the tip of the prepuce.
diagnostics: Perform clinical examination  -  attempt gentle retraction of foreskin to assess degree of narrowing, identify fibrotic band, and inspect glans and preputial mucosa for BXO changes (white sclerotic plaques, fissuring, hemorrhagic patches)
Classify phimosis severity using Kikiros classification (Grade 0  -  full retraction to Grade 5  -  no retraction possible; or Meuli classification)

Biopsy foreskin tissue if BXO suspected or if squamous cell carcinoma risk (chronic BXO has 4-8% lifetime SCC risk)  -  histology shows lichenoid infiltrate, basal layer vacuolization, homogenized collagen in upper dermis
Order urinalysis and urine culture to evaluate for concurrent UTI (phimosis causes urinary stasis under the prepuce)
Order HbA1c or fasting glucose in adult-onset phimosis  -  new phimosis in adults should prompt diabetes screening (recurrent candidal balanitis is often the presenting sign of undiagnosed diabetes)
Assess uroflowmetry if obstructive voiding symptoms present  -  pinpoint phimosis can cause significant functional obstruction with ballooning of the prepuce during voiding
Order STI screening if sexually active (phimosis increases STI acquisition risk  -  retained preputial microenvironment harbors pathogens)
Photograph preputial appearance for serial monitoring of topical corticosteroid treatment response
management: Prescribe topical betamethasone 0.05% cream applied to the tight preputial band BID for 4-6 weeks  -  first-line treatment for phimosis in children and adults; success rate 65-90%
Prescribe topical clobetasol 0.05% cream for BXO-associated phimosis  -  higher potency needed for autoimmune fibrotic disease; apply BID for 6-8 weeks, then taper to maintenance (2-3 times weekly)
Instruct on gentle retraction exercises during topical steroid application  -  progressive stretching of the preputial ring while corticosteroid reduces inflammation and collagen deposition
Refer for preputioplasty (dorsal slit or triple-incision technique) when topical therapy fails  -  foreskin-preserving surgical options that widen the preputial ring without complete circumcision
Refer for circumcision as definitive treatment for refractory phimosis, BXO-associated phimosis, or patient preference  -  complete removal of fibrotic tissue eliminates recurrence risk
Treat concurrent balanitis  -  prescribe clotrimazole 1% cream BID for 2 weeks for candidal infection; fluconazole 150 mg single dose for recurrent episodes
Screen for and manage diabetes in adult-onset phimosis with recurrent candidal balanitis  -  glycemic optimization reduces infection recurrence
Prescribe topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment as second-line for BXO refractory to topical corticosteroids  -  calcineurin inhibitor suppresses T-cell activation without steroid-induced atrophy risk

Educate parents on physiologic phimosis  -  reassure that inability to retract foreskin in children <5 years is normal; never forcibly retract (creates scarring and converts physiologic to pathologic)
nursingActions: Educate parents on normal preputial development  -  physiologic phimosis resolves naturally in >95% of boys by puberty through progressive keratinization and intermittent erections

Teach proper topical steroid application technique  -  apply thin layer to the narrowed preputial ring (not the entire foreskin or glans) with gentle retraction stretches during application, BID for prescribed duration
Monitor for corticosteroid side effects  -  skin thinning, telangiectasia, striae at application site (uncommon with short-term use at recommended potency)

Educate on hygiene  -  once foreskin is retractable, daily gentle retraction and warm water cleansing (no soap under foreskin  -  disrupts normal flora and pH); replace foreskin to anatomic position after cleaning

Assess for paraphimosis during clinical visits  -  if foreskin is retracted for examination, always return it to anatomic position (trapped retracted foreskin causes venous congestion and glans ischemia  -  urologic emergency)
Post-circumcision care  -  apply petroleum jelly gauze to wound, keep diaper area clean, mild bleeding and swelling expected for 48-72 hours, report excessive bleeding, purulent discharge, or fever
Monitor BXO patients for disease progression  -  regular genital skin examination for extension of sclerotic plaques to glans, meatus, and urethra (may require meatoplasty or urethroplasty)
Screen for depression and sexual health concerns in adult phimosis patients  -  phimosis causes dyspareunia, difficulty with condom use, and body image distress
Educate on paraphimosis emergency recognition  -  swollen, painful glans with retracted, constricting foreskin ring  -  seek immediate medical attention (manual reduction or dorsal slit required)
Assess adherence to topical steroid regimen  -  treatment success depends on consistent application with stretching exercises for the full 4-8 week course

delayed-puberty-rpn.title: Gecikmiş Ergenlik
delayed-puberty-rpn.pathophysiology: Delayed puberty occurs when secondary sexual characteristics fail to develop at the expected age. In girls, this means no breast development by age 13 or no menarche by age 15. In boys, it means no testicular enlargement by age 14. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis normally activates during puberty, triggering the release of sex hormones that drive physical development. When this axis fails to activate on schedule or the gonads do not respond appropriately, puberty is delayed. Constitutional delay (a normal variant where puberty starts late but progresses normally) is the most common cause.
diagnostics: Expect bone age X-ray to compare skeletal maturity with chronological age
Monitor growth charts for height velocity decline
Expect referral to endocrinology for hormone level evaluation
Monitor Tanner staging progression at each visit
Expect thyroid function tests to rule out hypothyroidism
management: Support emotional well-being and body image concerns
Encourage adequate nutrition and caloric intake
Administer hormone therapy as prescribed if ordered by provider
Monitor growth and development milestones at each visit
Ensure follow-up appointments with endocrinology are scheduled
nursingActions: Assess Tanner stage and document findings accurately
Monitor height and weight at each visit and plot on growth chart
Report absence of expected developmental milestones to provider
Provide age-appropriate education about puberty and development
Offer emotional support and reassurance to patient and family
Educate family that constitutional delay is the most common cause and is self-resolving
Report signs of nutritional deficiency or eating disorder behaviors

precocious-puberty-rpn.title: Erken Ergenlik
precocious-puberty-rpn.pathophysiology: Precocious puberty occurs when secondary sexual characteristics develop before age 8 in girls or before age 9 in boys. The hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis activates prematurely, causing early production of sex hormones. This leads to early breast development, pubic hair growth, and menarche in girls, or testicular enlargement and pubic hair in boys. Early exposure to sex hormones accelerates bone maturation, which initially causes a growth spurt but leads to early epiphyseal closure and ultimately shorter adult height. The condition may be central (brain-mediated) or peripheral (from ovarian, testicular, or adrenal sources).
diagnostics: Expect bone age X-ray (will show advanced skeletal maturity)
Monitor Tanner staging progression carefully
Expect referral to pediatric endocrinology
Monitor growth velocity (accelerated growth rate)
Expect brain MRI if central precocious puberty is suspected
management: Administer GnRH agonist injections as prescribed
Monitor for injection site reactions and medication side effects
Support the child's psychosocial adjustment
Ensure regular follow-up with endocrinology
Educate family on treatment goals and expected outcomes
nursingActions: Assess and document Tanner staging at each visit
Monitor height, weight, and growth velocity on growth charts
Administer GnRH agonist injections as ordered and monitor for side effects
Report progression of sexual development to provider
Provide age-appropriate education to the child about body changes
Assess for psychosocial distress, bullying, or behavioral changes
Educate parents about protecting the child from body-image issues
Report any headaches, visual changes, or neurological symptoms (may indicate CNS cause)

dysmenorrhea-rpn.title: Dismenore
dysmenorrhea-rpn.pathophysiology: Dysmenorrhea is painful menstruation caused by excessive uterine contractions. Primary dysmenorrhea occurs without underlying pelvic pathology and is caused by overproduction of prostaglandins (PGF2α and PGE2) from the shedding endometrium. These prostaglandins cause intense myometrial contractions that compress uterine blood vessels, leading to tissue ischemia and pain. Secondary dysmenorrhea is caused by an underlying condition such as endometriosis, adenomyosis, or uterine fibroids. Primary dysmenorrhea typically begins 1-2 years after menarche when ovulatory cycles are established, while secondary dysmenorrhea may develop later in life.
diagnostics: Expect thorough menstrual history and pain assessment
Monitor pain characteristics (timing, location, severity, duration)
Expect pelvic examination if secondary dysmenorrhea suspected
Monitor response to NSAID therapy
Expect pelvic ultrasound if secondary causes suspected
management: Administer NSAIDs as ordered  -  most effective when started before or at onset of menses
Apply heat to lower abdomen or lower back (heating pad, warm bath)
Encourage comfortable positioning (knee-to-chest position may help)
Administer hormonal contraceptives as prescribed if ordered
Encourage gentle exercise and stretching
nursingActions: Assess pain using a standardized pain scale (location, severity, timing)
Administer prescribed analgesics and monitor effectiveness
Apply non-pharmacological comfort measures (heat, positioning, relaxation)
Educate patient on timing of NSAID administration (start before menses onset for best effect)
Report severe pain unresponsive to standard treatment to provider
Report any new symptoms suggesting secondary causes (progressive worsening, intermenstrual pain, heavy bleeding)
Document menstrual cycle patterns and pain episodes
Educate on lifestyle modifications (regular exercise, adequate sleep, stress management)

abnormal-uterine-bleeding-rpn.title: Anormal Uterin Kanama
abnormal-uterine-bleeding-rpn.pathophysiology: Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) refers to any change in menstrual bleeding pattern that deviates from the normal cycle. This includes bleeding that is heavier than normal (menorrhagia), more frequent, prolonged, or occurs between periods (metrorrhagia). AUB can result from structural causes such as polyps, fibroids, or malignancy, or from non-structural causes such as hormonal imbalances (anovulation), coagulation disorders, or medication effects. Excessive blood loss can lead to iron-deficiency anemia, causing fatigue, pallor, and tachycardia. Monitoring bleeding patterns and recognizing signs of hemodynamic compromise are essential RPN responsibilities.
diagnostics: Expect complete blood count to assess for anemia
Monitor vital signs for signs of hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension)
Expect pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy-related causes
Monitor menstrual pad count and saturation
Expect thyroid function tests
Expect pelvic ultrasound if structural cause suspected
management: Monitor pad/tampon count and degree of saturation
Administer iron supplementation as ordered for anemia
Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed to regulate bleeding
Maintain accurate intake and output records
Prepare for diagnostic procedures as ordered
nursingActions: Assess and document bleeding patterns (frequency, duration, volume, clots)
Monitor pad count  -  report soaking more than one pad per hour
Monitor vital signs for signs of hypovolemia (tachycardia, hypotension, dizziness)
Assess for signs of iron-deficiency anemia (fatigue, pallor, shortness of breath, tachycardia)
Educate patient on tracking menstrual cycles and bleeding patterns
Administer iron supplements as ordered and educate on taking with vitamin C for absorption
Report heavy or prolonged bleeding to provider immediately
Provide emotional support for anxiety related to abnormal bleeding

pcos-rpn.title: Polikistik Over Sendromu (PCOS)
pcos-rpn.pathophysiology: Polycystic ovary syndrome is a common hormonal disorder in women of reproductive age characterized by excess androgen production and irregular ovulation. Insulin resistance plays a central role  -  elevated insulin levels stimulate the ovaries to produce excess male hormones (androgens). These excess androgens disrupt normal follicle development, preventing ovulation and causing multiple small, immature follicles to accumulate in the ovaries. The resulting hormonal imbalance leads to irregular periods, acne, excess hair growth (hirsutism), and weight gain. PCOS also increases the risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and endometrial cancer due to chronic anovulation.
diagnostics: Expect fasting blood glucose and insulin levels
Monitor for signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne)
Expect pelvic ultrasound showing multiple small follicles
Monitor blood pressure and lipid panel for metabolic risks
Monitor menstrual cycle regularity
Expect HbA1c for diabetes screening
management: Encourage lifestyle modifications (balanced diet, regular exercise)
Administer metformin as ordered for insulin resistance
Administer oral contraceptives as prescribed for cycle regulation
Monitor blood glucose levels as ordered
Support weight management goals (5-10% weight loss improves symptoms)
nursingActions: Assess for signs of hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, hair thinning)
Monitor blood glucose and report elevated levels to provider
Educate on lifestyle modifications for weight management and insulin resistance
Administer medications as prescribed and monitor for side effects
Monitor for metformin GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) and educate to take with food
Provide emotional support for body image concerns related to hirsutism and acne
Educate on the importance of regular menstrual cycle tracking
Report irregular or absent periods lasting more than 3 months to provider
Encourage adherence to follow-up appointments for metabolic screening

pid-rpn.title: Pelvik İnflamatuar Hastalık (PID)
pid-rpn.pathophysiology: Pelvic inflammatory disease is an infection of the upper female reproductive tract including the uterus, fallopian tubes, and ovaries. It is most commonly caused by sexually transmitted bacteria, particularly Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae, which ascend from the cervix and vagina into the upper genital tract. The infection causes inflammation and swelling of the fallopian tubes (salpingitis), uterine lining (endometritis), and surrounding tissues. If untreated, scar tissue and adhesions form in the fallopian tubes, increasing the risk of ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Tubo-ovarian abscess is a serious complication requiring urgent treatment.
diagnostics: Expect endocervical swabs for gonorrhea and chlamydia testing
Monitor temperature for fever
Monitor vital signs for sepsis indicators
Expect CBC (elevated WBC)
Expect pelvic ultrasound if abscess suspected
Expect pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered (full course must be completed)
Administer analgesics as prescribed for pain management
Position in semi-Fowler's position to promote pelvic drainage
Ensure sexual partners are notified and treated
Maintain bedrest during acute phase as ordered
nursingActions: Assess and document pain characteristics (location, severity, onset)
Monitor temperature and report fever above 38°C (100.4°F)
Administer antibiotics as prescribed and on schedule
Educate on completing the full antibiotic course even if feeling better
Report worsening abdominal pain, high fever, or signs of sepsis immediately
Educate on STI prevention including consistent condom use
Ensure partner notification and treatment to prevent reinfection
Educate on avoiding sexual intercourse until treatment is complete and provider clears
Assess for nausea and vomiting (may indicate worsening infection)
Report signs of peritonitis (rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, guarding)

vaginitis-rpn.title: Vajinit
vaginitis-rpn.pathophysiology: Vaginitis is inflammation of the vagina caused by infection, hormonal changes, or irritants. The three most common infectious types are bacterial vaginosis (BV), vulvovaginal candidiasis (yeast infection), and trichomoniasis. In a healthy vagina, Lactobacillus bacteria maintain an acidic pH (3.8-4.5) that prevents overgrowth of harmful organisms. When this balance is disrupted, pathogenic organisms overgrow. BV results from a shift in vaginal flora with overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria (primarily Gardnerella vaginalis). Candidal vaginitis results from overgrowth of Candida yeast, commonly after antibiotic use. Trichomoniasis is caused by the protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis and is sexually transmitted.
diagnostics: Expect vaginal swab for wet mount microscopy
Monitor vaginal pH (BV and trichomoniasis have elevated pH above 4.5)
Expect whiff test (fishy odor with KOH indicates BV)
Expect STI screening if trichomoniasis confirmed
Monitor for cervical inflammation on examination
management: Administer prescribed medications as ordered (specific to type of vaginitis)
Educate on proper perineal hygiene (front to back wiping)
Educate on avoiding douching and irritating products
Treat sexual partners if trichomoniasis diagnosed
Encourage wearing cotton underwear and loose-fitting clothing
nursingActions: Assess and document vaginal discharge characteristics (color, consistency, odor, amount)
Collect vaginal specimens as ordered using proper technique
Administer prescribed antifungal or antibiotic medications
Educate on completing the full course of treatment
Educate on proper perineal hygiene practices
Report persistent or worsening symptoms to provider
Educate patient to avoid douching, scented products, and tight clothing
Educate on STI prevention if trichomoniasis diagnosed
Assess for signs of secondary infection or treatment failure

bacterial-vaginosis-rpn.title: Bakteriyel Vaginoz
bacterial-vaginosis-rpn.pathophysiology: Bacterial vaginosis (BV) is the most common vaginal infection in women of reproductive age. It occurs when the protective Lactobacillus bacteria that normally dominate the vaginal environment are replaced by an overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria, primarily Gardnerella vaginalis and Atopobium vaginae. The healthy vagina maintains an acidic pH (3.8-4.5) through lactic acid produced by Lactobacillus. When these protective bacteria are depleted  -  by douching, new sexual partners, or other disruptions  -  anaerobic organisms proliferate, raising the vaginal pH above 4.5. These anaerobes produce amines that cause the characteristic fishy odor, especially when exposed to alkaline substances such as semen or potassium hydroxide (KOH). BV is not considered a sexually transmitted infection but is associated with sexual activity.
diagnostics: Expect vaginal swab for wet mount examination
Monitor vaginal pH (elevated above 4.5 in BV)
Expect positive whiff test (fishy odor when KOH added to discharge)
Expect microscopy showing clue cells (epithelial cells covered with bacteria)
Expect Nugent score or Amsel criteria application by provider
management: Administer metronidazole (oral or vaginal) as prescribed
Educate patient on strict alcohol avoidance during metronidazole therapy
Reinforce completion of full course of antibiotics
Educate on avoidance of douching and scented vaginal products
Encourage cotton underwear and breathable clothing
nursingActions: Assess and document vaginal discharge characteristics (thin, grayish-white, fishy odor)
Collect vaginal specimens using proper aseptic technique
Administer prescribed antibiotics on schedule
Educate on NO alcohol during metronidazole treatment and for 48 hours after completion
Educate on proper perineal hygiene (front-to-back wiping)
Report recurrent symptoms or treatment failure to provider
Educate patient that BV is not an STI but is associated with sexual activity
Assess for signs of concurrent STI and report findings

cervicitis-rpn.title: Servisit
cervicitis-rpn.pathophysiology: Cervicitis is inflammation of the cervix, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted infections such as Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. The cervix is lined with columnar epithelial cells that are particularly vulnerable to bacterial invasion. When pathogens infect the cervical mucosa, they trigger an inflammatory response that produces mucopurulent discharge (yellow or green mucus with pus). If left untreated, the infection can ascend from the cervix into the uterus and fallopian tubes, leading to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), which carries risks of infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain. Cervicitis can also be caused by non-infectious irritants such as chemical douches or latex allergies, though infectious causes are most common.
diagnostics: Expect endocervical swab for nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for chlamydia and gonorrhea
Monitor for mucopurulent cervical discharge on examination
Expect wet mount microscopy
Expect pregnancy test
Monitor temperature for signs of ascending infection
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics as ordered
Ensure sexual partners are notified and treated
Educate on abstaining from sexual intercourse until treatment is complete for patient and partner
Reinforce completion of full antibiotic course
Monitor for signs of ascending infection (PID)
nursingActions: Assess and document discharge characteristics (mucopurulent, yellow-green)
Collect cervical specimens for STI testing using proper technique
Administer prescribed antibiotics on schedule
Educate on completing full course of antibiotics even if symptoms improve
Educate on importance of partner notification and treatment
Educate on abstaining from intercourse until treatment is completed by both patient and partner
Report signs of ascending infection (increasing pelvic pain, fever, peritoneal signs) to provider immediately
Provide emotional support and non-judgmental care
Educate on STI prevention (consistent condom use, reducing number of partners)

vulvodynia-rpn.title: Vulvodini
vulvodynia-rpn.pathophysiology: Vulvodynia is a chronic vulvar pain condition lasting at least 3 months without an identifiable cause such as infection, dermatological condition, or malignancy. The pain is thought to result from an increase in nerve fiber density and sensitivity in the vulvar tissue, combined with heightened inflammatory responses. Nerve fibers in the affected tissue become hypersensitive, causing pain signals from stimuli that would not normally be painful (allodynia) or exaggerated pain from mildly painful stimuli (hyperalgesia). The condition may be provoked (triggered by touch or pressure, such as tampon insertion or intercourse) or unprovoked (constant burning pain without a trigger). Pelvic floor muscle dysfunction often coexists, with chronic muscle tension contributing to pain.
diagnostics: Expect cotton swab test (Q-tip test) to map areas of tenderness
Monitor pain characteristics using standardized pain scales
Expect cultures to rule out infectious causes
Expect examination to rule out dermatological conditions
Expect referral to gynecology or vulvar pain specialist
management: Apply comfort measures (cool compresses, sitz baths with plain water)
Educate on wearing cotton underwear and avoiding tight clothing
Educate on avoiding vulvar irritants (scented soaps, douches, fabric softeners)
Administer prescribed topical or oral medications as ordered
Support referral to pelvic floor physiotherapy
nursingActions: Assess pain using standardized pain tools (location, quality, intensity, triggers)
Document pain patterns and any aggravating or alleviating factors
Provide emotional support  -  chronic pain conditions significantly affect quality of life
Educate on vulvar care (gentle cleansing with water only, pat dry, cotton underwear)
Educate on avoidance of irritants (scented products, tight clothing, prolonged sitting)
Encourage use of lubricants during intercourse if applicable
Report increasing pain severity or failure to respond to treatment to provider
Support adherence to pelvic floor physiotherapy if prescribed
Assess for anxiety and depression and report findings to provider

bartholinitis-rpn.title: Bartolinit
bartholinitis-rpn.pathophysiology: Bartholinitis is inflammation of the Bartholin glands, two small pea-sized glands located on either side of the vaginal opening at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions. These glands produce mucus that lubricates the vaginal vestibule. When the gland duct becomes blocked, secretions accumulate and form a painless cyst. If the cyst becomes infected  -  often by a mix of bacteria including E. coli, anaerobes, or STI organisms  -  it develops into a painful abscess. The abscess fills with pus, causing significant swelling, redness, and tenderness of the affected labium. The patient may have difficulty sitting, walking, or engaging in intercourse due to the swelling and pain.
diagnostics: Expect physical examination showing unilateral labial swelling
Monitor for signs of abscess formation (increasing pain, warmth, fluctuance)
Expect wound culture if abscess is drained
Monitor temperature for fever indicating infection
Expect STI screening if abscess is present
management: Apply warm compresses or sitz baths 3-4 times daily for cyst
Administer analgesics as prescribed for pain management
Prepare patient for incision and drainage (I&D) if abscess present
Provide post-procedure wound care after I&D or Word catheter placement
Administer antibiotics as ordered if cellulitis or systemic infection present
nursingActions: Assess labial swelling (size, location, tenderness, warmth, fluctuance)
Monitor vital signs and temperature for signs of systemic infection
Apply warm compresses or assist with sitz baths for comfort and drainage promotion
Provide post-I&D wound care as ordered (keep area clean, monitor drainage)
Educate on sitz baths 3-4 times daily after I&D procedure
Monitor Word catheter placement if present (should remain in place 4-6 weeks)
Report signs of worsening infection (increasing pain, fever, spreading redness) to provider
Administer prescribed analgesics and antibiotics on schedule
Educate on perineal hygiene to prevent recurrence

uterine-prolapse-rpn.title: Uterin Prolaps
uterine-prolapse-rpn.pathophysiology: Uterine prolapse occurs when the uterus descends from its normal position into or beyond the vaginal canal due to weakening of the pelvic floor support structures. The pelvic floor consists of muscles (primarily the levator ani group) and connective tissue (endopelvic fascia) that hold the uterus, bladder, and rectum in place. When these structures weaken  -  from childbirth trauma, aging, chronic straining, or loss of estrogen after menopause  -  the uterus drops downward. Prolapse may be mild (the cervix descends into the upper vagina) or severe (the uterus protrudes outside the vaginal opening). Associated conditions include cystocele (bladder bulging into the vagina) and rectocele (rectum bulging into the vagina).
diagnostics: Expect pelvic examination to assess degree of prolapse
Monitor for associated cystocele or rectocele
Expect assessment using POP-Q staging system by provider
Monitor urinary function (incontinence, retention)
Expect referral to urogynecology if surgical intervention considered
management: Teach and encourage pelvic floor exercises (Kegel exercises)
Assist with pessary insertion and care if prescribed
Encourage weight management and avoidance of heavy lifting
Educate on managing constipation to avoid straining
Prepare for surgical intervention if conservative measures fail
nursingActions: Assess for symptoms of prolapse (vaginal bulging, heaviness, pressure)
Teach Kegel exercises (contract pelvic floor muscles for 5-10 seconds, relax, repeat 10-15 times, 3 sets daily)
Assist with pessary fitting and educate on care (regular removal, cleaning, and reinsertion)
Monitor for pessary complications (vaginal erosion, discharge, discomfort)
Educate on avoiding heavy lifting, straining, and prolonged standing
Encourage high-fiber diet and adequate fluids to prevent constipation
Report worsening prolapse symptoms or new urinary/bowel symptoms to provider
Assess for urinary incontinence or difficulty voiding and report findings
Provide emotional support for body image and quality-of-life concerns

benign-ovarian-cysts-rpn.title: İyi Huylu Over Kistleri
benign-ovarian-cysts-rpn.pathophysiology: Benign ovarian cysts are fluid-filled sacs that develop on or within the ovary. The most common types are functional cysts, which arise from the normal ovulatory process. A follicular cyst forms when a follicle fails to release its egg (ovulate) and continues to grow, filling with fluid. A corpus luteum cyst forms when the corpus luteum (the structure remaining after ovulation) fails to regress and fills with fluid or blood. These functional cysts are usually small, asymptomatic, and resolve spontaneously within 1-3 menstrual cycles. Other benign cyst types include dermoid cysts (mature teratomas containing tissue such as hair, teeth, and fat) and endometriomas (blood-filled cysts from endometriosis). Complications include cyst rupture (causing acute pelvic pain and possible internal bleeding) and ovarian torsion (twisting of the ovary on its blood supply, a surgical emergency).
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound to determine cyst size, type, and characteristics
Monitor vital signs if rupture or torsion suspected
Expect pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy
Expect CBC if hemorrhage suspected
Monitor pain characteristics and severity
management: Monitor small functional cysts expectantly (watchful waiting)
Administer analgesics as prescribed for pain management
Educate patient on signs of complications requiring emergency care
Prepare for surgical intervention if cyst is large, persistent, or symptomatic
Administer oral contraceptives if prescribed to prevent new cyst formation
nursingActions: Assess and document pain characteristics (location, severity, onset, duration)
Monitor vital signs for signs of hemorrhage if rupture suspected (tachycardia, hypotension)
Report sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain to provider immediately (may indicate torsion or rupture)
Educate patient on warning signs requiring emergency care (sudden severe pain, dizziness, fainting)
Administer prescribed analgesics and monitor effectiveness
Educate on the benign and self-resolving nature of most functional cysts
Prepare patient for pelvic ultrasound as ordered
Monitor for peritoneal signs (rebound tenderness, guarding) if rupture suspected
Provide reassurance and emotional support

endometrial-polyp-rpn.title: Endometrial Polipler
endometrial-polyp-rpn.pathophysiology: Endometrial polyps are localized overgrowths of the endometrial lining (the inner lining of the uterus) that project into the uterine cavity. They are composed of endometrial glands, stroma, and blood vessels. Polyps develop when areas of the endometrium respond excessively to estrogen stimulation, causing focal overgrowth. They can range in size from a few millimeters to several centimeters and may be single or multiple. Most endometrial polyps are benign, but they commonly cause abnormal uterine bleeding  -  including intermenstrual bleeding, heavy menstrual bleeding, and postmenopausal bleeding. The risk of malignant transformation is low (approximately 1-3%) but increases in postmenopausal women, those on tamoxifen therapy, and those with larger polyps.
diagnostics: Expect transvaginal ultrasound to visualize endometrial thickening
Expect saline infusion sonography (SIS) for better polyp visualization
Expect hysteroscopy for direct visualization and possible removal
Expect endometrial biopsy to rule out malignancy
Monitor menstrual pad count and bleeding patterns
management: Monitor bleeding patterns and pad count
Prepare patient for hysteroscopy and polypectomy if indicated
Provide pre-procedure and post-procedure education and care
Administer prescribed medications for bleeding management
Monitor for signs of heavy bleeding or hemorrhage
nursingActions: Assess and document abnormal bleeding patterns (timing, amount, frequency, clots)
Monitor pad count  -  track number and saturation of pads used
Educate patient on tracking bleeding patterns and reporting changes
Prepare patient for transvaginal ultrasound or hysteroscopy procedures
Provide post-hysteroscopy care (monitor for bleeding, cramping, signs of infection)
Educate on post-procedure expectations (mild cramping and spotting are normal)
Report heavy post-procedure bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour) to provider
Monitor for signs of anemia if chronic bleeding (fatigue, pallor, tachycardia)
Administer iron supplements as ordered for anemia

endometriosis-rpn.title: Endometriozis
endometriosis-rpn.pathophysiology: Endometriosis occurs when tissue similar to the endometrial lining of the uterus grows outside the uterus in abnormal locations such as the ovaries, fallopian tubes, pelvic peritoneum, and bowel. This misplaced tissue responds to the same hormonal changes of the menstrual cycle - it thickens, breaks down, and bleeds each month. However, because the tissue is located outside the uterus, the blood and debris have no way to exit the body. This leads to chronic inflammation, pain, scar tissue formation (adhesions), and the development of blood-filled cysts on the ovaries called endometriomas (chocolate cysts). The condition is estrogen-dependent, meaning it is fueled by estrogen and tends to improve after menopause when estrogen levels decline.
diagnostics: Expect thorough pain assessment and menstrual history documentation
Monitor pain characteristics (cyclic pelvic pain worsening with menses)
Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for endometriomas
Expect referral to gynecology for further evaluation
Expect CA-125 blood test (may be elevated but not diagnostic)
Expect laparoscopy for definitive diagnosis
management: Administer prescribed analgesics (NSAIDs) for pain management
Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed (oral contraceptives, progestins)
Apply heat to lower abdomen for comfort during pain episodes
Encourage comfortable positioning during pain episodes
Prepare patient for surgical procedures if scheduled (laparoscopy)
Ensure follow-up appointments are scheduled with gynecology
nursingActions: Assess pain using a standardized pain scale - document location, severity, timing, and relationship to menstrual cycle
Monitor for cyclic pelvic pain that worsens with menses
Administer prescribed medications and monitor effectiveness
Apply non-pharmacological comfort measures (heat application, positioning, relaxation techniques)
Educate patient on the importance of taking NSAIDs at onset of pain or before menses for best effect
Report acute severe pelvic pain to provider immediately (may indicate ruptured endometrioma or torsion)
Provide emotional support - endometriosis is a chronic condition that can affect quality of life and fertility
Educate on the relationship between menstrual cycle and symptom patterns
Report dyspareunia (painful intercourse) findings to provider

infertility-rpn.title: Kısırlık
infertility-rpn.pathophysiology: Infertility is defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse (or 6 months if the woman is over age 35). Conception requires a complex sequence of events - ovulation of a healthy egg, transport of sperm through the cervix and uterus, fertilization in the fallopian tube, and implantation in the uterine lining. Infertility can result from problems at any step in this process. Female factors include ovulatory dysfunction (such as PCOS or hypothalamic amenorrhea), tubal damage (from PID or endometriosis), and uterine abnormalities. Male factors include problems with sperm production, motility, or delivery. In many couples, both partners contribute to the difficulty conceiving. Infertility is a deeply emotional experience and requires compassionate nursing support.
diagnostics: Expect basal body temperature charting to assess ovulation patterns
Expect semen analysis for male partner evaluation
Expect hormone level testing (FSH, LH, estradiol, progesterone)
Monitor ovulation predictor kit results
Expect hysterosalpingogram (HSG) to assess tubal patency
Expect transvaginal ultrasound for follicle monitoring
management: Provide emotional support throughout the evaluation and treatment process
Administer fertility medications as prescribed and on correct timing
Assist with specimen collection procedures (semen analysis)
Educate on proper timing of intercourse relative to ovulation
Ensure follow-up appointments for monitoring are scheduled
Prepare patient for procedures (HSG, IUI) as indicated
nursingActions: Assess emotional well-being at every visit - infertility causes significant grief and stress
Administer fertility medications as ordered and educate on proper timing and technique
Monitor for side effects of fertility medications (bloating, mood changes, pelvic pain)
Report signs of ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (severe bloating, rapid weight gain, difficulty breathing, decreased urine output) to provider immediately
Assist with semen specimen collection and ensure proper handling (warm, to lab within 30-60 minutes)
Educate on basal body temperature tracking and ovulation signs
Provide privacy and dignity during all procedures and discussions
Report medication side effects to provider promptly
Connect patients with support groups and counseling resources

galactorrhea-rpn.title: Galaktore
galactorrhea-rpn.pathophysiology: Galactorrhea is the spontaneous flow of milk or milky nipple discharge unrelated to breastfeeding or childbirth. It results from elevated levels of prolactin (hyperprolactinemia), a hormone produced by the anterior pituitary gland. Prolactin is normally kept in check by dopamine, which inhibits its release. When dopamine's inhibitory effect is disrupted - by pituitary tumors (prolactinomas), certain medications (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, some antidepressants), or hypothyroidism - prolactin levels rise and stimulate breast tissue to produce milk.
