Galactorrhea can occur in women or men, though it is more common in women. It is usually benign but requires investigation to identify and treat the underlying cause.
diagnostics: Expect prolactin blood level measurement
Expect thyroid function tests (TSH, T4) to rule out hypothyroidism
Expect comprehensive medication history review
Expect pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy
Expect MRI of the pituitary if prolactin is significantly elevated
Monitor and document nipple discharge characteristics (color, consistency, bilateral vs unilateral)
management: Document discharge characteristics accurately (color, amount, bilateral vs unilateral, spontaneous vs expressed)
Assist with medication history review to identify causative drugs
Administer dopamine agonist therapy as prescribed if ordered
Provide emotional support - nipple discharge can be distressing
Educate patient to avoid nipple stimulation as this can worsen discharge
nursingActions: Assess and document nipple discharge (color, consistency, amount, unilateral vs bilateral, spontaneous vs expressed)
Review current medication list thoroughly - many medications can cause hyperprolactinemia
Monitor for signs and symptoms of hypothyroidism (fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation)
Educate patient to avoid excessive nipple stimulation or manipulation
Provide emotional support - galactorrhea can cause significant embarrassment and anxiety
Report any visual changes (visual field deficits, headaches) to provider immediately - may indicate pituitary mass effect
Educate on the importance of follow-up for prolactin monitoring
Report medication side effects from dopamine agonist therapy (nausea, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension)

breast-cancer-rpn.title: Meme Kanseri
breast-cancer-rpn.pathophysiology: Breast cancer develops when cells in the breast tissue grow uncontrollably and form a malignant tumor. Most breast cancers begin in the cells lining the milk ducts (ductal carcinoma) or the lobules that produce milk (lobular carcinoma). These abnormal cells accumulate genetic mutations that allow them to divide without normal regulatory control, invade surrounding tissues, and potentially spread (metastasize) to lymph nodes and distant organs. Breast cancer can be classified by its receptor status - whether the cancer cells have receptors for estrogen (ER), progesterone (PR), or the HER2 protein - which determines treatment options. Early detection through screening and self-examination significantly improves outcomes.
diagnostics: Expect clinical breast examination findings documentation
Expect diagnostic mammography and/or breast ultrasound
Expect breast biopsy for tissue diagnosis
Monitor for lymph node enlargement (axillary)
Expect staging studies if cancer confirmed (CT, bone scan, MRI)
Monitor surgical drain output post-operatively
management: Provide pre-operative teaching for surgical procedures (lumpectomy, mastectomy)
Monitor surgical site and drain output post-operatively
Administer prescribed medications (pain management, antiemetics during chemotherapy)
Perform arm measurements to monitor for lymphedema development
Provide wound care and drain management education
Ensure follow-up appointments are scheduled
nursingActions: Teach and reinforce breast self-examination technique (monthly, 7-10 days after menses)
Educate on recognizing abnormal breast changes (new lump, skin dimpling, peau d'orange, nipple retraction, bloody nipple discharge)
Monitor post-surgical incision site for signs of infection (redness, swelling, warmth, drainage, fever)
Measure and record surgical drain output (Jackson-Pratt drain) and report excessive drainage
Assess for signs of lymphedema in the affected arm (swelling, heaviness, tightness)
Educate patient to avoid blood pressure measurements, venipuncture, and IV lines in the affected arm
Provide emotional support throughout diagnosis, treatment, and recovery
Report any new lumps, skin changes, or lymph node enlargement to provider
Monitor for chemotherapy side effects (nausea, neutropenia, fatigue, mucositis) and report findings

urethritis-rpn.title: Üretrit
urethritis-rpn.pathophysiology: Urethritis is inflammation of the urethra, most commonly caused by sexually transmitted pathogens. The two main types are gonococcal urethritis (caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae) and non-gonococcal urethritis (most often caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, but also Mycoplasma genitalium and Ureaplasma urealyticum). The bacteria infect the urethral mucosal epithelium, triggering an inflammatory response that causes mucosal swelling, discharge production, and pain with urination. Urethritis is more symptomatic in men (with visible urethral discharge and dysuria) than in women, where it may be asymptomatic or mimic a urinary tract infection. If untreated, the infection can ascend and cause more serious complications such as epididymitis in men or pelvic inflammatory disease in women.
diagnostics: Expect nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for Chlamydia and Gonorrhea
Expect urethral swab or first-void urine specimen collection
Monitor for urethral discharge characteristics (color, consistency)
Expect urinalysis to rule out UTI
Expect testing for other STIs (HIV, syphilis, hepatitis B)
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics as ordered - full course must be completed
Ensure sexual partner notification and treatment
Educate on abstaining from sexual intercourse until treatment is completed and symptoms resolve
Provide STI prevention education (condom use, reducing number of partners)
Schedule test-of-cure follow-up as indicated
nursingActions: Assess and document symptoms (dysuria, urethral discharge, frequency, urgency)
Collect specimens properly - first-void urine or urethral swab as ordered
Administer antibiotics as prescribed and educate on completing full course
Educate on the importance of partner notification and treatment to prevent reinfection
Report persistent symptoms after treatment to provider (may indicate resistant organism or reinfection)
Provide non-judgmental STI education and prevention counseling
Educate on proper condom use to prevent future infections
Document discharge characteristics (color, amount, odor)
Report any signs of ascending infection (fever, scrotal pain, pelvic pain) to provider immediately

urethral-stricture-rpn.title: Üretral Darlık
urethral-stricture-rpn.pathophysiology: A urethral stricture is a narrowing of the urethra caused by scar tissue formation (fibrosis) within or around the urethral wall. This scar tissue develops in response to injury, infection, or chronic inflammation. Common causes include prior catheterization, urethral instrumentation (cystoscopy, surgery), sexually transmitted infections (particularly gonorrhea), and traumatic injury to the perineum or pelvis. The scar tissue reduces the diameter of the urethral lumen, creating resistance to urine flow. This leads to difficulty urinating, weak stream, straining, incomplete bladder emptying, and potentially urinary retention. Strictures occur almost exclusively in males due to the longer urethral length and are most common in the bulbar urethra.
diagnostics: Expect voiding history assessment (stream strength, straining, hesitancy)
Expect uroflowmetry (urine flow rate measurement)
Expect retrograde urethrogram for stricture visualization
Monitor post-void residual volume (bladder scan)
Expect referral to urology for evaluation
Monitor intake and output carefully
management: Monitor urinary output and voiding patterns closely
Assist with urethral dilation procedures if scheduled
Provide catheter care if indwelling catheter is placed
Administer prescribed medications (analgesics, antibiotics)
Monitor for urinary retention signs and report
Ensure follow-up with urology is scheduled
nursingActions: Assess voiding pattern - document stream strength, hesitancy, straining, and completeness of emptying
Monitor for signs of urinary retention (distended bladder, suprapubic discomfort, dribbling, inability to void)
Report inability to void to provider immediately - may require catheterization
Monitor intake and output accurately
Provide post-procedure care after urethral dilation (monitor for bleeding, pain, urinary retention)
Provide catheter care if indwelling or suprapubic catheter is in place
Educate patient on signs of urinary retention to watch for at home
Report hematuria (blood in urine) after procedures to provider
Educate on adequate fluid intake to maintain urine flow

phimosis-rpn.title: Fimosis
phimosis-rpn.pathophysiology: Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin (prepuce) over the glans penis. It can be physiologic or pathologic. Physiologic phimosis is normal in infants and young children - the foreskin is naturally adherent to the glans at birth and gradually becomes retractable over the first several years of life. By age 3, most boys have some degree of retractability, and by puberty, the foreskin should fully retract. Pathologic phimosis occurs when scar tissue or chronic inflammation (such as balanitis xerotica obliterans/lichen sclerosus) causes the foreskin opening to narrow and become non-retractable even in older children or adults. Complications include difficulty with hygiene, recurrent infections (balanitis), pain during erection, and in severe cases, urinary obstruction. Paraphimosis is a related emergency where a retracted foreskin becomes trapped behind the glans and cannot be returned to its normal position, causing vascular compromise.
diagnostics: Expect physical examination to assess degree of foreskin retraction
Monitor for signs of infection (redness, swelling, discharge)
Expect assessment of urinary stream (ballooning of foreskin during voiding suggests significant phimosis)
Monitor response to topical steroid therapy
Expect referral to urology if conservative management fails
management: Apply topical corticosteroid cream as prescribed to the phimotic ring
Educate on gentle foreskin retraction exercises as directed by provider
Provide hygiene education for proper foreskin care
Prepare patient for circumcision or preputioplasty if indicated
Provide post-surgical wound care after circumcision
nursingActions: Assess degree of foreskin retractability and document findings
Apply topical steroid cream as prescribed and educate patient/parent on proper application technique
Educate on gentle foreskin retraction - NEVER force the foreskin back (can cause tearing and scarring)
Provide hygiene education - gentle cleaning under the foreskin when retractable
Monitor for signs of balanitis (redness, swelling, discharge under foreskin)
Report paraphimosis immediately - this is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY (retracted foreskin trapped behind glans causing swelling)
Provide post-circumcision wound care (petroleum jelly gauze, monitor for bleeding, pain management)
Educate parents that physiologic phimosis in young boys is NORMAL and does not require intervention
Report ballooning of the foreskin during urination or difficulty urinating to provider

priapism-rpn.title: Priapizm
priapism-rpn.pathophysiology: Priapism is a prolonged, persistent penile erection lasting more than 4 hours that is unrelated to sexual stimulation or desire. It occurs when blood becomes trapped in the erectile tissue (corpora cavernosa) of the penis and cannot drain properly. The most common type is ischemic (low-flow) priapism, where venous outflow is blocked, trapping deoxygenated blood in the corpora cavernosa. Without blood flow, the tissue becomes ischemic (oxygen-deprived), and if untreated beyond 4-6 hours, permanent damage to the erectile tissue can occur, leading to fibrosis and erectile dysfunction. Priapism is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY. Common causes include sickle cell disease (the most common cause in children), medications such as PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil), intracavernosal injections, antipsychotics, and blood disorders.
diagnostics: Expect focused history (duration of erection, associated medications, sickle cell status)
Monitor vital signs including pain assessment
Expect blood gas analysis of aspirated corporal blood
Expect complete blood count (CBC) if sickle cell or blood disorder suspected
Expect urology consultation urgently
management: This is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - notify provider immediately upon recognition
Apply ice packs to the perineum as a temporizing measure
Monitor vital signs and pain level continuously
Prepare for potential corporal aspiration and irrigation by urology
Administer prescribed analgesics for pain management
If sickle cell related - prepare for IV hydration, oxygen therapy, and pain management
nursingActions: Recognize priapism as a MEDICAL EMERGENCY - erection lasting more than 4 hours requires immediate intervention
Notify provider and urology immediately upon identification
Assess and document duration of erection, pain level, and any identifiable cause
Review medication history thoroughly (PDE5 inhibitors, antipsychotics, trazodone)
Apply ice packs to perineum as directed
Monitor vital signs including pain assessment using pain scale
If sickle cell patient - initiate IV access, administer IV fluids and oxygen as ordered
Provide privacy and emotional support - this is an embarrassing and frightening condition
Monitor post-procedure for recurrence and ability to void
Report any signs of worsening ischemia to provider

balanitis-rpn.title: Balanit
balanitis-rpn.pathophysiology: Balanitis is inflammation of the glans penis (the head of the penis). When inflammation extends to the foreskin (prepuce), it is called balanoposthitis. The most common cause is poor hygiene combined with an intact foreskin, which creates a warm, moist environment under the prepuce where organisms can thrive. Candida albicans (yeast) is the most common infectious cause, especially in patients with diabetes mellitus. Bacterial infections, irritant contact dermatitis (from soaps, detergents, or condoms), and skin conditions can also cause balanitis. The inflammation leads to redness, swelling, tenderness, and discharge of the glans and foreskin. Recurrent balanitis can lead to scarring of the foreskin, causing or worsening phimosis.
diagnostics: Expect physical examination of the glans and foreskin
Monitor for discharge characteristics (white cheesy discharge suggests Candida)
Expect wound/discharge swab for culture if bacterial cause suspected
Expect blood glucose testing to screen for diabetes
Expect referral to urology or dermatology if recurrent
management: Provide hygiene education - gentle daily cleaning with warm water, avoid harsh soaps
Apply topical antifungal or antibiotic cream as prescribed
Administer prescribed oral medications if topical therapy insufficient
Educate on keeping the area clean and dry
Manage underlying conditions (blood glucose control for diabetic patients)
nursingActions: Assess and document glans appearance (redness, swelling, discharge, lesions, odor)
Apply prescribed topical medications (antifungal or antibacterial cream) as directed
Educate on proper hygiene - retract foreskin gently, clean with warm water, dry thoroughly, and replace foreskin
Educate to avoid irritating soaps, bubble baths, and harsh detergents in the genital area
Monitor blood glucose if diabetic - uncontrolled diabetes predisposes to recurrent yeast infections
Report worsening symptoms (increasing redness, swelling, inability to retract foreskin) to provider
Report development of phimosis (inability to retract foreskin) to provider
Educate on wearing loose-fitting cotton underwear to reduce moisture
Provide emotional support - genital conditions can cause embarrassment

penile-cancer-rpn.title: Penis Kanseri
penile-cancer-rpn.pathophysiology: Penile cancer is a rare malignancy that most commonly presents as squamous cell carcinoma arising from the skin of the penis. The cancer typically develops on the glans penis or the inner surface of the foreskin (prepuce). It begins as abnormal cells that grow uncontrollably, forming a visible lesion that may appear as a painless lump, ulcer, or wart-like growth. The cancer can be locally destructive, invading deeper penile tissues, and can spread to regional lymph nodes in the groin (inguinal lymph nodes) and eventually to distant sites. Human papillomavirus (HPV) types 16 and 18 are strongly associated with penile cancer development. Other risk factors include phimosis (non-retractable foreskin), poor hygiene, chronic inflammation, and smoking.
diagnostics: Expect thorough physical examination of the penile lesion
Expect biopsy of suspicious lesions for tissue diagnosis
Monitor for inguinal lymph node enlargement
Expect imaging studies for staging (CT, MRI) if cancer confirmed
Expect referral to urology-oncology
management: Provide wound care for penile lesions as directed
Prepare patient for biopsy or surgical procedures as indicated
Provide post-surgical care (wound care, drain management, pain control)
Provide emotional support throughout diagnosis and treatment
Educate on the importance of HPV vaccination for prevention
nursingActions: Assess and document penile lesion characteristics (size, location, color, borders, drainage, odor)
Report any new or changing penile lesions to provider - especially persistent painless lesions
Assess inguinal lymph nodes for enlargement and report findings
Provide wound care to surgical sites as directed
Monitor post-surgical drain output and wound healing
Provide emotional support - diagnosis of penile cancer causes profound anxiety, fear, and body image concerns
Educate on HPV vaccination as a prevention strategy (ideally given before sexual debut)
Report signs of infection at wound sites (redness, swelling, warmth, purulent drainage, fever)
Assess for pain and administer analgesics as prescribed
Support body image and sexual health concerns with empathy and appropriate referrals

cryptorchidism-rpn.title: Kryptorkidizm
cryptorchidism-rpn.pathophysiology: Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is a condition where one or both testes fail to descend from the abdomen into the scrotum during fetal development. During normal development, the testes form in the abdomen and gradually descend through the inguinal canal into the scrotum, typically completing this journey by the time of birth or within the first few months of life. The descent is guided by a structure called the gubernaculum and driven by hormones including testosterone and INSL3. When this process fails, the testis remains in the abdomen, inguinal canal, or high scrotal position. An undescended testis is exposed to higher body temperature than normal (the scrotum is 2-3 degrees cooler than body temperature), which damages the developing germ cells over time and increases the risk of infertility and testicular cancer later in life. Surgical correction (orchiopexy) is recommended between 6-12 months of age.
diagnostics: Expect physical examination of scrotum and inguinal area at birth and well-baby visits
Monitor for palpable vs non-palpable testis position
Expect referral to pediatric urology if testis not descended by 6 months
Expect ultrasound or MRI if testis is non-palpable on examination
Monitor contralateral (opposite) testis for normal position
management: Monitor testicular position at each well-baby visit
Educate parents on the condition and the importance of timely surgical correction
Prepare infant and family for orchiopexy procedure when indicated
Provide post-operative care after orchiopexy
Ensure follow-up for monitoring testicular position and growth
nursingActions: Assess scrotal contents during newborn examination and at each well-baby visit
Document testicular position (scrotal, inguinal, non-palpable) accurately
Educate parents that cryptorchidism is common, especially in premature infants
Explain that spontaneous descent may occur in the first 3-6 months but surgical correction is needed if it does not
Prepare family for orchiopexy procedure - explain it is a same-day surgery performed between 6-12 months of age
Provide post-orchiopexy care (pain management, wound care, activity restrictions)
Monitor surgical site for bleeding, swelling, and signs of infection
Report if the testis appears to have re-ascended after surgery (retractile vs re-ascent)
Educate parents on the importance of long-term follow-up for testicular growth and cancer screening

orchitis-rpn.title: Orşit
orchitis-rpn.pathophysiology: Orchitis is inflammation of the testis, most commonly caused by viral or bacterial infection. Viral orchitis is most often associated with the mumps virus - mumps orchitis typically develops 4-8 days after parotid gland swelling and affects post-pubertal males. Bacterial orchitis usually occurs as an extension of epididymitis (epididymo-orchitis) caused by urinary tract pathogens or sexually transmitted organisms. The infection and resulting inflammation cause testicular swelling, pain, warmth, and erythema. Inflammation can damage the seminiferous tubules where sperm are produced, potentially leading to testicular atrophy and infertility. It is critical to differentiate orchitis from testicular torsion - torsion is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY where the spermatic cord twists, cutting off blood supply to the testis. Torsion presents with sudden onset severe pain, whereas orchitis typically has a more gradual onset with fever.
diagnostics: Expect testicular examination (swelling, tenderness, warmth)
Expect Doppler ultrasound to assess blood flow (differentiates orchitis from torsion)
Monitor temperature for fever
Expect urinalysis and urine culture
Expect STI testing if sexually transmitted cause suspected
Monitor for Prehn sign (elevation of the scrotum relieves pain in orchitis but not torsion)
management: Provide scrotal support (scrotal elevation with a rolled towel or athletic supporter)
Apply ice packs to the scrotum (wrapped in cloth - never directly on skin)
Administer prescribed analgesics for pain management
Administer antibiotics as ordered if bacterial cause identified
Encourage bed rest during acute phase
Monitor for signs of testicular torsion and report immediately
nursingActions: Assess and document testicular pain (onset, severity, unilateral vs bilateral, associated symptoms)
CRITICAL: Differentiate from testicular torsion - sudden onset severe unilateral pain with nausea/vomiting and absent cremasteric reflex is torsion until proven otherwise - report IMMEDIATELY
Apply scrotal support and elevation for comfort
Apply ice packs wrapped in cloth to scrotum as directed
Administer prescribed medications (antibiotics, analgesics) and monitor effectiveness
Monitor temperature and report fever to provider
Educate on the importance of completing the full antibiotic course
Assess for scrotal swelling progression and report worsening
Provide emotional support - testicular conditions can be distressing
Educate on mumps vaccination (MMR) as prevention for viral orchitis

prostatitis-rpn.title: Prostatit
prostatitis-rpn.pathophysiology: Prostatitis is inflammation of the prostate gland, a walnut-sized organ located below the bladder that surrounds the urethra in males. The condition can be bacterial (acute or chronic) or non-bacterial (chronic pelvic pain syndrome). Acute bacterial prostatitis is caused by bacteria ascending from the urethra or bladder into the prostate gland, triggering an intense inflammatory response with swelling, pain, and systemic infection (fever, chills). The inflamed prostate swells and compresses the urethra, causing urinary symptoms such as dysuria, frequency, urgency, and potentially urinary retention. Chronic bacterial prostatitis involves recurrent or persistent bacterial infection of the prostate. Chronic pelvic pain syndrome (the most common form) involves prostatic pain and urinary symptoms without identifiable bacterial infection.
diagnostics: Expect thorough symptom assessment (dysuria, frequency, urgency, perineal pain)
Monitor temperature for fever (especially in acute bacterial prostatitis)
Expect urinalysis and urine culture
Expect digital rectal examination by provider (prostate will be tender and boggy in acute infection)
Expect prostatic secretion culture in chronic cases
Monitor for urinary retention
management: Administer prescribed antibiotics as ordered - full course must be completed (4-6 weeks for acute bacterial)
Administer prescribed analgesics and anti-inflammatory medications
Encourage sitz baths for perineal comfort (warm water, 15-20 minutes, 2-3 times daily)
Encourage adequate hydration to promote urinary flushing
Monitor for urinary retention and report inability to void
nursingActions: Assess urinary symptoms (dysuria, frequency, urgency, hesitancy, incomplete emptying)
Monitor temperature and report fever to provider
Administer antibiotics as prescribed and on schedule - educate on completing the FULL course (often 4-6 weeks)
Educate on sitz bath technique for perineal pain relief (warm water, 15-20 minutes)
Monitor for urinary retention (inability to void, suprapubic distension) and report to provider immediately
Encourage adequate fluid intake (2-3 liters per day unless contraindicated)
Assess pain and administer prescribed analgesics
Educate to avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods which can worsen symptoms
Report signs of worsening infection (increasing fever, chills, urinary retention) to provider
Provide emotional support - chronic prostatitis significantly impacts quality of life

endometriosis.title: Endometriozis
endometriosis.pathophysiology: Endometrial tissue grows outside the uterus, most commonly on the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and pelvic peritoneum. This ectopic tissue responds to cyclic hormonal stimulation (estrogen and progesterone), undergoing proliferation, secretion, and bleeding with each menstrual cycle. Because there is no outlet for the trapped blood, it causes chronic inflammation, adhesion formation, and fibrosis. Repeated cycles of bleeding and scarring lead to pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and potential infertility due to tubal distortion and ovarian damage.
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for endometriomas
Monitor pain patterns in relation to menstrual cycle
Expect laparoscopy for definitive diagnosis and staging
Monitor CA-125 levels if ordered (may be elevated)
Expect MRI for deep infiltrating endometriosis
management: Administer NSAIDs as ordered for pain management
Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed (oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists)
Prepare patient for laparoscopic excision or ablation if ordered
Apply heat to lower abdomen or back for comfort
Educate on importance of follow-up and symptom tracking
nursingActions: Assess pain characteristics using a standardized pain scale
Monitor for medication side effects (GnRH agonists cause menopausal symptoms)
Educate patient on relationship between estrogen and symptom flares
Provide emotional support for chronic pain and fertility concerns
Report uncontrolled pain or new symptoms to provider
Document menstrual cycle patterns and pain episodes

polycystic-ovary-syndrome.title: Polikistik Over Sendromu (PCOS)
polycystic-ovary-syndrome.pathophysiology: PCOS involves a complex hormonal imbalance characterized by excess androgen production from the ovaries and adrenal glands. Insulin resistance leads to hyperinsulinemia, which stimulates ovarian theca cells to produce excess androgens. Elevated androgens disrupt the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, impairing follicular development and preventing ovulation. Multiple immature follicles accumulate in the ovaries (polycystic appearance). Chronic anovulation leads to unopposed estrogen exposure on the endometrium, increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer.
diagnostics: Expect serum testosterone and DHEA-S levels (elevated androgens)
Monitor fasting glucose and insulin levels for insulin resistance
Expect pelvic ultrasound showing multiple small peripheral follicles
Monitor lipid panel for metabolic syndrome
Expect LH-to-FSH ratio assessment
Monitor for signs of endometrial hyperplasia
management: Encourage weight loss through diet and exercise (5-10% reduction improves symptoms)
Administer metformin as ordered for insulin resistance
Administer combined oral contraceptives as prescribed to regulate cycles
Administer spironolactone as ordered for hirsutism
Educate on long-term metabolic and cardiovascular risks
nursingActions: Assess for signs of hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism, alopecia)
Monitor blood glucose and HbA1c as ordered
Educate on lifestyle modifications for weight management
Provide emotional support for body image concerns
Report irregular bleeding patterns or signs of endometrial hyperplasia
Monitor for metformin GI side effects and educate to take with food

ovarian-cysts.title: Over Kistleri
ovarian-cysts.pathophysiology: Ovarian cysts are fluid-filled sacs that develop on or within the ovary. Functional cysts are the most common type and arise from normal ovulatory processes. Follicular cysts form when a mature follicle fails to rupture and release the ovum, continuing to enlarge. Corpus luteum cysts form when the corpus luteum fails to regress after ovulation and fills with fluid or blood. Cyst enlargement causes ovarian capsule distension, resulting in pelvic pain. Rupture causes sudden, sharp pain and potential intraperitoneal bleeding. Ovarian torsion occurs when a cyst causes the ovary to twist on its vascular pedicle, compromising blood supply and causing ischemic necrosis.
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound (transvaginal) to characterize cyst size and type
Monitor serial ultrasounds for cyst resolution or growth
Expect serum hCG to rule out ectopic pregnancy
Monitor CBC if rupture suspected (hemoglobin drop)
Expect tumor markers (CA-125) if malignancy concern in postmenopausal patients
management: Observe and monitor functional cysts (most resolve in 8-12 weeks)
Administer analgesics as ordered for pain management
Administer oral contraceptives as prescribed to prevent new cyst formation
Prepare for surgical intervention if torsion, rupture with hemodynamic instability, or persistent complex cyst
Maintain NPO status if surgical intervention anticipated
nursingActions: Assess pain location, intensity, and onset characteristics
Monitor vital signs for signs of hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension) if rupture suspected
Report sudden, severe unilateral pelvic pain immediately (may indicate torsion or rupture)
Educate patient on signs requiring emergency care
Monitor intake and output if surgery planned
Provide emotional support and education about condition

uterine-fibroids.title: Uterin Fibroidler (Leiomyomlar)
uterine-fibroids.pathophysiology: Uterine fibroids are benign smooth muscle tumors arising from the myometrium. They are estrogen and progesterone dependent, growing during reproductive years and shrinking after menopause. Fibroids cause symptoms based on their size and location: submucosal fibroids distort the endometrial cavity causing heavy menstrual bleeding, intramural fibroids enlarge the uterus causing pressure symptoms, and subserosal fibroids cause pelvic pressure on adjacent organs. Heavy menstrual bleeding results from increased endometrial surface area, disrupted uterine contractility, and impaired hemostasis. Chronic blood loss leads to iron-deficiency anemia.
diagnostics: Expect pelvic ultrasound to determine fibroid number, size, and location
Monitor CBC for iron-deficiency anemia from chronic blood loss
Expect MRI for surgical planning or complex cases
Monitor menstrual pattern changes and bleeding volume
Expect endometrial biopsy to rule out malignancy in abnormal bleeding
management: Administer iron supplementation as ordered for anemia
Administer GnRH agonists as prescribed to shrink fibroids preoperatively
Prepare patient for myomectomy or hysterectomy as indicated
Administer tranexamic acid as ordered for heavy menstrual bleeding
Educate on uterine artery embolization as a minimally invasive option
nursingActions: Assess menstrual bleeding patterns (number of pads/tampons, clot passage, duration)
Monitor hemoglobin and signs of anemia (fatigue, pallor, tachycardia)
Educate patient on iron supplementation (take with vitamin C, avoid dairy)
Report signs of acute hemorrhage or hemodynamic instability
Provide preoperative and postoperative education if surgery planned
Assess for urinary frequency or constipation from fibroid pressure effects

ovarian-cancer.title: Over Kanseri
ovarian-cancer.pathophysiology: Ovarian cancer arises from ovarian epithelial cells, germ cells, or stromal cells, with epithelial ovarian carcinoma being the most common and lethal form. It is often called the 'silent killer' because early symptoms are vague and nonspecific. The cancer spreads primarily through direct peritoneal seeding, leading to widespread intra-abdominal dissemination. Malignant cells produce excess fluid causing ascites. BRCA1 and BRCA2 gene mutations significantly increase lifetime risk. Ovarian cancer is frequently diagnosed at advanced stages (Stage III or IV) due to the lack of effective screening methods and the deep pelvic location of the ovaries.
diagnostics: Expect CA-125 level (often elevated but not diagnostic alone)
Expect pelvic and transvaginal ultrasound
Monitor CT or MRI for staging
Expect surgical staging with debulking for tissue diagnosis
Monitor for ascites development on imaging
management: Prepare patient for surgical debulking (cytoreductive surgery)
Administer chemotherapy as ordered (platinum-taxane regimen)
Manage ascites with paracentesis as ordered
Monitor nutritional status and support caloric intake
Coordinate referral to genetic counseling for BRCA testing
nursingActions: Monitor abdominal girth for ascites progression
Assess nutritional status and manage nausea from chemotherapy
Monitor for signs of bowel obstruction (nausea, vomiting, absent bowel sounds)
Provide emotional support for advanced-stage diagnosis
Report new or worsening abdominal distension
Educate on chemotherapy side effects and infection prevention
Assess pain and administer analgesics as ordered

pelvic-inflammatory-disease.title: Pelvik İnflamatuar Hastalık (PID)
pelvic-inflammatory-disease.pathophysiology: Pelvic inflammatory disease is an ascending infection from the lower genital tract (cervix and vagina) to the upper genital tract (uterus, fallopian tubes, ovaries). It is most commonly caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae and Chlamydia trachomatis, though polymicrobial infection is typical. Bacteria ascend through the cervical os, causing endometritis, salpingitis, and peritonitis. Inflammation damages the tubal epithelium, causing adhesion formation and tubal scarring. This scarring increases the risk of ectopic pregnancy, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Tubo-ovarian abscess is a serious complication that may require surgical drainage.
diagnostics: Expect endocervical swabs for gonorrhea and chlamydia testing
Monitor CBC (elevated WBC indicates infection)
Expect erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein (elevated)
Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate for tubo-ovarian abscess
Monitor temperature trends
Expect pregnancy test to rule out ectopic pregnancy
management: Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics as ordered (cover gonorrhea, chlamydia, and anaerobes)
Treat sexual partners to prevent reinfection
Position in semi-Fowler's to promote pelvic drainage
Administer analgesics as ordered for pain management
Hospitalize if tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, or failed outpatient therapy
nursingActions: Assess pain characteristics and pelvic tenderness
Monitor vital signs for fever trends
Administer antibiotics as ordered and on schedule
Educate on completing full antibiotic course even if symptoms improve
Report signs of peritonitis (rigid abdomen, rebound tenderness, high fever)
Educate on STI prevention including condom use
Ensure partner notification and treatment

benign-prostatic-hyperplasia.title: İyi Huylu Prostat Hiperplazisi (BPH)
benign-prostatic-hyperplasia.pathophysiology: BPH is a non-malignant enlargement of the prostate gland caused by hyperplasia of stromal and glandular cells in the transitional zone surrounding the prostatic urethra. Age-related changes in the androgen-estrogen balance, combined with the action of dihydrotestosterone (DHT), stimulate prostatic growth. The enlarged prostate compresses the urethra, increasing resistance to urinary outflow. This causes bladder outlet obstruction leading to urinary hesitancy, weak stream, incomplete emptying, and urinary retention. Chronic obstruction leads to detrusor muscle hypertrophy, trabeculation, and eventually bladder decompensation. Prolonged obstruction can cause hydronephrosis and renal impairment.
diagnostics: Expect prostate-specific antigen (PSA) to rule out malignancy
Monitor post-void residual volume by bladder scan
Expect urinalysis to rule out infection
Monitor renal function (BUN, creatinine) for obstructive nephropathy
Expect digital rectal exam findings (smooth, symmetrical enlargement)
Expect uroflowmetry to measure urinary flow rate
management: Administer alpha-adrenergic blockers as ordered (tamsulosin)
Administer 5-alpha reductase inhibitors as ordered (finasteride)
Educate on timed voiding and double voiding techniques
Avoid medications that worsen retention (anticholinergics, decongestants)
Prepare for transurethral resection of the prostate (TURP) if medical therapy fails
nursingActions: Assess urinary elimination patterns (frequency, urgency, nocturia, stream force)
Monitor post-void residual volumes as ordered
Report urinary retention or inability to void
Educate on medication timing (tamsulosin 30 minutes after same meal daily)
Advise to avoid caffeine and alcohol, especially before bedtime
Monitor for orthostatic hypotension with alpha-blocker therapy
Post-TURP: Monitor for hemorrhage and continuous bladder irrigation patency

testicular-cancer.title: Testis Kanseri
testicular-cancer.pathophysiology: Testicular cancer most commonly arises from germ cells, classified as seminomas or non-seminomas. Seminomas are the most common single type, growing slowly and being highly radiosensitive. Non-seminomas (embryonal carcinoma, yolk sac tumor, choriocarcinoma, teratoma) are more aggressive. Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) is the strongest risk factor, as the abnormal thermal environment impairs normal germ cell development and increases malignant transformation risk. Cancer presents as a painless, firm testicular mass. Tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) are elevated based on tumor type and aid in diagnosis, staging, and treatment monitoring. Testicular cancer has excellent cure rates even in advanced stages.
diagnostics: Expect testicular ultrasound for initial mass evaluation
Expect serum tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH)
Expect CT scan of abdomen and chest for staging
Monitor tumor markers post-orchiectomy for treatment response
Expect radical inguinal orchiectomy for tissue diagnosis (NOT transscrotal biopsy)
management: Prepare for radical inguinal orchiectomy (primary treatment)
Administer chemotherapy as ordered (BEP regimen: bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin)
Prepare for retroperitoneal lymph node dissection if indicated
Offer sperm banking before treatment
Educate on testicular self-examination for contralateral testis
nursingActions: Provide emotional support for body image and fertility concerns
Educate on sperm banking options before initiating treatment
Monitor for chemotherapy side effects (bleomycin pulmonary toxicity)
Assess for signs of metastasis (back pain, dyspnea, lymphadenopathy)
Report any respiratory changes during bleomycin therapy immediately
Teach monthly testicular self-examination of remaining testis
Provide post-orchiectomy wound care education

erectile-dysfunction.title: Erektil Disfonksiyon
erectile-dysfunction.pathophysiology: Erectile dysfunction (ED) is the inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual intercourse. Normal erection requires intact neural signaling (parasympathetic nervous system), adequate arterial blood flow, functional smooth muscle relaxation, and venous occlusion within the corpora cavernosa. Nitric oxide (NO) released from endothelial cells activates cyclic GMP, causing smooth muscle relaxation and increased penile blood flow. ED results from vascular disease (atherosclerosis reduces arterial inflow), neurogenic damage (diabetes, spinal cord injury), hormonal deficiency (low testosterone), or psychogenic factors. ED is often an early marker of systemic cardiovascular disease.
diagnostics: Expect cardiovascular risk assessment (lipid panel, fasting glucose, blood pressure)
Monitor testosterone levels
Expect medication review for contributory drugs
Monitor psychosocial assessment for depression and anxiety
Expect vascular studies if peripheral vascular disease suspected
management: Administer PDE5 inhibitors as ordered (first-line pharmacotherapy)
Encourage lifestyle modifications (exercise, smoking cessation, weight loss)
Adjust contributing medications in consultation with provider
Consider testosterone replacement if hypogonadism confirmed
Refer for psychosexual counseling if psychogenic component identified
nursingActions: Assess sexual health history in a nonjudgmental manner
Educate on PDE5 inhibitor use and contraindications
Screen for depression and relationship distress
Report ED in younger men as potential early cardiovascular disease marker
Educate on avoiding nitrate use with PDE5 inhibitors (life-threatening hypotension)
Assess for medication side effects contributing to ED

sexually-transmitted-infections.title: Cinsel Yolla Bulaşan Enfeksiyonlar (STIs)
sexually-transmitted-infections.pathophysiology: STIs encompass bacterial, viral, and parasitic infections transmitted through sexual contact. Chlamydia trachomatis is the most commonly reported bacterial STI, causing mucopurulent cervicitis and urethritis; untreated, it ascends to cause PID. Neisseria gonorrhoeae causes purulent urethritis and cervicitis with risk of disseminated gonococcal infection. Treponema pallidum (syphilis) progresses through stages: primary (chancre), secondary (widespread rash, condylomata lata), latent, and tertiary (gummas, neurosyphilis, cardiovascular syphilis). Herpes simplex virus (HSV-2) establishes latency in sacral ganglia with recurrent painful vesicular outbreaks. Many STIs are asymptomatic initially, facilitating transmission and delayed treatment.
diagnostics: Expect nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for chlamydia and gonorrhea
Monitor RPR or VDRL for syphilis screening, confirmed with FTA-ABS
Expect HIV testing with fourth-generation antigen/antibody assay
Monitor viral cultures or PCR for herpes simplex
Expect wet mount or NAAT for Trichomonas vaginalis
Monitor for co-infection with multiple STIs simultaneously
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered based on pathogen identified
Treat all sexual partners (expedited partner therapy when appropriate)
Educate on barrier methods (condoms) for prevention
Report notifiable diseases to public health as required
Administer recommended vaccinations (HPV, hepatitis B)
Screen for concurrent STIs when one is identified
nursingActions: Obtain specimens using proper collection technique before antibiotic administration
Educate on completing full antibiotic course
Ensure partner notification and treatment
Provide nonjudgmental counseling on safer sexual practices
Report notifiable STIs to public health authorities
Educate on abstaining from sexual contact until treatment is completed and symptoms resolve
Screen for intimate partner violence during STI evaluation

menopause.title: Menopoz
menopause.pathophysiology: Menopause is the permanent cessation of menstruation resulting from the loss of ovarian follicular activity. As follicular reserves deplete, the ovaries produce progressively less estrogen and progesterone. Declining inhibin B levels reduce negative feedback on the pituitary, causing elevated FSH and LH. Estrogen deficiency causes vasomotor instability (hot flashes, night sweats), urogenital atrophy (vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, urinary symptoms), and accelerated bone loss. Decreased estrogen also alters lipid metabolism, increasing LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular disease risk. Menopause is confirmed after 12 consecutive months of amenorrhea in a woman over 45 without other pathologic causes.
diagnostics: Expect elevated FSH level (confirms ovarian failure)
Monitor lipid panel for cardiovascular risk changes
Expect bone density scan (DEXA) for osteoporosis screening
Monitor thyroid function to rule out thyroid disease as cause of symptoms
Expect endometrial biopsy if postmenopausal bleeding occurs
management: Administer hormone replacement therapy (HRT) as prescribed for moderate to severe vasomotor symptoms
Educate on non-hormonal alternatives (SSRIs, gabapentin) for hot flashes
Encourage weight-bearing exercise for bone health
Recommend calcium and vitamin D supplementation as ordered
Educate on vaginal moisturizers and lubricants for urogenital atrophy
nursingActions: Assess vasomotor symptom frequency and severity
Educate on HRT benefits and risks (individualized risk assessment)
Monitor for abnormal vaginal bleeding on HRT (report immediately)
Encourage regular cardiovascular screening and bone density testing
Educate on lifestyle modifications (exercise, diet, stress management)
Provide emotional support for mood changes and sleep disturbances
Assess for depression and refer as appropriate

infertility.title: Kısırlık
infertility.pathophysiology: Infertility is defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of unprotected intercourse (or 6 months if the woman is over 35). Female factors include ovulatory dysfunction (anovulation from PCOS, hypothalamic dysfunction), tubal damage (PID, endometriosis causing adhesions), uterine abnormalities (fibroids, polyps), and cervical factors. Male factors include impaired spermatogenesis (varicocele, cryptorchidism, toxic exposures), obstructive azoospermia, and ejaculatory dysfunction. Hormonal imbalances affecting the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis in either partner disrupt gametogenesis. Age is a significant factor, as oocyte quality and quantity decline with advancing maternal age, particularly after age 35.
diagnostics: Expect semen analysis for male partner evaluation
Monitor basal body temperature charting for ovulation confirmation
Expect hormone panel (FSH, LH, estradiol, progesterone, thyroid, prolactin)
Expect hysterosalpingography (HSG) to assess tubal patency
Monitor ovulation with serial ultrasounds
Expect anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) for ovarian reserve assessment
management: Administer clomiphene citrate as ordered for ovulation induction
Prepare for intrauterine insemination (IUI) if indicated
Prepare for in vitro fertilization (IVF) if indicated
Address modifiable risk factors (weight, smoking, alcohol, stress)
Refer for surgical correction of anatomic abnormalities (varicocele repair, tubal surgery)
nursingActions: Provide emotional support and acknowledge grief and frustration
Educate on optimal timing of intercourse (fertility window)
Teach basal body temperature and ovulation predictor kit use
Monitor for ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) with fertility medications
Report signs of OHSS (rapid weight gain, abdominal distension, dyspnea)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care with reproductive endocrinology
Assess coping strategies and refer for counseling as needed

ectopic-pregnancy.title: Dış Gebelik
ectopic-pregnancy.pathophysiology: Ectopic pregnancy occurs when a fertilized ovum implants outside the uterine cavity, most commonly in the fallopian tube (tubal pregnancy). The extrauterine tissue cannot support normal placental development or fetal growth. As the embryo grows, it stretches the surrounding structure, eventually leading to rupture with potentially life-threatening hemorrhage. Risk factors include previous ectopic pregnancy, pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal surgery, endometriosis, IUD use, and assisted reproduction. Clinical presentation includes unilateral lower abdominal or pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding (often irregular), and amenorrhea followed by abnormal bleeding. Rupture produces sudden severe pain, signs of hypovolemic shock (tachycardia, hypotension, diaphoresis), referred shoulder pain from diaphragmatic irritation by blood in the peritoneal cavity, and peritoneal signs. A positive pregnancy test with no intrauterine gestational sac on ultrasound is a critical diagnostic clue. Serial beta-hCG levels that fail to double appropriately suggest ectopic pregnancy. This is a surgical/medical emergency requiring rapid intervention to prevent maternal death from hemorrhage.

delayed-puberty.title: Gecikmiş Ergenlik
delayed-puberty.pathophysiology: Delayed puberty is defined as the absence of breast development by age 13 in females or testicular enlargement by age 14 in males. The underlying pathophysiology centers on disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis. Normally, the hypothalamus begins pulsatile secretion of gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) at puberty, stimulating the anterior pituitary to release luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), which in turn stimulate gonadal sex steroid production (estrogen or testosterone). Delayed puberty is classified as hypogonadotropic hypogonadism (low LH/FSH due to hypothalamic or pituitary failure) or hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (elevated LH/FSH due to primary gonadal failure). Constitutional delay is the most common cause and represents a physiologic variant where the HPG axis activates later than average but functions normally. Pathological causes include Kallmann syndrome (defective GnRH neuron migration with anosmia), Turner syndrome (45,X karyotype causing ovarian dysgenesis), and Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY causing testicular dysfunction). Chronic illness, malnutrition, excessive exercise, and hypothalamic-pituitary tumors can also suppress GnRH pulsatility through disruption of kisspeptin signaling pathways.
diagnostics: Expect serum LH and FSH levels to differentiate hypogonadotropic from hypergonadotropic causes
Expect bone age radiograph (left hand and wrist) to assess skeletal maturity
Monitor growth velocity and plot on standardized growth charts
Expect karyotype analysis if gonadal dysgenesis suspected
Expect brain MRI if central cause suspected (hypothalamic or pituitary pathology)
Expect serum estradiol or testosterone levels to assess gonadal function
Monitor thyroid function tests and prolactin levels to rule out contributing endocrine disorders
management: Administer sex steroid replacement therapy as prescribed to induce secondary sexual characteristics
Coordinate with endocrinology for individualized hormone induction protocols
Administer calcium and vitamin D supplementation as ordered for bone health
Prepare for genetic counseling referral if chromosomal abnormality identified
Educate on expected timeline of pubertal changes with treatment
nursingActions: Assess Tanner staging at each visit to monitor pubertal progression
Monitor growth velocity and track on appropriate growth charts
Assess psychosocial impact including peer relationships, self-esteem, and body image
Provide emotional support and age-appropriate education about pubertal development
Report failure to progress on hormone therapy to provider
Educate family on importance of adherence to hormone replacement regimen
Screen for signs of depression or social withdrawal related to delayed development

precocious-puberty.title: Erken Ergenlik
precocious-puberty.pathophysiology: Precocious puberty is defined as the onset of secondary sexual characteristics before age 8 in females or age 9 in males. It is classified as central (GnRH-dependent) or peripheral (GnRH-independent). Central precocious puberty (CPP) results from premature activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, with early pulsatile GnRH secretion triggering LH and FSH release, which stimulates gonadal sex steroid production. The normal inhibitory mechanisms that keep the HPG axis quiescent during childhood - including gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) tone and makorin ring finger protein 3 (MKRN3) - fail prematurely. Kisspeptin neurons in the arcuate nucleus activate GnRH neurons earlier than expected. CPP is idiopathic in 80-90% of girls but often has an identifiable CNS cause in boys (hypothalamic hamartoma, CNS tumors, hydrocephalus). Peripheral precocious puberty (PPP) is GnRH-independent and results from autonomous sex steroid production from adrenal tumors, ovarian cysts, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, or McCune-Albright syndrome (activating GNAS1 mutation causing constitutive cAMP signaling). The key clinical consequence is accelerated linear growth with premature epiphyseal fusion, resulting in initial tall stature but ultimately compromised adult height.
diagnostics: Expect serum LH and FSH levels (elevated in central, suppressed in peripheral)
Expect GnRH stimulation test (pubertal LH response confirms central precocious puberty)
Expect bone age radiograph showing skeletal age advancement beyond chronological age
Expect brain MRI in all boys and girls under age 6 with central precocious puberty to exclude CNS lesions
Monitor serum estradiol or testosterone levels
Expect adrenal androgens (DHEA-S, 17-hydroxyprogesterone) if peripheral cause suspected
Expect pelvic ultrasound to evaluate ovarian and uterine maturation
management: Administer GnRH agonist therapy as prescribed for central precocious puberty to suppress the HPG axis
Coordinate with pediatric endocrinology for ongoing management
Monitor growth velocity and bone age progression during treatment
Prepare for surgical referral if CNS lesion or hormone-secreting tumor identified
Educate family on treatment goals: slowing pubertal progression to preserve adult height
nursingActions: Assess Tanner staging at each visit to monitor pubertal progression or suppression
Monitor height velocity and plot on growth charts
Administer GnRH agonist injections according to prescribed schedule
Assess for injection site reactions and rotate sites
Provide age-appropriate education to the child about body changes
Provide psychosocial support for emotional and behavioral challenges
Report breakthrough pubertal signs during treatment to provider
Educate parents on importance of treatment adherence for height preservation

dysmenorrhea.title: Dismenore
dysmenorrhea.pathophysiology: Dysmenorrhea is painful menstruation caused by excessive uterine contractions. Primary dysmenorrhea occurs in the absence of pelvic pathology and is driven by prostaglandin overproduction. During the luteal phase, declining progesterone triggers endometrial cell membrane phospholipid breakdown via phospholipase A2, releasing arachidonic acid. Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) converts arachidonic acid to prostaglandins, primarily prostaglandin F2-alpha (PGF2α) and prostaglandin E2 (PGE2). PGF2α is a potent myometrial stimulant that causes sustained uterine contractions exceeding 150-180 mmHg of intrauterine pressure. These intense contractions compress myometrial blood vessels, causing ischemia-reperfusion injury to the uterine muscle. The resulting tissue hypoxia activates pain receptors (nociceptors) and generates reactive oxygen species that amplify inflammation. PGE2 sensitizes peripheral nerve endings to pain, lowering the pain threshold. Vasopressin also contributes by enhancing myometrial contractility and vasoconstriction. Secondary dysmenorrhea results from identifiable pelvic pathology such as endometriosis, adenomyosis (endometrial glands invading the myometrium), uterine fibroids, or pelvic inflammatory disease. In adenomyosis, ectopic endometrial tissue within the myometrium responds to cyclic hormonal changes, causing localized prostaglandin release, muscle disruption, and chronic uterine enlargement with painful, heavy periods.
diagnostics: Expect thorough menstrual and pain history to distinguish primary from secondary dysmenorrhea
Expect pelvic examination to assess for masses, tenderness, or uterine enlargement
Expect pelvic ultrasound if secondary cause suspected (fibroids, adenomyosis, ovarian pathology)
Monitor response to NSAID therapy as a diagnostic indicator (primary dysmenorrhea typically responds well)
Expect laparoscopy if endometriosis suspected and imaging is inconclusive
Expect MRI for adenomyosis evaluation if ultrasound findings are equivocal
management: Administer NSAIDs as first-line treatment, initiated 1-2 days before expected menses and continued for 2-3 days
Administer hormonal contraceptives as prescribed (oral, patch, ring, or IUD) to reduce prostaglandin production
Apply continuous low-level topical heat (40°C) to the lower abdomen for pain relief
Encourage regular aerobic exercise to reduce prostaglandin levels and improve blood flow
Prepare for levonorgestrel IUD placement as ordered for secondary dysmenorrhea or NSAID-refractory cases
Educate on complementary therapies (TENS, omega-3 fatty acids, magnesium)
nursingActions: Assess pain using a standardized scale including location, quality, timing, and aggravating/relieving factors
Educate on proper NSAID timing (initiate before pain onset for maximal prostaglandin synthesis inhibition)
Monitor for NSAID side effects including GI irritation, renal effects, and bleeding risk
Assess for red flags suggesting secondary dysmenorrhea (progressive worsening, dyspareunia, abnormal bleeding patterns, new onset after age 25)
Report symptoms unresponsive to NSAIDs and hormonal therapy to provider for further workup
Educate on non-pharmacological comfort measures (heat, positioning, relaxation techniques)
Provide education differentiating normal menstrual discomfort from symptoms requiring evaluation

abnormal-uterine-bleeding.title: Anormal Uterin Kanama (AUB)
abnormal-uterine-bleeding.pathophysiology: Abnormal uterine bleeding encompasses any deviation from normal menstrual parameters in frequency, regularity, duration, or volume. The FIGO PALM-COEIN classification system organizes causes into structural (PALM) and non-structural (COEIN) categories. PALM represents Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma (fibroids), and Malignancy/hyperplasia - all identifiable structural lesions. COEIN represents Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial causes, Iatrogenic factors, and Not yet classified. Normal endometrial physiology depends on coordinated estrogen-driven proliferation, progesterone-mediated secretory transformation, and organized withdrawal bleeding when hormone levels decline. In ovulatory dysfunction (the most common non-structural cause), chronic anovulation produces sustained unopposed estrogen exposure without progesterone opposition. This causes disordered endometrial proliferation, fragile vasculature formation, and irregular shedding. The endometrium becomes thickened and unstable, breaking down irregularly and unpredictably. Without progesterone-induced stromal compaction and organized spiral arteriole vasoconstriction, hemostasis is impaired and bleeding is prolonged and heavy. In coagulopathies (von Willebrand disease being the most common inherited cause), defective platelet adhesion or clotting factor deficiency impairs normal hemostatic mechanisms at the endometrial level, causing heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche. Endometrial causes involve primary disorders of local hemostasis, including deficiencies in endothelin-1, prostaglandin F2α (vasoconstrictors), and excess tissue plasminogen activator (fibrinolytic), leading to heavy but regular bleeding.
diagnostics: Expect detailed menstrual history with pictorial blood assessment chart (PBAC) scoring
Expect pregnancy test to rule out pregnancy-related bleeding
Monitor CBC for iron-deficiency anemia (low hemoglobin, low MCV, low ferritin)
Expect thyroid function tests (TSH)
Expect coagulation studies (PT, PTT, von Willebrand panel) especially in adolescents with heavy menstrual bleeding from menarche
Expect transvaginal ultrasound to evaluate endometrial thickness and structural pathology
Expect endometrial biopsy in women over 45 or with risk factors for endometrial hyperplasia
Monitor serum ferritin to assess iron stores
management: Administer hormonal therapy as prescribed based on underlying cause (combined oral contraceptives, progestins, levonorgestrel IUD)
Administer tranexamic acid as ordered for acute heavy menstrual bleeding
Administer IV conjugated estrogen as ordered for acute severe uterine hemorrhage
Replace iron stores with oral iron supplementation and dietary counseling
Prepare for endometrial biopsy or hysteroscopy as indicated
Prepare for surgical management if medical therapy fails (endometrial ablation, hysterectomy)
Administer blood transfusion as ordered for hemodynamically significant anemia
nursingActions: Assess bleeding patterns including frequency, duration, volume (pad/tampon count), and clot passage
Monitor vital signs for hemodynamic instability in acute hemorrhage (tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes)
Monitor hemoglobin, hematocrit, and iron studies as ordered
Report signs of hemodynamic instability or bleeding not responsive to initial treatment
Educate on iron supplementation (take with vitamin C for absorption, expect dark stools, avoid with dairy and antacids)
Educate on hormonal therapy expected effects and potential side effects
Assess for signs of endometrial hyperplasia or malignancy (postmenopausal bleeding, persistent irregular bleeding in perimenopausal women)
Provide emotional support for the impact of chronic heavy bleeding on quality of life

vaginitis.title: Vajinit
vaginitis.pathophysiology: Vaginitis is inflammation of the vaginal mucosa resulting from infection, irritation, or hormonal changes. The healthy vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species, which ferment glycogen (stored in estrogen-stimulated vaginal epithelium) to produce lactic acid and hydrogen peroxide, maintaining vaginal pH at 3.8-4.5. This acidic environment suppresses pathogenic organisms. Bacterial vaginosis (BV) results from a shift in the vaginal microbiome from Lactobacillus dominance to an overgrowth of anaerobic bacteria, particularly Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, Prevotella, and Mobiluncus species. These organisms form a polymicrobial biofilm on the vaginal epithelium that is resistant to antibiotic penetration and produces amines (putrescine, cadaverine, trimethylamine) responsible for the characteristic fishy odor, especially when exposed to alkaline substances. The vaginal pH rises above 4.5 as Lactobacillus populations decline. Vulvovaginal candidiasis (VVC) results from overgrowth of Candida species, most commonly Candida albicans. Candida transitions from commensal yeast form to pathogenic hyphal form, penetrating the vaginal epithelium and triggering an intense inflammatory response mediated by neutrophils and pro-inflammatory cytokines. This produces the characteristic thick, white, cottage cheese-like discharge with intense pruritus. Trichomoniasis is caused by Trichomonas vaginalis, a flagellated protozoan that adheres to vaginal epithelial cells via surface adhesins, causing direct cytotoxic damage and triggering a robust neutrophilic inflammatory response. The intense inflammation produces a frothy, yellow-green, malodorous discharge and vaginal erythema.
diagnostics: Expect vaginal pH testing (greater than 4.5 in BV and trichomoniasis, normal 3.8-4.5 in candidiasis)
Expect wet mount microscopy (clue cells in BV, budding yeast and pseudohyphae in candidiasis, motile trichomonads in trichomoniasis)
Expect whiff test (positive in BV: fishy odor with KOH addition to vaginal discharge)
Expect KOH preparation to visualize fungal elements
Expect nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for Trichomonas if wet mount is negative
Monitor for concurrent STI co-infections (gonorrhea, chlamydia) with trichomoniasis
Expect Amsel criteria assessment for BV diagnosis (3 of 4: homogeneous discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells)
management: Administer metronidazole (oral or vaginal) as prescribed for BV and trichomoniasis
Administer fluconazole or topical azole as prescribed for vulvovaginal candidiasis
Treat sexual partners concurrently for trichomoniasis to prevent reinfection
Educate on avoiding douching and vaginal irritants
Educate on completing full course of prescribed antibiotics
Recommend cotton underwear and avoidance of tight-fitting synthetic garments
nursingActions: Obtain detailed history including discharge characteristics, odor, pruritus, sexual history, and recent antibiotic use
Collect vaginal specimens using proper technique for wet mount, pH testing, and cultures
Assess vulvar and vaginal mucosa for erythema, edema, and excoriation
Educate on medication administration technique for vaginal preparations
Educate on alcohol avoidance with metronidazole therapy (disulfiram-like reaction)
Report recurrent infections (4 or more VVC episodes per year or recurrent BV) to provider for long-term management
Provide STI prevention education including condom use for trichomoniasis
Educate on vaginal hygiene practices (avoid douching, scented products, and tight synthetic clothing)

bacterial-vaginosis.title: Bakteriyel Vaginoz
bacterial-vaginosis.pathophysiology: Bacterial vaginosis (BV) represents a polymicrobial dysbiosis of the vaginal ecosystem rather than a true infection. The healthy vaginal microbiome is dominated by Lactobacillus species (primarily L. crispatus, L. jensenii, and L. iners), which metabolize glycogen deposited in estrogen-stimulated squamous epithelial cells to produce lactic acid and hydrogen peroxide, maintaining an acidic pH of 3.8-4.5 that suppresses pathogenic organisms. In BV, a dramatic ecological shift occurs: Lactobacillus populations decline precipitously while anaerobic and facultative organisms proliferate, including Gardnerella vaginalis, Atopobium vaginae, Prevotella bivia, Mobiluncus species, Sneathia, and Megasphaera. Gardnerella vaginalis initiates the process by adhering to the vaginal epithelium via pili and producing sialidase and vaginolysin, which degrade the protective mucus layer and damage epithelial cells. This allows formation of a dense polymicrobial biofilm on the vaginal epithelial surface that is highly resistant to antibiotic penetration and host immune defenses. Atopobium vaginae synergizes with Gardnerella within the biofilm, contributing to recurrence rates exceeding 50% within 12 months. The anaerobic overgrowth produces volatile amines - putrescine, cadaverine, and trimethylamine - through decarboxylation of amino acids, generating the characteristic fishy odor that intensifies with alkaline exposure (semen, KOH). The vaginal pH rises above 4.5 as lactic acid production ceases. Clue cells form when Gardnerella and other bacteria heavily coat vaginal epithelial cells, obscuring their borders on microscopy. The shift from Lactobacillus dominance to a diverse anaerobic community represents a fundamental disruption of vaginal innate immunity.
diagnostics: Expect Amsel criteria assessment (3 of 4 required: homogeneous thin grayish-white discharge, vaginal pH >4.5, positive whiff test with KOH, clue cells on wet mount)
Expect Nugent scoring on Gram stain (score 7-10 confirms BV based on relative morphotype proportions)
Expect vaginal pH measurement using pH paper (>4.5 in BV)
Expect whiff test (addition of 10% KOH to vaginal secretions releases fishy amine odor)
Expect wet mount microscopy for clue cells (epithelial cells with stippled borders from adherent bacteria)
Monitor for concurrent STI screening as BV increases susceptibility to Chlamydia, gonorrhea, and HIV acquisition
management: Administer metronidazole 500 mg orally twice daily for 7 days as prescribed (first-line therapy)
Administer metronidazole 0.75% vaginal gel once daily for 5 days as alternative
Administer clindamycin 2% vaginal cream at bedtime for 7 days as alternative
Educate on completing full antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve
Educate on avoiding douching, scented vaginal products, and vaginal irritants
Educate on alcohol avoidance during metronidazole therapy and for 72 hours after completion
Discuss recurrence prevention strategies including vaginal probiotics (Lactobacillus-containing)
Screen and treat BV in pregnancy due to association with preterm birth and premature rupture of membranes
nursingActions: Obtain detailed history of discharge characteristics, odor timing, sexual practices, and douching habits
Collect vaginal specimens using proper swab technique for wet mount, pH testing, and Gram stain
Assess vaginal discharge for characteristic thin, homogeneous, grayish-white appearance coating vaginal walls
Educate on metronidazole disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol (nausea, vomiting, flushing, tachycardia)
Educate on vaginal clindamycin cream weakening latex condoms and diaphragms for 5 days after use
Report recurrent BV (3 or more episodes in 12 months) to provider for suppressive therapy consideration
Provide education on vaginal hygiene: avoid douching, use unscented soap externally only, wear cotton underwear
Educate pregnant patients on importance of BV treatment to reduce preterm birth risk

cervicitis.title: Servisit
cervicitis.pathophysiology: Cervicitis is inflammation of the uterine cervix, predominantly affecting the columnar epithelium of the endocervical canal. The cervix serves as a critical anatomical barrier between the lower and upper reproductive tract, and its disruption creates a pathway for ascending infection. The transformation zone - where columnar epithelium meets squamous epithelium - is particularly vulnerable to infection and inflammation. Infectious cervicitis is most commonly caused by Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Chlamydia trachomatis is an obligate intracellular pathogen that infects columnar epithelial cells through a unique biphasic developmental cycle: infectious elementary bodies (EBs) attach to epithelial cells via heparan sulfate proteoglycans and are internalized into membrane-bound inclusions. Within the inclusion, EBs differentiate into metabolically active reticulate bodies (RBs) that replicate by binary fission, eventually reorganizing back into EBs that are released to infect adjacent cells. This intracellular lifecycle allows Chlamydia to evade humoral immune responses while triggering a chronic inflammatory response mediated by Th1 cytokines (IFN-gamma, TNF-alpha, IL-1). Neisseria gonorrhoeae attaches to cervical epithelial cells via type IV pili and Opa proteins, invading through both transcellular and paracellular pathways. Gonococcal lipooligosaccharide (LOS) triggers intense neutrophilic inflammation, producing the characteristic mucopurulent discharge. The organism evades immune defenses through antigenic variation of its surface pili and Opa proteins and through IgA1 protease production. Chronic cervical inflammation damages the epithelial barrier, upregulates pro-inflammatory cytokines, and increases expression of HIV co-receptors (CCR5, CXCR4) on cervical immune cells, significantly increasing susceptibility to HIV acquisition. Untreated cervicitis allows ascending infection through the endocervical canal into the endometrium and fallopian tubes, leading to pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), tubal scarring, and potential infertility or ectopic pregnancy.
diagnostics: Expect nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae (gold standard, sensitivity >95%)
Expect endocervical swab for Gram stain showing >30 PMNs per high-power field
Expect speculum examination revealing mucopurulent endocervical discharge (yellow or green exudate visible at the os or on a white swab)
Monitor for friability (easily induced endocervical bleeding on swab contact)
Expect concurrent testing for Trichomonas vaginalis, Mycoplasma genitalium, and HSV if clinically indicated
Expect HIV testing and syphilis screening as part of comprehensive STI evaluation
Monitor for signs of ascending infection (PID): cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, fever
management: Administer empiric antibiotic therapy before NAAT results if mucopurulent cervicitis is present (CDC guidelines)
Administer ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose for gonococcal coverage as prescribed
Administer doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 7 days for chlamydial coverage as prescribed
Ensure expedited partner therapy (EPT) or partner referral for STI treatment
Educate on abstaining from sexual intercourse until both patient and partner complete treatment and are symptom-free
Educate on condom use for STI prevention
Schedule test-of-cure NAAT at 4 weeks for gonorrhea (due to increasing resistance) and at 3 months for reinfection screening
Evaluate for PID if cervical motion tenderness, adnexal tenderness, or fever is present
nursingActions: Obtain comprehensive sexual history including number of partners, condom use, prior STIs, and symptoms in partners
Perform or assist with speculum examination to visualize cervix for mucopurulent discharge and friability
Collect endocervical specimens using proper NAAT collection technique (endocervical swab or self-collected vaginal swab)
Administer IM ceftriaxone using Z-track technique in the dorsogluteal or ventrogluteal muscle
Educate on importance of completing full doxycycline course (7 days) even if symptoms resolve
Educate on doxycycline administration: take with food, avoid dairy/antacids within 2 hours, use sun protection
Coordinate partner notification and treatment through health department services if needed
Report positive gonorrhea and chlamydia results to public health department as required by law
Assess for signs of PID and report immediately: fever, lower abdominal pain, cervical motion tenderness

vulvodynia.title: Vulvodini
vulvodynia.pathophysiology: Vulvodynia is a chronic vulvar pain condition lasting at least 3 months without an identifiable infectious, inflammatory, neoplastic, or neurological cause. The pathophysiology involves complex neuroinflammatory mechanisms at both peripheral and central levels. At the peripheral level, histological studies of affected vestibular tissue reveal a significant increase in intraepithelial nerve fiber density (hyperinnervation), with proliferation of nociceptive C-fibers expressing increased transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 (TRPV1) receptors and nerve growth factor (NGF) receptors. These sensitized nerve endings have lowered activation thresholds, responding to normally innocuous stimuli (allodynia) and generating amplified responses to painful stimuli (hyperalgesia). Mast cell proliferation and degranulation in the vestibular submucosa release histamine, tryptase, heparinase, and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, NGF) that directly sensitize adjacent nerve fibers, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of neurogenic inflammation. The close anatomical relationship between mast cells and nerve fibers in the vestibule facilitates bidirectional communication: nociceptive fibers release substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP), which further activate mast cells, while mast cell mediators sensitize nerve endings. At the central level, persistent peripheral nociceptive input induces central sensitization in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord, with upregulation of N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors and increased excitability of second-order neurons. This central sensitization amplifies pain perception and can lead to referred pain in adjacent dermatomes. Pelvic floor muscle dysfunction (hypertonicity and myofascial trigger points in the levator ani and obturator internus muscles) frequently develops as a secondary response to chronic pain, creating additional nociceptive input and perpetuating the pain cycle. The condition is classified as either provoked (triggered by touch or pressure, most commonly localized to the vestibule as provoked vestibulodynia) or unprovoked (spontaneous, generalized vulvar pain), with provoked vestibulodynia being the most common subtype.
diagnostics: Expect cotton swab test (Q-tip test) to map areas of vulvar tenderness and allodynia, particularly at the vestibule (3-9 o'clock positions)
Expect thorough history differentiating provoked versus unprovoked pain, onset, duration, and associated factors
Expect exclusion of identifiable causes: cultures for candida, HSV testing, dermatologic evaluation for lichen sclerosus or lichen planus
Monitor pain using validated instruments (visual analog scale, vulvar pain functional questionnaire)
Expect pelvic floor muscle assessment for hypertonicity and trigger points
Expect psychosocial assessment for comorbid anxiety, depression, and sexual dysfunction impact
management: Educate on eliminating vulvar irritants: switch to cotton underwear, avoid synthetic fabrics, remove scented products
Recommend sitz baths with lukewarm water for comfort (avoid additives)
Apply topical lidocaine 5% ointment to vestibule 15-20 minutes before intercourse or as needed for pain relief
Refer for pelvic floor physical therapy with internal myofascial release and biofeedback
Administer low-dose tricyclic antidepressant (amitriptyline 10-25 mg nightly) as prescribed for neuropathic pain modulation
Administer anticonvulsant (gabapentin 300-3600 mg daily in divided doses) as prescribed for nerve pain
Recommend cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) for pain coping strategies and addressing psychosexual impact
Apply topical estrogen cream if vestibular atrophy or hormonal contribution is suspected
nursingActions: Conduct thorough vulvar pain assessment including location, quality, triggers, duration, and impact on daily activities and sexual function
Perform cotton swab mapping of the vestibule and vulva to identify specific areas of tenderness
Educate on pain self-management strategies: ice packs wrapped in cloth, sitz baths, topical lidocaine use
Provide emotional support and validate the chronic pain experience (many patients report feeling dismissed)
Educate on pelvic floor relaxation exercises (reverse Kegels, diaphragmatic breathing)
Report worsening pain, new symptoms, or signs of depression to provider
Educate on lubricant use during intercourse (water-based, unscented, free of glycerin and parabens)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care: pelvic floor PT, pain management, psychology, gynecology

bartholinitis.title: Bartolinit
bartholinitis.pathophysiology: The Bartholin glands (greater vestibular glands) are bilateral pea-sized glands located at the 4 and 8 o'clock positions of the vaginal vestibule, deep within the posterior labia majora. Each gland drains through a 2-2.5 cm duct that opens onto the inner surface of the labia minora at the junction of the posterior third and anterior two-thirds of the vestibular margin. The glands secrete mucoid fluid during sexual arousal, contributing to vaginal lubrication. Bartholinitis begins with obstruction of the excretory duct, most commonly due to inspissated mucus, trauma, or inflammation. When the duct becomes occluded, glandular secretions accumulate, forming a Bartholin cyst - a painless, fluctuant, unilateral labial swelling typically 1-3 cm in diameter. The cyst fluid is initially sterile. Secondary bacterial infection of the cyst transforms it into a Bartholin abscess. The infection is typically polymicrobial, involving a combination of vaginal flora (Bacteroides fragilis, Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella), enteric organisms (Escherichia coli), and occasionally sexually transmitted organisms (Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis).
