Bacterial colonization triggers a vigorous inflammatory response with neutrophil infiltration, liquefactive necrosis, and purulent material accumulation. The abscess cavity becomes walled off by a pyogenic membrane. Clinically, the abscess presents as a rapidly enlarging, exquisitely tender, erythematous, and warm unilateral labial mass with surrounding cellulitis. The intense inflammation causes significant edema that can impair ambulation and sitting. Systemic signs including fever and leukocytosis may develop with larger abscesses or surrounding tissue infection. The overlying skin may become thinned and develop a pointing area where spontaneous rupture may occur. Recurrence is common after simple incision and drainage because the duct epithelium re-obstructs, necessitating procedures that create a permanent new drainage tract (marsupialization) or maintain patency (Word catheter placement).
diagnostics: Expect physical examination revealing unilateral labial mass at 4 or 8 o'clock position (cyst: non-tender, fluctuant; abscess: tender, erythematous, warm)
Expect wound culture and sensitivity of abscess fluid after drainage to guide antibiotic therapy
Expect NAAT testing for Chlamydia and gonorrhea if STI is suspected
Monitor vital signs for systemic infection indicators (fever, tachycardia)
Monitor WBC count if systemic infection is suspected
Expect biopsy consideration in women over 40 to rule out Bartholin gland carcinoma (adenocarcinoma or squamous cell carcinoma)
management: Apply warm compresses or sitz baths 3-4 times daily for 10-15 minutes to promote spontaneous drainage of small cysts
Prepare patient for incision and drainage (I&D) with Word catheter placement for abscesses (catheter remains 4-6 weeks to create epithelialized tract)
Prepare patient for marsupialization if recurrent (surgical creation of permanent drainage opening)
Administer antibiotics as prescribed for surrounding cellulitis or systemic infection (typically broad-spectrum covering anaerobes and gram-negatives)
Administer analgesics as prescribed (NSAIDs, acetaminophen, or opioids for severe abscess pain)
Educate on post-procedure wound care: sitz baths, keeping Word catheter in place, signs of recurrence
nursingActions: Assess labial mass for size, location, tenderness, erythema, warmth, fluctuance, and presence of cellulitis
Assess pain level and impact on mobility, sitting, and activities of daily living
Monitor vital signs for fever indicating systemic spread of infection
Provide warm sitz baths as ordered to promote comfort and drainage
Assist with or prepare patient for I&D procedure under local anesthesia
Provide post-I&D wound care education: keep Word catheter in place for 4-6 weeks, continue sitz baths, no tampon use or intercourse until healed
Educate on signs requiring immediate return: increasing pain, fever, expanding erythema, inability to sit or walk
Report signs of systemic infection (high fever, rigors, expanding cellulitis) to provider immediately

uterine-prolapse.title: Uterin Prolapsus
uterine-prolapse.pathophysiology: Uterine prolapse is the descent of the uterus through the pelvic floor into or beyond the vaginal canal, resulting from failure of the pelvic support structures. The pelvic floor is a complex musculofascial unit comprising three levels of support described by DeLancey: Level I (cardinal and uterosacral ligaments suspending the upper vagina and cervix from the sacrum), Level II (paravaginal attachments connecting the mid-vagina laterally to the arcus tendineus fasciae pelvis), and Level III (perineal body and urogenital diaphragm supporting the lower vagina). The levator ani muscle group (pubococcygeus, puborectalis, and iliococcygeus) forms the muscular floor of the pelvis, maintaining a tonic contraction that closes the urogenital hiatus and supports pelvic organs. Uterine prolapse results from progressive deterioration of these support systems. At the molecular level, collagen and elastin - the primary structural proteins of pelvic connective tissue - undergo qualitative and quantitative degradation. Matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-1, MMP-2, MMP-9) are upregulated in prolapsed tissue, accelerating collagen degradation while tissue inhibitors of metalloproteinases (TIMPs) are downregulated. The ratio of collagen type I (strong, tensile) to collagen type III (thin, distensible) shifts toward type III predominance, weakening tissue integrity. Elastin fibers fragment and lose their recoil capacity. Vaginal childbirth is the most significant risk factor: the fetal head descending through the birth canal causes direct mechanical injury to the levator ani muscle (avulsion from the pubic bone in up to 15-30% of vaginal deliveries), stretching and tearing of endopelvic fascia, and pudendal nerve neuropathy from compression against the ischial spine. Denervation of the levator ani leads to muscle atrophy over time. Estrogen deficiency after menopause further accelerates connective tissue deterioration, as estrogen receptors in pelvic floor tissues normally stimulate collagen synthesis, fibroblast proliferation, and tissue vascularity. Chronic intra-abdominal pressure from obesity, chronic cough, constipation, or heavy lifting provides the ongoing mechanical force that drives progressive descent through already weakened support structures. The POP-Q (Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification) system classifies prolapse severity: Stage 0 (no descent), Stage I (most distal portion >1 cm above the hymen), Stage II (most distal portion within 1 cm of the hymen), Stage III (most distal portion >1 cm beyond the hymen but not complete eversion), Stage IV (complete vaginal vault eversion or procidentia). Uterine prolapse frequently coexists with anterior vaginal wall prolapse (cystocele) and posterior vaginal wall prolapse (rectocele) due to shared pelvic support mechanisms.
diagnostics: Expect POP-Q (Pelvic Organ Prolapse Quantification) staging assessment during Valsalva maneuver on examination
Expect speculum examination with split speculum (posterior blade only) to assess anterior and posterior compartments independently
Expect assessment for concurrent cystocele (anterior wall prolapse), rectocele (posterior wall prolapse), and enterocele
Monitor post-void residual volume to assess for urinary retention from urethral kinking
Expect urodynamic studies if urinary incontinence is present to differentiate stress, urge, and overflow components
Expect renal ultrasound if complete prolapse with concern for ureteral obstruction
management: Teach pelvic floor muscle exercises (Kegels): identify correct muscles by stopping urine midstream, then perform 10 repetitions of 10-second holds, 3 times daily
Fit and manage pessary device (ring or Gellhorn type) for non-surgical management of symptomatic prolapse
Apply vaginal estrogen cream as prescribed to improve mucosal tissue integrity and reduce pessary-related erosion
Encourage weight loss if obese to reduce chronic intra-abdominal pressure
Treat chronic cough and constipation to eliminate modifiable risk factors
Prepare patient for surgical repair if conservative measures fail: vaginal hysterectomy with vault suspension, sacrocolpopexy, or colpocleisis in select elderly patients
Educate on avoiding heavy lifting (>20 lbs) and Valsalva straining
nursingActions: Assess degree of prolapse by inspecting the introitus with the patient bearing down (Valsalva) in lithotomy and standing positions
Assess impact on quality of life: urinary symptoms (incontinence, frequency, incomplete emptying), bowel symptoms (constipation, splinting), sexual dysfunction
Teach Kegel exercises with proper technique: contract pelvic floor muscles as if stopping urine flow, hold 10 seconds, relax 10 seconds, avoid contracting abdominal/gluteal muscles
Educate on pessary self-care: daily removal and cleaning (or provider-managed every 3 months), recognizing signs of erosion (bleeding, discharge, pain)
Monitor for pessary complications: vaginal erosion, ulceration, foul-smelling discharge, incarceration
Educate on lifestyle modifications: weight management, high-fiber diet for constipation prevention, proper body mechanics for lifting
Report new or worsening urinary retention, vaginal bleeding, or irreducible prolapse to provider
Provide pre- and post-operative education for patients undergoing surgical repair including activity restrictions

benign-ovarian-cysts.title: Benign Over Kistleri
benign-ovarian-cysts.pathophysiology: Ovarian cysts are fluid-filled or semi-solid sacs arising from or on the surface of the ovary. They are broadly classified as functional (physiologic) or non-functional (pathologic but benign). Functional cysts are the most common type and arise from normal ovarian follicular activity. Follicular cysts develop when a dominant ovarian follicle fails to rupture during ovulation (anovulation) and continues to enlarge due to ongoing fluid accumulation stimulated by follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH). The granulosa cells lining the cyst continue to produce estradiol, which may cause endometrial proliferation and delayed or anovulatory bleeding. Follicular cysts are typically 2-6 cm and usually resolve spontaneously within 1-3 menstrual cycles as the trapped follicular fluid is resorbed. Corpus luteum cysts form when the corpus luteum (formed after ovulation) fails to regress normally and fills with blood or fluid. The corpus luteum is a vascular structure, and hemorrhage into the central cavity creates a hemorrhagic corpus luteum cyst. These cysts produce progesterone, which may cause menstrual irregularity or a missed period, mimicking ectopic pregnancy. Corpus luteum cysts can rupture and cause significant intraperitoneal hemorrhage due to their rich blood supply. Non-functional benign cysts include dermoid cysts (mature cystic teratomas), endometriomas, and serous/mucinous cystadenomas. Dermoid cysts arise from totipotent germ cells that undergo abnormal differentiation, forming a cyst containing ectodermal derivatives (skin, hair, teeth, sebaceous material), mesodermal elements (fat, bone, cartilage), and endodermal tissue (thyroid, respiratory epithelium). They are the most common ovarian neoplasm in women under 30 and grow slowly. Torsion risk increases with cyst size >5 cm due to the weight of the cyst contents acting as a fulcrum on the ovarian pedicle. Endometriomas (chocolate cysts) contain old hemorrhagic fluid from ectopic endometrial tissue implanted on the ovarian surface that undergoes cyclic bleeding. Ovarian torsion occurs when the cyst causes the ovary and its vascular pedicle (infundibulopelvic ligament) to rotate on its axis, compressing the ovarian artery and vein. Venous and lymphatic obstruction occurs first (lower pressure), causing ovarian edema and congestion, followed by arterial obstruction leading to ischemic necrosis if not emergently detorsed. Cyst rupture releases fluid into the peritoneal cavity, causing peritoneal irritation and acute abdominal pain. Hemorrhagic cyst rupture can cause hemoperitoneum with hemodynamic instability.
diagnostics: Expect transvaginal ultrasound as first-line imaging (characterize cyst as simple vs complex, solid components, septations, vascularity)
Expect serum beta-hCG to exclude ectopic pregnancy in reproductive-age women with pelvic pain
Monitor serum CA-125 in postmenopausal women with ovarian cysts (elevated in ovarian cancer, endometriosis, PID)
Expect serial ultrasound surveillance for simple cysts <5 cm (repeat in 6-8 weeks to document resolution)
Expect CBC if ruptured hemorrhagic cyst is suspected (monitor hemoglobin for hemoperitoneum)
Expect Doppler ultrasound if ovarian torsion is suspected (absent or decreased ovarian blood flow, though normal flow does not exclude torsion)
Monitor vital signs for hemodynamic instability with ruptured hemorrhagic cysts
management: Reassure and observe for simple functional cysts <5 cm in premenopausal women (most resolve within 1-3 cycles)
Administer analgesics as prescribed for cyst-related pain (NSAIDs first-line, avoid aspirin due to bleeding risk)
Administer combined oral contraceptives as prescribed to suppress future ovulation and prevent new functional cyst formation (does not resolve existing cysts)
Prepare patient for laparoscopic cystectomy for persistent cysts >5-7 cm, symptomatic cysts, or complex cysts with concerning features
Prepare patient for emergent laparoscopy/laparotomy for suspected ovarian torsion (surgical emergency requiring detorsion within 6-8 hours to salvage ovary)
Provide hemodynamic stabilization with IV fluids and blood products for hemorrhagic cyst rupture with significant hemoperitoneum
Educate on symptoms requiring emergency care: sudden severe unilateral pelvic pain, nausea/vomiting, syncope
nursingActions: Assess pain characteristics: location (unilateral pelvic), onset (sudden with rupture/torsion vs gradual), severity, and associated symptoms
Obtain serum beta-hCG to exclude ectopic pregnancy before any imaging or intervention
Monitor vital signs closely for signs of hemorrhagic shock in suspected cyst rupture (tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes)
Assess abdomen for peritoneal signs (rebound tenderness, guarding, rigidity) suggesting rupture or torsion
Administer IV fluids and analgesics as ordered for acute presentations
Educate on expected course: functional cysts typically resolve spontaneously; repeat ultrasound confirms resolution
Educate on warning signs to report immediately: sudden severe pelvic pain, dizziness, fainting, heavy vaginal bleeding
Prepare patient for surgery if torsion suspected: NPO status, IV access, pre-operative labs, consent

endometrial-polyp.title: Endometrial Polip
endometrial-polyp.pathophysiology: Endometrial polyps are localized overgrowths of endometrial glands, stroma, and blood vessels that project from the endometrial surface into the uterine cavity. They represent a focal area of endometrial hyperplasia driven primarily by estrogen stimulation without adequate progesterone opposition. At the molecular level, polyp tissue demonstrates overexpression of estrogen receptors (ER-alpha) compared to surrounding normal endometrium, making polyp tissue more responsive to circulating estrogen. Aromatase enzyme expression is upregulated within polyp stromal cells, enabling local conversion of androgens to estrogens and creating a microenvironment of estrogen excess independent of systemic hormone levels. Additionally, polyp tissue shows relative resistance to the antiproliferative effects of progesterone due to decreased progesterone receptor (PR) expression, disrupting the normal estrogen-progesterone balance that regulates endometrial growth and shedding. Key molecular alterations include loss of PTEN (phosphatase and tensin homolog) tumor suppressor function in some polyps, removing a critical brake on the PI3K/AKT proliferative signaling pathway. Increased expression of Bcl-2 (an anti-apoptotic protein) in polyp glandular cells reduces programmed cell death, allowing continued accumulation of endometrial cells. The polyp develops a central vascular stalk (feeder vessel) that supplies blood flow to the growing tissue mass. Polyps range from a few millimeters to several centimeters and may be sessile (broad-based) or pedunculated (attached by a narrow stalk). They can be solitary or multiple. The abnormal bleeding associated with polyps results from several mechanisms: surface erosion and ulceration of the polyp as it protrudes into the uterine cavity, disruption of normal endometrial hemostasis at the polyp surface, vascular fragility of the polyp's feeder vessels, and interference with normal endometrial shedding patterns. Polyps that prolapse through the cervical os may cause intermenstrual bleeding, post-coital bleeding, and cramping. Tamoxifen, a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used in breast cancer treatment, has a paradoxical estrogen-agonist effect on the endometrium, stimulating endometrial proliferation and increasing polyp incidence by 20-35% in treated women. The overall malignant transformation rate of endometrial polyps is approximately 0.5-3%, with higher risk in postmenopausal women, women with abnormal uterine bleeding, polyps >1.5 cm, and women on tamoxifen therapy.
diagnostics: Expect transvaginal ultrasound showing focal endometrial thickening or intracavitary mass (may show feeder vessel on Doppler)
Expect saline infusion sonography (SIS/sonohysterography) for enhanced visualization of intracavitary polyps (saline distends cavity, outlining polyp margins)
Expect hysteroscopy for direct visualization and simultaneous biopsy or removal (gold standard for diagnosis and treatment)
Expect endometrial biopsy (office Pipelle) to sample endometrium, though may miss polyps due to focal sampling
Monitor endometrial thickness on ultrasound (increased thickness >4 mm in postmenopausal women warrants investigation)
Expect histopathologic examination of removed polyp tissue to assess for atypia or malignancy
management: Observe small, asymptomatic polyps in premenopausal women (some regress spontaneously, especially <1 cm)
Prepare patient for hysteroscopic polypectomy for symptomatic polyps, polyps >1.5 cm, postmenopausal polyps, or polyps in women with risk factors for malignancy
Ensure histopathologic examination of all removed polyps to rule out atypia or endometrial cancer
Administer progestin therapy as prescribed to reduce recurrence risk in women with recurrent polyps or endometrial hyperplasia
Address underlying risk factors: weight loss for obesity, evaluate hormone therapy regimen, discuss tamoxifen alternatives with oncology
Evaluate for concurrent endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma in postmenopausal women with bleeding
Schedule post-polypectomy follow-up ultrasound to assess for recurrence (recurrence rate 15-45%)
nursingActions: Obtain detailed menstrual history: intermenstrual bleeding, postmenopausal bleeding, menorrhagia, post-coital spotting
Educate on pad count documentation to quantify bleeding severity
Educate on saline infusion sonography procedure: mild cramping expected, take ibuprofen 30-60 minutes before, schedule in early follicular phase
Provide pre-hysteroscopy patient education: NPO requirements, anesthesia type, expected post-procedure cramping and spotting
Monitor for post-hysteroscopy complications: heavy bleeding, fever, increasing pelvic pain (signs of endometritis or perforation)
Educate postmenopausal women that any vaginal bleeding requires evaluation to rule out endometrial cancer
Coordinate pathology follow-up to ensure histology results are reviewed and communicated to patient
Report persistent or recurrent abnormal uterine bleeding after polypectomy to provider for further evaluation

galactorrhea.title: Galaktore
galactorrhea.pathophysiology: Galactorrhea is the spontaneous or expressible milky nipple discharge unrelated to pregnancy or lactation. It results from hyperprolactinemia, which disrupts the normal hypothalamic-pituitary regulatory axis. Prolactin is secreted by lactotroph cells of the anterior pituitary gland and is uniquely regulated by tonic inhibition from dopamine released by the tuberoinfundibular dopaminergic neurons of the hypothalamus. Dopamine binds to D2 receptors on lactotrophs, suppressing prolactin gene transcription and secretion. When dopamine inhibition is disrupted - by pituitary adenomas compressing the stalk, medications blocking D2 receptors (antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone), or hypothyroidism elevating TRH (which stimulates lactotrophs) - prolactin levels rise. Elevated prolactin acts on mammary epithelial cells, stimulating milk protein synthesis (casein, lactalbumin) and secretion through JAK2/STAT5 signaling. Hyperprolactinemia also suppresses GnRH pulsatility, leading to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism with amenorrhea, anovulation, and decreased libido.
diagnostics: Order serum prolactin level (fasting, morning draw - elevated above upper limit confirms hyperprolactinemia)
Order TSH to rule out hypothyroidism as cause
Order pregnancy test (beta-hCG) to exclude physiologic cause
Order MRI of sella turcica with gadolinium if prolactin persistently elevated - evaluate for pituitary adenoma
Review complete medication history for dopamine-blocking agents
Order visual field testing (perimetry) if macroadenoma suspected - assess for optic chiasm compression
management: Discontinue or substitute offending medications if medication-induced
Initiate dopamine agonist therapy (cabergoline or bromocriptine) for prolactinomas
Treat underlying hypothyroidism with levothyroxine to normalize TRH and prolactin
Monitor prolactin levels serially to assess treatment response
Surgical referral (transsphenoidal resection) for macroadenomas resistant to medical therapy or causing visual field deficits
Monitor menstrual cycle restoration as indicator of treatment adequacy
nursingActions: Assess characteristics of nipple discharge - color, volume, unilateral vs bilateral, spontaneous vs expressible
Obtain thorough medication history including antipsychotics, antiemetics, antidepressants
Provide emotional support - address body image concerns and anxiety about symptoms
Educate on medication adherence for dopamine agonist therapy
Monitor for side effects of cabergoline/bromocriptine - nausea, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension
Teach signs of pituitary apoplexy (sudden severe headache, visual changes, altered consciousness) - report immediately

urethritis.title: Üretrit
urethritis.pathophysiology: Urethritis is inflammation of the urethra, classified as gonococcal (caused by Neisseria gonorrhoeae) or non-gonococcal (most commonly caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, Mycoplasma genitalium, Ureaplasma urealyticum, and Trichomonas vaginalis). Gonococcal urethritis involves Neisseria gonorrhoeae attaching to urethral columnar epithelial cells via type IV pili and Opa proteins, then invading through transcytosis. The organism's lipooligosaccharide (LOS) triggers an intense neutrophilic inflammatory response, producing the characteristic thick, purulent urethral discharge. Non-gonococcal urethritis due to Chlamydia trachomatis involves the organism's unique biphasic lifecycle: infectious elementary bodies invade epithelial cells, transform into replicating reticulate bodies within membrane-bound inclusions, then release new elementary bodies to infect adjacent cells. This intracellular lifecycle evades humoral immunity while producing a chronic inflammatory response. Mycoplasma genitalium adheres to epithelial cells via MgPa adhesin protein and causes persistent, often subclinical inflammation. Untreated urethritis can ascend to cause epididymitis in males or PID in females, leading to potential infertility.
diagnostics: Order nucleic acid amplification test (NAAT) for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis - urine sample or urethral swab
Order M. genitalium NAAT if available (emerging important pathogen)
Urethral Gram stain showing greater than 5 WBCs per high-power field with or without gram-negative intracellular diplococci
Order urinalysis - first-void urine for leukocyte esterase (screening)
Consider Trichomonas vaginalis NAAT in persistent or recurrent cases
Order RPR/VDRL and HIV testing as part of STI workup
management: Empiric treatment before NAAT results for symptomatic patients
Gonococcal urethritis: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 7 days (covers possible chlamydial coinfection)
Non-gonococcal urethritis: doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 7 days
M. genitalium urethritis: azithromycin 1 g day 1 then 500 mg days 2-4 (or moxifloxacin if macrolide-resistant)
Abstain from sexual intercourse until patient and partner complete treatment (minimum 7 days)
Treat all sexual partners from the past 60 days - expedited partner therapy when appropriate
nursingActions: Obtain thorough sexual history - number of partners, condom use, history of STIs, last sexual contact
Collect first-void urine specimen for NAAT (instruct patient to hold urine for at least 1 hour before collection)
Educate on completing the full antibiotic course even if symptoms resolve
Counsel on partner notification and treatment - all partners within 60 days must be evaluated
Educate on consistent condom use for future STI prevention
Schedule follow-up NAAT in 3 months to test for reinfection (high reinfection rates)
Provide non-judgmental counseling and ensure confidentiality

urethral-stricture.title: Üretral Darlık
urethral-stricture.pathophysiology: Urethral stricture is narrowing of the urethral lumen caused by scar tissue formation (spongiofibrosis) within the corpus spongiosum surrounding the urethra. The pathophysiology involves injury to the urethral epithelium from trauma, infection, instrumentation, or inflammation, triggering an abnormal wound healing cascade. Damaged urothelial cells release pro-inflammatory cytokines and transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-beta), which activates fibroblasts to proliferate and deposit excessive collagen (types I and III) in the periurethral tissue. This fibrosis contracts the urethral lumen, increasing resistance to urine flow. The resulting obstructive uropathy causes the detrusor muscle to hypertrophy in compensation, but progressive obstruction leads to detrusor decompensation, urinary retention, and potential upper tract damage (hydroureter, hydronephrosis) if untreated. Iatrogenic causes (urethral catheterization, cystoscopy, TURP) are the most common etiology in developed countries, while infectious causes (untreated gonococcal urethritis) remain significant in developing regions.
diagnostics: Retrograde urethrogram (RUG) to visualize stricture location, length, and severity
Voiding cystourethrogram (VCUG) to assess proximal extent
Uroflowmetry - decreased peak flow rate (Qmax less than 15 mL/sec suggests obstruction)
Post-void residual volume measurement (elevated with significant obstruction)
Cystourethroscopy for direct visualization - defines stricture caliber and mucosal appearance
Urinalysis and urine culture to evaluate for concurrent UTI
management: Urethral dilation - gradual stretching with sequential dilators for short, simple strictures
Direct visual internal urethrotomy (DVIU) - endoscopic incision of stricture under direct vision
Urethroplasty (surgical reconstruction) for recurrent or complex strictures - gold standard with highest long-term success
Buccal mucosal graft urethroplasty for longer strictures
Clean intermittent self-catheterization (CISC) to maintain lumen patency after dilation or DVIU
Suprapubic catheter placement for acute urinary retention when urethral passage is impossible
nursingActions: Monitor urinary output - assess stream strength, straining, dribbling, incomplete emptying
Perform accurate intake and output measurement
Assess for signs of urinary retention - suprapubic distension, discomfort, inability to void
Provide post-procedure catheter care - maintain patency, monitor for bleeding, prevent traction
Teach clean intermittent self-catheterization technique if prescribed
Educate on adequate fluid intake and importance of follow-up uroflowmetry
Report inability to void, significant hematuria, or signs of UTI immediately

phimosis.title: Fimozis
phimosis.pathophysiology: Phimosis is the inability to retract the foreskin (prepuce) over the glans penis. It is classified as physiologic or pathologic. Physiologic phimosis is a normal developmental condition in uncircumcised males - the prepuce is adherent to the glans at birth due to natural adhesions between the inner preputial epithelium and glans epithelium. These adhesions gradually separate through intermittent erections, keratinization, and desquamation over the first several years of life. By age 3, approximately 90% of boys have a retractable foreskin. Pathologic phimosis results from scarring and fibrosis of the preputial opening, most commonly caused by balanitis xerotica obliterans (BXO), also known as lichen sclerosus. BXO is an autoimmune chronic inflammatory condition that causes progressive fibrosis and whitening of the preputial tissue. The inflammatory process involves T-lymphocyte infiltration, basement membrane thickening, and collagen homogenization in the lamina propria, creating a tight, non-elastic fibrotic ring. Recurrent balanitis (from poor hygiene, diabetes, or infection) can also cause preputial scarring and secondary phimosis. Pathologic phimosis increases the risk of recurrent infections, urinary obstruction, and squamous cell carcinoma due to chronic inflammation and smegma retention.
diagnostics: Clinical examination - assess degree of foreskin retractability using Kikiros grading scale
Differentiate physiologic (soft, non-scarred preputial opening) from pathologic (white fibrotic ring at preputial tip)
Assess for concurrent balanitis - erythema, discharge, or edema of glans or foreskin
Monitor voiding - observe for ballooning of foreskin during urination (indicates significant narrowing)
Urinalysis if UTI suspected from urinary obstruction
Consider biopsy of fibrotic tissue if BXO suspected to confirm diagnosis
management: Physiologic phimosis in children under 3: reassurance and observation - normal developmental variant requiring no intervention
Topical corticosteroid therapy (betamethasone 0.05% or clobetasol 0.05%) applied to the preputial opening twice daily for 4-8 weeks
Gentle retraction exercises in conjunction with topical steroid therapy
Preputioplasty (dorsal slit or Z-plasty) for foreskin preservation when topical therapy fails
Circumcision as definitive treatment for recurrent pathologic phimosis or BXO
Treat underlying balanitis or BXO concurrently
nursingActions: Assess foreskin retractability and document degree of phimosis
Educate parents on physiologic phimosis - reassure that forced retraction is contraindicated and harmful
Teach proper application of topical corticosteroid - thin layer to the tight preputial opening, gentle retraction stretching after steroid softens tissue
Provide post-circumcision wound care education - petroleum jelly gauze application, signs of infection
Educate on hygiene - gentle retraction of foreskin for cleaning when retractable, avoid harsh soaps
Report paraphimosis immediately (foreskin trapped behind glans - urologic emergency)

priapism.title: Priapizm
priapism.pathophysiology: Priapism is a persistent penile erection lasting more than 4 hours that is unrelated to sexual stimulation. It is classified into ischemic (low-flow, veno-occlusive) and non-ischemic (high-flow, arterial) types. Ischemic priapism is a urologic emergency accounting for over 95% of cases. Normal erection physiology involves nitric oxide release from cavernous nerve terminals and endothelial cells, which activates guanylate cyclase to increase cyclic GMP (cGMP). cGMP relaxes corporal smooth muscle via protein kinase G-mediated calcium channel closure and potassium channel opening, allowing arterial blood to fill the sinusoidal spaces of the corpora cavernosa. Erection is terminated when phosphodiesterase 5 (PDE5) degrades cGMP, restoring smooth muscle tone and venous outflow. In ischemic priapism, venous outflow is obstructed, trapping deoxygenated blood in the corpora cavernosa. This creates a compartment syndrome-like condition - stagnant blood becomes acidotic and hypoxic, causing endothelial injury, smooth muscle edema, and progressive fibrosis. After 4-6 hours, irreversible smooth muscle necrosis begins. After 24-48 hours, extensive fibrosis leads to permanent erectile dysfunction. Sickle cell disease is the most common cause in children and African American males - sickling of red blood cells in the low-oxygen corporal sinusoids causes mechanical venous obstruction. Non-ischemic priapism results from unregulated arterial inflow after perineal or penile trauma creating a cavernosal artery-sinusoidal fistula.
diagnostics: Clinical diagnosis based on persistent erection greater than 4 hours with pain
Corporal blood gas analysis to differentiate ischemic from non-ischemic - ischemic: dark blood with pH less than 7.25, pO2 less than 30 mmHg, pCO2 greater than 60 mmHg
Non-ischemic: bright red blood with arterial gas values
Color Doppler ultrasound - ischemic shows absent or minimal cavernosal blood flow; non-ischemic shows turbulent flow at fistula site
Complete blood count to evaluate for leukemia or sickle cell crisis
Hemoglobin electrophoresis if sickle cell disease not previously diagnosed
Reticulocyte count and peripheral blood smear
management: Ischemic priapism is a UROLOGIC EMERGENCY - immediate intervention required
First-line: corporal aspiration of stagnant blood using a large-bore needle (19-gauge) inserted into the lateral corpus cavernosum
Intracavernosal injection of phenylephrine (alpha-1 agonist) - 100-500 mcg every 5-10 minutes, maximum 1000 mcg (monitor blood pressure and heart rate)
If aspiration and phenylephrine fail, surgical shunt creation (distal: Winter or Al-Ghorab; proximal: Quackels or Barry)
Non-ischemic priapism: observation is usually safe as tissue oxygenation is maintained - selective arterial embolization if persistent
Sickle cell-associated: concurrent hydration, oxygen therapy, exchange transfusion, pain management
nursingActions: Recognize priapism as a time-sensitive emergency - prioritize rapid assessment and provider notification
Monitor vital signs closely during phenylephrine administration - watch for hypertension, bradycardia, cardiac arrhythmia
Apply ice packs to perineum for comfort (limited efficacy but may provide mild vasoconstriction)
Obtain medication history - PDE5 inhibitors, trazodone, antipsychotics, intracavernosal injections
Assess pain level and administer analgesics as ordered
Monitor for complications - penile swelling, skin changes, signs of ischemia
Provide emotional support - address embarrassment, anxiety, and fear of erectile dysfunction
Educate sickle cell patients on priapism risk and importance of seeking care within 4 hours

balanitis.title: Balanit
balanitis.pathophysiology: Balanitis is inflammation of the glans penis, often accompanied by posthitis (inflammation of the foreskin) - collectively termed balanoposthitis. The warm, moist microenvironment beneath the prepuce creates conditions favorable for microbial overgrowth when hygiene is inadequate. Candida albicans is the most common infectious cause, particularly in diabetic patients where glycosuria provides a substrate for fungal proliferation. Candida forms biofilms on the glans epithelium, secreting aspartyl proteases that degrade host tissue proteins and triggering an inflammatory response with erythema, edema, and satellite lesions. Bacterial balanitis involves mixed flora (streptococci, staphylococci, anaerobes) invading macerated skin. Irritant balanitis results from exposure to harsh soaps, latex, or retained smegma that triggers a non-infectious inflammatory response. Zoon balanitis (plasma cell balanitis) is a chronic, benign inflammatory condition of unknown etiology, characterized by dense plasma cell infiltration and thinning of the glans epithelium in middle-aged to elderly uncircumcised men. Chronic or recurrent balanitis can lead to preputial scarring, phimosis, and in rare cases, progression to premalignant conditions (erythroplasia of Queyrat).
diagnostics: Clinical examination - assess glans for erythema, edema, discharge, satellite lesions, fissures
Fungal swab and culture if Candida suspected (KOH prep shows pseudohyphae and budding yeast)
Bacterial swab and culture if purulent discharge present
Blood glucose testing to screen for undiagnosed diabetes (recurrent candidal balanitis is often the first presentation of diabetes)
Biopsy if lesion is persistent, atypical, or unresponsive to treatment - rule out Zoon balanitis, erythroplasia of Queyrat (CIS), or squamous cell carcinoma
STI screening if sexually active with new partner
management: Candidal balanitis: topical clotrimazole 1% or miconazole 2% cream twice daily for 2 weeks
Severe or recurrent candidal balanitis: oral fluconazole 150 mg single dose
Bacterial balanitis: topical mupirocin or oral antibiotics based on culture
Irritant balanitis: remove offending agent, saline soaks, emollient application
Optimize glycemic control in diabetic patients
Circumcision for recurrent balanitis unresponsive to conservative management
Zoon balanitis: topical tacrolimus or circumcision (definitive)
nursingActions: Assess glans and foreskin - document erythema, discharge characteristics, lesion distribution, satellite lesions
Teach proper genital hygiene - gentle retraction, warm water cleansing, complete drying, avoid harsh soaps
Educate on correct topical medication application - apply thin layer to entire affected area after cleaning
Screen for diabetes if recurrent candidal balanitis - educate on blood glucose monitoring
Educate sexual partners if STI-related balanitis is suspected
Report persistent lesions unresponsive to 2 weeks of treatment - may require biopsy to rule out malignancy

cryptorchidism.title: Kriptorşidizm
cryptorchidism.pathophysiology: Cryptorchidism is the failure of one or both testes to descend from the abdominal cavity into the scrotum through the inguinal canal. Normal testicular descent occurs in two phases: the transabdominal phase (8-15 weeks gestation), mediated by insulin-like factor 3 (INSL3) secreted by Leydig cells acting on the gubernaculum, causing it to swell and anchor the testis near the internal inguinal ring; and the inguinoscrotal phase (25-35 weeks gestation), mediated primarily by testosterone and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) from the genitofemoral nerve, guiding the testis through the inguinal canal into the scrotum. Disruption at either phase results in cryptorchidism. The undescended testis is exposed to core body temperature (37 C) rather than the 2-3 C lower scrotal temperature required for normal spermatogenesis. This elevated temperature causes progressive germ cell degeneration through apoptosis, reduced spermatogonial stem cell proliferation, and Sertoli cell dysfunction. Histologic changes begin by 6 months of age and become significant by 12-18 months, providing the rationale for early orchiopexy. Cryptorchidism increases the risk of testicular germ cell tumors (seminoma) by 5-10 fold, even after orchiopexy - the risk is intrinsic to the dysgenetic testis and not solely due to temperature.
diagnostics: Physical examination - palpate inguinal canal and scrotum in warm environment with relaxed cremasteric muscle
Differentiate true cryptorchidism from retractile testis (retractile testis can be manually brought to scrotum and stays temporarily)
Ultrasound of inguinal canal and abdomen for non-palpable testis
MRI or laparoscopy for non-palpable testis not visualized on ultrasound
Serum testosterone, FSH, LH, and anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) to assess testicular function
hCG stimulation test for bilateral non-palpable testes - testosterone response confirms functional testicular tissue (anorchia if no response)
Karyotype if bilateral cryptorchidism with hypospadias (evaluate for disorder of sex development)
management: Observation until 6 months of age (spontaneous descent occurs in most premature infants)
Referral to pediatric urology if testis has not descended by 6 months of age
Orchiopexy (surgical fixation of testis in scrotum) is the definitive treatment - recommended between 6-12 months of age
Standard orchiopexy for palpable inguinal testis - inguinal approach with mobilization and scrotal fixation
Laparoscopic orchiopexy (Fowler-Stephens procedure) for non-palpable abdominal testis - staged if high intra-abdominal position
Orchiectomy if testis is atrophic, dysgenetic, or non-viable (especially in post-pubertal patients)
Long-term surveillance for testicular malignancy - teach testicular self-examination at puberty
nursingActions: Perform careful scrotal and inguinal examination in newborns and at well-child visits
Document testicular position - scrotal, inguinal, or non-palpable for each side
Educate parents on the importance of early surgical correction (before 12 months) to preserve fertility potential
Provide pre-operative education for orchiopexy - explain procedure, expected recovery, scrotal swelling
Post-operative orchiopexy care - monitor scrotal swelling, wound site, pain management, activity restrictions
Teach testicular self-examination beginning at puberty - educate about increased malignancy risk even after orchiopexy
Monitor testicular position at follow-up visits - ascent can occur after orchiopexy (rare but possible)

orchitis.title: Orşit
orchitis.pathophysiology: Orchitis is inflammation of the testis, most commonly caused by viral infection (mumps) or bacterial infection ascending from the epididymis (epididymo-orchitis). Mumps orchitis occurs in 20-30% of postpubertal males infected with mumps virus (paramyxovirus). The virus demonstrates tropism for Sertoli cells and Leydig cells of the testis, reaching the organ through viremic hematogenous spread. Viral replication within Sertoli cells disrupts the blood-testis barrier - a specialized tight junction complex (claudins, occludins, ZO-1) between adjacent Sertoli cells that separates the immunologically privileged adluminal compartment from the systemic immune system. Barrier disruption exposes haploid spermatids and spermatozoa (which express unique antigens not recognized by the immune system as self) to circulating immune cells, triggering autoimmune anti-sperm antibody formation. The resulting inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes, macrophages) and edema increase intratesticular pressure, compressing the seminiferous tubules and causing ischemic damage to germinal epithelium. Bacterial orchitis typically extends from epididymitis - organisms include Escherichia coli, Klebsiella, and Pseudomonas in older males and sexually transmitted organisms (N. gonorrhoeae, C. trachomatis) in younger males. The inflammatory response damages Leydig cells (reducing testosterone production) and Sertoli cells (impairing spermatogenesis), potentially causing testicular atrophy and infertility.
diagnostics: Doppler ultrasound of the scrotum - CRITICAL to differentiate orchitis from testicular torsion (orchitis shows INCREASED blood flow, torsion shows ABSENT flow)
Urinalysis and urine culture to identify bacterial pathogen
NAAT for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis in sexually active males under 35
Serum mumps IgM antibody if viral orchitis suspected
CBC showing leukocytosis with bacterial infection
Assess cremasteric reflex - PRESENT in orchitis, ABSENT in torsion
Prehn sign - elevation of testis relieves pain in orchitis but NOT in torsion (limited reliability)
management: Bacterial epididymo-orchitis in males under 35 (likely STI): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 10 days
Bacterial epididymo-orchitis in males over 35 (likely enteric organisms): levofloxacin 500 mg PO daily for 10 days
Mumps orchitis: supportive care only (no antiviral treatment available) - rest, scrotal elevation, ice application, analgesics
Scrotal support with athletic supporter or towel roll for comfort
NSAIDs for pain and inflammation
Surgical exploration if testicular torsion cannot be definitively excluded - torsion is a surgical emergency
nursingActions: Assess testicular pain onset - SUDDEN onset suggests torsion (surgical emergency), GRADUAL onset suggests orchitis/epididymitis
Apply scrotal support and elevation to reduce edema and improve comfort
Apply ice packs intermittently (20 minutes on, 20 minutes off) to reduce swelling
Monitor temperature and administer antipyretics as ordered
Administer analgesics and antibiotics as prescribed
Educate on the importance of completing full antibiotic course
Report sudden worsening of pain or new onset unilateral testicular pain immediately - may indicate torsion requiring emergent surgery
Educate on MMR vaccination to prevent mumps orchitis

prostatitis.title: Prostatit
prostatitis.pathophysiology: Prostatitis is inflammation of the prostate gland, classified by the NIH into four categories. Category I (acute bacterial prostatitis) is an acute infection typically caused by gram-negative enteric bacteria (E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas) or less commonly by sexually transmitted organisms. Bacteria ascend from the urethra through prostatic ducts or reach the prostate via hematogenous or lymphatic spread. The acute inflammatory response causes prostatic edema and swelling, compressing the prostatic urethra and causing obstructive voiding symptoms. Prostatic abscesses can form in severe cases. Category II (chronic bacterial prostatitis) involves persistent or recurrent bacterial infection, often maintained by bacterial biofilms on prostatic calculi. Biofilm-dwelling bacteria are resistant to antibiotic penetration and host immune responses, causing relapsing infections. Category III (chronic prostatitis/chronic pelvic pain syndrome - CP/CPPS) is the most common form (90-95% of prostatitis cases), subdivided into inflammatory (IIIA) and non-inflammatory (IIIB). The pathophysiology is multifactorial, involving prostatic inflammation, pelvic floor muscle dysfunction, neurogenic pain sensitization, and psychological factors. Elevated intraprostatic pressure from dysfunctional voiding causes intraprostatic ductal reflux of urine, triggering chemical inflammation. Category IV (asymptomatic inflammatory prostatitis) is incidentally discovered on biopsy or semen analysis.
diagnostics: Urinalysis and urine culture (midstream clean-catch) - pyuria and bacteriuria in acute bacterial prostatitis
Digital rectal examination (DRE) - acutely tender, boggy, swollen prostate in Category I (AVOID vigorous massage in acute prostatitis - risk of bacteremia)
Expressed prostatic secretions (EPS) or post-prostatic massage urine (VB3) culture for chronic bacterial prostatitis
PSA may be elevated in acute prostatitis (do NOT use for cancer screening during active infection - recheck in 6-8 weeks)
CT or transrectal ultrasound if prostatic abscess suspected
Uroflowmetry to assess voiding dysfunction
4-glass test (Meares-Stamey) for chronic prostatitis classification - compares pre-massage urine, EPS, and post-massage urine cultures
management: Category I (acute bacterial): IV antibiotics initially (fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX) with transition to oral therapy for total 4-6 weeks
Category II (chronic bacterial): oral fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX for 4-6 weeks (prolonged course for prostatic tissue penetration)
Category III (CP/CPPS): multimodal approach - alpha-blockers for voiding symptoms, NSAIDs for inflammation, pelvic floor physical therapy, cognitive behavioral therapy
Prostatic abscess drainage (transrectal or transperineal) if abscess greater than 2 cm
Sitz baths for symptomatic relief
Stool softeners to reduce straining and perineal pressure
Adequate hydration and avoidance of caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods (irritants)
nursingActions: Assess voiding patterns - frequency, urgency, dysuria, incomplete emptying, straining
Monitor temperature and systemic signs of infection (acute prostatitis can progress to sepsis)
Educate on prolonged antibiotic course (4-6 weeks) - emphasize importance of completing full course even if symptoms improve
Provide sitz bath instructions - warm water for 15-20 minutes, 2-3 times daily for comfort
Advise on dietary modifications - avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods that can exacerbate symptoms
Monitor for urinary retention - assess suprapubic distension, post-void residual
Report inability to void, high fever, or signs of sepsis immediately
Provide emotional support for chronic prostatitis - address impact on quality of life, sexual function, and mental health

abg-advanced-np.title: Gelişmiş ABG Yorumlama
abg-advanced-np.pathophysiology: Arterial blood gases reflect the balance between metabolic (HCO3⁻) and respiratory (PaCO2) buffering systems regulated by the kidneys and lungs respectively. The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation governs pH: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3⁻] / 0.03 × PaCO2). Compensation occurs when one system offsets the other's primary disorder, but pH does not fully normalize unless correction is achieved.
diagnostics: Order ABG from arterial line or radial artery puncture
Calculate anion gap: Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO3⁻); normal 8-12 mEq/L
Calculate delta-delta ratio for mixed disorders
Assess expected compensation using Winter's formula (metabolic acidosis)
Order basic metabolic panel concurrently
Evaluate lactate for tissue perfusion assessment
management: Identify primary disorder first (pH direction determines primary)
Determine if compensation is appropriate or a mixed disorder exists
Treat underlying cause (e.g., insulin for DKA, ventilator adjustments for respiratory acidosis)
Order repeat ABG 20-30 minutes after intervention
Adjust ventilator settings for respiratory acid-base corrections
Consider sodium bicarbonate for pH < 7.1 with hemodynamic instability

airway-assessment-rn.title: Kapsamlı Hava Yolu Değerlendirmesi
airway-assessment-rn.pathophysiology: The upper airway (nasopharynx, oropharynx, larynx) warms, humidifies, and filters inspired air before it reaches the lower airways. Airway patency depends on intact neuromuscular tone, adequate mucociliary clearance, and absence of obstruction. Loss of consciousness, edema, secretions, or foreign bodies can compromise the airway at any level, leading to hypoxemia within minutes.
diagnostics: Assess airway patency using look, listen, feel approach
Evaluate breath sounds bilaterally with auscultation
Monitor continuous pulse oximetry and capnography (EtCO2)
Assess work of breathing (accessory muscle use, retractions)
Evaluate cough effectiveness and ability to manage secretions
Perform modified Allen test before arterial puncture if ABG needed
management: Position patient for optimal airway patency (head-tilt chin-lift or jaw thrust)
Suction oropharynx as needed using Yankauer catheter
Insert oropharyngeal (OPA) or nasopharyngeal (NPA) airway as indicated
Administer supplemental oxygen per protocol
Prepare for advanced airway management if patient deteriorating
Activate rapid response for impending airway compromise
nursingActions: Perform systematic airway assessment every 1-2 hours and with any status change
Maintain head of bed ≥ 30 degrees unless contraindicated
Keep suction equipment at bedside and functioning
Monitor SpO2 and EtCO2 continuously in at-risk patients
Report stridor, new-onset hoarseness, or increased work of breathing immediately
Document airway assessment findings and interventions

alpha-1-antitrypsin-deficiency-np.title: Alpha-1 Antitripsin Eksikliği
alpha-1-antitrypsin-deficiency-np.pathophysiology: Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) is a serine protease inhibitor produced by hepatocytes that protects alveolar tissue from neutrophil elastase. In AAT deficiency (most commonly PiZZ genotype), unopposed elastase activity destroys alveolar walls, causing panacinar emphysema predominantly in the lung bases. Hepatic accumulation of misfolded Z-protein can also cause liver cirrhosis.
diagnostics: Order serum AAT level (< 11 µmol/L is diagnostic)
Order AAT phenotyping or genotyping to confirm variant
Pulmonary function tests showing obstructive pattern
CT chest showing basilar-predominant panacinar emphysema
Liver function tests and hepatic elastography
Screen all COPD patients diagnosed before age 45
management: Initiate IV augmentation therapy (Prolastin/Zemaira) for patients with FEV1 30-65% predicted
Prescribe standard COPD pharmacotherapy (LABA/LAMA/ICS as indicated)
Counsel on absolute smoking cessation
Refer for pulmonary rehabilitation
Screen family members with AAT level
Refer to hepatology if liver involvement suspected
Evaluate for lung transplant if FEV1 < 25% predicted

ards-management-np.title: ARDS Gelişmiş Yönetimi
ards-management-np.pathophysiology: ARDS involves a massive inflammatory cascade triggering neutrophil activation, endothelial injury, and increased alveolar-capillary permeability. This leads to protein-rich pulmonary edema flooding the alveoli, surfactant inactivation, and alveolar collapse. The result is severe V/Q mismatch and refractory hypoxemia. The Berlin criteria classify severity by PaO2/FiO2 ratio on PEEP ≥ 5: mild (200-300), moderate (100-200), severe (< 100).
diagnostics: Order ABG to calculate P/F ratio (PaO2/FiO2)
Chest X-ray showing bilateral opacities not explained by effusions or atelectasis
Echocardiogram to rule out cardiogenic pulmonary edema
Order BNP to differentiate from heart failure
Monitor ventilator mechanics (plateau pressure, compliance)
Order procalcitonin and cultures if infectious etiology suspected
management: Initiate lung-protective ventilation: Vt 4-6 mL/kg IBW, plateau pressure < 30 cmH2O
Titrate PEEP using FiO2/PEEP table or best compliance method
Order prone positioning for 16+ hours/day if P/F < 150
Implement conservative fluid strategy (target CVP 4-6, even fluid balance)
Consider neuromuscular blockade (cisatracurium) for P/F < 150 in first 48 hours
Prescribe dexamethasone for moderate-severe ARDS
Refer for ECMO evaluation if refractory hypoxemia despite maximal therapy

asthma-emergency-np.title: Astım Acil Yönetimi
asthma-emergency-np.pathophysiology: Status asthmaticus is a severe, life-threatening asthma exacerbation unresponsive to standard bronchodilator therapy. Massive bronchoconstriction, mucus plugging, and inflammatory edema create near-complete airway obstruction. Progressive air trapping increases intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP), impairs venous return, and can cause hemodynamic collapse. Without intervention, respiratory muscle fatigue leads to respiratory arrest.
diagnostics: Order ABG: initial respiratory alkalosis transitions to respiratory acidosis (ominous)
Order chest X-ray to rule out pneumothorax or pneumonia
Monitor continuous SpO2 and consider capnography
Peak flow < 25% predicted indicates severe obstruction
Order CBC, BMP, magnesium level
Order VBG as alternative if ABG not feasible
management: Prescribe continuous nebulized albuterol 10-15 mg/hour
Order ipratropium bromide 0.5 mg q20min × 3 doses
Prescribe IV methylprednisolone 125 mg or equivalent
Order IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 minutes for severe exacerbation
Consider IV terbutaline if refractory to inhaled bronchodilators
Prepare for intubation if respiratory failure imminent (ketamine is preferred induction agent)
Avoid aggressive mechanical ventilation post-intubation (permissive hypercapnia strategy)

asthma-outpatient-np.title: Astım Ayaktan Yönetimi
asthma-outpatient-np.pathophysiology: Asthma involves chronic eosinophilic airway inflammation driven by Th2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13) causing bronchial hyperresponsiveness, goblet cell hyperplasia with mucus hypersecretion, and smooth muscle hypertrophy. Airway remodeling with subepithelial fibrosis occurs over time if inflammation is poorly controlled. Stepwise therapy aims to achieve control with the lowest effective medication dose.
diagnostics: Order spirometry with bronchodilator reversibility (≥ 12% and 200 mL improvement in FEV1)
Assess asthma control using validated tools (ACT score, frequency of symptoms/rescue inhaler use)
Order FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) to assess eosinophilic inflammation
Consider allergy testing (skin prick or specific IgE)
Order CBC with differential (eosinophil count)
Review peak flow diary trends
management: Classify severity and prescribe stepwise therapy per GINA guidelines
Step 1-2: Low-dose ICS-formoterol as needed (preferred) or low-dose ICS + SABA PRN
Step 3: Low-dose ICS-LABA maintenance
Step 4: Medium-dose ICS-LABA
Step 5: High-dose ICS-LABA + add-on therapy (LAMA, biologic)
Develop written Asthma Action Plan with green/yellow/red zones
Address comorbidities: GERD, allergic rhinitis, obesity
Review and step down therapy every 3 months if well-controlled

bronchiectasis-management-np.title: Bronşiektazi Yönetimi
bronchiectasis-management-np.pathophysiology: Bronchiectasis involves permanent, abnormal dilation of bronchi caused by a vicious cycle of infection, inflammation, and impaired mucociliary clearance (Cole's vicious cycle). Neutrophilic inflammation releases elastase and other proteases that destroy elastic and muscular components of the bronchial wall. Dilated airways pool secretions, promoting chronic bacterial colonization (often Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Haemophilus influenzae).
diagnostics: Order high-resolution CT chest (gold standard  -  shows signet ring sign, tram tracks)
Order sputum culture and sensitivity (identify colonizing organisms)
Order immunoglobulin levels (IgG, IgA, IgM) to screen for immunodeficiency
Pulmonary function tests (obstructive pattern)
Order CBC with differential
Consider testing for cystic fibrosis (sweat chloride), ABPA (IgE, Aspergillus-specific IgE)
management: Prescribe airway clearance therapy (chest physiotherapy, oscillatory PEP devices, hypertonic saline nebulization)
Prescribe long-term macrolide therapy (azithromycin 250 mg 3×/week) for frequent exacerbations
Treat acute exacerbations with culture-directed antibiotics (14 days minimum)
Prescribe inhaled hypertonic saline (7%) to improve mucociliary clearance
Consider inhaled tobramycin for chronic Pseudomonas colonization
Refer for pulmonary rehabilitation
Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations

bronchiectasis-rpn.title: Bronşiektazi Temelleri (/)
bronchiectasis-rpn.pathophysiology: Bronchiectasis is an irreversible dilation and destruction of the bronchial walls due to chronic infection and inflammation. Damaged airways lose their ability to clear mucus effectively, leading to pooling of secretions and recurrent infections. Patients produce large volumes of purulent sputum daily and experience progressive decline in lung function.
diagnostics: Monitor respiratory rate, effort, and oxygen saturation
Observe and report sputum color, consistency, amount, and odor
Monitor temperature for signs of infection
Auscultate breath sounds as delegated and report abnormalities
management: Assist with airway clearance techniques as ordered (chest physiotherapy, postural drainage)
Administer nebulized medications as ordered
Encourage adequate fluid intake to thin secretions
Position patient for optimal drainage as ordered
Provide oral hygiene after expectoration
nursingActions: Collect sputum specimens as ordered (early morning, before meals)
Monitor and record sputum characteristics every shift
Report changes in sputum (increased volume, color change, blood-tinged)
Assist with and encourage prescribed airway clearance regimen
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress and report promptly
Encourage adherence to vaccination schedule

bronchopulmonary-dysplasia-rpn.title: Bronkopulmoner Displazi (/)
bronchopulmonary-dysplasia-rpn.pathophysiology: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) is a chronic lung disease of prematurity resulting from immature lung tissue exposed to supplemental oxygen and mechanical ventilation. Oxidative stress and barotrauma arrest alveolar and vascular development, leading to fewer, larger alveoli with thickened interstitium. This impairs gas exchange and increases work of breathing in the developing infant.
diagnostics: Monitor oxygen saturation continuously (target per unit protocol, often 90-95%)
Monitor respiratory rate, effort, and work of breathing
Observe for signs of respiratory distress (retractions, nasal flaring, grunting)
Monitor weight daily (fluid status assessment)
Report oxygen requirement changes to RN
management: Administer supplemental oxygen as ordered to maintain target SpO2
Administer diuretics and bronchodilators as ordered
Support adequate caloric intake (120-150 kcal/kg/day needed for growth)
Minimize handling and cluster care to reduce oxygen consumption
Position for optimal breathing (elevated head of crib)
Maintain strict fluid balance as ordered
nursingActions: Monitor continuous pulse oximetry and report desaturation events
Assess respiratory effort every 2-4 hours (retractions, nasal flaring, grunting)
Monitor daily weight and strict intake/output
Administer medications as ordered and monitor for side effects
Feed slowly and monitor for feeding-related desaturation
Educate parents on home oxygen therapy if applicable
Support developmental care and kangaroo care as tolerated

chest-tube-basics-rpn.title: Göğüs Tüpü Temelleri (/)
chest-tube-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: The pleural space normally contains a thin layer of serous fluid (~5-15 mL) that provides lubrication between the visceral and parietal pleurae during respiration. Negative intrapleural pressure (approximately -5 cmH2O) maintains lung expansion. When air (pneumothorax), blood (hemothorax), or fluid (pleural effusion) enters this space, the lung collapses as the negative pressure is lost. Chest tubes restore negative pressure by draining the pathological collection.
diagnostics: Monitor chest tube drainage (amount, color, consistency) every 1-2 hours
Observe for tidaling (fluctuation) in the water seal chamber
Monitor for air leaks (continuous bubbling in water seal chamber)
Monitor respiratory status (rate, effort, SpO2)
Observe insertion site for crepitus, drainage, or dislodgment
management: Maintain chest drainage system below the level of the chest at all times
Keep tubing free of kinks, loops, or dependent drainage
Ensure all connections are tight and secured with tape
NEVER clamp a chest tube without a physician order
Encourage coughing, deep breathing, and incentive spirometry as ordered
Maintain occlusive dressing at insertion site
nursingActions: Assess chest tube system every 1-2 hours and document findings
Report sudden cessation of drainage, new or increased air leak, or respiratory distress
Keep petroleum gauze and sterile occlusive dressing at bedside for emergent dislodgement
Monitor and document drainage output hourly (report > 200 mL/hour)
Assist patient with positioning and comfort measures
Report any tubing disconnection immediately
Do NOT strip or milk chest tubes without an order

copd-outpatient-np.title: KOAH Ayaktan Yönetimi
copd-outpatient-np.pathophysiology: COPD is characterized by persistent, progressive airflow limitation due to enhanced chronic inflammatory response in the airways and lungs to noxious particles or gases. Neutrophil-dominated inflammation in chronic bronchitis causes mucus gland hypertrophy and goblet cell metaplasia. In emphysema, protease-antiprotease imbalance leads to destruction of alveolar walls and loss of elastic recoil. GOLD staging uses post-bronchodilator FEV1 to classify severity.
diagnostics: Order post-bronchodilator spirometry: FEV1/FVC < 0.70 confirms diagnosis
ABCD assessment: combine exacerbation history and symptom burden (mMRC/CAT)
Order CBC (polycythemia from chronic hypoxemia)
Order AAT level if age < 45 or family history
Order chest X-ray (hyperinflation, flattened diaphragm)
management: Group A: Bronchodilator (SABA or SAMA PRN)
Group B: LABA or LAMA maintenance
Group E (exacerbator): LABA + LAMA; add ICS if eosinophils ≥ 300
Prescribe pulmonary rehabilitation for all symptomatic patients
Prescribe supplemental oxygen if PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤ 88%
Counsel smoking cessation and prescribe pharmacotherapy (varenicline, NRT)
Vaccinations: influenza annually, PCV20, Tdap, COVID-19, RSV if ≥ 60

cystic-fibrosis-adult-np.title: Kistik Fibrozis Yetişkin Yönetimi
cystic-fibrosis-adult-np.pathophysiology: Cystic fibrosis results from mutations in the CFTR gene (most commonly F508del) encoding a chloride and bicarbonate channel on epithelial cells. Defective CFTR causes impaired chloride secretion and excessive sodium and water absorption, producing thick, dehydrated secretions in the lungs, pancreas, liver, intestines, and reproductive tract. In the lungs, viscous mucus impairs mucociliary clearance, promotes chronic bacterial infection (Pseudomonas, Staphylococcus aureus, Burkholderia), and triggers a sustained neutrophilic inflammatory response leading to progressive bronchiectasis.
diagnostics: Order pulmonary function tests quarterly (FEV1 is primary outcome measure)
Order sputum culture with sensitivities at every visit
Screen for CFRD with oral glucose tolerance test annually after age 10
Monitor fat-soluble vitamin levels (A, D, E, K) annually
Order liver function tests and hepatic ultrasound periodically
Order DEXA scan for bone density every 2-5 years
Consider CFTR genotyping if not previously done (guide modulator therapy)
management: Prescribe CFTR modulator therapy based on genotype (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor for F508del)
Prescribe aggressive airway clearance (high-frequency chest wall oscillation, PEP devices)
Prescribe dornase alfa (Pulmozyme) daily nebulization
Prescribe hypertonic saline 7% nebulization BID
Prescribe pancreatic enzyme replacement (PERT) with meals and snacks
Manage CFRD with insulin therapy (not oral hypoglycemics)
Prescribe fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (ADEK)
Treat acute exacerbations with IV antipseudomonal antibiotics (14-21 days)

cystic-fibrosis-basics-rpn.title: Kistik Fibrozis Temelleri (/)
cystic-fibrosis-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disease that causes the body to produce thick, sticky mucus that clogs the lungs and digestive system. A defective chloride channel prevents normal salt and water movement across cell membranes, leading to dehydrated, viscous secretions. In the lungs, this thick mucus traps bacteria and causes repeated infections that progressively damage the airways.
diagnostics: Monitor respiratory status (rate, effort, oxygen saturation)
Observe and report sputum characteristics (color, amount, consistency)
Monitor daily weight and nutritional intake
Assess for abdominal distension and stool characteristics
Monitor temperature for infection signs
management: Assist with chest physiotherapy and airway clearance as ordered
Administer nebulized medications as ordered (bronchodilators, mucolytics)
Administer pancreatic enzymes as ordered with meals and snacks
Provide high-calorie, high-fat diet as ordered
Encourage adequate fluid intake
Maintain infection control precautions (CF patients should be separated)
nursingActions: Perform or assist with chest physiotherapy as ordered (30 minutes before meals or 1 hour after)
Administer pancreatic enzymes at the beginning of each meal and snack
Monitor and document stool pattern (report steatorrhea  -  fatty, foul-smelling stools)
Monitor for signs of respiratory infection and report promptly
Encourage physical activity as tolerated
Provide emotional support to patient and family

cystic-fibrosis-peds-rpn.title: Kistik Fibrozis Pediatri (/)
cystic-fibrosis-peds-rpn.pathophysiology: In pediatric patients, CF manifests early with meconium ileus in neonates (10-15% of CF infants), failure to thrive due to pancreatic insufficiency, and recurrent respiratory infections. The immature immune system combined with impaired mucociliary clearance makes infants and children particularly vulnerable to Staphylococcus aureus and Haemophilus influenzae initially, with Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonization typically occurring by school age.
diagnostics: Monitor growth parameters (weight, length/height, head circumference) at every visit
Monitor respiratory rate and effort
Observe feeding tolerance and caloric intake
Monitor stool pattern (frequency, character, odor)
Observe for rectal prolapse during defecation
Monitor oxygen saturation during respiratory illness
management: Administer pancreatic enzymes as ordered with all meals and snacks
Assist with age-appropriate airway clearance (percussion, vest therapy, PEP devices)
Provide high-calorie, high-fat diet with supplemental vitamins (ADEK)
Administer nebulized medications as ordered
Support development-appropriate play and activity
Maintain strict hand hygiene and infection prevention
nursingActions: Weigh daily during hospitalization; plot growth curves regularly
Administer enzymes at start of feeds  -  for infants, open capsule and mix beads with applesauce (not formula)
Perform chest physiotherapy 30 minutes before meals
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress in infants (nasal flaring, grunting, retractions)
Educate parents on airway clearance techniques appropriate to child's age
Report poor weight gain or feeding intolerance promptly
Ensure CF patients are not in rooms with other CF patients (prevent cross-infection)

hemothorax-management-np.title: Hemotoraks Yönetimi
hemothorax-management-np.pathophysiology: Hemothorax is the accumulation of blood in the pleural space, most commonly resulting from trauma to intercostal vessels, internal mammary artery, or lung parenchyma. A massive hemothorax (> 1500 mL or > 200 mL/hour drainage) constitutes a surgical emergency. Blood in the pleural space compresses the lung causing ventilatory compromise and, if large, causes hypovolemic shock from hemorrhage. Retained hemothorax can lead to empyema or fibrothorax if not adequately drained.
diagnostics: Order STAT chest X-ray (may show blunting of costophrenic angle, opacification)
Order FAST exam (extended) for rapid trauma assessment
Order CT chest with contrast if hemodynamically stable
Order CBC, type and crossmatch, coagulation studies
Order serial hemoglobin levels every 4-6 hours
Monitor chest tube output hourly
management: Place large-bore (32-36 Fr) chest tube for drainage
Order massive transfusion protocol if massive hemothorax (> 1500 mL immediate drainage)
Consult thoracic surgery for: initial drainage > 1500 mL, ongoing output > 200 mL/hr × 2-4 hours, or hemodynamic instability
Order autotransfusion of chest tube blood if available
Correct coagulopathy (FFP, platelets, TXA as indicated)
Order VATS for retained hemothorax not resolving with chest tube

interstitial-lung-disease-np.title: Interstitial Akciğer Hastalığı
interstitial-lung-disease-np.pathophysiology: Interstitial lung diseases (ILDs) encompass over 200 conditions causing inflammation and/or fibrosis of the pulmonary interstitium (the tissue between alveoli). Fibroblast proliferation and excessive collagen deposition thicken the alveolar walls, impairing gas exchange. This creates a restrictive pattern on PFTs with reduced DLCO. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) is the most common and lethal ILD, with a median survival of 3-5 years.
diagnostics: Order high-resolution CT chest (UIP pattern in IPF: basilar, peripheral honeycombing, traction bronchiectasis)
Order pulmonary function tests (restrictive pattern: reduced FVC with preserved/elevated FEV1/FVC ratio, reduced DLCO)
Order autoimmune panel (ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-Scl-70, myositis panel)
Order 6-minute walk test (assess functional capacity and desaturation)
Consider surgical lung biopsy if diagnosis uncertain after HRCT
Multidisciplinary discussion (MDD) is gold standard for ILD diagnosis
management: Prescribe antifibrotic therapy for IPF: pirfenidone or nintedanib
Treat underlying autoimmune disease if connective tissue disease-associated ILD
Prescribe supplemental oxygen for exertional or resting hypoxemia
Refer for pulmonary rehabilitation
Vaccinations: influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19
Refer for lung transplant evaluation if progressive disease (FVC < 80% predicted or declining)
Screen for and treat comorbid GERD, pulmonary hypertension, and OSA

lung-auscultation.title: Akciğer Osültasyonu
lung-auscultation.pathophysiology: Normal breath sounds are generated by turbulent airflow through large airways and transmitted through aerated lung tissue. Vesicular sounds (soft, low-pitched, heard peripherally) are normal over most lung fields. Bronchovesicular sounds are heard near the mainstem bronchi. Adventitious (abnormal) sounds occur when air passes through narrowed airways (wheezes), fluid (crackles), or inflamed pleura (friction rubs). Sound transmission is enhanced through consolidated tissue and diminished through air or fluid collections.
diagnostics: Auscultate systematically: anterior (6 sites), posterior (8 sites), lateral
Compare bilateral lung fields side-to-side
Listen for at least one full respiratory cycle at each site
Assess during both quiet breathing and deep breathing
Note location, timing (inspiratory vs expiratory), and character of sounds
management: Document findings clearly using standardized terminology
Report abnormal findings to the appropriate provider
Compare findings to baseline assessments
Correlate auscultation findings with other assessment data

lung-cancer-basics-rpn.title: Akciğer Kanseri Temelleri (/)
lung-cancer-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: Lung cancer is the leading cause of cancer death worldwide. It is broadly classified as non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC, ~85%) and small cell lung cancer (SCLC, ~15%). NSCLC includes adenocarcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and large cell carcinoma. Carcinogens (primarily cigarette smoke) cause DNA mutations leading to uncontrolled cell proliferation. Tumors can obstruct airways, invade surrounding structures, and metastasize to brain, bone, liver, and adrenal glands.
diagnostics: Monitor respiratory status (rate, effort, oxygen saturation)
Observe for new or changing cough, hemoptysis
Monitor pain levels and effectiveness of pain management
Monitor weight and nutritional status
Observe for signs of superior vena cava syndrome (facial/neck swelling)
Report any neurological changes (possible brain metastasis)
management: Administer medications as ordered (analgesics, antiemetics, bronchodilators)
Position for comfort and optimal breathing
Provide oxygen therapy as ordered
Assist with nutrition (high-calorie, high-protein diet)
Provide emotional support and comfort measures
Assist with activities of daily living as needed
nursingActions: Assess pain regularly using appropriate pain scale and administer analgesics as ordered
Monitor for side effects of chemotherapy/radiation (nausea, mucositis, fatigue, myelosuppression)
Report hemoptysis immediately
Monitor for signs of SVC syndrome and report (facial edema, JVD, dyspnea)
Provide mouth care for patients with mucositis
Monitor nutritional intake and report weight loss > 5%
Support patient and family coping

lung-cancer-staging-np.title: Akciğer Kanseri Evreleme ve Tedavi
lung-cancer-staging-np.pathophysiology: Lung cancer development involves accumulation of driver mutations in oncogenes (EGFR, ALK, KRAS, ROS1, BRAF) and tumor suppressor genes (TP53, RB1). NSCLC staging uses the TNM system (8th edition): T = tumor size/invasion, N = lymph node involvement, M = distant metastasis. Molecular profiling is essential as targetable mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1) determine therapy. SCLC is staged as limited (confined to one hemithorax) or extensive (beyond one radiation port).
diagnostics: Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for staging
Order PET-CT for metabolic staging (excludes M1 disease)
Order brain MRI (all NSCLC Stage II+ and all SCLC)
Order tissue biopsy for histology and molecular profiling (EGFR, ALK, ROS1, PD-L1, KRAS G12C, BRAF V600E)
Order PFTs for surgical candidacy assessment
Order CBC, CMP, LDH
Consider EBUS for mediastinal lymph node sampling
management: Stage I-II NSCLC: Surgical resection (lobectomy preferred) ± adjuvant chemotherapy
Stage III NSCLC: Concurrent chemoradiation ± durvalumab consolidation
Stage IV NSCLC: Systemic therapy based on molecular markers and PD-L1 expression
EGFR+ → osimertinib; ALK+ → alectinib; PD-L1 ≥ 50% → pembrolizumab monotherapy
Limited SCLC: Concurrent chemoradiation (cisplatin + etoposide) ± prophylactic cranial irradiation
Extensive SCLC: Platinum + etoposide + atezolizumab (IMpower133)
Refer to palliative care early for all Stage IV patients

lung-sounds-assessment-rpn.title: Akciğer Sesleri Değerlendirmesi (/)
lung-sounds-assessment-rpn.pathophysiology: Breath sounds are produced by air turbulence in the large airways and transmitted through lung tissue to the chest wall. Normal vesicular breath sounds are soft and low-pitched, heard over peripheral lung fields. Adventitious (abnormal) sounds occur when air flows through narrowed or fluid-filled airways, or when inflamed pleural surfaces rub together. Changes in breath sounds are often the earliest detectable sign of respiratory deterioration.
diagnostics: Auscultate with diaphragm of stethoscope
Listen to anterior, posterior, and lateral lung fields bilaterally
Compare side-to-side at each level
Note the location, timing, and quality of any abnormal sounds
Report all abnormal findings to the nurse promptly
management: Position patient upright for optimal auscultation
Instruct patient to breathe deeply through an open mouth
Document findings clearly using correct terminology
Report changes from baseline lung sounds immediately
nursingActions: Perform lung auscultation at the beginning of each shift and as needed
Report new crackles, wheezes, or diminished breath sounds to the RN
Assess breath sounds before and after respiratory treatments
Document findings including location, character, and timing
Compare current findings to baseline and report changes

non-invasive-ventilation-modes-np.title: İnvaziv Olmayan Ventilasyon Modları
non-invasive-ventilation-modes-np.pathophysiology: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) delivers positive airway pressure through a mask interface (nasal, oronasal, or full-face) without an endotracheal tube. CPAP provides continuous positive pressure maintaining alveolar recruitment and improving oxygenation. BiPAP provides two pressure levels: higher inspiratory (IPAP) for ventilatory support and lower expiratory (EPAP) for oxygenation and preventing alveolar collapse. NIV reduces work of breathing, improves gas exchange, and avoids intubation-related complications.
diagnostics: Order ABG before and 1-2 hours after NIV initiation
Monitor continuous SpO2 and respiratory rate
Assess patient-ventilator synchrony and mask fit
Monitor tidal volumes and minute ventilation (if available on device)
Reassess clinical status every 1-2 hours for improvement or need for intubation
Establish intubation criteria before NIV initiation
management: CPAP 5-10 cmH2O for cardiogenic pulmonary edema
BiPAP starting IPAP 10-12, EPAP 4-6 for COPD exacerbation
Titrate IPAP for ventilation (reduce PaCO2) and EPAP for oxygenation
Set backup rate for patients at risk of apnea
Select appropriate mask interface and ensure proper fit
Define failure criteria: worsening ABG at 1-2 hours, inability to protect airway, hemodynamic instability, unable to tolerate mask

occupational-lung-disease-np.title: Mesleki Akciğer Hastalığı
occupational-lung-disease-np.pathophysiology: Occupational lung diseases result from chronic inhalation of mineral dusts (pneumoconioses), organic dusts, chemicals, or fumes. Inhaled particles deposit in the distal airways and alveoli, triggering macrophage phagocytosis and an inflammatory cascade. Silicosis involves silica crystal-induced macrophage death releasing inflammatory cytokines, forming characteristic silicotic nodules. Asbestosis causes diffuse interstitial fibrosis with pleural plaques. Coal workers' pneumoconiosis involves coal macule formation progressing to progressive massive fibrosis.
diagnostics: Detailed occupational history (essential  -  type of exposure, duration, use of PPE)
Order chest X-ray with ILO classification for pneumoconiosis
Order HRCT chest for detailed assessment
Order pulmonary function tests (restrictive, obstructive, or mixed pattern depending on disease)
Order DLCO (reduced in fibrotic disease)
Screen for latent TB if silicosis suspected (silicosis increases TB risk 3-fold)
management: Remove from ongoing exposure (most critical intervention)
No curative treatment for most pneumoconioses  -  management is supportive
