Prescribe supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia
Refer for pulmonary rehabilitation
Screen and treat comorbidities (silicosis + TB, asbestosis + lung cancer/mesothelioma)
Report to workers' compensation and occupational health authorities
Lung transplant evaluation for progressive disease

oxygen-therapy-basics.title: Oksijen Tedavisi Temelleri
oxygen-therapy-basics.pathophysiology: Oxygen is transported in blood primarily bound to hemoglobin (98%) and dissolved in plasma (2%). The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve describes the relationship between PaO2 and hemoglobin saturation. Tissue oxygenation depends on adequate ventilation, diffusion across the alveolar-capillary membrane, sufficient hemoglobin, and adequate cardiac output. Supplemental oxygen increases the FiO2 (fraction of inspired oxygen) above room air (21%) to correct hypoxemia.
diagnostics: Monitor oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry (SpO2)
Assess respiratory rate, depth, and pattern
Monitor for signs of hypoxia (restlessness, confusion, tachycardia, cyanosis)
ABG analysis for precise PaO2 and SaO2 measurement
Assess hemoglobin level (SpO2 may be normal with severe anemia)
management: Match oxygen delivery device to patient's needs and FiO2 requirement
Nasal cannula: 1-6 L/min (FiO2 ~24-44%)
Simple face mask: 6-10 L/min (FiO2 ~40-60%)
Non-rebreather mask: 10-15 L/min (FiO2 ~80-95%)
Venturi mask: precise FiO2 delivery (24-50%)
High-flow nasal cannula: up to 60 L/min with precise FiO2

oxygen-therapy-setup-rpn.title: Oksijen Tedavisi Kurulumu (/)
oxygen-therapy-setup-rpn.pathophysiology: Oxygen delivery devices are classified as low-flow (nasal cannula, simple mask, non-rebreather) or high-flow (Venturi mask, high-flow nasal cannula) systems. Low-flow systems deliver supplemental O2 that mixes with room air during inspiration, so the actual FiO2 varies with the patient's respiratory pattern. High-flow systems provide all or most of the patient's inspiratory flow, delivering a more consistent FiO2. Proper setup and maintenance of equipment ensures safe and effective oxygen delivery.
diagnostics: Verify the ordered flow rate and delivery device
Check oxygen saturation before and after setup
Assess skin integrity under and around the device (ears, nares, face)
Verify oxygen source is functioning and gauge reads adequate supply
Report SpO2 readings outside ordered target range
management: Set up oxygen delivery device per order
Ensure proper fit of nasal cannula or mask
Set flow rate as ordered (never adjust without an order)
Humidify oxygen at flow rates > 4 L/min (prevents mucosal drying)
Post oxygen safety signs (no smoking, no open flames)
Secure tubing to prevent tripping hazards
nursingActions: Verify the order for oxygen therapy (device, flow rate, target SpO2)
Set up equipment and verify flow is reaching the patient
Monitor SpO2 continuously or as ordered and document
Assess for skin breakdown at device contact points (ears, nares, bridge of nose)
Apply water-based lubricant to nares to prevent drying and cracking
Ensure humidifier is filled and functioning for flow rates > 4 L/min
Educate patient and visitors on oxygen safety (no smoking, no petroleum-based products near O2)
Report any equipment malfunction immediately

peds-respiratory.title: Pediatrik Solunum Koşulları
peds-respiratory.pathophysiology: Pediatric airways are anatomically and physiologically different from adults: they are shorter, narrower, and more compliant. The infant's tongue is proportionally larger, the larynx is higher (C3-C4 vs C4-C5 in adults), and the epiglottis is omega-shaped. Subglottic stenosis is the narrowest point in children (vs glottis in adults). These differences make children more susceptible to airway obstruction from even mild inflammation or edema. Small reductions in airway radius dramatically increase resistance (Poiseuille's law  -  resistance ∝ 1/r⁴).
diagnostics: Monitor respiratory rate (age-specific normals)
Assess work of breathing (nasal flaring, retractions, head bobbing in infants)
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Assess hydration status (fontanelle, mucous membranes, urine output)
Observe for behavioral changes (irritability, lethargy)
Auscultate lung sounds
management: Position for optimal airway patency (sniffing position for infants)
Administer oxygen to maintain SpO2 > 94%
Suction nares before feeding in infants
Maintain hydration (oral or IV as indicated)
Administer medications as ordered
Keep emergency airway equipment accessible
nursingActions: Perform respiratory assessment every 1-2 hours in acute illness
Monitor for signs of respiratory failure (decreasing RR in a previously tachypneic child is ominous)
Assess feeding tolerance  -  poor feeding is an early sign of respiratory distress in infants
Report any increase in work of breathing immediately
Educate parents on signs and symptoms requiring emergency care
Maintain developmental care and comfort measures

pleural-effusion-np.title: Plevral Effüzyon Yönetimi
pleural-effusion-np.pathophysiology: Pleural effusions occur when fluid accumulates in the pleural space faster than it can be reabsorbed. Transudative effusions result from systemic factors altering hydrostatic or oncotic pressure (heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome). Exudative effusions result from local inflammatory or malignant processes increasing capillary permeability (pneumonia, malignancy, PE, TB). Light's criteria distinguish transudative from exudative: exudate if pleural/serum protein > 0.5, pleural/serum LDH > 0.6, or pleural LDH > 2/3 upper normal serum LDH.
diagnostics: Order chest X-ray (blunting of costophrenic angle, > 200-300 mL needed for detection)
Order chest ultrasound (can detect effusions as small as 20 mL, guides thoracentesis)
Order diagnostic thoracentesis with fluid analysis: protein, LDH, glucose, pH, cell count/differential, cytology, cultures
Apply Light's criteria to classify transudative vs exudative
Order CT chest with contrast if malignancy suspected
Order BNP if heart failure suspected
management: Treat the underlying cause (diuretics for HF, antibiotics for infection)
Perform therapeutic thoracentesis for symptomatic relief (limit to 1-1.5 L per session to prevent re-expansion pulmonary edema)
Order chest tube for empyema or complicated parapneumonic effusion (pH < 7.2, glucose < 60, positive culture)
Consider indwelling pleural catheter for recurrent malignant effusions
Consider pleurodesis (talc) for recurrent effusions
Refer to interventional pulmonology or thoracic surgery for complex cases

pneumonia-management-np.title: Pnömoni Yönetimi
pneumonia-management-np.pathophysiology: Pneumonia involves microbial invasion of the normally sterile lower respiratory tract, triggering an intense inflammatory response. Neutrophil infiltration, alveolar macrophage activation, and cytokine release cause alveolar filling with purulent exudate (consolidation). The result is V/Q mismatch, intrapulmonary shunting, and hypoxemia. Severity scoring (CURB-65, PSI/PORT) guides disposition decisions: outpatient, inpatient, or ICU admission.
diagnostics: Order chest X-ray (lobar consolidation, interstitial infiltrates, or bilateral patchy opacities)
Order CBC with differential (leukocytosis with left shift)
Order BMP, procalcitonin (bacterial vs viral differentiation)
Order blood cultures × 2 before antibiotics (for ICU patients)
Order sputum culture and sensitivity
Calculate CURB-65 or PSI/PORT score for disposition
Order ABG if hypoxemic or critically ill
Consider CT chest if complications suspected (empyema, lung abscess)
management: Outpatient CAP: Amoxicillin 1g TID (healthy, no risk factors) OR amoxicillin + macrolide (comorbidities)
Inpatient non-ICU: Beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam or ceftriaxone) + macrolide OR respiratory fluoroquinolone alone
ICU: Beta-lactam + macrolide (add anti-MRSA if risk factors)
HAP/VAP: Piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem + vancomycin or linezolid (anti-MRSA)
Initiate antibiotics within 4 hours (1 hour if septic)
Duration: CAP 5-7 days (minimum 5 days, afebrile ≥ 48 hours)
De-escalate based on cultures

pulmonary-fibrosis-basics-rpn.title: Pulmoner Fibrozis Temelleri (/)
pulmonary-fibrosis-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: Pulmonary fibrosis involves progressive scarring (fibrosis) of lung tissue, making the lungs stiff and difficult to expand. Normal, elastic alveolar tissue is replaced by thick collagen deposits that impair gas exchange. Patients experience worsening dyspnea because the lungs cannot expand fully (restrictive disease) and oxygen cannot efficiently diffuse across thickened alveolar walls. The disease is progressive and irreversible.
diagnostics: Monitor respiratory rate, effort, and oxygen saturation
Auscultate breath sounds (listen for fine 'Velcro-like' crackles at bases)
Monitor for increasing dyspnea and exercise intolerance
Assess oxygen needs (at rest and with activity)
Monitor for clubbing of fingertips
management: Administer oxygen as ordered (may need supplemental O2 with activity)
Position upright for optimal breathing
Assist with activities of daily living and energy conservation
Encourage adherence to pulmonary rehabilitation exercises
Provide emotional support (progressive disease can cause anxiety and depression)
nursingActions: Monitor SpO2 at rest and with activity  -  report desaturation with exertion
Assess breath sounds every shift (fine bibasilar crackles are expected but worsening should be reported)
Encourage the patient to pace activities and rest between tasks
Administer medications as ordered and monitor for side effects
Report any acute worsening of dyspnea immediately (may indicate acute exacerbation)
Encourage adequate nutrition (increased caloric needs from increased work of breathing)
Provide psychological support and refer to support groups

respiratory-assessment-rn.title: Solunum Değerlendirme Protokolü
respiratory-assessment-rn.pathophysiology: The RN's respiratory assessment follows a systematic approach integrating subjective data (dyspnea severity, triggers, alleviating factors) with objective findings (inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation). Clinical deterioration follows a predictable trajectory: tachypnea → accessory muscle use → altered mental status → respiratory failure. Early recognition through protocolized assessment and use of early warning scores (MEWS, NEWS) enables timely intervention and prevents adverse outcomes.
diagnostics: Perform systematic respiratory assessment using inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation
Calculate NEWS/MEWS score for early warning detection
Monitor continuous pulse oximetry and capnography for high-risk patients
Assess cough strength and ability to protect airway
Evaluate sputum characteristics if productive
Perform focused assessment with any change in status
Correlate respiratory findings with hemodynamics and labs
management: Implement nursing protocols based on assessment findings
Initiate supplemental oxygen per protocol for SpO2 < 94%
Position for optimal ventilation (HOB ≥ 30 degrees)
Implement aspiration precautions for at-risk patients
Activate rapid response for acute deterioration (NEWS ≥ 7)
Coordinate respiratory therapy consults as indicated
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status minimum every 4 hours and with any status change
Document using standardized respiratory assessment tool
Report NEWS score ≥ 5 to charge RN and provider
Implement evidence-based interventions (IS, early mobilization, HOB elevation)
Assess readiness for oxygen weaning when clinically appropriate
Provide patient education on breathing exercises and activity tolerance
Communicate changes using SBAR framework

respiratory-failure-np.title: Solunum Yetmezliği Yönetimi
respiratory-failure-np.pathophysiology: Respiratory failure occurs when the respiratory system cannot maintain adequate gas exchange. Type I (hypoxemic): PaO2 < 60 mmHg on room air with normal or low PaCO2, caused by V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion impairment (e.g., ARDS, pneumonia, PE). Type II (hypercapnic): PaCO2 > 50 mmHg with respiratory acidosis, caused by alveolar hypoventilation (e.g., COPD, neuromuscular disease, drug overdose). Type III (perioperative) and Type IV (shock-related) are also recognized.
diagnostics: Order ABG (Type I: PaO2 < 60 with normal PaCO2; Type II: PaCO2 > 50 with acidosis)
Calculate A-a gradient: (FiO2 × 713) - (PaCO2/0.8) - PaO2; normal < 10-15 mmHg
Order chest X-ray (bilateral infiltrates, consolidation, hyperinflation)
Calculate P/F ratio for ARDS severity if applicable
Order CBC, BMP, lactate, procalcitonin
Monitor continuous SpO2 and capnography
Assess for underlying cause (PE, infection, drug overdose, neuromuscular)
management: Type I: Supplemental oxygen, HFNC, or NIV; intubation if refractory
Type II: NIV (BiPAP) first-line for COPD; intubation if NIV fails or patient cannot protect airway
Treat underlying cause aggressively
For opioid-induced: naloxone titration
For myasthenic crisis: plasma exchange or IVIG
For PE: anticoagulation ± thrombolysis
Intubation criteria: inability to protect airway, worsening ABG despite NIV, hemodynamic instability, GCS ≤ 8

respiratory-mechanics-np.title: Solunum Mekaniği
respiratory-mechanics-np.pathophysiology: Respiratory mechanics encompass the forces governing lung ventilation: compliance (lung and chest wall distensibility), resistance (airflow through airways), and the work of breathing. Compliance is reduced in fibrotic lung disease, ARDS, and pulmonary edema (stiff lungs). Resistance is increased in asthma, COPD, and any cause of airway narrowing. The diaphragm generates most of the negative inspiratory pressure, and accessory muscle recruitment indicates increased work of breathing. Understanding these mechanics is essential for ventilator management.
diagnostics: Measure static compliance: Vt / (Plateau pressure - PEEP); normal 60-100 mL/cmH2O
Measure dynamic compliance: Vt / (Peak pressure - PEEP); reflects compliance + resistance
Measure airway resistance: (Peak pressure - Plateau pressure) / Flow rate
Interpret pressure-volume curves on the ventilator
Assess auto-PEEP (intrinsic PEEP) with expiratory hold maneuver
Calculate dead space ratio (VD/VT) using capnography
management: Optimize ventilator settings based on mechanics measurements
If low compliance: Consider PEEP titration, recruitment maneuvers, positioning
If high resistance: Bronchodilator therapy, check for tube obstruction, consider larger ETT
If auto-PEEP present: Reduce RR, increase expiratory time (lower I:E ratio), treat bronchospasm
Target plateau pressure < 30 cmH2O to prevent ventilator-induced lung injury
Consider prone positioning for ARDS (improves compliance and V/Q matching)

respiratory-pattern-rpn.title: Solunum Desenleri (/)
respiratory-pattern-rpn.pathophysiology: Breathing is controlled by the respiratory center in the medulla oblongata, which responds to changes in PaCO2, pH, and PaO2 via central and peripheral chemoreceptors. Normal breathing is regular with a rate of 12-20 breaths/min in adults. Abnormal patterns reflect underlying pathology: Kussmaul breathing (deep, rapid) indicates metabolic acidosis, Cheyne-Stokes (crescendo-decrescendo with apnea) suggests heart failure or brain injury, and Biot's breathing (irregular with apnea) indicates brainstem damage.
diagnostics: Count respiratory rate for a full 60 seconds without the patient knowing
Observe depth, rhythm, and pattern of breathing
Note use of accessory muscles
Observe chest wall movement symmetry
Report any abnormal breathing patterns to the nurse immediately
management: Document the specific breathing pattern observed (Kussmaul, Cheyne-Stokes, Biot, agonal)
Position patient for optimal breathing unless contraindicated
Maintain patent airway
Report changes in respiratory pattern promptly
nursingActions: Assess respiratory pattern at least every 4 hours and with any status change
Count rate for full 60 seconds  -  do not estimate
Document pattern, rate, depth, and any abnormalities
Report new abnormal patterns immediately to the RN
Monitor oxygen saturation alongside respiratory pattern assessment
Compare findings to baseline

sarcoidosis-pulmonary-np.title: Pulmoner Sarkoidoz
sarcoidosis-pulmonary-np.pathophysiology: Sarcoidosis is a systemic granulomatous disease of unknown etiology characterized by non-caseating granulomas in affected organs. The lungs and hilar lymph nodes are involved in > 90% of cases. CD4+ T-helper cells and macrophages form granulomas driven by an exaggerated immune response to an unknown antigen. Elevated ACE levels reflect granuloma burden. Unlike TB granulomas, sarcoid granulomas are non-caseating (no central necrosis). Most patients have spontaneous remission, but 20-25% develop progressive pulmonary fibrosis.
diagnostics: Order chest X-ray (Scadding staging: Stage I bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, Stage II BHL + infiltrates, Stage III infiltrates without BHL, Stage IV fibrosis)
Order HRCT chest for detailed pulmonary assessment
Order serum ACE level (elevated in 40-80%, not diagnostic but useful for monitoring)
Order PFTs (may show restrictive, obstructive, or mixed pattern with reduced DLCO)
Order tissue biopsy showing non-caseating granulomas (gold standard for diagnosis)
Order calcium levels (hypercalcemia from granuloma vitamin D production)
Ophthalmologic exam (uveitis screening)
ECG and consider cardiac MRI if cardiac involvement suspected
management: Observe Stage I sarcoidosis  -  60-80% resolve spontaneously within 2 years
Prescribe oral prednisone 20-40 mg/day for progressive or symptomatic pulmonary disease
Taper steroids over 6-12 months to lowest effective dose
Prescribe methotrexate or azathioprine as steroid-sparing agents for chronic disease
Monitor for extrapulmonary involvement (cardiac, neurologic, hepatic, ocular, skin)
Annual ophthalmologic exam for uveitis
Screen for vitamin D deficiency but use cautiously (risk of hypercalcemia)

tb-basics-rpn.title: Tüberküloz Temelleri (/)
tb-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, an acid-fast bacillus transmitted through airborne droplet nuclei. When inhaled, the bacteria reach the alveoli where macrophages attempt to contain them, forming granulomas (tubercles). In most people (90-95%), the immune system contains the infection in a latent state. Reactivation occurs when immunity weakens, allowing bacteria to multiply and cause active disease with cavitary lesions typically in the upper lobes.
diagnostics: Monitor temperature and report fever
Observe for chronic cough, night sweats, and weight loss
Collect sputum specimens as ordered (early morning × 3 consecutive days)
Monitor adherence to medication regimen
Report any adverse effects of TB medications (nausea, jaundice, visual changes)
management: Maintain airborne precautions (negative pressure room, N95 for all staff)
Administer RIPE therapy as ordered (Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol)
Ensure directly observed therapy (DOT)  -  watch patient swallow each dose
Maintain isolation until 3 consecutive negative sputum cultures
Encourage adequate nutrition
Educate on covering cough and proper disposal of tissues
nursingActions: Don N95 respirator before entering the patient's room
Collect early morning sputum specimens as ordered
Administer TB medications as ordered and ensure DOT is followed
Monitor for medication side effects and report promptly
Teach patient that Rifampin turns urine, tears, and sweat orange/red (expected)
Educate patient on importance of completing the full course of treatment (6-9 months)
Report non-adherence to the nurse immediately

tb-management-advanced-np.title: TB Yönetimi İleri Düzey
tb-management-advanced-np.pathophysiology: Drug-resistant TB emerges through selection pressure from inadequate treatment regimens, poor adherence, or transmission of resistant strains. MDR-TB is resistant to at least isoniazid and rifampin. XDR-TB (extensively drug-resistant) is MDR-TB plus resistance to a fluoroquinolone and at least one injectable agent. Treatment requires prolonged courses (9-20+ months) with more toxic second-line drugs. The BPaL regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid) represents a breakthrough for treatment-resistant cases.
diagnostics: Order rapid molecular testing: GeneXpert MTB/RIF for rifampin resistance detection (results in 2 hours)
Order full drug susceptibility testing (DST) on all positive cultures
Order liquid culture (BACTEC MGIT) for faster results vs solid media
Order line probe assays for rapid detection of INH and rifampin resistance
Whole genome sequencing for comprehensive resistance profiling
Order baseline audiometry, visual acuity, hepatic panel, renal function, CBC
Monitor therapeutic drug levels for second-line agents
management: MDR-TB: Build regimen with ≥ 4 effective drugs based on DST (WHO Group A: levofloxacin/moxifloxacin + bedaquiline + linezolid; Group B: clofazimine; Group C: others)
WHO-recommended shorter MDR-TB regimen: BPaL (bedaquiline + pretomanid + linezolid) for 6 months (TB-PRACTECAL trial)
XDR-TB: Individualized regimen based on comprehensive DST
All drug-resistant TB requires DOT
Report to public health immediately
Monthly sputum cultures until conversion
Screen all close contacts

tb-management-basics-rpn.title: TB Yönetimi Temelleri (/)
tb-management-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: TB treatment requires multiple medications taken for an extended period (6-9 months minimum) to kill actively replicating bacteria AND dormant bacilli within granulomas. The standard regimen is RIPE therapy: Rifampin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol for the first 2 months (intensive phase), followed by Rifampin and Isoniazid for 4 months (continuation phase). Treatment failure from non-adherence leads to drug-resistant TB, which is much harder to treat.
diagnostics: Monitor for medication side effects at each visit
Monitor weight monthly
Report signs of hepatotoxicity (jaundice, dark urine, RUQ pain, nausea)
Report visual changes (ethambutol toxicity)
Report numbness or tingling in extremities (INH neuropathy)
Collect follow-up sputum specimens as ordered
management: Administer RIPE medications as ordered
Ensure directly observed therapy (DOT) for each dose
Co-administer pyridoxine (vitamin B6) with isoniazid as ordered
Educate patient on expected side effects vs reportable side effects
Encourage completion of full treatment course
Maintain airborne precautions until cleared by physician
nursingActions: Observe the patient swallow each dose (DOT)
Monitor for and report hepatotoxicity symptoms: nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, jaundice, dark urine
Educate: Rifampin makes body fluids orange/red (expected)
Educate: Avoid alcohol during treatment (increases hepatotoxicity risk)
Monitor for peripheral neuropathy (numbness, tingling)  -  INH side effect
Report any visual changes immediately  -  Ethambutol can cause optic neuritis
Emphasize that stopping medication early leads to drug-resistant TB

tb-management-np.title: TB Yönetimi
tb-management-np.pathophysiology: TB treatment exploits different bacterial populations: actively multiplying bacilli (killed by INH and rifampin), semi-dormant bacilli in acidic environments within macrophages (killed by pyrazinamide), and slowly replicating bacilli (killed by rifampin). The intensive phase (2 months of RIPE) rapidly reduces bacterial burden and prevents resistance. The continuation phase (4 months of INH + rifampin) eliminates persister organisms. Directly observed therapy (DOT) is the standard of care to ensure adherence and prevent resistance.
diagnostics: Order TST (Mantoux) or IGRA (QuantiFERON-Gold, T-SPOT) for screening
TST interpretation: ≥ 5 mm (HIV, close contacts, CXR changes, immunosuppressed), ≥ 10 mm (high-risk groups), ≥ 15 mm (no risk factors)
Order chest X-ray for all positive screening tests
Order 3 sputum specimens for AFB smear and culture if active TB suspected
Order GeneXpert MTB/RIF for rapid diagnosis and rifampin resistance
Baseline labs: CBC, CMP (hepatic panel), visual acuity, uric acid
management: Latent TB: 4 months rifampin (preferred) or 3 months INH + rifapentine (3HP weekly × 12 doses) or 6-9 months INH daily
Active TB intensive phase: RIPE × 2 months
Active TB continuation phase: INH + rifampin × 4 months (extend to 7 months for cavitary disease with positive 2-month culture)
DOT for all active TB patients (preferred for latent TB as well)
Monthly monitoring: symptoms, adherence, hepatic panel (if baseline abnormal, HIV, alcohol use)
Report all active TB cases to public health within 24 hours
Contact investigation required for all active cases

tracheobronchial-injury-np.title: Trakeobronşiyal Yaralanma
tracheobronchial-injury-np.pathophysiology: Tracheobronchial injuries involve disruption of the trachea or main bronchi, most commonly from blunt chest trauma (deceleration injury causing shearing at the carina or cricotracheal junction) or penetrating trauma. The majority of injuries occur within 2.5 cm of the carina. Complete rupture can cause massive air leak, tension pneumothorax, and mediastinal emphysema. Many patients with complete transection die before reaching the hospital. Delayed diagnosis is common (up to 50% of cases) and leads to stenosis and atelectasis.
diagnostics: Order CT chest with 3D airway reconstruction
Order bronchoscopy (gold standard for diagnosis and characterization)
Order chest X-ray (may show pneumomediastinum, persistent pneumothorax, 'fallen lung' sign)
Monitor for persistent air leak despite chest tube placement
Order CT angiography if vascular injury suspected
Monitor continuous SpO2 and hemodynamics
management: Secure airway immediately  -  may need awake fiberoptic intubation or surgical airway
Place chest tube(s) for pneumothorax
Consult thoracic surgery urgently for all confirmed tracheobronchial injuries
Surgical repair for injuries > 1/3 circumference or complete transection
Small injuries (< 1/3 circumference) may heal conservatively with close bronchoscopic monitoring
Maintain continuous bronchoscopic surveillance during intubation to avoid advancing ETT past injury

tracheostomy-basics-rpn.title: Trakeostomi Temelleri (/)
tracheostomy-basics-rpn.pathophysiology: A tracheostomy is a surgical opening in the anterior trachea (usually between the 2nd and 4th tracheal rings) with insertion of a tracheostomy tube to establish an airway. It bypasses the upper airway, eliminating the nose and mouth's ability to warm, humidify, and filter inspired air. The cuff (when inflated) seals the airway for mechanical ventilation and prevents aspiration. Loss of airflow through the vocal cords eliminates the patient's ability to speak unless a speaking valve is used.
diagnostics: Assess tracheostomy site for redness, swelling, drainage, or skin breakdown
Monitor respiratory rate, effort, and oxygen saturation
Assess stoma and surrounding skin integrity
Monitor for tube displacement or obstruction
Observe inner cannula for secretion buildup
management: Maintain tracheostomy in secure position with ties or holder
Keep obturator and spare tracheostomy tube (same size and one size smaller) at bedside at ALL times
Humidify inspired air (trach collar with humidification)
Suction as needed using sterile technique
Provide stoma care every 8 hours and as needed
Keep head of bed elevated 30-45 degrees
nursingActions: Perform tracheostomy care every 8 hours as ordered (clean inner cannula, clean stoma site, change trach ties)
Suction only as needed  -  not on a schedule (assess for adventitious sounds, visible secretions, desaturation)
Use sterile technique for suctioning (apply suction only while withdrawing, maximum 10-15 seconds per pass)
Monitor cuff pressure every 8 hours if applicable (20-25 cmH2O)
Keep emergency equipment at bedside: obturator, spare trach tube (same size + one size smaller), suction
Report any signs of tube dislodgement, skin breakdown, or respiratory distress immediately

tracheostomy-care-rpn.title: Trakeostomi Bakımı (/)
tracheostomy-care-rpn.pathophysiology: Tracheostomy care involves maintaining the patency and cleanliness of the artificial airway and surrounding stoma site. The warm, moist environment around the stoma promotes bacterial colonization and skin breakdown if not properly maintained. Inner cannula cleaning removes accumulated mucus and biofilm that can obstruct airflow. Proper trach tie management prevents accidental decannulation while avoiding excessive pressure on surrounding skin. Consistent, skilled care prevents complications including infection, granulation tissue formation, and tracheal stenosis.
diagnostics: Assess stoma site for signs of infection (redness, purulent drainage, warmth, odor)
Assess peristomal skin integrity (breakdown, maceration, granulation tissue)
Assess inner cannula patency
Monitor tracheostomy tie tension (should fit one finger between tie and neck)
Assess for proper tube positioning
management: Clean inner cannula every 8 hours or as ordered (hydrogen peroxide or NS per facility policy, then rinse with NS)
Clean stoma site with NS and pat dry with gauze
Change tracheostomy ties when soiled (two-person procedure for safety)
Apply split gauze dressing around stoma (do NOT cut gauze  -  loose fibers can be aspirated)
Provide skin barrier cream around stoma as ordered
nursingActions: Gather all supplies before beginning care (inner cannula brush, NS, suction equipment, clean ties, split gauze)
Perform hand hygiene and don clean gloves
Suction the tracheostomy before cleaning (removes secretions for better assessment)
Remove and clean inner cannula using facility-approved solution, rinse with NS, reinsert
Clean stoma with NS-moistened gauze using circular motions from stoma outward
Apply pre-cut (not scissor-cut) trach dressing under faceplate
Change trach ties with a second person present (one person holds tube to prevent dislodgement)
Document care provided and assessment findings

ventilator-assessment-rn.title: Ventilatör Değerlendirmesi
ventilator-assessment-rn.pathophysiology: Mechanical ventilation delivers positive pressure breaths to support gas exchange when the patient cannot maintain adequate ventilation or oxygenation independently. Positive pressure reverses normal physiologic negative-pressure breathing, which impacts hemodynamics (decreased venous return, potential hypotension) and requires vigilant monitoring. The nurse must understand ventilator settings (mode, rate, FiO2, PEEP, tidal volume), alarm parameters, and how to rapidly assess and intervene when alarms activate.
diagnostics: Verify ventilator settings match the order at the beginning of each shift and after any changes
Monitor and document ventilator settings every 2-4 hours (mode, RR, Vt, FiO2, PEEP, peak pressure, plateau pressure)
Assess patient-ventilator synchrony
Monitor ABGs as ordered (30-60 minutes after setting changes)
Monitor continuous SpO2 and EtCO2 if available
Assess readiness for weaning using SBT criteria
management: Maintain head of bed ≥ 30-45 degrees (VAP prevention)
Perform oral care with chlorhexidine 0.12% every 12 hours (or per facility protocol)
Assess sedation level using RASS scale; target per order
Implement daily sedation interruption protocol as ordered
Assess readiness for spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) daily
Respond to ventilator alarms immediately  -  NEVER silence an alarm without assessing the patient first
nursingActions: Verify ETT position and mark at teeth/lip at beginning of each shift
Perform comprehensive ventilator check every 2-4 hours (settings, alarms, circuit, humidifier)
Respond to high-pressure alarm: assess for biting, coughing, bronchospasm, mucus plug, pneumothorax, fighting ventilator
Respond to low-pressure alarm: assess for disconnection, cuff leak, circuit leak
Suction only as needed (not routinely) using closed suction system
Maintain ETT cuff pressure 20-30 cmH2O
Implement VAP bundle (HOB elevation, oral care, daily sedation vacation, daily SBT assessment, DVT prophylaxis, stress ulcer prophylaxis)
Monitor for signs of auto-PEEP in obstructive disease patients

ventilator-associated-pneumonia-np.title: Ventilatörle İlişkili Pnömoni
ventilator-associated-pneumonia-np.pathophysiology: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) is a hospital-acquired pneumonia developing ≥ 48 hours after endotracheal intubation. Microaspiration of oropharyngeal secretions past the ETT cuff is the primary mechanism. Biofilm formation on the ETT provides a reservoir for pathogenic organisms. Common causative organisms include Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella, and Acinetobacter. VAP increases mortality by 20-30%, prolongs ICU stay by 4-13 days, and significantly increases healthcare costs.
diagnostics: Clinical criteria: new/progressive infiltrate on CXR + 2 of: fever > 38°C, leukocytosis or leukopenia, purulent secretions
Order quantitative tracheal aspirate or BAL culture (≥ 10⁵ CFU/mL for tracheal aspirate, ≥ 10⁴ for BAL)
Order blood cultures × 2
Order procalcitonin to guide antibiotic duration
Calculate CPIS (Clinical Pulmonary Infection Score) if available
Order CBC with differential, CMP
management: Initiate empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of suspected VAP
Empiric coverage: antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or meropenem) + coverage for MRSA (vancomycin or linezolid) if risk factors
De-escalate antibiotics within 48-72 hours based on culture results
Duration: 7 days (do not extend unless inadequate response)
Discontinue anti-MRSA agent if cultures are negative for MRSA
Use procalcitonin to guide antibiotic discontinuation

ventilator-bundle-np.title: Ventilatör Paketi: Kanıta Dayalı Uygulama
ventilator-bundle-np.pathophysiology: The ventilator bundle is a set of evidence-based interventions that, when implemented together, significantly reduce VAP incidence and ventilator days. Each component addresses a specific pathophysiologic mechanism: HOB elevation reduces gastric aspiration, oral care reduces oropharyngeal bacterial colonization, sedation vacations allow neurologic assessment and reduce delirium, daily SBT assessment facilitates early liberation, DVT and stress ulcer prophylaxis prevent complications of critical illness. Bundle compliance has been shown to reduce VAP rates by 50-70%.
diagnostics: Monitor bundle compliance rates daily (checklist-based)
Track VAP rates per 1000 ventilator days
Audit daily SBT documentation
Verify sedation level (RASS) documentation every 4 hours
Monitor days on mechanical ventilation
Track extubation success rates
management: Implement the 5-component ventilator bundle:
1. HOB elevation 30-45 degrees (prevents aspiration)
2. Oral care with chlorhexidine 0.12% every 12 hours (reduces oropharyngeal colonization)
3. Daily sedation interruption (assess neurologic status, reduce delirium)
4. Daily assessment for SBT readiness (FiO2 ≤ 40%, PEEP ≤ 8, hemodynamically stable, no active sedation drip increase)
5. DVT prophylaxis (enoxaparin or SCD) + stress ulcer prophylaxis (PPI or H2 blocker)
Use daily multidisciplinary rounds to ensure bundle compliance
Order SBT protocol: 30-120 minute trial on PSV 5-8/PEEP 5 or T-piece

copd-exacerbation.title: KOAH: Hava Yolu ve Oksijenleme
copd-exacerbation.pathophysiology:
Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) is a progressive, largely irreversible obstructive airway disease characterized by persistent airflow limitation due to chronic inflammation of the airways and lung parenchyma. COPD encompasses two overlapping pathological processes: chronic bronchitis and emphysema. Understanding the cellular and molecular mechanisms driving COPD is essential for nurses to anticipate clinical deterioration, interpret diagnostic findings, and deliver evidence-based care during acute exacerbations.

Cellular Pathophysiology of COPD:

The pathogenesis of COPD begins with chronic exposure to noxious particles and gases, most commonly cigarette smoke. Tobacco smoke contains over 4,700 chemical compounds, including oxidants, aldehydes, and particulate matter, which directly injure the respiratory epithelium and trigger a sustained inflammatory response.

1. Inflammatory Cell Recruitment and Activation:
Inhaled irritants activate alveolar macrophages and airway epithelial cells, which release chemotactic factors including interleukin-8 (IL-8), leukotriene B4 (LTB4), and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α). These mediators recruit neutrophils, CD8+ cytotoxic T-lymphocytes, and additional macrophages to the airways and lung parenchyma. Unlike asthma, which is predominantly an eosinophilic process mediated by CD4+ T-helper cells, COPD inflammation is primarily neutrophilic and involves CD8+ T-cells. This distinction is clinically significant because COPD inflammation responds poorly to corticosteroids compared to asthma.

2. Protease-Antiprotease Imbalance:
Activated neutrophils release serine proteases, particularly neutrophil elastase, which degrades elastin fibers in the alveolar walls. Normally, alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT), a serine protease inhibitor produced by the liver, neutralizes neutrophil elastase and protects lung tissue. In COPD, the overwhelming neutrophilic inflammation tips the balance in favor of proteolytic destruction. Patients with genetic alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (homozygous PiZZ phenotype) develop severe, early-onset emphysema (often by age 30-40) because they lack this critical protective mechanism. Oxidative stress from cigarette smoke also directly inactivates alpha-1 antitrypsin, further amplifying tissue destruction.

3. Oxidative Stress:
Cigarette smoke introduces massive quantities of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and free radicals into the airways. These oxidants directly damage lipid membranes, proteins, and DNA in epithelial cells. Additionally, activated neutrophils and macrophages generate endogenous ROS through the NADPH oxidase pathway during the respiratory burst. The resulting oxidative stress overwhelms the lung's antioxidant defenses (glutathione, superoxide dismutase, catalase), leading to epithelial injury, mucus hypersecretion, plasma exudation, and amplification of the inflammatory cascade. Oxidative stress also activates nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-κB), a transcription factor that upregulates the expression of inflammatory genes, creating a self-perpetuating cycle of inflammation and tissue damage.

4. Emphysema: Alveolar Wall Destruction:
The protease-antiprotease imbalance and oxidative stress destroy the alveolar septa (walls between adjacent alveoli). Loss of alveolar walls has several critical consequences at the cellular and organ level:
- Reduced Gas Exchange Surface Area: The normal lung has approximately 300 million alveoli providing 70 square meters of gas exchange surface. Destruction of alveolar septa dramatically reduces this surface area, impairing oxygen diffusion from the alveoli into pulmonary capillary blood. This manifests clinically as progressive exertional dyspnea and declining DLCO on pulmonary function testing.
- Loss of Elastic Recoil: Elastin fibers in the alveolar walls provide the elastic recoil force that drives passive expiration. When these fibers are destroyed, the lung loses its ability to recoil during expiration, leading to air trapping and hyperinflation. The residual volume (RV) and total lung capacity (TLC) increase, while the inspiratory capacity decreases. Clinically, this manifests as the barrel-shaped chest characteristic of advanced emphysema.
- Small Airway Collapse: The alveolar septa normally provide radial traction that holds small airways open during expiration. When the septa are destroyed, small airways lose this structural support and collapse prematurely during expiration, trapping air distally. This dynamic airway collapse is worsened during forced expiration (coughing, exertion), which increases intrathoracic pressure and further compresses the unsupported airways.
- Pulmonary Capillary Destruction: The alveolar septa contain the pulmonary capillary bed. Destruction of septa simultaneously destroys capillaries, reducing the total cross-sectional area of the pulmonary vascular bed. Over time, this contributes to pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale (right heart failure).

5. Chronic Bronchitis: Airway Inflammation and Mucus Hypersecretion:
Chronic irritation of the bronchial mucosa causes hypertrophy and hyperplasia of mucus-secreting goblet cells and submucosal glands (Reid index >50%). The excessive mucus production narrows the airway lumen and impairs mucociliary clearance. Chronic inflammation causes thickening of the bronchial wall from edema, inflammatory cell infiltration, and peribronchial fibrosis. Smooth muscle hypertrophy adds to airway narrowing. The combination of luminal mucus plugging, mucosal edema, and wall thickening creates fixed airflow obstruction in the conducting airways.

6. Ventilation-Perfusion (V/Q) Mismatch:
COPD causes both V/Q mismatch and shunting, which are the primary mechanisms of hypoxemia:
- Low V/Q areas (shunt effect): Mucus-plugged or poorly ventilated alveoli that are still perfused result in blood passing through the lungs without adequate gas exchange. This is the predominant mechanism in chronic bronchitis.
- High V/Q areas (dead space): Destroyed alveoli with intact ventilation but lost perfusion create dead space ventilation. This is characteristic of emphysema.
- The resulting hypoxemia stimulates the hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction reflex, where pulmonary arterioles constrict in response to low alveolar oxygen tension. While this reflex optimizes V/Q matching in localized disease, widespread hypoxic vasoconstriction in advanced COPD increases pulmonary vascular resistance, leading to pulmonary hypertension.

7. CO2 Retention and the Hypoxic Drive:
In advanced COPD, chronic hypoventilation (from increased dead space and respiratory muscle fatigue) leads to chronic CO2 retention (hypercapnia). The kidneys compensate by retaining bicarbonate (metabolic compensation), partially normalizing the pH. Over time, the central chemoreceptors in the medulla become desensitized to elevated CO2 levels. The patient's respiratory drive shifts from the normal CO2-driven mechanism to a hypoxic drive, where peripheral chemoreceptors in the carotid and aortic bodies sense low PaO2 and stimulate breathing. This is why high-flow oxygen can be dangerous in COPD patients: raising the PaO2 too quickly removes the hypoxic stimulus to breathe, potentially causing respiratory depression and CO2 narcosis. Target SpO2 for COPD patients is 88-92%.

Clinical Manifestations and Pathophysiological Rationale:
- Barrel Chest: Chronic hyperinflation from air trapping increases the anteroposterior diameter of the thorax.
- Pursed-Lip Breathing: Patients instinctively exhale through pursed lips to create back-pressure that stents open the collapsing small airways, reducing air trapping. This is a compensatory mechanism that nurses should encourage.
- Clubbing of Fingers: Chronic hypoxemia causes proliferation of connective tissue in the nail beds, resulting in a bulbous appearance of the distal phalanges.
- Productive Cough: Chronic mucus hypersecretion in bronchitis leads to daily sputum production. A change in sputum color from white/clear to green/yellow suggests bacterial superinfection and exacerbation.
- Accessory Muscle Use: As airflow obstruction worsens, the diaphragm becomes flattened from hyperinflation and loses its mechanical advantage. Patients recruit sternocleidomastoid, scalene, and intercostal muscles to generate the negative intrathoracic pressure needed for inspiration.
- Respiratory Acidosis: Chronic CO2 retention results in a compensated respiratory acidosis (elevated PaCO2 with elevated HCO3 and near-normal pH). During an acute exacerbation, the PaCO2 rises further, overwhelming compensation and causing acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis with falling pH.
- CO2 Narcosis: Severely elevated CO2 acts as a central nervous system depressant, causing confusion, somnolence, headache, and eventually coma. This is a medical emergency requiring ventilatory support.
- Silent Chest: In severe exacerbation, airflow may be so severely limited that wheezing disappears (no air movement to generate the sound). A silent chest in a COPD patient is an ominous sign of impending respiratory arrest.

Exacerbation Triggers and Cellular Mechanisms:
Acute COPD exacerbations are typically triggered by viral infections (rhinovirus, influenza), bacterial infections (Haemophilus influenzae, Moraxella catarrhalis, Streptococcus pneumoniae), air pollution, or non-compliance with maintenance medications. Infections amplify the baseline inflammation, increasing neutrophil and macrophage activation, worsening mucus production, and causing additional bronchospasm. The resulting increased airflow obstruction worsens air trapping, V/Q mismatch, and hypoxemia, potentially tipping the patient from stable chronic disease into acute respiratory failure.

Nursing Implications:
- Oxygen therapy must be titrated carefully (1-2 L/min via nasal cannula, target SpO2 88-92%) to avoid suppressing the hypoxic drive.
- Bronchodilators (beta-2 agonists and anticholinergics) relax bronchial smooth muscle and reduce airflow obstruction. They do not reverse the underlying structural damage.
- Systemic corticosteroids during exacerbations reduce airway inflammation and edema, shortening recovery time. However, long-term systemic steroids carry significant risks (osteoporosis, immunosuppression, hyperglycemia, myopathy).
- Smoking cessation is the only intervention proven to slow the rate of FEV1 decline and alter the natural history of COPD. Nurses play a critical role in motivational interviewing and referral to cessation programs.
- Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance, reduces dyspnea, and decreases hospitalization rates, even though it does not improve lung function. The benefits come from improved skeletal muscle conditioning, breathing technique optimization, and psychosocial support.

Cor Pulmonale and Right Heart Failure:
Chronic hypoxemia in advanced COPD causes sustained pulmonary vasoconstriction, which progressively increases pulmonary vascular resistance. Additionally, the destruction of pulmonary capillaries within emphysematous tissue reduces the total cross-sectional area of the pulmonary vascular bed. The right ventricle, which is a thin-walled, low-pressure chamber designed to pump blood through the normally low-resistance pulmonary circulation, must generate increasingly higher pressures to overcome this elevated resistance. Over time, the right ventricle undergoes compensatory hypertrophy, but eventually the myocardial oxygen demand exceeds supply, and right ventricular dilation and failure ensue. Clinical signs of cor pulmonale include jugular venous distension (JVD), peripheral edema (particularly dependent edema in the lower extremities and sacrum), hepatomegaly (from hepatic congestion), and a right-sided S3 heart sound. Nurses should monitor for these signs in all patients with advanced COPD, as the development of cor pulmonale significantly worsens prognosis.

Acute Exacerbation Management Pathway:
During an acute COPD exacerbation, the nursing priorities follow a systematic approach:
1. Assess airway patency and work of breathing immediately upon presentation.
2. Apply supplemental oxygen via nasal cannula at 1-2 L/min, targeting SpO2 88-92%. Use a Venturi mask if precise FiO2 delivery is needed.
3. Obtain arterial blood gas (ABG) to establish baseline PaCO2 and pH. Compare with previous ABGs to differentiate acute from chronic hypercapnia.
4. Administer nebulized short-acting bronchodilators: albuterol (SABA) combined with ipratropium (SAMA) for synergistic bronchodilation via different receptor mechanisms.
5. Administer systemic corticosteroids (prednisone 40 mg PO daily for 5 days, or methylprednisolone IV if unable to take oral) to reduce airway inflammation.
6. Initiate antibiotics if there are signs of bacterial infection: increased sputum purulence, increased sputum volume, and worsening dyspnea (Anthonisen criteria).
7. Consider non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP) for hypercapnic respiratory failure (PaCO2 >45 with pH <7.35). BiPAP reduces work of breathing, improves alveolar ventilation, and decreases the need for intubation.
8. Prepare for intubation if the patient shows signs of respiratory failure despite BiPAP: deteriorating mental status, worsening acidosis, hemodynamic instability.

GOLD Classification and Staging:
The Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) classifies COPD severity by post-bronchodilator FEV1 percentage predicted:
- GOLD 1 (Mild): FEV1 ≥80% predicted
- GOLD 2 (Moderate): FEV1 50-79% predicted
- GOLD 3 (Severe): FEV1 30-49% predicted
- GOLD 4 (Very Severe): FEV1 <30% predicted
Understanding the GOLD stage helps nurses anticipate the level of respiratory impairment, medication requirements, and risk of exacerbation. Patients with GOLD 3-4 are at highest risk for acute exacerbations requiring hospitalization.

Patient Education Priorities:
- Proper inhaler technique is critical: studies show that up to 90% of patients use inhalers incorrectly, dramatically reducing medication delivery to the lungs. Nurses should demonstrate technique and have patients perform return demonstration at every visit.
- Annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination reduce the risk of respiratory infections that trigger exacerbations.
- Recognition of early exacerbation signs (increased dyspnea, change in sputum color/volume, increased cough) empowers patients to seek treatment early, reducing hospitalization rates.
- Energy conservation techniques (pacing activities, using assistive devices, organizing the home environment) help patients maintain independence despite limited respiratory reserve.
- Nutritional counseling is important because many COPD patients develop cachexia from the increased metabolic demands of breathing, chronic inflammation, and the anorexic effects of dyspnea. Small, frequent, high-calorie meals are better tolerated than large meals, which can cause diaphragmatic compression and worsen dyspnea.

Pulmonary rehabilitation following acute COPD exacerbation addresses the systemic consequences of chronic hypoxia and deconditioning at the cellular level. Skeletal muscle dysfunction in COPD involves a shift from Type I (slow-twitch, oxidative) muscle fibers to Type II (fast-twitch, glycolytic) fibers, reducing exercise capacity and endurance. This fiber-type shift results from chronic systemic inflammation (elevated TNF-alpha, IL-6, and IL-8), physical inactivity, corticosteroid-induced myopathy, and tissue hypoxia that impairs mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation in peripheral muscles. The reduced mitochondrial density and oxidative enzyme activity (citrate synthase, succinate dehydrogenase) in peripheral muscles of COPD patients means that even modest physical activity rapidly depletes available ATP through anaerobic glycolysis, producing lactic acid that triggers dyspnea perception through metaboreceptor stimulation. Pulmonary rehabilitation programs that combine aerobic and resistance training can partially reverse this fiber-type shift by upregulating peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma coactivator 1-alpha (PGC-1alpha), the master regulator of mitochondrial biogenesis, increasing oxidative enzyme expression and capillary density in trained muscles. This improves peripheral oxygen extraction efficiency, reducing ventilatory demand for a given workload and improving exercise tolerance and dyspnea scores. Early mobilization within 24-48 hours of exacerbation stabilization reduces the deconditioning spiral and is associated with shorter hospital stays and reduced 90-day readmission rates.
diagnostics: Monitor oxygen saturation continuously (target 88-92%)
Expect ABG to be drawn to assess CO2 retention
Expect chest X-ray to be ordered
Monitor sputum color, amount, and consistency
Expect CBC and metabolic panel
Expect pulmonary function tests when stable
management: Position in high-Fowler's or tripod position
Administer low-flow oxygen as ordered (1-2 L/min)
Administer bronchodilators and steroids as prescribed
Encourage pursed-lip breathing
Maintain adequate hydration to thin secretions
Assist with energy conservation techniques
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory rate, depth, and oxygen saturation frequently
Report O2 sat < 88% or signs of CO2 narcosis immediately
Assess breath sounds every shift and report changes
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring)
Encourage and assist with incentive spirometry
Document sputum characteristics
Educate on smoking cessation and inhaler technique

asthma-emergency.title: Astım ve Status Asthmaticus
asthma-emergency.pathophysiology:
Asthma is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways characterized by variable airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and underlying airway inflammation. During an acute asthma attack, three pathological processes converge to severely limit airflow: bronchoconstriction, airway edema, and mucus plugging. Status Asthmaticus represents the most severe and life-threatening end of the spectrum—a prolonged, severe asthma attack that is refractory to standard bronchodilator therapy and constitutes a medical emergency requiring aggressive intervention to prevent respiratory arrest and death.

Cellular Pathophysiology of Asthma:

1. The Inflammatory Cascade:
Asthma is fundamentally an immune-mediated inflammatory disease. In allergic (atopic) asthma, which accounts for approximately 60-80% of cases, the process begins with sensitization to environmental allergens (dust mites, pollen, animal dander, mold spores). Antigen-presenting cells in the airway mucosa (dendritic cells) capture and process inhaled allergens, presenting them to naive CD4+ T-helper lymphocytes. This drives differentiation toward the Th2 phenotype, which releases a characteristic cytokine profile:
- Interleukin-4 (IL-4): Stimulates B-lymphocyte class switching to produce allergen-specific IgE antibodies.
- Interleukin-5 (IL-5): Recruits and activates eosinophils, the hallmark inflammatory cell in asthma.
- Interleukin-13 (IL-13): Induces goblet cell hyperplasia (mucus hypersecretion) and promotes airway smooth muscle contractility and subepithelial fibrosis.

The allergen-specific IgE antibodies bind to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on the surface of mast cells in the airway mucosa. Upon re-exposure to the allergen, cross-linking of surface IgE molecules triggers mast cell degranulation—the explosive release of preformed and newly synthesized mediators that initiate the acute asthmatic response.

2. Early-Phase Asthmatic Response (Minutes):
Mast cell degranulation releases preformed mediators stored in cytoplasmic granules:
- Histamine: Causes rapid bronchial smooth muscle contraction, increased vascular permeability (mucosal edema), and mucus secretion. Acts on H1 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle.
- Tryptase: A serine protease that activates protease-activated receptors (PARs) on epithelial cells and smooth muscle, amplifying bronchoconstriction.
- Heparin: Modulates the local inflammatory response.

Simultaneously, arachidonic acid is liberated from mast cell membranes by phospholipase A2 and metabolized through two enzymatic pathways:
- Cyclooxygenase (COX) pathway: Produces prostaglandin D2 (PGD2), a potent bronchoconstrictor and vasodilator.
- Lipoxygenase pathway: Produces cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4)—formerly known as 'slow-reacting substance of anaphylaxis' (SRS-A). These are the most potent bronchoconstrictors known, approximately 1,000 times more potent than histamine. They also increase vascular permeability and stimulate mucus secretion. This is the mechanism targeted by leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast/Singulair).

The early-phase response occurs within 5-30 minutes of allergen exposure and causes acute bronchoconstriction. It typically resolves within 1-2 hours.

3. Late-Phase Asthmatic Response (4-8 Hours):
Chemotactic factors released during the early phase recruit inflammatory cells to the airways, initiating the late-phase response:
- Eosinophils: The signature cell of asthma. Eosinophils release major basic protein (MBP), eosinophil cationic protein (ECP), and eosinophil peroxidase (EPO), which are directly cytotoxic to airway epithelial cells. Epithelial denudation exposes sensory nerve endings, making the airways hyperresponsive to stimuli. Eosinophil-derived mediators also cause sustained bronchoconstriction and mucus hypersecretion.
- Neutrophils: More prominent in severe, steroid-resistant asthma. Release elastase and reactive oxygen species that amplify tissue damage.
- Basophils: Additional source of histamine and leukotrienes.
- Lymphocytes: CD4+ Th2 cells perpetuate the inflammatory cycle through continued cytokine production.

The late-phase response causes prolonged bronchoconstriction, persistent airway edema, and mucus plugging that can last 24 hours or longer. This phase is more responsive to corticosteroids than the early phase.

4. Bronchoconstriction—The Mechanical Obstruction:
Airway smooth muscle in asthmatic patients is both quantitatively increased (hypertrophy and hyperplasia) and qualitatively abnormal (enhanced contractility). During an attack, multiple stimuli trigger smooth muscle contraction:
- Direct mediator effects (histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins)
- Parasympathetic (vagal) reflexes via acetylcholine acting on muscarinic M3 receptors
- Sensory nerve stimulation (substance P, neurokinin A)

The bronchial smooth muscle wraps helically around the airways. When it contracts, the airway lumen narrows concentrically. Because airway resistance is inversely proportional to the fourth power of the radius (Poiseuille's law), even a small reduction in airway diameter causes a dramatic increase in resistance. A 50% reduction in radius increases resistance 16-fold.

5. Mucus Plugging—The Silent Killer:
Goblet cell hyperplasia (driven by IL-13) and submucosal gland hypertrophy produce excessive, abnormally thick mucus. In severe asthma, this mucus can form dense plugs composed of inspissated mucus, shed epithelial cells, eosinophils, and Charcot-Leyden crystals (crystallized eosinophil proteins). These mucus plugs physically occlude the airway lumen, creating areas of complete obstruction. Air can enter past partial plugs during inspiration (when negative intrathoracic pressure dilates the airways) but cannot escape during expiration (when positive pressure compresses the airways around the plug), causing progressive air trapping, hyperinflation, and ventilation-perfusion mismatch.

In fatal asthma cases, autopsy consistently reveals extensive mucus plugging as the primary cause of asphyxiation, often more significant than bronchospasm alone. This underscores the importance of aggressive hydration and mucolytics in severe attacks.

6. Airway Remodeling—The Long-Term Consequence:
Repeated cycles of injury and repair in poorly controlled asthma lead to irreversible structural changes:
- Subepithelial Fibrosis: Deposition of collagen types I, III, and V below the basement membrane thickens the airway wall and contributes to fixed airflow obstruction.
- Smooth Muscle Hypertrophy/Hyperplasia: Increases the mass of contractile tissue, amplifying bronchoconstriction.
- Mucous Gland Hyperplasia: Increases baseline mucus production.
- Neovascularization: New blood vessel formation in the bronchial wall increases the capacity for mucosal edema and inflammatory cell delivery.
- Epithelial Changes: Loss of ciliated epithelium and replacement with squamous cells impairs mucociliary clearance.

These structural changes explain why long-standing, poorly controlled asthma can develop a fixed obstructive component that mimics COPD and no longer fully responds to bronchodilators.

Status Asthmaticus—The Critical Emergency:

Status asthmaticus is defined as a severe asthma exacerbation that fails to respond to repeated doses of inhaled beta-2 agonists and systemic corticosteroids. At the cellular level, this represents overwhelming inflammation with extensive mucus plugging, maximal bronchospasm, and severe airway edema that creates near-complete airflow obstruction.

Progressive Pathophysiology:
1. Severe air trapping leads to dynamic hyperinflation: the functional residual capacity (FRC) progressively increases, placing the diaphragm at a mechanical disadvantage (flattened, shortened).
2. The patient must generate increasingly negative intrathoracic pressures to inhale, dramatically increasing the work of breathing.
3. Respiratory muscle fatigue develops as oxygen demand exceeds supply. The metabolically active respiratory muscles consume up to 25% of total cardiac output (compared to 5% at rest).
4. As air trapping worsens, intrathoracic pressure rises, impeding venous return to the heart. This can cause pulsus paradoxus (>10 mmHg drop in systolic BP during inspiration)—a sign of severe obstruction.
5. Progressive hyperinflation can compress the heart, reducing cardiac output and causing hemodynamic compromise.
6. Eventually, respiratory muscle fatigue leads to hypoventilation: PaCO2 rises (initially normalized from hyperventilation, then elevating). A 'normal' or rising PaCO2 in an acutely asthmatic patient is an ominous sign of impending respiratory failure.

Critical Clinical Signs and Their Cellular Basis:
- Expiratory Wheezing: Air turbulence through narrowed airways produces the characteristic musical sound. Wheezing is heard during expiration because positive intrathoracic pressure further narrows already obstructed airways.
- Silent Chest: The most dangerous sign. Airflow is so severely restricted that there is insufficient air movement to generate wheezing. This indicates near-complete airway obstruction and imminent respiratory arrest. The absence of wheezing in a dyspneic patient is far more alarming than loud wheezing.
- Paradoxical Breathing: When the diaphragm fatigues, it moves paradoxically (upward during inspiration instead of downward). The abdomen retracts inward during inspiration instead of expanding outward. This is a sign of respiratory muscle failure and mandates immediate intubation.
- Inability to Speak: The patient cannot generate enough airflow to produce voice. Speech requires coordinated expiratory airflow, and the inability to speak in full sentences (or at all) quantifies the severity of obstruction.
- Pulsus Paradoxus: A drop in systolic blood pressure >10 mmHg during inspiration reflects the exaggerated intrathoracic pressure swings caused by severe air trapping.
- Respiratory Acidosis: Initially, asthma attacks cause respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation lowers PaCO2). As the attack progresses and muscles fatigue, PaCO2 normalizes, then rises. A PaCO2 >42 mmHg in an acute asthma attack indicates severe obstruction and impending respiratory failure.

Nursing Implications for Emergency Management:
- Peak Flow Monitoring: Peak expiratory flow rate (PEFR) objectively quantifies airflow obstruction. Values <50% of personal best indicate severe exacerbation; <25% is life-threatening.
- Continuous Pulse Oximetry: SpO2 <90% indicates severe hypoxemia. However, SpO2 may be preserved until late in the attack because hyperventilation maintains oxygenation even as CO2 rises.
- Beta-2 Agonist Administration: Albuterol binds to beta-2 adrenergic receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, activating adenylate cyclase, increasing intracellular cAMP, and causing smooth muscle relaxation. In status asthmaticus, continuous nebulization (10-15 mg/hour) may be required.
- Systemic Corticosteroids: Methylprednisolone or prednisone suppress the inflammatory cascade at multiple points: inhibiting phospholipase A2 (blocking leukotriene and prostaglandin synthesis), reducing cytokine production, decreasing eosinophil survival, and reducing mucosal edema. Effects take 4-6 hours, underscoring the need for early administration.
- Magnesium Sulfate: IV magnesium acts as a smooth muscle relaxant by competing with calcium at the neuromuscular junction and directly relaxing bronchial smooth muscle. Reserved for severe, refractory cases.
- Avoid Beta-Blockers: Beta-blockers (propranolol, atenolol, metoprolol) block beta-2 receptors in the bronchial smooth muscle, preventing bronchodilation and potentially triggering severe, fatal bronchospasm. This includes ophthalmic beta-blocker eye drops (timolol), which can be absorbed systemically.
- Heliox: A mixture of helium and oxygen (typically 80:20 or 70:30) that has lower density than nitrogen-oxygen mixtures. The reduced gas density decreases turbulent airflow through narrowed airways, reducing the work of breathing and improving ventilation. Used as a bridge therapy in severe cases.
- Preparation for Intubation: If the patient shows signs of respiratory failure (rising PaCO2, altered consciousness, paradoxical breathing, silent chest), endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation are required. Post-intubation management of status asthmaticus is challenging due to severe air trapping and high airway pressures.

Ventilator Management in Status Asthmaticus:
Mechanical ventilation in severe asthma requires a fundamentally different approach than ventilation for other causes of respiratory failure:
- Permissive Hypercapnia: The priority is to allow adequate exhalation time to reduce air trapping, even if this means accepting elevated PaCO2. Target pH >7.20 rather than normal PaCO2.
- Prolonged Expiratory Time: Use low respiratory rates (8-12 breaths/min) with short inspiratory times to maximize the expiratory phase (I:E ratio of 1:3 to 1:5). This allows trapped air to escape before the next breath.
- Low Tidal Volumes: 6-8 mL/kg ideal body weight to minimize peak airway pressures and reduce the risk of barotrauma.
- Monitor for Auto-PEEP: Air trapping creates intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP) that is not displayed on the ventilator. Auto-PEEP increases intrathoracic pressure, reduces venous return, and can cause cardiovascular collapse. The nurse should observe for signs: increasing peak pressures, hemodynamic instability, or failure of the ventilator flow waveform to return to zero before the next breath.
- Deep Sedation: Often required to prevent patient-ventilator dyssynchrony. Ketamine is sometimes preferred because it has bronchodilatory properties in addition to its sedative effects.

Biologic Therapies for Severe Asthma:
For patients with severe, refractory asthma that is poorly controlled despite maximal inhaler therapy and oral corticosteroids, biologic monoclonal antibody therapies targeting specific inflammatory pathways have transformed management:
- Omalizumab (Xolair): Anti-IgE antibody. Binds circulating IgE, preventing it from attaching to mast cells. Indicated for moderate-severe allergic asthma with elevated serum IgE.
- Mepolizumab (Nucala), Reslizumab (Cinqair), Benralizumab (Fasenra): Anti-IL-5 or anti-IL-5 receptor antibodies. Target eosinophilic inflammation by reducing eosinophil production and survival. Indicated for severe eosinophilic asthma.
- Dupilumab (Dupixent): Anti-IL-4/IL-13 receptor antibody. Blocks both Th2 cytokines, reducing eosinophilic inflammation, mucus production, and airway remodeling.
These therapies are administered by subcutaneous injection every 2-8 weeks and have significantly reduced exacerbation rates and oral corticosteroid dependence in eligible patients. Nurses administering biologics should monitor for injection site reactions and anaphylaxis (observe patient for 30 minutes after first several doses).

Asthma Action Plans:
Every asthma patient should have a written asthma action plan based on the traffic light system:
- Green Zone (>80% personal best peak flow): Well-controlled. Continue daily controller medications.
- Yellow Zone (50-80% personal best): Caution. Symptoms increasing. Add rescue inhaler, may need short course of oral steroids. Contact provider.
- Red Zone (<50% personal best): Medical emergency. Use rescue inhaler immediately, take oral steroids, call 911 or go to ED.
Nurses play a critical role in educating patients on how to use peak flow meters, maintain their action plans, and recognize deterioration early to prevent progression to status asthmaticus.

Status asthmaticus that fails to respond to standard bronchodilator therapy may require consideration of heliox (helium-oxygen mixture), typically 70-80 percent helium and 20-30 percent oxygen. The rationale is based on gas physics: helium has a density approximately one-seventh that of nitrogen, reducing the Reynolds number in narrowed airways and converting turbulent flow to laminar flow. Since resistance to turbulent flow is proportional to gas density while resistance to laminar flow is independent of density, heliox reduces the work of breathing in severely obstructed airways, buying time for corticosteroids and bronchodilators to take effect.

epiglottitis-peds.title: Epiglottit: Hava Yolu Acil Durumu
epiglottitis-peds.pathophysiology: A life-threatening bacterial infection (often H. influenzae type b) causing sudden, massive swelling of the epiglottis, which can completely occlude the airway within minutes.
diagnostics: Expect lateral neck X-ray (thumbprint sign) to be ordered
Monitor oxygen saturation continuously
Expect blood cultures after airway is secured
Monitor respiratory effort and airway patency closely
Expect CBC with differential to be drawn
management: NEVER examine the throat with tongue depressor
Keep child calm and in position of comfort (sitting upright)
Have emergency airway equipment at bedside (intubation/trach tray)
Maintain oxygen delivery as ordered
Administer antibiotics AFTER airway is secured as prescribed
Allow parent to stay to reduce child's anxiety
nursingActions: Monitor for signs of complete airway obstruction (stridor, cyanosis)
Keep child as calm as possible (avoid crying/agitation)
Report worsening stridor or respiratory distress immediately
Maintain NPO status
Do NOT attempt throat examination or cultures until airway secured
Stay with the child at all times during acute phase
Document respiratory status and interventions frequently

ards-management.title: ARDS Yönetimi
ards-management.pathophysiology:
Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS) is a severe, life-threatening form of acute respiratory failure characterized by diffuse alveolar damage, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema, and refractory hypoxemia. ARDS is not a primary disease but rather a clinical syndrome that develops as a consequence of an underlying insult—most commonly sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, or trauma. Understanding the cellular and molecular pathophysiology of ARDS is essential for nurses to anticipate the clinical trajectory, understand the rationale behind lung-protective ventilation strategies, and deliver evidence-based critical care.

Definition and Diagnostic Criteria (Berlin Definition, 2012):
ARDS is defined by four criteria, all of which must be present:
1. Timing: Acute onset within 1 week of a known clinical insult or new/worsening respiratory symptoms.
2. Chest Imaging: Bilateral opacities on chest X-ray or CT scan that are not fully explained by effusions, lobar/lung collapse, or nodules.
3. Origin of Edema: Respiratory failure not fully explained by cardiac failure or fluid overload (must exclude cardiogenic pulmonary edema; may require echocardiography if clinical picture is unclear).
4. Oxygenation (PaO2/FiO2 ratio on minimum PEEP 5 cmH2O):
   - Mild: PaO2/FiO2 200-300 mmHg (mortality ~27%)
   - Moderate: PaO2/FiO2 100-200 mmHg (mortality ~32%)
   - Severe: PaO2/FiO2 <100 mmHg (mortality ~45%)

Cellular Pathophysiology of ARDS:

ARDS progresses through three overlapping pathological phases, each with distinct cellular events:

Phase 1: Exudative Phase (Days 1-7):
This is the acute inflammatory phase characterized by diffuse alveolar damage (DAD), the histological hallmark of ARDS.

1. Initiating Insult and Inflammatory Activation:
The triggering event (sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, trauma) activates both the innate immune system and the coagulation cascade. In sepsis-induced ARDS, bacterial endotoxins (lipopolysaccharide/LPS from gram-negative organisms) or exotoxins activate pattern recognition receptors (Toll-like receptors, particularly TLR-4) on alveolar macrophages and type II pneumocytes. This triggers the release of a massive inflammatory mediator cascade:
- Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-α): The master pro-inflammatory cytokine. Released within minutes, it activates endothelial cells, increases vascular permeability, and recruits additional inflammatory cells.
- Interleukin-1 beta (IL-1β): Synergizes with TNF-α to amplify inflammation, induce fever, and activate the coagulation cascade.
- Interleukin-8 (IL-8): The primary neutrophil chemoattractant. Creates a concentration gradient that directs neutrophil migration from the pulmonary capillaries into the alveolar space.
- Interleukin-6 (IL-6): Drives the systemic acute phase response and correlates with ARDS severity and mortality.

2. Neutrophil-Mediated Injury:
Neutrophils are the primary effector cells in early ARDS. IL-8 and other chemotactic factors recruit massive numbers of neutrophils to the pulmonary capillaries. Activated neutrophils undergo the following sequence:
- Margination: Neutrophils adhere to the activated pulmonary capillary endothelium via selectins (E-selectin, P-selectin) and integrins (ICAM-1).
- Diapedesis: Neutrophils migrate through the endothelial cell junctions into the interstitial space and then through the alveolar epithelium into the alveolar lumen.
- Degranulation and Respiratory Burst: Once in the alveolar space, activated neutrophils release their destructive arsenal:
  - Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS): Superoxide radicals (O2-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radicals (OH·) generated by the NADPH oxidase system directly damage alveolar epithelial and endothelial cell membranes through lipid peroxidation.
  - Proteases: Neutrophil elastase, matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), and collagenase degrade the structural proteins of the alveolar basement membrane and extracellular matrix.
  - Neutrophil Extracellular Traps (NETs): Webs of chromatin and antimicrobial proteins released by dying neutrophils that trap pathogens but also damage host tissue and promote microvascular thrombosis.

3. Alveolar-Capillary Barrier Disruption:
The combined effects of inflammatory mediators, neutrophil-derived oxidants, and proteases destroy the integrity of the alveolar-capillary membrane. This barrier normally consists of three layers: the capillary endothelium, the shared basement membrane, and the alveolar epithelium (Type I pneumocytes). In ARDS:
- Endothelial Injury: Gaps form between endothelial cells, increasing capillary permeability. Protein-rich plasma fluid (containing albumin, fibrinogen, and other plasma proteins) leaks from the capillaries into the interstitial space and then floods into the alveoli.
- Epithelial Injury: Type I pneumocytes (which comprise 95% of the alveolar surface area and are responsible for gas exchange) are particularly vulnerable to oxidant injury. Their destruction exposes the basement membrane and allows unrestricted fluid entry into the alveolar space. Type II pneumocytes (which produce surfactant and serve as progenitor cells for Type I cell regeneration) are also damaged, critically impairing surfactant production.

4. Non-Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema:
The protein-rich edema fluid that floods the alveoli is fundamentally different from the transudative edema of heart failure:
- Cardiogenic edema: Low protein content (transudate), caused by elevated hydrostatic pressure. Responds to diuretics and afterload reduction.
- ARDS edema: High protein content (exudate), caused by increased permeability. Does NOT respond to diuretics (fluid is driven by permeability, not pressure). This distinction is critical for nursing care: aggressive diuresis helps cardiogenic edema but does not resolve ARDS edema and may cause hypovolemia.

The protein-rich edema fluid in the alveoli dilutes and inactivates surfactant, causing the surface tension at the air-liquid interface to increase dramatically. Without functional surfactant, alveoli collapse during expiration (atelectasis) and require greater pressure to re-open during inspiration. This explains the decreased lung compliance (stiff lungs) that is the clinical hallmark of ARDS.

5. Hyaline Membrane Formation:
Fibrinogen in the edema fluid polymerizes into fibrin, which combines with dead epithelial cell debris and plasma proteins to form dense, eosinophilic hyaline membranes lining the alveolar surfaces. These membranes physically impede gas diffusion across the respiratory membrane, worsening hypoxemia. Hyaline membranes are pathognomonic for DAD and are identical to those seen in neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (where surfactant deficiency is the primary problem).

6. Surfactant Dysfunction:
Surfactant dysfunction in ARDS occurs through multiple mechanisms:
- Type II pneumocyte injury reduces surfactant production.
- Plasma proteins in the edema fluid (fibrinogen, albumin, hemoglobin) competitively inhibit surfactant function at the air-liquid interface.
- Inflammatory mediators and proteases degrade surfactant proteins (SP-A, SP-B, SP-C, SP-D).
- Oxidative damage alters the phospholipid composition of surfactant.
The loss of surfactant function increases alveolar surface tension, promotes alveolar collapse, and increases the work of breathing. This is why exogenous surfactant replacement, while life-saving in neonatal RDS, has failed in adult ARDS trials: the inflammatory environment rapidly inactivates administered surfactant.

7. Ventilation-Perfusion Mismatch and Shunt:
Fluid-filled and collapsed alveoli cannot participate in gas exchange, but they continue to receive perfusion. Blood passing through these non-ventilated regions returns to the left heart without being oxygenated, creating intrapulmonary shunt. This is the mechanism behind the refractory hypoxemia that defines ARDS: unlike V/Q mismatch in COPD or pneumonia, true shunt does not respond to supplemental oxygen (the blood never contacts an aerated alveolus, so increasing FiO2 cannot improve oxygenation in the shunted fraction). Only recruitment of collapsed alveoli (through PEEP) can reduce shunt and improve oxygenation.

Phase 2: Proliferative Phase (Days 7-21):
If the patient survives the acute exudative phase, the proliferative phase begins:
- Type II pneumocytes proliferate along the denuded basement membrane, attempting to restore the alveolar epithelium. Type II cells are the stem cells of the alveolar epithelium and will eventually differentiate into Type I pneumocytes to restore normal gas exchange architecture.
- Fibroblasts migrate into the alveolar space and begin depositing collagen within the organizing exudate.
- The edema fluid is gradually reabsorbed through active sodium transport by the regenerating epithelium (via epithelial sodium channels/ENaC on the apical surface and Na+/K+-ATPase on the basolateral surface).
- Some patients show significant improvement during this phase as inflammation resolves and the alveolar epithelium regenerates.

Phase 3: Fibrotic Phase (After Day 21):
In a subset of patients (particularly those with prolonged mechanical ventilation), the proliferative phase progresses to fibrosis:
- Extensive collagen deposition replaces normal alveolar architecture with dense, non-functional scar tissue.
