The Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) and Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) joint guidelines recommend that ACO patients should never receive LABA monotherapy without ICS, as this may increase the risk of severe exacerbations and asthma-related death by masking worsening airway inflammation while providing symptomatic relief through bronchodilation. Biomarker-guided therapy is increasingly important: blood eosinophil counts above 300 cells per microliter strongly predict ICS responsiveness, while counts below 100 cells per microliter suggest predominantly neutrophilic inflammation where ICS provides minimal benefit and may increase pneumonia risk. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide (FeNO) levels above 50 parts per billion indicate active eosinophilic airway inflammation responsive to corticosteroids. These biomarkers help clinicians phenotype individual patients and tailor therapy to their specific inflammatory pattern rather than applying a one-size-fits-all approach.
diagnostics: Peak flow meter readings
Oxygen saturation monitoring
Breath sounds assessment (wheezing, diminished sounds)
Respiratory rate and effort assessment
Sputum assessment (color, amount, consistency)
management: Administer bronchodilators as ordered (SABA first for acute exacerbation)
Administer oxygen as ordered (COPD: 1-2 L/min, target SpO2 88-92%)
Position in high Fowler's or tripod position for breathing ease
Encourage smoking cessation
Administer corticosteroids as ordered for exacerbations
nursingActions: Assess respiratory status and breath sounds frequently
Administer medications via MDI or nebulizer as ordered
Monitor oxygen therapy (COPD patients: low-flow to avoid suppressing hypoxic drive)
Educate on inhaler technique and medication sequence (bronchodilator before corticosteroid)
Teach pursed-lip breathing for COPD patients
Report worsening respiratory distress immediately

pneumonia-basics.title: Pnömoni: Sınıflandırma ve Yönetim
pneumonia-basics.pathophysiology:
Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma characterized by inflammation and consolidation of the alveolar spaces, resulting in impaired gas exchange. It remains one of the leading causes of morbidity and mortality worldwide, particularly among the very young, the elderly, and immunocompromised individuals. Understanding pneumonia at the cellular and molecular level is essential for nurses because it explains the pathogenesis of clinical findings, guides assessment priorities, and provides the rationale for nursing interventions.

Normal Pulmonary Defense Mechanisms:
The respiratory system has multiple layers of defense that must be overcome for pneumonia to develop. The upper airway warms, humidifies, and filters inspired air. The nasal turbinates create turbulent airflow that impacts larger particles onto the mucosa. The epiglottis and cough reflex protect against aspiration. The mucociliary escalator, consisting of ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium covered by a thin mucus blanket, continuously sweeps trapped particles and microorganisms toward the pharynx for swallowing or expectoration. The cilia beat in a coordinated, wavelike motion at approximately 1,000 to 1,500 beats per minute, moving the mucus blanket upward at about 1 centimeter per minute.

Alveolar macrophages are the primary immune cells of the lower respiratory tract. These phagocytic cells patrol the alveolar surface, engulfing inhaled microorganisms and particulate matter. They also present antigens to T-lymphocytes, initiating adaptive immune responses when needed. Surfactant proteins A and D (collectins) produced by type II alveolar cells opsonize bacteria, enhancing phagocytosis. Secretory IgA in the airway mucosa neutralizes pathogens and prevents their adherence to epithelial surfaces.

Pneumonia develops when these defense mechanisms are overwhelmed or impaired. Risk factors that compromise pulmonary defenses include smoking (which paralyzes cilia, increases mucus production, and impairs macrophage function), alcohol use (which suppresses the cough reflex and impairs neutrophil function), immunosuppression (HIV, chemotherapy, corticosteroids), mechanical disruption of the airway (endotracheal intubation, tracheostomy), altered consciousness (stroke, sedation, seizures leading to aspiration), and chronic lung disease (COPD, which causes structural damage and impaired clearance).

Pathogenesis of Bacterial Pneumonia:
Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) most commonly develops through microaspiration of oropharyngeal flora, particularly Streptococcus pneumoniae, the most common bacterial pathogen. S. pneumoniae possesses several virulence factors that facilitate infection. Its polysaccharide capsule inhibits phagocytosis by preventing complement deposition and recognition by macrophages. Pneumolysin, a pore-forming toxin, directly damages respiratory epithelial cells and alveolar macrophages while also activating complement and stimulating inflammatory cytokine production.

Once bacteria reach the alveoli and overcome initial macrophage defenses, the infection triggers an intense inflammatory response. Alveolar macrophages release pro-inflammatory cytokines including tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-alpha), interleukin-1 (IL-1), and interleukin-8 (IL-8). These cytokines have several effects. They activate the vascular endothelium of the pulmonary capillaries, causing endothelial cells to express adhesion molecules (selectins, ICAM-1) that capture circulating neutrophils. They increase capillary permeability, allowing plasma proteins (including complement, immunoglobulins, and fibrinogen) to leak into the alveolar space. They stimulate the release of chemokines that create a concentration gradient attracting neutrophils from the bloodstream into the alveoli.

Neutrophils are the dominant effector cells in bacterial pneumonia. They migrate from the capillaries through the alveolar wall (diapedesis), following chemotactic signals to the site of infection. Once in the alveoli, neutrophils phagocytose opsonized bacteria and kill them through oxidative burst (generation of reactive oxygen species including superoxide, hydrogen peroxide, and hypochlorous acid via the myeloperoxidase system) and degranulation (release of antimicrobial proteins including defensins, lysozyme, and lactoferrin). However, these powerful antimicrobial weapons also damage surrounding host tissue, contributing to lung injury.

Stages of Lobar Pneumonia:
Classic lobar pneumonia progresses through four histological stages. The stage of congestion (first 24 hours) is characterized by vascular engorgement and serous exudate in the alveoli. The alveolar capillaries are dilated and filled with blood. A protein-rich edema fluid begins to accumulate in the alveolar spaces, providing a medium for bacterial multiplication. At this stage, the lung is heavy, boggy, and red. Clinically, the patient develops sudden onset of high fever (as pyrogens stimulate the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center), chills and rigors (from rapid temperature changes), pleuritic chest pain (inflammation of the visceral pleura, which is innervated by somatic nerves sensitive to sharp pain with breathing), and a cough that is initially dry.

The stage of red hepatization (days 2 to 3) is named because the affected lung resembles liver tissue in consistency. The alveoli are filled with a dense exudate containing red blood cells (from capillary leakage), neutrophils, fibrin strands, and bacteria. The consolidation is so complete that no air remains in the affected alveoli, which is why percussion over consolidated lung produces dullness rather than resonance, and why breath sounds are bronchial (transmitted through solid tissue) rather than vesicular. The sputum becomes purulent and may be rust-colored due to the breakdown of red blood cells in the exudate.

The stage of gray hepatization (days 4 to 6) represents the peak of the inflammatory response. The alveoli are packed with fibrin and degenerating neutrophils. Red blood cells have been lysed and cleared, giving the consolidated lung a grayish appearance. Macrophages begin to appear in increasing numbers, initiating the cleanup phase.

The stage of resolution (day 7 onward) involves enzymatic digestion of the consolidated exudate. Macrophages phagocytose cellular debris, bacteria, and fibrin. The fibrin is dissolved by plasmin and other proteases. The liquefied exudate is either coughed up (productive cough during recovery) or absorbed via lymphatic drainage. Normal alveolar architecture is restored without permanent scarring in uncomplicated cases.

Gas Exchange Impairment and Clinical Consequences:
The fundamental physiological derangement in pneumonia is ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) mismatch. The consolidated alveoli continue to receive blood flow from the pulmonary capillaries (perfusion is maintained), but they cannot participate in gas exchange because they are filled with inflammatory exudate (ventilation is abolished). This creates an intrapulmonary shunt: blood passes through the pulmonary capillaries without being oxygenated and returns to the left heart with low oxygen content, which mixes with oxygenated blood from ventilated regions, reducing overall arterial oxygen tension.

Hypoxemia (low PaO2) is the hallmark blood gas abnormality in pneumonia. Unlike conditions that cause V/Q mismatch through impaired ventilation (asthma, COPD), where supplemental oxygen can compensate, true shunting in extensive pneumonia may be poorly responsive to supplemental oxygen because the blood bypassing the consolidated alveoli never contacts the oxygen-enriched gas. This is why severe pneumonia may require high-flow oxygen, non-invasive positive pressure ventilation, or mechanical ventilation.

The systemic inflammatory response to pneumonia can lead to sepsis and septic shock. Bacterial cell wall components (lipopolysaccharide from gram-negative organisms, lipoteichoic acid and peptidoglycan from gram-positive organisms) activate immune cells throughout the body via toll-like receptors, triggering a cytokine storm that causes systemic vasodilation (distributive shock), capillary leak, DIC, and multi-organ dysfunction.

Viral Pneumonia vs Bacterial Pneumonia:
Viral pneumonia, most commonly caused by influenza, RSV, and SARS-CoV-2, has a different cellular pathology. Viruses infect and destroy alveolar epithelial cells (primarily type I pneumocytes), causing diffuse alveolar damage. The inflammatory response is lymphocyte-predominant rather than neutrophil-predominant. The damage tends to be diffuse and bilateral rather than lobar. Viral pneumonia predisposes to secondary bacterial pneumonia because viral destruction of the respiratory epithelium impairs mucociliary clearance, and viral-induced immune dysregulation impairs bacterial killing.

Nursing Assessment and Interventions:
Respiratory assessment is the priority in pneumonia patients. Auscultation findings vary by stage: early pneumonia may produce only fine crackles (from air opening fluid-filled alveoli), while established consolidation produces bronchial breath sounds, increased tactile fremitus (vibrations transmit better through solid tissue than air), and egophony (E-to-A changes on auscultation, where spoken E sounds like A over consolidated lung). The nurse should assess respiratory rate (tachypnea is an early sign of respiratory distress and an independent predictor of mortality), oxygen saturation (target SpO2 greater than or equal to 92 percent in most patients), work of breathing (accessory muscle use, nasal flaring, retractions), and mental status (confusion is a sensitive indicator of hypoxemia and a criterion in severity scoring systems like CURB-65).

Positioning is a key nursing intervention. The semi-Fowler or high-Fowler position maximizes diaphragmatic excursion and reduces the work of breathing. For unilateral pneumonia, positioning with the good lung down (good lung dependent) optimizes V/Q matching because gravity preferentially directs blood flow to the dependent lung, which in this position is the unaffected lung with good ventilation. Incentive spirometry every 1 to 2 hours while awake promotes deep breathing, prevents atelectasis, and mobilizes secretions. Adequate hydration (2 to 3 liters per day unless fluid-restricted) thins secretions, making them easier to expectorate.

Sputum specimen collection for culture and sensitivity testing should be obtained before antibiotic administration when possible. The specimen should be collected from a deep cough, placed in a sterile container, and transported to the laboratory within 30 minutes. An adequate sputum specimen contains greater than 25 neutrophils and fewer than 10 epithelial cells per low-power field, confirming it represents lower respiratory tract secretions rather than oral contamination.

The resolution phase of pneumonia involves complex tissue repair mechanisms that determine whether the lung returns to normal architecture or develops fibrotic scarring. Once the pathogen is controlled, the inflammatory response must be actively terminated through a process called resolution, mediated by specialized pro-resolving mediators (SPMs) including lipoxins, resolvins, protectins, and maresins. These lipid mediators are synthesized from omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) through lipoxygenase pathways and actively promote resolution by inhibiting neutrophil recruitment and activation, stimulating macrophage efferocytosis (phagocytosis of apoptotic neutrophils and cellular debris), reducing pro-inflammatory cytokine production, and promoting tissue repair. Macrophages undergo a phenotypic switch from the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype (classically activated by IFN-gamma, producing TNF-alpha, IL-1, IL-6, and reactive oxygen species) to the anti-inflammatory M2 phenotype (alternatively activated by IL-4 and IL-13, producing IL-10, TGF-beta, and growth factors that promote tissue repair). M2 macrophages clear apoptotic neutrophils through efferocytosis, a process that further reinforces the anti-inflammatory program by triggering macrophage release of TGF-beta and IL-10. If resolution fails, the persistent inflammatory exudate undergoes organization: fibroblasts migrate into the alveolar space, proliferate, and deposit collagen, creating organizing pneumonia (formerly called BOOP) that may require corticosteroid treatment to prevent permanent fibrotic scarring.

Nursing assessment of pneumonia severity guides disposition decisions and treatment intensity. The CURB-65 scoring system assigns one point each for Confusion (new mental confusion), Urea greater than 7 mmol/L (BUN greater than 20 mg/dL), Respiratory rate 30 or more breaths per minute, Blood pressure (systolic less than 90 or diastolic 60 or less mmHg), and age 65 or older. Scores of 0-1 suggest outpatient management, 2 suggests short inpatient stay or supervised outpatient treatment, and 3-5 warrants hospitalization with consideration of ICU admission for scores of 4-5. The physiological rationale for each criterion reflects the systemic impact of severe infection: confusion indicates cerebral hypoperfusion or direct inflammatory effects on the blood-brain barrier from circulating cytokines; elevated BUN indicates dehydration, renal hypoperfusion, or a hypercatabolic state; tachypnea reflects increased dead space ventilation and hypoxemia driving compensatory hyperventilation through peripheral and central chemoreceptor activation; and hypotension indicates sepsis-induced vasodilation from excessive nitric oxide production and capillary leak from endothelial dysfunction. Oxygenation assessment using SpO2 and arterial blood gases is essential: the PaO2/FiO2 ratio (P/F ratio) provides a standardized assessment of oxygenation efficiency independent of the amount of supplemental oxygen being administered. A P/F ratio below 300 indicates acute lung injury, while below 200 meets the Berlin criteria for moderate-to-severe ARDS, necessitating escalation of respiratory support and potential mechanical ventilation.

Aspiration pneumonia and aspiration pneumonitis represent distinct clinical entities with different pathophysiology and treatment approaches that are frequently confused. Aspiration pneumonitis is a chemical injury to the lung parenchyma caused by aspiration of sterile gastric contents with pH less than 2.5. The acidic gastric fluid causes direct chemical burn to the alveolar epithelium, triggering an intense inflammatory response with neutrophil recruitment, alveolar edema, and surfactant destruction within 1-2 hours. This is the pathophysiology described in Mendelson syndrome and does not initially involve bacterial infection; therefore, antibiotics are not indicated unless secondary bacterial infection develops after 48-72 hours. Aspiration pneumonia, in contrast, is a bacterial infection caused by aspiration of oropharyngeal secretions containing pathogenic organisms into the lower respiratory tract. Risk factors include dysphagia (from stroke, neurological disorders, or sedation), impaired cough reflex, poor oral hygiene (which increases the bacterial load and virulence of oropharyngeal flora), and conditions promoting gastroesophageal reflux. The aspirated bacteria are typically anaerobic organisms from the gingival crevices (Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium, Prevotella, Bacteroides) and microaerophilic streptococci, with gram-negative bacilli more common in hospital-acquired aspiration. Treatment requires antibiotic coverage for these organisms, with aspiration prevention strategies including elevating the head of bed to at least 30 degrees, performing oral care with chlorhexidine to reduce oropharyngeal bacterial colonization, and implementing dysphagia screening protocols before oral intake in high-risk patients.
diagnostics: Respiratory assessment (breath sounds: crackles, rhonchi)
Temperature monitoring
Oxygen saturation monitoring
Sputum characteristics assessment (color, amount, odor)
Monitor for signs of sepsis
management: Administer antibiotics as ordered
Encourage deep breathing, coughing, and incentive spirometry
Maintain adequate hydration
Administer oxygen therapy as ordered
Position in semi-Fowler's to optimize breathing
nursingActions: Monitor respiratory status (breath sounds, SpO2, respiratory effort)
Administer antibiotics as ordered and on time
Encourage incentive spirometry use 10 times every hour while awake
Monitor temperature and report persistent fever
Maintain adequate hydration to thin secretions
Assess for complications (sepsis, respiratory failure, pleural effusion)

abg-basics.title: ABG Yorumlama Temelleri
abg-basics.pathophysiology:
Arterial blood gas (ABG) analysis provides critical information about a patient's acid-base status, oxygenation, and ventilation. Understanding acid-base physiology at the cellular level is essential for accurate interpretation.

Normal ABG Values:
• pH: 7.35-7.45 (reflects the overall acid-base balance)
• PaCO2: 35-45 mmHg (respiratory component: regulated by the lungs)
• HCO3-: 22-26 mEq/L (metabolic component: regulated by the kidneys)
• PaO2: 80-100 mmHg (oxygenation: not part of acid-base analysis but critical)
• SaO2: 95-100% (oxygen saturation of arterial hemoglobin)
• Base Excess/Deficit: -2 to +2 mEq/L (reflects metabolic component)

Acid-Base Physiology:
• Acids are produced continuously by cellular metabolism: CO2 (volatile acid: exhaled by lungs, ~15,000 mmol/day) and non-volatile acids (lactic acid, ketoacids, phosphoric acid: excreted by kidneys, ~50-100 mEq/day).
• Buffer Systems: Bicarbonate buffer (HCO3-/H2CO3: most important extracellular buffer), phosphate buffer, protein buffer (hemoglobin: most important intracellular buffer).
• Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3-] / [0.03 × PaCO2]). The ratio of HCO3- to PaCO2 determines pH, not their absolute values.

Step-by-Step ABG Interpretation:

Step 1: Look at the pH
• pH <7.35 = Acidosis (too much acid / too little base)
• pH >7.45 = Alkalosis (too much base / too little acid)
• pH 7.35-7.45 = Normal (but may be compensated: check other values)

Step 2: Look at the PaCO2 (Respiratory Component)
• PaCO2 >45 = Respiratory acidosis (hypoventilation: retaining CO2)
• PaCO2 <35 = Respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation: blowing off CO2)

Step 3: Look at the HCO3- (Metabolic Component)
• HCO3- <22 = Metabolic acidosis (loss of bicarbonate or accumulation of acid)
• HCO3- >26 = Metabolic alkalosis (gain of bicarbonate or loss of acid)

Step 4: Match: Which component explains the pH?
• If pH is acidotic and PaCO2 is elevated → Respiratory acidosis
• If pH is acidotic and HCO3- is low → Metabolic acidosis
• If pH is alkalotic and PaCO2 is low → Respiratory alkalosis
• If pH is alkalotic and HCO3- is high → Metabolic alkalosis

Step 5: Is there Compensation?
• The body attempts to normalize pH through the opposite system:
  - Respiratory disorders → Kidneys compensate (takes 24-48 hours for full renal compensation)
  - Metabolic disorders → Lungs compensate (begins within minutes: rapid but limited)
• Uncompensated: pH is abnormal, one value is abnormal, the other is normal
• Partially Compensated: pH is abnormal, BOTH PaCO2 and HCO3- are abnormal
• Fully Compensated: pH is NORMAL (7.35-7.45), but both PaCO2 and HCO3- are abnormal. Look at which side of 7.40 the pH falls to determine the primary disorder.

ROME Mnemonic:
R-espiratory = O-pposite: In respiratory disorders, PaCO2 and pH move in OPPOSITE directions (↑CO2 = ↓pH)
M-etabolic = E-qual: In metabolic disorders, HCO3- and pH move in the SAME direction (↑HCO3- = ↑pH)

Common Clinical Scenarios:
• Respiratory Acidosis: COPD exacerbation, opioid overdose, neuromuscular disease, severe asthma, pneumonia. Treatment: Improve ventilation.
• Respiratory Alkalosis: Anxiety/hyperventilation, pain, PE, early sepsis, salicylate toxicity, high-altitude. Treatment: Treat underlying cause.
• Metabolic Acidosis: DKA, lactic acidosis (shock, sepsis), renal failure, diarrhea, salicylate toxicity. Treatment: Treat underlying cause; bicarbonate if pH <7.1.
• Metabolic Alkalosis: Vomiting, NG suction (loss of HCl), diuretic use (contraction alkalosis), excessive antacid intake. Treatment: Fluid and electrolyte replacement.
diagnostics: pH assessment (7.35-7.45 normal range)
PaCO2 assessment (35-45 mmHg normal)
HCO3 assessment (22-26 mEq/L normal)
PaO2 assessment (80-100 mmHg normal)
SaO2 assessment (>95% normal)
management: Report critical ABG values immediately to RN/physician
Monitor respiratory status in conjunction with ABG results
Apply ROME method (Respiratory Opposite, Metabolic Equal)
Assess for compensation (full vs. partial)
Correlate ABG findings with patient clinical status
nursingActions: Understand normal ABG values (pH 7.35-7.45, PaCO2 35-45, HCO3 22-26)
Apply ROME method for interpretation (Respiratory = Opposite of pH, Metabolic = Equal to pH)
Report critical values immediately
Assist with ABG specimen collection (apply pressure 5 min post-draw, 10 min if anticoagulated)
Place specimen on ice and transport to lab within 30 minutes
Document FiO2 and ventilator settings at time of draw

abg-interpretation-rn.title: İleri Düzey ABG Yorumlama
abg-interpretation-rn.pathophysiology:
Advanced ABG interpretation requires the ability to identify mixed disorders (two or more primary acid-base disturbances occurring simultaneously) and calculate the anion gap to narrow the differential diagnosis of metabolic acidosis.

Anion Gap (AG):
The anion gap represents unmeasured anions in the blood. It helps classify metabolic acidosis into two categories.

Formula: AG = Na+ - (Cl- + HCO3-)
Normal: 8-12 mEq/L (some labs use 12 ± 4 depending on albumin correction)

• Elevated Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (AGMA): Accumulation of unmeasured acids. Each acid provides an H+ (which consumes HCO3-) and an unmeasured anion (which increases the gap). MUDPILES: Methanol, Uremia, DKA (beta-hydroxybutyrate), Propylene glycol, Isoniazid/Iron, Lactic acidosis (shock, sepsis, ischemia), Ethylene glycol, Salicylates.

• Non-Anion Gap (Normal AG) Metabolic Acidosis (NAGMA): Loss of bicarbonate OR gain of chloride (hyperchloremic). Causes: Diarrhea (loss of HCO3-rich intestinal fluid), Renal Tubular Acidosis, early renal failure, excessive NS infusion (dilutional acidosis), acetazolamide, ureteral diversion.

Delta-Delta (Δ/Δ) Analysis: Identifying Hidden Disorders:
In elevated AG metabolic acidosis, the delta-delta ratio helps identify coexisting metabolic disorders:

Δ Anion Gap = Calculated AG - Normal AG (12)
Δ HCO3 = Normal HCO3 (24) - Measured HCO3
Ratio = Δ AG / Δ HCO3

• Ratio < 1: The HCO3 dropped MORE than expected for the AG rise → Concurrent non-AG metabolic acidosis (e.g., DKA + diarrhea)
• Ratio 1-2: Pure anion gap metabolic acidosis (the HCO3 drop equals the AG rise)
• Ratio > 2: The HCO3 dropped LESS than expected for the AG rise → Concurrent metabolic alkalosis (e.g., DKA + vomiting)

Expected Compensation Calculations:
These formulas help identify whether compensation is appropriate or if a mixed disorder exists:

• Metabolic Acidosis → Respiratory compensation (Winters' Formula):
  Expected PaCO2 = (1.5 × HCO3) + 8 (± 2)
  If actual PaCO2 > expected → concurrent respiratory acidosis
  If actual PaCO2 < expected → concurrent respiratory alkalosis

• Metabolic Alkalosis → Respiratory compensation:
  Expected PaCO2 = (0.7 × HCO3) + 21 (± 2)

• Acute Respiratory Acidosis → Metabolic compensation:
  Expected HCO3 = 24 + [(PaCO2 - 40) / 10] (increases 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg rise in CO2)

• Chronic Respiratory Acidosis → Metabolic compensation:
  Expected HCO3 = 24 + [3.5 × (PaCO2 - 40) / 10] (increases 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg rise in CO2)

• Acute Respiratory Alkalosis → Metabolic compensation:
  Expected HCO3 = 24 - [2 × (40 - PaCO2) / 10]

• Chronic Respiratory Alkalosis → Metabolic compensation:
  Expected HCO3 = 24 - [5 × (40 - PaCO2) / 10]

Common Mixed Disorders:
• Metabolic acidosis + Respiratory acidosis: Cardiac arrest, severe pneumonia with sepsis (inability to compensate). Most dangerous combination: pH drops precipitously.
• Metabolic acidosis + Respiratory alkalosis: Salicylate toxicity (direct medullary stimulation + metabolic acid production), early sepsis.
• Metabolic acidosis + Metabolic alkalosis: DKA with vomiting, renal failure with NG suction. The pH may appear deceptively normal.
• Respiratory acidosis + Metabolic alkalosis: COPD patient on diuretics (chronic CO2 retention + contraction alkalosis). Very common.
diagnostics: Systematic ABG interpretation: pH → PaCO2 → HCO3 → compensation → oxygenation
Anion gap calculation [Na - (Cl + HCO3)] normal 8-12
Calculate A-a gradient for oxygenation assessment
Correlate ABG with electrolytes and clinical presentation
Differentiate acute vs chronic respiratory acid-base disorders
Expected compensation formulas (Winter's formula for metabolic acidosis)
management: Initiate appropriate interventions based on interpretation (oxygen for hypoxemia, ventilator adjustment for ventilation issues)
Correct underlying cause of acid-base disturbance
Administer sodium bicarbonate only for severe metabolic acidosis (pH < 7.1) as ordered
Adjust ventilator settings based on ABG results per protocol
Repeat ABG 20-30 minutes after interventions to assess response
nursingActions: Interpret ABG results using systematic approach and correlate with clinical picture
Calculate anion gap for metabolic acidosis (MUDPILES mnemonic)
Differentiate compensated vs uncompensated acid-base disorders
Report critical values and anticipated interventions to provider
Monitor for clinical signs of acidosis (Kussmaul breathing, confusion) and alkalosis (tetany, seizures)
Document ABG interpretation and clinical correlation

oxygen-therapy.title: Oksijen Verme Cihazları ve Tedavisi
oxygen-therapy.pathophysiology:
Supplemental oxygen therapy corrects hypoxemia by increasing the fraction of inspired oxygen (FiO2) above the atmospheric level of 21%. Understanding each delivery device's capabilities, limitations, and appropriate clinical use is essential for safe oxygen administration.

Oxygen Transport Physiology:
• Oxygen binds to hemoglobin (97% as oxyhemoglobin) and dissolves in plasma (3% as PaO2).
• Each gram of hemoglobin carries 1.34 mL of O2 when fully saturated.
• Oxygen delivery (DO2) = CO × (1.34 × Hb × SaO2) + (0.003 × PaO2). The dissolved component is clinically insignificant under normal conditions.
• The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve describes the relationship between PaO2 and hemoglobin saturation. The sigmoid shape means: Above PaO2 60 (SpO2 ~90%), large PaO2 changes cause small SpO2 changes (flat portion: safety buffer). Below PaO2 60, small PaO2 changes cause DRAMATIC SpO2 drops (steep portion: danger zone).

Low-Flow Devices (Variable FiO2: depends on patient's respiratory pattern):
• Nasal Cannula:
  - Flow rate: 1-6 L/min
  - FiO2: ~24% (1L) to ~44% (6L), approximately +4% per liter
  - Advantages: Comfortable, allows eating/talking, well-tolerated
  - Limitations: FiO2 varies with respiratory rate and depth (mouth breathing dilutes O2), max effective flow 6 L/min (higher flows dry nasal mucosa without significantly increasing FiO2), skin breakdown behind ears
  - Humidification: Not required at ≤4 L/min; recommended at >4 L/min

• Simple Face Mask:
  - Flow rate: 5-10 L/min (minimum 5 L/min to prevent CO2 rebreathing)
  - FiO2: ~40-60%
  - Advantages: Higher FiO2 than nasal cannula
  - Limitations: Must be removed for eating/drinking, uncomfortable, risk of aspiration if vomiting, CO2 rebreathing if flow <5 L/min

• Non-Rebreather Mask (NRB):
  - Flow rate: 10-15 L/min (reservoir bag must remain inflated at least 2/3 during inspiration)
  - FiO2: 60-95% (with good mask seal)
  - Features: Reservoir bag stores 100% O2 between breaths, one-way valves prevent exhaled air from entering bag and room air from entering through side ports
  - Use: Emergency situations requiring high FiO2: trauma, acute respiratory failure, CO poisoning
  - CRITICAL: If reservoir bag deflates, patient is breathing NOTHING: increase flow rate immediately or remove mask

High-Flow Devices (Fixed/Precise FiO2: independent of patient's respiratory pattern):
• Venturi Mask (Venti-Mask):
  - Color-coded adapters deliver precise FiO2: Blue (24% at 4L), Yellow (28% at 4L), White (31% at 6L), Green (35% at 8L), Pink (40% at 8L), Orange (50% at 12L)
  - Principle: Bernoulli principle / air entrainment: oxygen jet entrains a fixed proportion of room air through calibrated side ports, delivering a precise FiO2
  - Best use: COPD patients requiring controlled, precise oxygen delivery to prevent CO2 narcosis
  - Advantage: FiO2 is consistent regardless of respiratory pattern

• High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC):
  - Flow rate: 20-60 L/min
  - FiO2: 21-100% (adjustable independently of flow rate)
  - Features: Heated and humidified to 37°C and 100% relative humidity, generates low-level PEEP (2-5 cmH2O at flows >40 L/min), reduces anatomic dead space (washes out nasopharyngeal CO2), improved mucociliary clearance
  - Use: Type I respiratory failure, post-extubation support, may prevent intubation in mild-moderate ARDS
  - Evidence: FLORALI trial showed HFNC reduced intubation rate and 90-day mortality compared to standard O2 and NIV in severe acute hypoxemic respiratory failure

Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV):
• CPAP (Continuous Positive Airway Pressure): Delivers constant positive pressure throughout the respiratory cycle. Splints airways open, recruits alveoli, reduces work of breathing. Used for: OSA, acute pulmonary edema, post-extubation support.
• BiPAP (Bilevel Positive Airway Pressure): Delivers two levels of pressure: higher during inspiration (IPAP, 10-20 cmH2O) and lower during expiration (EPAP, 5-10 cmH2O). The difference (pressure support = IPAP - EPAP) augments tidal volume. Used for: COPD exacerbation with hypercapnia (Type II respiratory failure), hypercapnic respiratory failure, bridge to intubation, some sleep-disordered breathing.

Oxygen Titration Principles:
• Target SpO2: 94-98% for most patients. 88-92% for COPD patients with chronic CO2 retention.
• Titrate to lowest effective FiO2: prolonged high FiO2 (>60% for >24-48 hours) causes oxygen toxicity: free radical damage to alveolar-capillary membrane, absorption atelectasis (nitrogen washout → alveolar collapse), suppression of surfactant production.
• Fire safety: Oxygen supports combustion. No smoking, open flames, or sparking devices near oxygen. Post oxygen safety signage.
diagnostics: Oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry (target > 94% most patients, 88-92% for COPD)
Respiratory rate and effort assessment
Breath sounds assessment
Patient comfort and tolerance of oxygen device
Monitor for signs of oxygen toxicity with high FiO2
management: Apply oxygen device as ordered (NC 1-6 L/min, simple mask 6-10 L/min, NRB 10-15 L/min)
Titrate oxygen to maintain prescribed SpO2 target
For COPD: use low-flow oxygen (1-2 L/min) to maintain SpO2 88-92%
Humidify oxygen delivery at flows > 4 L/min
Never remove oxygen without provider order
nursingActions: Apply prescribed oxygen delivery device correctly
Monitor SpO2 continuously and titrate as ordered
Assess for skin breakdown under oxygen devices (nasal cannula, mask straps)
Ensure humidification for high-flow oxygen
Educate on oxygen safety (no smoking, no open flames near oxygen)
For COPD patients: use low-flow oxygen to avoid suppressing respiratory drive

tracheostomy-care.title: Trakeostomi Bakımı ve Yönetimi
tracheostomy-care.pathophysiology:
A tracheostomy is a surgically created opening in the anterior trachea (typically between the 2nd and 4th tracheal rings) through which a tracheostomy tube is inserted to establish an airway. It bypasses the upper airway (nose, pharynx, larynx) and provides direct access to the lower airway.

Indications:
• Prolonged mechanical ventilation (most common: reduces complications vs. prolonged endotracheal intubation)
• Upper airway obstruction (tumor, trauma, edema, bilateral vocal cord paralysis)
• Airway protection (severe neurologic impairment with inability to protect airway)
• Facilitation of pulmonary toilet (secretion management)
• Sleep apnea (severe, refractory to other treatments: rare indication)

Trach Tube Components:
• Outer Cannula: The main body of the tracheostomy tube that sits in the stoma. Has a flange (faceplate) that rests against the neck with holes for securing ties.
• Inner Cannula: A removable tube that fits inside the outer cannula. Can be removed for cleaning (prevents secretion buildup that narrows the airway) or replaced with a clean inner cannula. Disposable inner cannulas are available. NOT all tracheostomy tubes have inner cannulas.
• Obturator: A solid, rounded device inserted into the outer cannula during initial insertion or tube change. Creates a smooth, rounded tip that guides the tube through the stoma without trauma to the tracheal wall. REMOVE immediately after insertion to allow airflow through the tube.
• Cuff: An inflatable balloon around the distal outer cannula that creates a seal against the tracheal wall. Inflated during mechanical ventilation to prevent air leak and aspiration. Cuff pressure should be maintained at 20-25 cmH2O (too low = aspiration and air leak; too high = tracheal mucosal ischemia, necrosis, tracheomalacia, tracheoesophageal fistula).

Trach Tube Types:
• Cuffed: For mechanically ventilated patients or patients at high risk of aspiration. Prevents air leak and reduces aspiration.
• Uncuffed: For patients breathing spontaneously who do not require mechanical ventilation and are at low aspiration risk. Allows air to flow around the tube and up through the vocal cords (facilitates speech with finger occlusion or speaking valve).
• Fenestrated: Has openings in the outer cannula that allow air to pass through the vocal cords for phonation. Used during weaning/speaking trials. Remove inner cannula when using speaking valve.

Suctioning:
• Indications: Audible/visible secretions, increased work of breathing, coarse rhonchi on auscultation, decreased SpO2, ventilator high-pressure alarm.
• Technique (Sterile): Hyperoxygenate with 100% O2 before and after suctioning. Use appropriately sized suction catheter (no larger than half the internal diameter of the trach tube). Insert catheter WITHOUT suction applied until resistance is met or cough is elicited, then withdraw 1 cm and apply intermittent suction while rotating and withdrawing over 10-15 seconds. Limit to 2-3 passes per episode. Monitor SpO2 and heart rate during procedure.
• Complications: Hypoxia (interrupting ventilation), bradycardia (vagal stimulation), tracheal mucosal damage (excessive suction pressure or catheter too large), infection, bronchospasm, bleeding.
• Suction pressure: Adults 100-150 mmHg, Children 80-100 mmHg, Infants 60-80 mmHg.

Inner Cannula Care:
• Reusable inner cannulas: Remove, clean with hydrogen peroxide (or per facility policy), rinse with sterile saline, allow to air dry or immediately reinsert. Frequency: Every 4-8 hours or more frequently if secretions are thick.
• Disposable inner cannulas: Remove and replace with new sterile inner cannula. More convenient but more expensive.
• CRITICAL: Never leave the trach tube without an inner cannula for more than brief cleaning periods. The inner cannula prevents secretion buildup from narrowing the outer cannula lumen.

Stoma Care:
• Clean around stoma with half-strength hydrogen peroxide (or sterile saline per facility policy), rinse with sterile saline, pat dry.
• Assess stoma for signs of infection (redness, swelling, purulent drainage, odor), skin breakdown, and granulation tissue.
• Change trach ties when soiled. Ensure two fingers can fit between tie and neck (preventing skin breakdown while ensuring tube is secure). ALWAYS have a second person hold the tube in place while changing ties (prevents accidental decannulation).
• Change trach dressing (drain sponge) when moist: a wet dressing is a breeding ground for bacteria.

Emergency Management:
• Accidental Decannulation:
  - Established trach (>7 days): Attempt to reinsert trach tube. If unable, insert a tube one size smaller. If unable, attempt oral intubation.
  - Fresh trach (<7 days): The stoma tract is NOT mature: reinsertion may create a false passage. Do NOT attempt reinsertion: perform oral endotracheal intubation and notify surgeon.
• Equipment at bedside: Spare trach tube (same size AND one size smaller), obturator, trach ties, suction equipment, oxygen source, Ambu bag with trach adapter, trach tube spreader (hemostat), and call light.

Decannulation:
• Process of removing the tracheostomy tube permanently when it is no longer needed.
• Criteria: Able to manage secretions, adequate cough and swallow reflexes, no longer requires mechanical ventilation, able to breathe through upper airway (capping trial tolerated for 24-48 hours).
• Process: Downsizing (progressively smaller tubes) → Capping trial (cork or cap placed on trach to occlude it: patient must breathe entirely through upper airway) → Decannulation (remove tube, cover stoma with occlusive dressing: stoma closes spontaneously within days to weeks).
diagnostics: Assess tracheostomy site for signs of infection (redness, swelling, purulent drainage)
Monitor respiratory status (breath sounds, SpO2)
Assess inner cannula for secretion buildup
Monitor stoma patency and skin integrity
Assess cuff pressure (if cuffed trach)
management: Perform tracheostomy care per facility protocol
Suction as needed using sterile technique
Keep replacement tracheostomy tube and obturator at bedside at all times
Maintain humidification to prevent secretion drying
Provide communication tools for patient
nursingActions: Perform tracheostomy care every 8 hours and as needed
Suction using sterile technique (pre-oxygenate, limit to 10 seconds per pass)
Clean inner cannula with hydrogen peroxide and rinse with NS
Assess and change tracheostomy ties when soiled (secure new before removing old)
Keep replacement trach tube and obturator at bedside
Monitor for complications (mucus plugging, accidental decannulation, infection)

rhonchi-rpn.title: Rhonchi: Değerlendirme ve Klinik Önemi
rhonchi-rpn.pathophysiology:
Rhonchi are low-pitched, continuous, snoring or gurgling adventitious breath sounds produced by airflow turbulence through large airways (trachea, bronchi) that are partially obstructed by thick secretions, mucosal swelling, or foreign material. Unlike wheezing, which originates from narrowed smaller airways and produces a high-pitched musical tone, rhonchi arise from vibration of secretions and airway walls in the larger conducting airways.

Mechanism of Sound Production:
- Thick mucus or inflammatory exudate partially occludes the lumen of large airways.
- As air passes through the narrowed, secretion-filled passage, it creates turbulent flow that vibrates the mucus and surrounding airway walls.
- The result is a low-pitched, rumbling, sonorous sound often described as snoring or moaning.
- Rhonchi are heard during both inspiration and expiration, but are typically louder on expiration because expiratory airflow further compresses the airways.

Key Differentiating Features:
- Rhonchi vs. Wheezing: Rhonchi are low-pitched and coarse; wheezes are high-pitched and musical. Rhonchi originate in large airways; wheezes originate in smaller, narrowed airways.
- Rhonchi vs. Crackles: Rhonchi are continuous sounds (lasting >250 milliseconds); crackles are discontinuous, brief popping sounds caused by the sudden opening of collapsed small airways or alveoli.
- A hallmark feature of rhonchi is that they may clear or change character after the patient coughs, because coughing mobilizes the secretions responsible for the sound. This distinguishes rhonchi from wheezes and crackles, which do not typically resolve with coughing.

Common Conditions Producing Rhonchi:
- Pneumonia (secretion accumulation in large airways)
- Acute or chronic bronchitis (excessive mucus production and inflammation)
- COPD exacerbation (mucus hypersecretion combined with airway inflammation)
- Mucus plugging (post-operative patients, patients with weak cough)
- Aspiration (foreign material in large airways)
diagnostics: Auscultate all lung fields systematically (anterior, posterior, lateral)
Note location of rhonchi (bilateral, unilateral, specific lobes)
Note timing (inspiratory, expiratory, or both)
Reassess breath sounds after coughing to determine if rhonchi clear
Monitor respiratory rate, depth, and oxygen saturation
Report findings to RN with specific description of sounds and location
management: Encourage effective coughing and deep breathing exercises
Assist with position changes every 2 hours to promote secretion drainage
Elevate head of bed to at least 30 degrees
Ensure adequate oral hydration to thin secretions as permitted
Encourage use of incentive spirometry as ordered
Assist with ambulation as tolerated to promote lung expansion
nursingActions: Perform respiratory assessment including auscultation at beginning of shift and as needed
Describe rhonchi accurately in documentation (location, timing, character)
Report new onset or worsening rhonchi to the nurse promptly
Assist patient with positioning (upright, side-lying) to facilitate secretion clearance
Encourage and coach coughing and deep breathing exercises every 1 to 2 hours
Monitor for signs of respiratory distress (increased work of breathing, declining SpO2)
Provide oral care to reduce aspiration risk
Document whether rhonchi cleared or changed after coughing or repositioning

pressure-injury.title: İleri Düzey Yara Bakımı
pressure-injury.pathophysiology: Localized damage to the skin and underlying soft tissue usually over a bony prominence. Caused by intense or prolonged pressure in combination with shear.
diagnostics: Expect serum albumin and prealbumin to be ordered
Monitor wound measurements and staging progression
Expect wound cultures if signs of infection present
Expect nutritional assessment referral
Monitor Braden Scale score regularly
Expect CBC if infection suspected
management: Reposition every 2 hours on a turning schedule
Use pressure-redistribution surfaces (mattresses, cushions)
Maintain nutritional support (high protein, vitamin C) as ordered
Keep skin clean and dry (moisture barrier cream for incontinence)
Follow wound care protocol as ordered for each stage
Avoid positioning directly on the wound
nursingActions: Perform skin assessments every shift and document findings
Reposition patient every 2 hours and document position changes
Assess and document wound characteristics (size, depth, drainage, odor)
Monitor nutritional intake and report inadequate intake
Keep linens wrinkle-free to reduce friction
Report any skin changes or wound deterioration immediately
Educate patient and caregivers on pressure injury prevention

tinea-corporis.title: Tinea Corporis (Kırmızı Mantar)
tinea-corporis.pathophysiology: Superficial dermatophyte fungal infection. Dermatophytes digest keratin in skin. Spread by direct contact or fomites.
diagnostics: Expect KOH preparation of skin scraping for fungal hyphae
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection of lesions
Expect fungal culture if diagnosis uncertain
Monitor treatment response over 2-4 weeks
management: Keep affected area clean and dry
Apply topical antifungal as prescribed (extend 1 inch beyond border)
Avoid sharing personal items during treatment
Wash clothing and linens in hot water
Complete full course of treatment even if symptoms resolve
nursingActions: Assess lesion characteristics (size, borders, scaling)
Educate patient on proper application of topical medication
Report signs of secondary infection (increased redness, warmth, pus)
Teach infection prevention measures to family
Assess for lesions on other body areas (scalp, feet, groin)

oral-candidiasis.title: Oral Kandidiyaz (Pamukçuk)
oral-candidiasis.pathophysiology: Candida albicans overgrowth, opportunistic. Common in immunocompromised, infants, and inhaled corticosteroid users.
diagnostics: Expect visual oral examination for white patches
Monitor for spread to esophagus (dysphagia indicates esophageal involvement)
Expect KOH preparation if diagnosis uncertain
Assess immune status if recurrent episodes
management: Administer nystatin suspension as prescribed (swish and swallow)
Rinse mouth after inhaled corticosteroid use to prevent occurrence
Sterilize pacifiers and bottle nipples for infants
Maintain good oral hygiene
Remove dentures for antifungal treatment
nursingActions: Assess oral cavity for white patches on tongue and buccal mucosa
Educate on proper nystatin administration technique (hold in mouth before swallowing)
Report difficulty swallowing to provider (may indicate esophageal spread)
Remind patients using inhaled corticosteroids to rinse mouth after each use
Monitor nutritional intake if oral discomfort affects eating

oral-candidiasis-rn.title: Oral Kandidiyaz
oral-candidiasis-rn.pathophysiology:
Candida albicans is a dimorphic fungus that normally colonizes the oral cavity as a commensal yeast form. Under conditions of immune suppression or microbial dysbiosis, Candida undergoes a morphogenetic switch from yeast to hyphal form, which is critical for tissue invasion. The hyphal form produces invasive filaments that penetrate the epithelial barrier, triggering mucosal inflammation and the characteristic white pseudomembranous plaques.

Mucosal immune defense against Candida depends heavily on the T-helper 17 (Th17) cell axis. Th17 cells produce interleukin-17 (IL-17) and IL-22, which recruit neutrophils to the oral mucosa and stimulate epithelial cells to produce antimicrobial peptides such as β-defensins and histatins. When Th17 immunity is compromised  -  as in HIV/AIDS, chemotherapy-induced lymphopenia, or chronic corticosteroid use  -  the IL-17 signaling pathway is disrupted, allowing Candida to proliferate unchecked and transition to its invasive hyphal form.

Biofilm formation is another key virulence mechanism. Candida adheres to mucosal surfaces and denture materials, forming structured biofilms that are resistant to host immune clearance and antifungal penetration. Biofilm-associated Candida can be up to 1000 times more resistant to fluconazole than planktonic cells. This is clinically significant in denture wearers and patients with prolonged hospitalization.

In immunocompromised patients, oropharyngeal candidiasis can progress to esophageal candidiasis (an AIDS-defining illness when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/μL) and, in severe cases, candidemia with hematogenous dissemination to deep organs including the liver, spleen, kidneys, and endocardium. The transition from localized mucosal infection to systemic candidiasis carries significant mortality (30-40% for candidemia) and requires urgent systemic antifungal therapy.
diagnostics: Perform thorough oral examination documenting distribution and morphology of lesions
Differentiate pseudomembranous (white plaques) from erythematous (red atrophic) and angular cheilitis forms
Expect KOH preparation showing budding yeast and pseudohyphae
Monitor for dysphagia or odynophagia indicating esophageal extension
Assess CD4 count in HIV patients (esophageal candidiasis is AIDS-defining below 200)
Expect fungal culture with speciation for recurrent or refractory cases
Monitor hepatic function if systemic antifungals initiated
management: Administer nystatin suspension as prescribed for mild cases (swish and swallow technique)
Administer fluconazole as prescribed for moderate-severe or recurrent cases
Ensure mouth rinsing after each inhaled corticosteroid dose to prevent recurrence
Remove and disinfect dentures nightly with chlorhexidine or dilute bleach solution
Address underlying immunosuppression where possible
Monitor for esophageal extension and report dysphagia immediately
Implement oral hygiene protocol including gentle tooth brushing and antimicrobial rinses
nursingActions: Perform comprehensive oral assessment each shift documenting location, size, and type of lesions
Assess for clinical signs differentiating immunocompromised vs immunocompetent presentation
Monitor nutritional intake and caloric adequacy when oral discomfort limits eating
Educate on proper nystatin technique (hold in mouth 2-5 minutes before swallowing)
Assess for signs of systemic dissemination in high-risk patients (fever, hemodynamic instability)
Report new-onset dysphagia or odynophagia immediately as potential esophageal candidiasis
Coordinate with pharmacy regarding drug interactions if fluconazole initiated (CYP3A4 inhibitor)
Educate immunocompromised patients on daily oral self-inspection

oral-candidiasis-np.title: Oral Kandidiyaz
oral-candidiasis-np.pathophysiology:
Candida albicans pathogenicity is driven by a coordinated arsenal of virulence factors at the molecular level. Secreted aspartyl proteases (SAPs 1-10) are a family of extracellular enzymes that degrade host mucosal proteins including E-cadherin, secretory IgA, complement components, and extracellular matrix proteins. SAPs 1-3 are predominantly expressed during mucosal infection and facilitate tissue invasion by disrupting epithelial integrity. SAP expression is regulated by environmental pH and nutrient availability, with acidic conditions favoring SAP2 production.

Agglutinin-like sequence (ALS) adhesins, particularly ALS3, mediate initial attachment to host epithelial cells and are essential for biofilm initiation. ALS3 functions as an invasin that triggers receptor-mediated endocytosis by binding to E-cadherin and N-cadherin on host cells, enabling Candida to actively penetrate epithelial barriers without requiring hyphal mechanical force alone.

The yeast-to-hypha morphogenetic switch is governed by multiple signaling pathways including the cAMP-PKA pathway, MAP kinase cascades, and transcription factors (Efg1, Cph1, Rim101). Environmental cues such as temperature (37°C), serum, CO₂ levels, neutral pH, and N-acetylglucosamine trigger this transition. The hyphal form expresses hypha-specific genes including HWP1 (hyphal wall protein 1), which serves as a substrate for host transglutaminases, covalently bonding the fungus to epithelial cells.

Azole resistance in Candida involves multiple mechanisms: (1) upregulation of ERG11 encoding the target enzyme lanosterol 14α-demethylase, (2) point mutations in ERG11 reducing drug binding affinity, (3) overexpression of efflux pumps (CDR1/CDR2 belonging to the ABC transporter family and MDR1 of the major facilitator superfamily), and (4) biofilm-mediated resistance through extracellular matrix sequestration of drug molecules. Echinocandin resistance develops through mutations in FKS1 and FKS2 genes encoding the catalytic subunit of β-1,3-glucan synthase, the drug target. These hot-spot mutations (particularly S645 in FKS1) reduce enzyme sensitivity to echinocandins by 50-3000 fold.

Non-albicans species (C. glabrata, C. krusei, C. auris) are increasingly prevalent in immunocompromised populations. C. krusei has intrinsic fluconazole resistance, C. glabrata frequently develops acquired azole resistance, and C. auris presents a global emerging threat with multi-drug resistance including resistance to all three major antifungal classes.
diagnostics: Perform oral examination with speciation request on fungal culture (not just C. albicans)
Order antifungal susceptibility testing for recurrent or refractory cases
Evaluate CD4 count, HIV viral load, and complete immune panel
Order upper endoscopy (EGD) for suspected esophageal candidiasis with biopsy and brushings
Consider β-D-glucan assay and blood cultures if invasive candidiasis suspected
Assess hepatic and renal function before initiating systemic antifungals
Review medication list for CYP450 interactions with azoles
Order T-cell subset analysis if chronic mucocutaneous candidiasis suspected
management: Prescribe fluconazole 200 mg loading dose followed by 100-200 mg daily for 7-14 days for oropharyngeal disease
For esophageal candidiasis: fluconazole 400 mg loading then 200-400 mg daily for 14-21 days
For azole-refractory disease: prescribe itraconazole oral solution 200 mg daily or voriconazole 200 mg BID
For echinocandin-eligible refractory cases: micafungin 150 mg IV daily or caspofungin 70 mg load then 50 mg IV daily
Initiate secondary prophylaxis with fluconazole 100 mg daily in HIV patients until CD4 > 200 sustained
Address predisposing factors: optimize glycemic control, reduce immunosuppression when possible, manage xerostomia
For denture-associated candidiasis: prescribe miconazole denture lacquer or chlorhexidine soaks plus systemic therapy
Consider therapeutic drug monitoring for voriconazole (target trough 1-5.5 μg/mL)
nursingActions: Coordinate antifungal susceptibility testing and follow up on speciation results
Monitor for azole-associated hepatotoxicity (LFTs at baseline and 2 weeks)
Assess for QTc prolongation with azole therapy (baseline and follow-up ECG)
Monitor for echinocandin infusion reactions (histamine-mediated flushing)
Ensure appropriate dose adjustments for renal and hepatic impairment
Educate patients on long-term prophylaxis adherence and signs of breakthrough infection
Coordinate with infectious disease for refractory cases or multi-drug resistant species
Monitor for drug-drug interactions with azoles (warfarin, statins, calcineurin inhibitors, anticonvulsants)

cdiff-basics.title: C. Difficile Enfeksiyonu
cdiff-basics.pathophysiology: Clostridioides difficile produces toxins A and B causing colitis. Overgrowth after antibiotic use disrupts normal flora. Spore-forming organism.
diagnostics: Expect stool sample for C. diff toxin assay
Monitor stool frequency, consistency, and volume
Expect CBC (leukocytosis is common)
Monitor for dehydration indicators (electrolytes, BUN/creatinine)
Expect abdominal X-ray if toxic megacolon suspected
management: Implement contact precautions immediately
Use soap and water for hand hygiene (alcohol does NOT kill spores)
Use bleach-based disinfectant for environmental cleaning
Discontinue offending antibiotics if possible
Avoid antidiarrheals (trap toxins in the colon)
Maintain hydration and electrolyte replacement
nursingActions: Monitor stool frequency, amount, and characteristics
Implement strict contact precautions with dedicated equipment
Perform hand hygiene with soap and water before and after care
Monitor for dehydration (skin turgor, mucous membranes, urine output)
Report signs of toxic megacolon (severe distension, fever, tachycardia)
Educate patient and visitors on contact precautions and hand hygiene

pertussis-basics.title: Boğmaca (Kuşkulu Öksürük)
pertussis-basics.pathophysiology: Bordetella pertussis toxin damages respiratory cilia. Three stages: catarrhal (most contagious, URI symptoms), paroxysmal (severe coughing fits with inspiratory whoop), convalescent (gradual recovery).
diagnostics: Expect nasopharyngeal swab for PCR testing
Monitor oxygen saturation especially in infants
Expect CBC (lymphocytosis is characteristic)
Monitor respiratory status for apnea episodes in infants
Expect chest X-ray if pneumonia suspected
management: Implement droplet precautions until 5 days of antibiotic therapy completed
Administer prescribed azithromycin as ordered
Maintain oxygen therapy as needed
Monitor respiratory status closely in infants
Isolate patient from vulnerable populations
Ensure post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts
nursingActions: Implement droplet precautions and place patient in private room
Monitor respiratory status including oxygen saturation
Report apnea episodes in infants immediately
Suction secretions gently as needed
Provide calm environment to minimize coughing paroxysms
Educate on completing full course of antibiotics
Verify immunization status of patient and close contacts

atopic-dermatitis.title: Atopik Dermatit (Egzama)
atopic-dermatitis.pathophysiology: Chronic inflammatory skin condition. Impaired skin barrier (filaggrin deficiency) allows allergen penetration. IgE-mediated hypersensitivity. Part of atopic triad (asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema).
diagnostics: Expect clinical diagnosis based on presentation and history
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization)
Expect allergy testing if triggers unclear
Monitor serum IgE levels (often elevated)
Assess skin integrity with each visit
management: Apply emollients immediately after bathing (first-line treatment)
Use topical corticosteroids as prescribed for flares
Avoid known triggers (harsh soaps, wool, extreme temperatures)
Use lukewarm baths (not hot water)
Dress in cotton clothing
Keep fingernails short to prevent excoriation
nursingActions: Assess skin for erythema, scaling, lichenification, and excoriation
Educate on emollient application technique (apply within 3 minutes of bathing)
Report signs of secondary infection (increased redness, weeping, crusting)
Teach trigger avoidance strategies to patient and family
Monitor for side effects of topical corticosteroids (skin thinning)
Provide emotional support for chronic condition management

head-lice.title: Saç Bitleri (Pedikülozis Kapitis)
head-lice.pathophysiology: Pediculosis capitis is an infestation of the scalp by the human head louse (Pediculus humanus capitis). The louse is an obligate ectoparasite that feeds on human blood from the scalp, causing intense pruritus from an allergic reaction to louse saliva. Female lice lay eggs (nits) that cement firmly to the hair shaft near the scalp. Nits hatch in 7-10 days, and the louse life cycle is approximately 30 days. Transmission occurs primarily through direct head-to-head contact and less commonly through shared personal items (combs, hats, pillows). Head lice do NOT transmit disease and are not a sign of poor hygiene.
diagnostics: Visual inspection of scalp for nits (eggs) and live lice
Assess behind ears and at nape of neck (preferred sites)
Monitor for secondary excoriation from scratching
Assess family members and close contacts
management: Apply pediculicide shampoo as prescribed (permethrin)
Remove nits with fine-toothed comb after treatment
Wash all bedding and clothing in hot water (130°F)
Vacuum upholstered furniture and carpets
Repeat treatment in 7-10 days as directed
nursingActions: Educate family on proper application of pediculicide
Teach nit removal technique with fine-toothed comb
Instruct on environmental decontamination (bedding, brushes)
Educate that head lice do not jump or fly (direct contact transmission)
Screen household contacts for infestation
Reassure family that head lice are not a sign of poor hygiene

pinworms.title: Kıl Kurtları (Enterobiyazis)
pinworms.pathophysiology: Enterobiasis is caused by Enterobius vermicularis, a small white parasitic nematode (roundworm) that infects the human intestinal tract. The infection cycle begins with ingestion of microscopic pinworm eggs via the fecal-oral route (contaminated hands, food, or fomites). Eggs hatch in the small intestine, and larvae migrate to the cecum and appendix where they mature into adult worms. At night, gravid female worms migrate to the perianal area to deposit thousands of eggs, causing intense perianal pruritus. Scratching deposits eggs under fingernails, perpetuating the cycle through autoinoculation and person-to-person transmission. Pinworm is the most common helminth infection in the United States, predominantly affecting school-age children.
diagnostics: Tape test (transparent tape applied to perianal area first thing in morning)
Assess for perianal itching especially at night
Monitor for signs of secondary skin infection from scratching
management: Administer mebendazole or albendazole as prescribed
Treat all household members simultaneously
Wash all bedding and clothing in hot water
Maintain strict hand hygiene before meals and after toileting
Repeat treatment in 2 weeks as ordered
nursingActions: Educate family on tape test collection technique (early morning before bathing)
Teach thorough hand hygiene before eating and after toileting
Instruct on keeping fingernails short to prevent reinfection
Educate on washing bedding in hot water daily during treatment
Teach that all household members should be treated simultaneously
Monitor for perianal excoriation and provide skin care

diaper-candidiasis.title: Bez Kandidiyazı (Bez Mantar Enfeksiyonu)
diaper-candidiasis.pathophysiology: Diaper candidiasis is a superficial fungal infection of the perineal skin caused by Candida albicans, an opportunistic yeast that thrives in warm, moist, occluded environments such as the diaper area. Candida is part of normal GI flora and colonizes the perineum; overgrowth occurs when the skin barrier is compromised by prolonged moisture exposure, friction, and alkaline pH from urine and stool. The hallmark feature distinguishing candidal diaper dermatitis from irritant contact diaper rash is the presence of satellite lesions (small papules and pustules surrounding the main erythematous area) and involvement of skin folds (intertriginous areas). Candidal diaper rash typically develops after 3+ days of existing diaper rash that has not responded to standard barrier creams.
diagnostics: Visual assessment of rash (beefy red with satellite lesions)
Differentiate from irritant diaper dermatitis (sharp borders vs. diffuse)
Assess oral cavity for concurrent thrush
Monitor for secondary bacterial infection
management: Apply antifungal cream as prescribed (nystatin or clotrimazole)
Change diapers frequently to keep area dry
Allow diaper-free time for air exposure
Gently cleanse with mild soap and warm water
Avoid occlusive barrier creams over antifungal
nursingActions: Assess rash characteristics (beefy red, satellite lesions indicate Candida)
Apply antifungal medication before barrier cream
Educate parents on frequent diaper changes
Teach gentle cleansing technique (no wipes with alcohol/fragrance)
Monitor for improvement with treatment
Assess mouth for concurrent oral thrush (treat simultaneously)
